topics.title: Introduction - Environmental Legislation and Regulation - Water Quality Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT404 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction - Environmental Legislation and Regulation - Water Quality Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Environmental Engineering — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Environmental Engineering** is the branch of engineering concerned with the application of scientific and engineering principles to **protect human health, safeguard the natural environment, and improve the quality of life** by managing water, air, land, and noise resources. It encompasses the design, construction, and operation of systems that prevent, control, and remediate pollution.

As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, Environmental Engineering in the Civil Engineering context specifically covers:

- **Water Supply Engineering** – sourcing, treatment, and distribution of potable water
- **Wastewater Engineering (Sanitation)** – collection, treatment, and disposal of sewage
- **Air Pollution Control** – monitoring and abatement of atmospheric contaminants
- **Solid & Hazardous Waste Management** – collection, processing, and disposal
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** – predicting the environmental consequences of projects

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Housekeeping for a City" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box:** Think of Environmental Engineering as the *housekeeping and life-support system* of a city or an industry.
> 
> - **Water Supply** is the *kitchen* — bringing clean, drinkable water in.
> - **Wastewater Engineering** is the *bathroom plumbing* — taking dirty water out and cleaning it.
> - **Air Pollution Control** is the *ventilation* — keeping the air inside fresh and breathable.
> - **Solid Waste Management** is the *garbage disposal* — taking care of the trash responsibly.
> 
> Just as a body has kidneys, lungs, and a digestive system, a city needs Environmental Engineers to keep its "vital organs" functioning. Without them, the city "falls sick" — polluted rivers, smog-filled skies, and disease outbreaks.

### 1.3 Water Quality Standards — Why Do We Need Them?

Water quality standards are **numerical limits and qualitative criteria** that define what makes water "safe" for a given use (drinking, bathing, industrial, irrigation). They exist because:

1. **Public Health Protection** – Waterborne diseases (cholera, typhoid) claim millions of lives globally.
2. **Ecological Protection** – Aquatic life requires specific dissolved oxygen and pH conditions.
3. **Industrial Process Protection** – Boiler feed, cooling water, and pharmaceutical waters have strict purity needs.
4. **Legal Accountability** – Standards provide the legal yardstick for prosecution and compensation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard-Setting Bodies:**
> - **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** – IS 10500:2012 (Drinking Water)
> - **WHO (World Health Organization)** – Global Guidelines for Drinking Water
> - **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board)** – Effluent discharge standards
> - **USEPA** – United States benchmarks
> - **EC** – European Commission Drinking Water Directive

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Environmental Legislation in India — The Legal Framework

India has evolved a **comprehensive legal architecture** for environmental protection, primarily post-1970s, triggered by the **Stockholm Conference (1972)** and the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Indian Environmental Law:**
> 
> 1. **Constitutional Provisions** – Article 48A (State duty to protect environment), Article 51A(g) (Fundamental Duty of citizens)
> 2. **Umbrella Act** – Environment (Protection) Act, 1986
> 3. **Sector-Specific Acts** – Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981
> 4. **Rules & Notifications** – Hazardous Waste Rules, MSW Rules, EIA Notification
> 5. **Judicial Bodies** – National Green Tribunal (NGT), 2010

#### 2.1.1 Major Environmental Acts (Chronological)

| **Act / Rule** | **Year** | **Key Provisions & Salient Features** |
|---|---|---|
| Water (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act | **1974** | Created CPCB & SPCBs; prohibits discharge of pollutants into streams/wells without consent |
| Water Cess Act | **1977** | Imposes cess on water consumed by industries to fund pollution control |
| Air (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act | **1981** | First legislation to combat air pollution; established air quality standards |
| Environment (Protection) Act | **1986** | **Umbrella Act**; empowers Centre to coordinate environmental matters post-Bhopal |
| Hazardous Waste Management Rules | **1989** | Regulates handling, storage, transport of hazardous waste |
| Biomedical Waste Management Rules | **2016** | Mandates segregation, treatment, disposal of hospital waste |
| Solid Waste Management Rules | **2016** | Replaces MSW Rules 2000; promotes 3Rs (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle) |
| EIA Notification | **2006** | Mandates Environmental Impact Assessment for listed projects |
| National Green Tribunal Act | **2010** | Fast-track judicial body for environmental disputes |

#### 2.1.2 Key Regulatory Bodies

- **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board)** – Apex body at the national level, sets standards, advises Central Govt.
- **SPCB (State Pollution Control Board)** – Implements at state level, grants/refuses **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)**.
- **MoEFCC** – Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change.
- **NGT (National Green Tribunal)** – Adjudicates environmental cases; orders are binding and rarely appealable.

### 2.2 Water Quality Parameters — The Triad

Water quality is assessed under **three categories** of parameters:

#### 2.2.1 Physical Parameters
- **Turbidity** – Suspended matter (clay, silt, organic matter). Measured in **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)**.
- **Color** – True color (dissolved) vs. apparent color (suspended). Measured on **Hazen Scale** (Pt-Co units).
- **Odor and Taste** – Caused by dissolved gases (H₂S), organic matter, minerals.
- **Temperature** – Affects dissolved oxygen, biological activity, and chemical reactions.
- **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** – Filterable residue, mg/L.

#### 2.2.2 Chemical Parameters
- **pH** – Measure of H⁺ ion activity. Natural waters: 6.5–8.5.
- **Alkalinity** – Capacity to neutralize acids; primarily due to HCO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻, OH⁻.
- **Hardness** – Concentration of divalent cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺). Expressed as **mg/L of CaCO₃**.
- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** – Vital for aquatic life; surface waters > 5 mg/L acceptable.
- **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** – Oxygen consumed by microbes in 5 days at 20°C. **BOD₅ standard: < 3 mg/L for drinking**.
- **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)** – Oxygen equivalent of organic matter oxidized by strong oxidant.
- **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids)** – Salts and minerals in solution.
- **Chlorides, Sulfates, Nitrates, Fluorides** – Specific ion concentrations.
- **Heavy Metals** – Lead, Arsenic, Cadmium, Mercury, Chromium (all toxic at trace levels).

#### 2.2.3 Biological Parameters
- **Total Coliforms** – Indicator organism for fecal contamination.
- **Fecal Coliforms / E. coli** – More specific indicator of recent fecal pollution.
- **Pathogens** – Bacteria (cholera, typhoid), viruses, protozoa (Giardia, Cryptosporidium).

### 2.3 Water Quality Standards — The Numbers that Matter

#### 2.3.1 IS 10500:2012 — Indian Standard Drinking Water Specification

| **Parameter** | **Requirement (Acceptable Limit)** | **Permissible Limit (No Alternate Source)** |
|---|---|---|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 | No relaxation |
| Turbidity (NTU) | 1 | 5 |
| Total Hardness (mg/L as CaCO₃) | 200 | 600 |
| Total Dissolved Solids (mg/L) | 500 | 2000 |
| Iron (mg/L) | 0.3 | 1.0 |
| Chlorides (mg/L) | 250 | 1000 |
| Fluoride (mg/L) | 1.0 | 1.5 |
| Nitrates (mg/L) | 45 | No relaxation |
| Total Coliforms (MPN/100 mL) | 0 | 0 |
| E. coli | 0 | 0 |

#### 2.3.2 WHO Guidelines (4th Edition, 2017) — Key Limits

| **Parameter** | **Guideline Value** |
|---|---|
| Arsenic (mg/L) | 0.01 |
| Lead (mg/L) | 0.01 |
| Cadmium (mg/L) | 0.003 |
| Mercury (mg/L) | 0.006 |
| Fluoride (mg/L) | 1.5 |
| Nitrate (mg/L) | 50 |
| E. coli / Thermotolerant coliforms | 0 CFU/100 mL |

#### 2.3.3 CPCB Effluent Discharge Standards (Selected)

| **Industry** | **pH** | **BOD (mg/L)** | **COD (mg/L)** | **TSS (mg/L)** | **Oil & Grease (mg/L)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Domestic Sewage (to rivers) | 5.5–9.0 | 30 | 250 | 100 | 10 |
| Petroleum Refinery | 6.0–8.5 | 30 | 250 | 100 | 10 |
| Sugar Industry | 5.5–9.0 | 30 | 250 | 100 | 10 |
| Distillery | 5.5–9.0 | 30 | 250 | 450 | 10 |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Water Quality Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-yield for KTU exams. Master these.**

**1. Equivalent Weight (E):**

$$E = \frac{\text{Atomic Weight}}{\text{Valency}}$$

**2. Milliequivalents per Litre (meq/L):**

$$\text{meq/L} = \frac{\text{Concentration in mg/L}}{\text{Equivalent Weight of the substance}}$$

**3. Concentration in meq/L from mg/L as CaCO₃:**

$$\text{meq/L} = \frac{\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3}{50}$$

*(50 = equivalent weight of CaCO₃)*

**4. Conversion of ion concentration to "as CaCO₃" form:**

$$\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 = \text{mg/L of ion} \times \frac{50}{\text{Equivalent weight of ion}}$$

**5. Hardness Relationship:**

$$\text{Total Hardness} = 2.5 \times \text{[Ca}^{2+}\text{]} + 4.1 \times \text{[Mg}^{2+}\text{]}$$

*(where ion concentrations are in mg/L as CaCO₃ equivalent)*

**6. Molarity–Normality Relationship:**

$$N = M \times n \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Normality} = \text{Molarity} \times \text{Valency Factor}$$

**7. BOD Dilution Formula (BOD₅ at 20°C):**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{D_1 - D_2}{P}$$

where $D_1$ = initial DO of diluted sample, $D_2$ = final DO after 5 days, $P$ = dilution factor (decimal fraction).

**8. Specific Conductance to TDS (approximate):**

$$\text{TDS (mg/L)} \approx 0.55 \text{ to } 0.75 \times \text{EC (\mu S/cm)}$$

**9. Saturated DO (at sea level, 1 atm):**

$$\text{DO}_{\text{sat}} \approx 14.6 - 0.41T + 0.008T^2 - 0.000018T^3$$

(where T is in °C; at 20°C, DOₛₐₜ ≈ 9.2 mg/L)

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Constants Used:**
> - Equivalent weight of Ca²⁺ = 20; Mg²⁺ = 12.15
> - 1 m³ = 1000 L; 1 mg/L = 1 ppm (in dilute aqueous solutions)
> - 1 NTU = 1 FTU (Farrentine Turbidity Unit)
> - Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = **50 g/eq**
> - MPN: Most Probable Number (statistical estimate of bacterial density)

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Systems

- **Drinking Water Treatment Plants (DWTPs)** are designed to meet IS 10500:2012 limits, requiring the full train of coagulation-flocculation, sedimentation, filtration, and disinfection.
- **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** must comply with CPCB discharge norms to receive operating consent from SPCB.
- **Environmental Audits** use these standards to identify non-compliance and avoid prosecution under EPA 1986.
- **EIA Reports** cite these limits as the baseline against which predicted impacts are assessed.
- **Court Cases & NGT Orders** rely on these numerical standards to deliver judgments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expressing Ion Concentration "as CaCO₃"

**Why do engineers express hardness, alkalinity, and other ion concentrations "as CaCO₃"?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Different ions have different weights but contribute to the same chemical property (e.g., hardness). Expressing them all "as CaCO₃" is like converting all currencies to USD — it allows easy comparison and arithmetic. CaCO₃ is chosen because its equivalent weight (50) is a clean number.

**Derivation:**

Consider a divalent ion $X^{2+}$ with atomic mass $A_X$ and valency $2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Equivalent weight of } X^{2+} &= \frac{A_X}{2} \\[6pt]
\text{By definition, 1 meq/L of } X^{2+} &= \frac{A_X/2}{1000} \text{ g/L} \times 1000 \text{ mg/g} = \frac{A_X}{2} \text{ mg/L} \\[6pt]
\text{Also, 1 meq/L of CaCO}_3 &= \frac{50}{1000} \times 1000 = 50 \text{ mg/L} \\[6pt]
\text{Since meq/L is conserved in a chemical reaction:}\\[6pt]
\text{1 meq/L of } X^{2+} &= \text{1 meq/L of CaCO}_3 \\[6pt]
\therefore \frac{A_X}{2} \text{ mg/L of } X^{2+} &\equiv 50 \text{ mg/L of CaCO}_3 \\[6pt]
\therefore 1 \text{ mg/L of } X^{2+} &\equiv 50 \times \frac{2}{A_X} \text{ mg/L of CaCO}_3 \\[6pt]
\text{General formula: } \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 &= \text{mg/L of ion} \times \frac{50}{\text{Equivalent weight of ion}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A water sample contains 40 mg/L of Ca²⁺ and 12 mg/L of Mg²⁺. Express hardness as CaCO₃.

For Ca²⁺ (atomic weight = 40, valency = 2, equivalent weight = 20):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ca}^{2+} \text{ as CaCO}_3 &= 40 \times \frac{50}{20} = 40 \times 2.5 = 100 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

For Mg²⁺ (atomic weight = 24.3, valency = 2, equivalent weight = 12.15):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mg}^{2+} \text{ as CaCO}_3 &= 12 \times \frac{50}{12.15} = 12 \times 4.115 = 49.38 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Total Hardness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TH} &= 100 + 49.38 = 149.38 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3 \approx 150 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification:** Since 150 mg/L falls between 75 and 150, water is **moderately hard**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Molarity and Normality in Water Analysis

The molarity of a solution is the number of moles per litre; normality is the number of equivalents per litre. For acid-base titrations commonly used in water analysis (e.g., titration of H₂SO₄ with NaOH for alkalinity):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 V_1 &= N_2 V_2 \quad \text{(for a neutralization reaction)} \\[6pt]
M \times \text{Valency Factor} \times V_1 &= M \times \text{Valency Factor} \times V_2 \\[6pt]
\therefore N_1 V_1 &= N_2 V_2 \quad \text{(equivalence at neutralization point)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** If 25 mL of 0.1 N H₂SO₄ is required to neutralize 50 mL of NaOH solution, find the normality of NaOH.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 V_1 &= N_2 V_2 \\[6pt]
0.1 \times 25 &= N_2 \times 50 \\[6pt]
N_2 &= \frac{0.1 \times 25}{50} = 0.05 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Alkalinity Calculation from Titration

Alkalinity is measured by titrating water with standard H₂SO₄ using phenolphthalein (pH 8.3) and methyl orange (pH 4.5) indicators.

If $P$ = phenolphthalein endpoint titre and $T$ = total titre (to methyl orange), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Phenolphthalein Alkalinity (P)} &= \frac{P \times N \times 50000}{V_{\text{sample}}} \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3 \\[6pt]
\text{Total Alkalinity (T)} &= \frac{T \times N \times 50000}{V_{\text{sample}}} \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

where $N$ is the normality of H₂SO₄ and 50,000 = 50 × 1000 (50 = equivalent weight of CaCO₃, 1000 = mg/g).

**Numerical Example:** 100 mL water sample, titrated with 0.02 N H₂SO₄. Phenolphthalein endpoint at 5 mL; Methyl orange endpoint at 18 mL.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Phenolphthalein Alkalinity} &= \frac{5 \times 0.02 \times 50000}{100} = \frac{5000}{100} = 50 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3 \\[6pt]
\text{Total Alkalinity} &= \frac{18 \times 0.02 \times 50000}{100} = \frac{18000}{100} = 180 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3 \\[6pt]
\text{Therefore Hydroxide Alkalinity} &= 0 \text{ (since } P < T/2 \text{)} \\[6pt]
\text{Carbonate Alkalinity} &= 2P = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3 \\[6pt]
\text{Bicarbonate Alkalinity} &= T - 2P = 180 - 100 = 80 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Specific Conductance and TDS

Electrical Conductivity (EC) measures the ability of water to conduct electricity, which depends on dissolved ionic species. A practical empirical relationship (used in field testing):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TDS (mg/L)} = k \times \text{EC (\mu S/cm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k$ is a multiplier depending on ionic composition:
- For natural waters (mixed ions): $k \approx 0.55$ to $0.70$
- For NaCl-dominated waters: $k \approx 0.5$

**Numerical Example:** A water sample has EC = 1200 μS/cm. Estimate TDS using $k = 0.64$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TDS} &= 0.64 \times 1200 = 768 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since 768 mg/L > 500 mg/L but < 2000 mg/L, water is **permissible** (IS 10500) but not ideal for drinking.

### 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Standards

| **Aspect** | **IS 10500:2012 (BIS)** | **WHO Guidelines** | **CPCB Effluent Standards** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Applicability** | Drinking water in India | Global benchmark | Industrial discharge in India |
| **Mandatory?** | Yes (under EPA 1986) | Advisory (not legally binding in India) | Yes (under Water Act 1974) |
| **Issued By** | Bureau of Indian Standards | World Health Organization | Central Pollution Control Board |
| **Year (Current)** | 2012 | 2017 (4th ed.) | Updated periodically |
| **TDS Limit (mg/L)** | 500 (acceptable) | 1000 | Not specified (industry-wise) |
| **Fluoride Limit (mg/L)** | 1.0 (acceptable) | 1.5 | Not applicable |
| **BOD Limit (mg/L)** | Not specified | Not specified | 30 (typical) |
| **Coliforms** | 0 MPN/100 mL | 0 CFU/100 mL | Industry-specific |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Scope of Environmental Engineering (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    EE[ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING]
    EE --> WS[Water Supply Engineering]
    EE --> WW[Wastewater Engineering]
    EE --> AP[Air Pollution Control]
    EE --> SW[Solid and Hazardous Waste]
    EE --> NPL[Noise Pollution]

    EE --> LM[Environmental Legislation and Management]

    WS --> WT1[Intake and Treatment]
    WS --> WD1[Distribution Networks]

    WW --> STC[Sewage Collection]
    WW --> STP[Treatment and Disposal]

    AP --> APC[Stationary Sources]
    AP --> APM[Mobile Sources]
    AP --> AIM[Air Quality Monitoring]
```

### 4.2 Classification of Water Quality Parameters

```mermaid
graph TD
    WQP[WATER QUALITY PARAMETERS]
    WQP --> PHY[PHYSICAL]
    WQP --> CHE[CHEMICAL]
    WQP --> BIO[BIOLOGICAL]

    PHY --> P1[Turbidity in NTU]
    PHY --> P2[Color in Hazen Units]
    PHY --> P3[Odor and Taste]
    PHY --> P4[Temperature in degree C]
    PHY --> P5[Total Suspended Solids]

    CHE --> C1[pH and Alkalinity]
    CHE --> C2[Hardness as CaCO3]
    CHE --> C3[DO and BOD and COD]
    CHE --> C4[TDS and EC]
    CHE --> C5[Chlorides and Nitrates and Fluorides]
    CHE --> C6[Heavy Metals Pb and As and Cd and Hg]

    BIO --> B1[Total Coliforms]
    BIO --> B2[Fecal Coliforms and E coli]
    BIO --> B3[Pathogens and Viruses and Protozoa]
```

```mermaid
graph TD

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define environmental engineering. List its major scope areas.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Environmental Engineering** is the branch of civil engineering that deals with the protection and improvement of the environment through the design, construction, and operation of systems for water supply, wastewater disposal, air quality management, solid waste handling, and noise abatement.

**Major Scope Areas:**

1. Water Supply Engineering — treatment and distribution of potable water
2. Wastewater Engineering — collection, treatment, and disposal of sewage
3. Air Pollution Control — monitoring and abatement
4. Solid & Hazardous Waste Management
5. Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)
6. Environmental Legislation and Management

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for scope listing)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Differentiate between IS 10500:2012 and WHO drinking water guidelines.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **IS 10500:2012 (BIS)** | **WHO Guidelines (2017)** |
|---|---|---|
| Issuing Body | Bureau of Indian Standards (India) | World Health Organization (Global) |
| Legal Status | Mandatory under EPA 1986 in India | Advisory, not legally binding |
| TDS Limit (mg/L) | 500 (acceptable) / 2000 (permissible) | 1000 |
| Fluoride Limit (mg/L) | 1.0 (acceptable) / 1.5 (permissible) | 1.5 |
| Approach | Two-tier: Acceptable + Permissible | Single guideline value |
| Context | Indian hydrogeology, climate, dietary habits | Global applicability |

*(3 marks — 1 each for basis of difference with 1 mark for key technical distinction)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 1):**
> - **DO NOT** write generic "environmental pollution is bad" essays — KTU values **specific numerical standards, act names, sections, and year-wise data**.
> - **Always cite** the year of the Act/Rule (e.g., "EPA 1986", not just "Environment Act").
> - For water quality parameters, **tabulate** the IS 10500 limits — examiners reward structured answers.
> - Avoid the trap of confusing **CPCB** with **SPCB** — CPCB is advisory, SPCB enforces.
> - For "Scope of EE" questions, **do not skip** EIA and Legislation — these are syllabus-specific.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q A. (a) [7 Marks] Explain the salient features of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974.**

**Model Answer:**

The Water Act, 1974, enacted by the Indian Parliament, is the first comprehensive legislation to control water pollution in India.

**1. Preamble and Object:**
- To prevent and control water pollution and to maintain or restore the wholesomeness of water in streams, rivers, and wells.

**2. Salient Features:**

- **Section 3 & 4 – Constitution of Boards:** Established the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** at the national level and **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** in each state.
- **Section 20 – Functions of CPCB:**
  - Advise the Central Government on water pollution matters
  - Coordinate state boards' activities
  - Lay down standards for streams and wells
  - Collect and disseminate information
- **Section 25 – Consent Requirement:** No person shall establish or operate any industry that discharges sewage/trade effluent without prior **Consent of the State Board** (Consent to Establish and Consent to Operate).
- **Section 26 – Refusal of Consent:** SPCB may refuse consent if discharge is likely to pollute the stream.
- **Section 39 & 40 – Powers of State Boards** to enter, inspect, and take samples.
- **Section 43 – Penalty:** Imprisonment up to 6 years and/or fine for contravention.
- **Section 24 – Prohibition of use of stream for disposal:** No water body can be used as a place for disposal of polluting matter beyond prescribed standards.

**3. Amendments:** Amended in **1988** to align with EPA 1986; powers enhanced.

*(7 marks — 2 for preamble, 3 for sections with features, 2 for amendment & significance)*

---

**Q A. (b) [7 Marks] List and briefly explain the major physical and chemical parameters used to assess water quality.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Significance & Standard (IS 10500)** |
|---|---|
| **Turbidity** | Suspended matter; measured in NTU. Limit: 1 NTU. Indicates treatment efficiency. |
| **Color** | True color (dissolved). Measured in Hazen units. Aesthetic indicator. |
| **Odor & Taste** | Caused by organic matter, gases (H₂S). Consumer acceptability. |
| **Temperature** | Affects DO, chemical reactions, aquatic life. |
| **pH** | H⁺ ion concentration. Acceptable: 6.5–8.5. Affects corrosion, disinfection. |
| **Alkalinity** | Capacity to neutralize acids (HCO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻). Important for coagulation. |
| **Hardness** | Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ salts. Expressed as CaCO₃. Limit: 200 mg/L. Causes scaling in boilers. |
| **DO (Dissolved Oxygen)** | Vital for aquatic life. > 5 mg/L acceptable. Affected by temperature. |
| **BOD** | Organic strength. Standard: < 3 mg/L. Indicates biodegradability. |
| **COD** | Total oxidizable matter. Faster than BOD test. |
| **TDS** | Dissolved salts. Limit: 500 mg/L. Affects taste, irrigation suitability. |
| **Chlorides** | Saline indicator. Limit: 250 mg/L. |
| **Fluorides** | Beneficial in small amounts (1 mg/L); harmful above 1.5 mg/L (skeletal fluorosis). |
| **Nitrates** | Indicates fecal pollution; methemoglobinemia in infants. Limit: 45 mg/L. |
| **Heavy Metals** | Pb, As, Cd, Hg — toxic at trace levels; bioaccumulative. |

*(7 marks — 1 mark per key parameter with limit and significance, plus tabulation bonus for structure)*

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q B. (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, and its key provisions.**

**Model Answer:**

The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, is often called the **"Umbrella Act"** of Indian environmental law, enacted in the aftermath of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984)**.

**1. Background:**
- Enacted under Article 253 of the Constitution, following the Stockholm Conference (1972).
- An "Act to provide for the protection and improvement of the environment and for matters connected therewith."

**2. Key Provisions:**

- **Section 3 – Power of Central Government:** The Centre has the power to take all necessary measures to protect and improve the environment and to prevent environmental pollution.
- **Section 5 – Power to give Directions:** Central Government can issue directions to any person, officer, or authority for compliance.
- **Section 6 – Rules on Standards:** Empowers the Centre to prescribe **environmental standards** for emissions and effluents.
- **Section 7 – Prohibition:** No person shall discharge any environmental pollutant exceeding prescribed standards.
- **Section 8 – Handling of Hazardous Substances:** Central Government may lay down procedures and safeguards for handling hazardous chemicals and wastes.
- **Section 10 – Power of Entry & Inspection:** Authorized officers can enter, inspect, and take samples for analysis.
- **Section 15 – Penalty:** Imprisonment up to 5 years and/or fine up to ₹1 lakh, with additional fine of ₹5,000 per day for continuing violations.

**3. Significance:**
- Single, comprehensive legislation covering all environmental media (water, air, soil).
- Empowered the creation of CPCB effluent standards, EIA Notification 2006, MSW Rules, Biomedical Waste Rules, and many more.
- **Deficiency:** Provides for criminal liability but limited civil remedies; led to the creation of the **National Green Tribunal (NGT) in 2010**.

*(7 marks — 2 for background, 3 for sections, 2 for significance and limitations)*

---

**Q B. (b) [7 Marks] Explain the IS 10500:2012 specifications for drinking water with reference to key parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

The **IS 10500:2012** – "Drinking Water – Specification" – is published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**. It is a legally enforceable standard in India under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.

**1. Structure of the Standard:**
- Specifies **two-tier limits** for most parameters:
  - **Acceptable Limit** – The ideal value for consumption.
  - **Permissible Limit** – Used only when an alternate source is unavailable.

**2. Key Parameters and Limits:**

| **Parameter** | **Acceptable Limit** | **Permissible Limit** |
|---|---|---|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 | No relaxation |
| Turbidity (NTU) | 1 | 5 |
| Total Hardness (mg/L as CaCO₃) | 200 | 600 |
| Iron (mg/L) | 0.3 | 1.0 |
| Chlorides (mg/L) | 250 | 1000 |
| Fluoride (mg/L) | 1.0 | 1.5 |
| TDS (mg/L) | 500 | 2000 |
| Nitrates (mg/L) | 45 | No relaxation |
| Total Coliforms (MPN/100 mL) | 0 | 0 |
| E. coli | 0 | 0 |

**3. Significance of Specific Parameters:**

- **pH (6.5–8.5):** Outside this range, water becomes corrosive or scaling. Also affects disinfection efficiency.
- **Fluoride (1.0–1.5 mg/L):** Below 0.5 mg/L → dental caries; Above 1.5 mg/L → skeletal/dental fluorosis.
- **Nitrates (45 mg/L):** Above this causes **methemoglobinemia** (blue baby syndrome) in infants.
- **Total Coliforms (0 MPN/100 mL):** Indicator of fecal contamination; presence implies pathogenic risk.
- **TDS (500 mg/L):** Affects taste; above 2000 mg/L makes water unpalatable and causes scaling in pipes.

**4. Sampling & Testing:**
- BIS specifies sampling frequency, preservation methods, and analytical procedures.
- Tests are conducted at recognized labs; results used to issue potability certificates.

*(7 marks — 1 for structure, 3 for tabulated standards, 2 for significance, 1 for sampling reference)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

- **Environmental Engineering** = Water + Wastewater + Air + Solid Waste + Noise + EIA + Legislation. Know all **seven scope areas**.
- **Water Act 1974** → CPCB + SPCB formed; **Consent to Establish & Operate** mandatory.
- **Air Act 1981** → First air pollution law; extended to noise in 1987.
- **Environment Protection Act 1986** → **Umbrella Act** post-Bhopal; **Section 3** is the most powerful clause.
- **Constitution of India** → **Article 48A** (State duty) + **Article 51A(g)** (Fundamental Duty).
- **CPCB** = Central Pollution Control Board (advisory, sets standards).
- **SPCB** = State Pollution Control Board (implements, grants consent).
- **NGT (2010)** = National Green Tribunal for fast-track environmental justice.
- **IS 10500:2012** = Indian Drinking Water Standard; **two-tier** acceptable + permissible limits.
- **WHO** = Global advisory, not legally binding in India.
- **Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = 50** (memorize this — used in all hardness & alkalinity problems).
- **Conversion formula:** mg/L as CaCO₃ = (mg/L of ion) × (50 / equivalent weight of ion).
- **Hardness conversion factors:** Ca²⁺ → 2.5, Mg²⁺ → 4.115.
- **BOD dilution formula:** BOD₅ = (D₁ − D₂) / P.
- **TDS estimate from EC:** TDS ≈ 0.55 to 0.75 × EC (μS/cm).
- **Saturated DO at 20°C ≈ 9.2 mg/L** (sea level).
- **Fluoride sweet spot:** 0.5–1.5 mg/L; harmful above 1.5.
- **Nitrate limit:** 45 mg/L (no relaxation) — causes blue baby syndrome.
- **MPN/100 mL for coliforms** = 0 in drinking water (no relaxation).
- **Mandatory tests in water analysis:** pH, turbidity, total hardness, TDS, chlorides, residual chlorine, coliforms.
- **E. coli, fecal coliforms** = specific indicators of recent fecal contamination.
- **Heavy metals in drinking water:** Lead, Arsenic, Cadmium, Mercury, Chromium — all toxic at trace levels.
- **Key CPCB effluent limits:** pH 5.5–9.0; BOD 30 mg/L; COD 250 mg/L; TSS 100 mg/L (typical).

> **End of Module 1 Notes — PBCHT404 Environmental Engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources and Classification of Wastewater - Types of Water Pollutants and Their Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT404 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sources and Classification of Wastewater - Types of Water Pollutants and Their Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Wastewater?

**Wastewater** is any water that has been adversely affected in quality by anthropogenic (human) influence. It encompasses the liquid waste discharged from domestic residences, commercial properties, industrial facilities, agricultural operations, and storm runoff, which is collected in sewers and conveyed for treatment and disposal.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> Wastewater = Used water + Liquid wastes + Stormwater + Industrial discharges + Infiltration/Inflow that enters a sewer system.
> It is essentially **"used water from a community containing 99.9% water and 0.1% suspended/ dissolved solids."**

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The City is a Living Body"

Imagine a city as a **giant human body**:
- **Freshwater intake** (rivers, reservoirs) behaves like **oxygen-rich blood** entering the heart.
- The **sewer network** acts like the **venous system** — it carries away the "metabolic waste" generated by the "cells" (households, factories, farms).
- The **wastewater treatment plant (WWTP)** plays the role of the **kidneys and liver** — filtering, detoxifying, and returning clean fluid to the environment.
- If any part of this system fails (clogged arteries = choked sewers, failed kidneys = non-functional STP), the entire "body" (the city and its water bodies) gets **sick and polluted**.

This analogy helps us understand why *engineered disposal and treatment* are non-negotiable for survival.

## 1.3 Why Study Sources and Classification?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Perspective:**
> Before you can design a treatment plant, you **must** know:
> 1. **Where** the wastewater comes from (Source).
> 2. **What** it contains (Classification & Pollutants).
> 3. **How dangerous** it is to the environment (Effects).
> 
> This is the **diagnostic stage** of Environmental Engineering, just like a doctor must first identify the pathogen before prescribing medicine.

## 1.4 Fundamental Terminology

| Term | Standard Definition | Typical Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Water Supply (per capita)** | Freshwater supplied per person per day | **135 – 200 litres/person/day (lpcd)** |
| **Sewage / Wastewater (per capita)** | Wastewater generated per person per day | **80% of water supplied ≈ 120 lpcd** |
| **Sewerage System** | Conduit network for transporting wastewater | Gravity / Pressure / Vacuum |
| **Sullage** | Wastewater from bathrooms, kitchens (excluding toilet) | Greywater |
| **Sewage** | Sullage + Fecal matter (including toilet discharge) | Blackwater |
| **Industrial Effluent** | Liquid waste from manufacturing/processing | Highly variable |
| **Storm Sewage** | Surface runoff entering the sewer | Intermittent, high volume |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Wastewater — The Complete Hierarchy

Wastewater sources can be systematically classified as follows:

### A. Domestic / Municipal Sources
These originate from residential and institutional establishments:
- **Toilet flushing** (Blackwater containing fecal matter, urine, pathogens).
- **Kitchen sinks** (Food particles, oils, greases — FOGs).
- **Bathroom drains** (Sullage — soaps, shampoos, hair, dirt).
- **Laundry** (Detergents, phosphates, lint, dyes).
- **Hospital and institutional wastewaters** (contain pharmaceuticals, pathogens, radioactive isotopes).

### B. Industrial Sources
Industry-specific effluents are often the **most toxic** category. Examples:
- **Dairy and Food Processing:** High BOD, oils, suspended solids.
- **Tanneries:** Heavy metals (Chromium Cr⁶⁺), high TDS, sulfides.
- **Textile/Dye Industry:** Color, phenols, acids, alkalis, heavy metals.
- **Pulp and Paper:** Lignin, chlorinated compounds, high BOD/COD.
- **Petrochemical & Refineries:** Oils, phenols, hydrocarbons, PAHs.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Antibiotics, hormones, recalcitrant organics.
- **Electroplating:** Cyanides, heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, Ni, Zn, Cu).
- **Steel and Metallurgical:** Acids, cyanides, suspended solids.

### C. Agricultural Sources (Non-Point Source Pollution)
- **Livestock farms:** Manure, pathogens, nutrients (N, P).
- **Fertilizer and pesticide runoff:** Nitrates, phosphates, toxic organics.
- **Aquaculture and irrigation return flow:** Salinity, sediments.

### D. Stormwater Runoff (Urban Storm Sewage)
- Surface runoff picking up oil, grease, heavy metals, road salts, litter, and fecal coliforms (from pet/animal droppings).
- This is often the **largest volume** during rainfall events.

### E. Infiltration and Inflow (I/I)
- **Infiltration** = Groundwater entering through cracks/joints in sewers.
- **Inflow** = Direct entry of surface water via manholes, cross-connections.
- Excessive I/I **overloads treatment plants** and increases operational cost.

### F. Miscellaneous / Natural Sources
- Groundwater seepage, surface water intrusion, and natural organic matter decay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** In KTU 2024 scheme problems, "domestic sewage" is often the default assumption unless industrial loading is explicitly mentioned. Always check the problem statement first!

---

## 2.2 Classification of Wastewater

### Classification 1: Based on **Origin** (Most Common in KTU Exams)

| Category | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Domestic Sewage** | From residential areas | Kitchen, toilet, bathroom waste |
| **Industrial Effluent** | From factories/industry | Dye effluent, distillery spent wash |
| **Storm Sewage** | Surface runoff during rains | First-flush from roads |
| **Agricultural Drainage** | From farmlands | Fertilizer leachate |

### Classification 2: Based on **Physical State**

| State | Component | Removal Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Suspended Solids (SS)** | Floating/suspended particles | Screening, Sedimentation |
| **Dissolved Solids (TDS)** | Salts, minerals | Reverse Osmosis, Ion exchange |
| **Colloidal Solids** | Fine particles (0.001 – 1 µm) | Coagulation-Flocculation |
| **Emulsions / Foams** | Oils, detergents, surfactants | Skimming, DAF |

### Classification 3: Based on **Strength** (BOD Basis)

| Type | BOD₅ Strength (mg/L) | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Strong Sewage** | > 400 mg/L | Concentrated municipal, food industry |
| **Medium Sewage** | 200 – 400 mg/L | Normal municipal sewage |
| **Weak Sewage** | < 200 mg/L | Dilute sewage with high infiltration |

### Classification 4: Based on **Composition (Sanitary Engineering View)**

- **Sewage Proper** — Liquid waste from toilets.
- **Sullage** — Wastewater *excluding* toilet discharge.
- **Industrial Waste** — From factories.
- **Storm Water** — Rainwater with surface pollutants.

---

## 2.3 Types of Water Pollutants — The Eight Major Categories

According to KTU 2024 syllabus, water pollutants are classified into the following types:

### 1. **Physical Pollutants**
- Suspended Solids (TSS), Turbidity, Color, Odor, Temperature (thermal pollution), Floating debris, Oil & Grease.

### 2. **Chemical Pollutants**
- **Organic:** BOD, COD, proteins, carbohydrates, fats, oils, phenols, pesticides, hydrocarbons.
- **Inorganic:** Acids, alkalis, salts, heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, As, Cr, Ni, Cu, Zn), cyanides, sulfides, fluorides, nitrates.

### 3. **Biological Pollutants**
- **Pathogenic bacteria:** *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, *Vibrio cholerae*, *Shigella*.
- **Viruses:** Rotavirus, Hepatitis A/E, Poliovirus, Adenovirus.
- **Protozoa:** *Giardia*, *Cryptosporidium*, *Entamoeba*.
- **Helminths:** Roundworm, tapeworm eggs.
- **藻 (Algae):** Cause eutrophication.

### 4. **Nutrients (Nitrogen & Phosphorus compounds)**
- Ammonia, nitrites, nitrates, phosphates.
- Cause **eutrophication** → algal blooms → fish kills.

### 5. **Heavy Metals (Persistent Toxic Pollutants)**
- Lead, Mercury, Cadmium, Arsenic, Chromium, Nickel, Copper, Zinc.
- **Bioaccumulative** and **non-biodegradable** → cause cancers, neurological damage.

### 6. **Pesticides and Agrochemicals**
- Organochlorines (DDT, BHC), organophosphates (Malathion), herbicides (Atrazine).
- Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) — bioaccumulate in food chain.

### 7. **Radioactive Pollutants**
- Strontium-90, Cesium-137, Iodine-131, Radium-226.
- From nuclear plants, hospitals, mining.

### 8. **Emerging Pollutants (Modern Concern in KTU 2024)**
- **Pharmaceuticals & Personal Care Products (PPCPs):** Antibiotics, hormones, cosmetics.
- **Microplastics:** < 5 mm plastic particles.
- **Endocrine Disrupting Compounds (EDCs):** Bisphenol A, phthalates.
- **Nanomaterials:** Engineered nanoparticles from industries.

---

## 2.4 Effects of Water Pollutants — The Cascade of Damage

### A. Effects on **Human Health**

| Pollutant | Health Effect |
| :--- | :--- |
| Pathogens (E. coli, Coliforms) | Typhoid, Cholera, Dysentery, Hepatitis |
| Nitrates (NO₃⁻) | Methemoglobinemia ("Blue Baby Syndrome") |
| Fluorides (F⁻) > 1.5 mg/L | Dental and skeletal fluorosis |
| Lead (Pb) | Nervous system damage, especially in children |
| Arsenic (As) | Cancer (skin, lung, bladder), skin lesions |
| Mercury (Hg) | Minamata disease, neurological disorders |
| Cadmium (Cd) | Itai-Itai disease (renal & bone damage) |
| Pesticides (POPs) | Cancer, endocrine disruption, birth defects |
| Microplastics | Inflammation, chemical leaching, unknown chronic effects |

### B. Effects on **Aquatic Ecosystem**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Oxygen Sag Curve Principle:**
> When organic pollutants (high BOD) enter a river, **dissolved oxygen (DO) drops** → aquatic life suffocates → **eutrophication** occurs.

| Effect | Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- |
| **DO Depletion** | Aerobic bacteria consume O₂ while decomposing organics |
| **Fish Kill** | Sensitive species (trout, salmon) die when DO < 4 mg/L |
| **Eutrophication** | Nutrient enrichment → algal bloom → light blockage → plant death → decomposition → anoxia |
| **Bioaccumulation** | Heavy metals concentrate up the food chain (Biomagnification) |
| **Thermal Pollution** | Reduces DO, disrupts breeding, kills temperature-sensitive species |
| **Acidification** | pH < 6 → fish eggs die, metals leach, food web collapses |
| **Turbidity Increase** | Blocks sunlight → reduces photosynthesis → disrupts food chain |

### C. Effects on **Agriculture and Soil**

- **Salinity increase** → soil becomes infertile for crop growth.
- **Heavy metals** accumulate in crops → enter human food chain.
- **Pathogen contamination** of vegetables irrigated with raw sewage.

### D. Effects on **Economy and Society**

- Increased healthcare costs.
- Loss of tourism (beach and water-body pollution).
- Reduced property values near polluted water.
- Treatment costs (estimated **₹100–500 per kilolitre** for advanced treatment).
- Loss of aquatic food sources and livelihood of fishermen.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas for numerical problems in ESE. They are board-exam favorites.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q_{sewage} = 0.8 \times q \times P$ | Quantity of sewage | L/day or m³/day | 80% of supply |
| 2 | $q$ = per capita water supply | lpcd | 135 – 200 lpcd | 150 lpcd (Indian avg.) |
| 3 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \text{DO}_{\text{initial}} - \text{DO}_{\text{final}}$ | 5-day BOD at 20°C | mg/L | Domestic: 200 – 300 mg/L |
| 4 | $\text{COD}/\text{BOD}_5 \text{ ratio}$ | Biodegradability index | Dimensionless | Domestic: 1.5 – 2.5; Industrial: 3 – 10+ |
| 5 | $\text{Population Equivalent (PE)} = \dfrac{\text{Total BOD of industry (kg/day)}}{0.08}$ | Standard: 0.08 kg BOD/person/day | Persons | Used for industrial loading |
| 6 | $\text{SS} = \text{TSS}$ (in mg/L) | Total Suspended Solids | mg/L | Domestic: 200 – 400 mg/L |
| 7 | $\text{TDS} = \text{Total} - \text{SS}$ | Total Dissolved Solids | mg/L | Drinking limit: 500 mg/L |
| 8 | $\text{MLSS} = \text{MLVSS} \times 1.5$ | Activated sludge solids | mg/L | 2000 – 4000 mg/L |
| 9 | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V \times X}$ | Food to Microorganism ratio | day⁻¹ | 0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\text{SVI} = \dfrac{H_{30} \times 1000}{X}$ | Sludge Volume Index | mL/g | 50 – 150 mL/g (good) |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | Removal Efficiency | % | Primary: 30%; Secondary: 85–95% |

> **Note:** All these formulas are from standard Environmental Engineering texts (Tchobanoglous, Metcalf & Eddy, S.K. Garg) and the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantity of Wastewater Generated

**Problem Setup:** A residential colony has a population of $P$ persons. The per capita water supply is $q$ litres per capita per day (lpcd). The KTU standard assumption is that **80% of the supplied water appears as wastewater** (the remaining 20% is lost to evapotranspiration, leakage, garden use, etc.).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Total freshwater supplied per day to the colony.

$$
Q_{supply} = q \times P \quad \text{(litres/day)}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the wastewater generation factor (standard empirical coefficient = 0.80).

$$
Q_{sewage} = 0.80 \times Q_{supply}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 1 into Step 2.

$$
Q_{sewage} = 0.80 \times q \times P
$$

**Step 4:** Convert units if needed. Since $1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$,

$$
Q_{sewage} \text{ (in m}^3\text{/day)} = \dfrac{0.80 \times q \times P}{1000}
$$

### Worked Example (KTU Style):

> **Q:** A town has a population of **50,000**. The per capita water supply is **150 lpcd**. Assuming 80% of supplied water becomes sewage, calculate the average daily sewage flow.
>
> **Solution:**

$$
Q_{supply} = 150 \times 50{,}000 = 7{,}500{,}000 \text{ L/day}
$$

$$
Q_{sewage} = 0.80 \times 7{,}500{,}000 = 6{,}000{,}000 \text{ L/day}
$$

$$
Q_{sewage} = 6{,}000 \text{ m}^3\text{/day} = 6 \text{ MLD (Million Litres per Day)}
$$

> **Board Tip:** Always express the final answer in **MLD** (Million Litres per Day) for KTU problems. Examiners love this unit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Population Equivalent (PE)

**Concept:** When designing a municipal STP, we must account for **industrial discharge** as well. The Population Equivalent converts the BOD load of an industry into an equivalent number of people.

**Standard:** One person contributes **0.08 kg of BOD per day** (the standard per capita BOD load adopted by KTU and CPHEEO manuals).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Measure the average daily BOD load of the industry.

$$
\text{BOD}_{\text{industry}} = Q_{\text{industry}} \times C_{\text{BOD}} \quad \text{(in kg/day)}
$$

Where $Q_{\text{industry}}$ is in m³/day and $C_{\text{BOD}}$ is the BOD concentration in mg/L (equivalently, g/m³).

**Step 2:** Apply the conversion factor (1 PE = 0.08 kg BOD/day).

$$
\text{PE} = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_{\text{industry}} \text{ (kg/day)}}{0.08}
$$

**Step 3:** The total **equivalent population** for STP design is:

$$
P_{\text{equivalent}} = P_{\text{residential}} + \text{PE}_{\text{industrial}}
$$

### Worked Example (KTU Style):

> **Q:** A dairy discharges **1000 m³/day** of effluent with a BOD of **1200 mg/L**. Calculate its Population Equivalent.
>
> **Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute BOD load in kg/day.

$$
\text{BOD load} = Q \times C = 1000 \text{ m}^3\text{/day} \times 1200 \text{ g/m}^3
$$

$$
\text{BOD load} = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 1200 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply PE formula.

$$
\text{PE} = \dfrac{1200}{0.08} = 15{,}000 \text{ persons}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This dairy is polluting as much as a town of **15,000 people** in terms of organic load. Hence, when designing a common STP, the equivalent population is 15,000.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Estimation of Storm Sewage (Rational Method)

Stormwater is non-uniform, intermittent, and depends on rainfall intensity. KTU exams frequently test the **Rational Method** for peak stormwater flow.

### Formula:

$$
Q_{storm} = C \times I \times A
$$

Where:
- $Q_{storm}$ = Peak stormwater runoff (m³/s or L/s).
- $C$ = Runoff coefficient (dimensionless) — depends on surface type.
- $I$ = Rainfall intensity (mm/hr or m/s).
- $A$ = Catchment area (hectares or m²).

### Typical Runoff Coefficients (Memorize!):

| Surface Type | $C$ Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Pavement, Concrete, Asphalt | 0.70 – 0.95 |
| Roofs (impervious) | 0.75 – 0.95 |
| Packed soil, unpaved roads | 0.40 – 0.60 |
| Lawns, parks, gardens | 0.10 – 0.25 |
| Forests, agricultural land | 0.05 – 0.20 |
| Sandy soil, flat terrain | 0.05 – 0.10 |

### Worked Example:

> **Q:** A 5-hectare paved urban catchment experiences a rainfall of intensity 50 mm/hr. Calculate the peak stormwater flow. Take $C = 0.85$.
>
> **Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert units to SI.

$$
I = 50 \text{ mm/hr} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} \text{ m/s} = 1.389 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
A = 5 \text{ ha} = 5 \times 10^4 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Rational Formula.

$$
Q_{storm} = 0.85 \times 1.389 \times 10^{-5} \times 5 \times 10^4
$$

$$
Q_{storm} = 0.85 \times 0.6945 = 0.5903 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}
$$

$$
Q_{storm} = 590.3 \text{ L/s}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pollutants vs. Effects — Quick Cross-Reference

| Pollutant Category | Primary Source | Major Effect | Sensitive Receptors | Standard Limit (mg/L)* |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BOD (Organic)** | Sewage, food industry | DO depletion | Fish, aquatic life | BOD₅ < 30 (CPCB) |
| **COD (Refractory)** | Chemicals, pharma | Oxygen demand | Aquatic fauna | COD < 250 |
| **TSS** | Erosion, sewage | Turbidity, sludge | Aquatic plants | TSS < 100 |
| **Oil & Grease** | Petrochem, kitchens | Surface film, O₂ block | Birds, fish | < 10 |
| **Nitrate (NO₃)** | Fertilizers, sewage | Blue baby, eutrophication | Infants, aquatic life | < 45 |
| **Phosphate (PO₄)** | Detergents, fertilizers | Eutrophication | Lakes, reservoirs | < 5 |
| **Lead (Pb)** | Batteries, paint | Neurological damage | Children | < 0.05 |
| **Mercury (Hg)** | Chlor-alkali, thermometers | Minamata disease | Humans, fish | < 0.001 |
| **Cadmium (Cd)** | Electroplating, batteries | Itai-Itai, kidney damage | Humans, rice plants | < 0.01 |
| **Arsenic (As)** | Mining, pesticides | Cancer, hyperkeratosis | Humans | < 0.01 |
| **Chromium (Cr⁶⁺)** | Tanneries, metal finishing | Cancer, ulcers | Humans, fish | < 0.05 |
| **Fluoride (F⁻)** | Natural geology, industry | Fluorosis | Humans | < 1.5 |
| **Pathogens (Coliforms)** | Sewage, hospitals | Waterborne diseases | Humans | MPN < 100/100 mL |
| **Pesticides** | Agriculture, runoff | Cancer, bioaccumulation | Humans, food chain | < 0.001 (each) |
| **Microplastics** | Cosmetics, packaging, degradation | Trophic transfer, inflammation | Marine life, humans | Emerging (no std.) |

> *Values per **IS 10500 (Drinking Water)** and **CPCB General Standards for Discharge of Effluents**. Always verify for KTU 2024 updates.

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Wastewater." List any four major sources of wastewater generation in an urban area.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Wastewater is the spent or used water generated from domestic, commercial, industrial, agricultural, and storm runoff activities, which contains dissolved, suspended, organic, and inorganic contaminants. It is essentially "used water carrying 0.1% impurities."

**Four major sources (1.5 marks):**
1. **Domestic sewage** — From households (toilets, kitchens, bathrooms, laundry).
2. **Industrial effluents** — From manufacturing and processing industries (e.g., textile, dairy, tanneries).
3. **Stormwater runoff** — Surface water from rainfall collecting urban pollutants.
4. **Agricultural drainage** — Irrigation return flow with fertilizers and pesticides.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between "Sullage" and "Sewage." Briefly explain the term "Population Equivalent."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sullage | Sewage |
| :--- | :--- |
| Wastewater **excluding** toilet discharge. | Wastewater **including** toilet discharge (fecal matter). |
| Comes from bathrooms, kitchens, laundry. | Combination of sullage + blackwater. |
| Lesser health risk. | High pathogen content. |

**Population Equivalent (1.5 marks):**
Population Equivalent (PE) is the number of persons that would produce the same BOD load as the industrial effluent under consideration. It is computed as:

$$
\text{PE} = \dfrac{\text{Total BOD of industry (kg/day)}}{0.08 \text{ kg BOD/person/day}}
$$

PE helps in **designing common STPs** that treat both domestic and industrial waste.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> **Classify wastewater based on its origin. With neat diagrams, explain the various sources of wastewater. Also, discuss the physical, chemical, and biological characteristics of municipal sewage in detail.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Classification and Sources of Wastewater**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification Based on Origin (3.5 marks):**

Wastewater can be classified as:

1. **Domestic Sewage** — From residential areas, containing human waste, kitchen waste, laundry water. Typical flow = 80% of water supplied. Typical BOD₅ = 200–300 mg/L.

2. **Industrial Effluents** — From factories; composition varies by industry. Examples:
   - Dairy: BOD up to 3000 mg/L, oils.
   - Tanneries: Cr⁶⁺, sulfides, high TDS.
   - Textile: Color, phenols, acids.

3. **Storm Sewage** — Surface runoff during rain. Highly variable in volume, often contains road oil, litter, silt.

4. **Agricultural Drainage** — From farms with pesticides, fertilizers, livestock waste.

**(ii) Sources of Wastewater (3.5 marks):**

- **Blackwater (Toilet):** Feces, urine, toilet paper → pathogens, N, P.
- **Greywater (Sullage):** Kitchen, bath, laundry → soaps, food waste.
- **Hospital waste:** Pathogens, pharmaceuticals, radioactive substances.
- **Industries:** Process water, cooling water, washing effluents.
- **Storm runoff:** Roofs, roads, pavements → oils, metals, debris.
- **Infiltration/Inflow:** Groundwater entering through cracks.

> **Board Tip:** Always draw a flowchart or sketch of the sewer network connecting all sources to the STP for full marks. A diagram alone can fetch 1–2 marks.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Characteristics of Municipal Sewage**

**Model Answer:**

**Physical Characteristics (2 marks):**
- **Color:** Fresh sewage is grey, septic sewage turns black.
- **Odor:** Fresh = soapy/woolly; Septic = H₂S (rotten egg).
- **Turbidity:** 100–500 NTU; indicates suspended solids.
- **Temperature:** 10–20°C (slightly above supply water).
- **Solids:** Total ~700–1200 mg/L; Suspended ~200–400 mg/L.

**Chemical Characteristics (3 marks):**
- **Organic content:** BOD₅ (200–300 mg/L), COD (400–600 mg/L), TOC.
- **Inorganic content:** N (20–40 mg/L), P (5–10 mg/L), chlorides, sulfates.
- **pH:** Slightly alkaline (7.0–8.0).
- **Nutrients:** N, P → cause eutrophication.
- **Heavy metals:** Trace amounts from household chemicals.
- **Gases:** H₂S, CO₂, CH₄, NH₃ from anaerobic decomposition.

**Biological Characteristics (2 marks):**
- **Microorganisms:** 10⁶–10⁹ per mL.
- **Bacteria:** Coliforms (E. coli), Streptococcus, Clostridium.
- **Viruses:** Polio, Hepatitis, Rotavirus.
- **Protozoa and Helminths:** Entamoeba, Ascaris eggs.
- **Algae and fungi:** Common in oxidation ponds.

> **Conclusion:** The composite characteristics (physical + chemical + biological) define the **"strength"** of sewage, which determines the choice of treatment process.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** (Alternative Choice)

> **What are the major types of water pollutants? Explain the harmful effects of water pollution on (i) human health, (ii) aquatic ecosystem, and (iii) agriculture. Also, list the BIS drinking water standards for any five pollutants.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Types of Water Pollutants**

**Model Answer:**

Water pollutants are categorized into:

| # | Type | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Physical** | TSS, color, odor, temperature, oil & grease |
| 2 | **Chemical — Organic** | BOD, COD, pesticides, phenols, hydrocarbons |
| 3 | **Chemical — Inorganic** | Acids, alkalis, salts, cyanides, heavy metals |
| 4 | **Biological** | Bacteria, viruses, protozoa, helminths |
| 5 | **Nutrients** | Nitrates, phosphates, ammonia |
| 6 | **Heavy Metals** | Pb, Hg, Cd, As, Cr, Ni, Cu, Zn |
| 7 | **Pesticides/POPs** | DDT, BHC, Malathion, Atrazine |
| 8 | **Radioactive** | Sr-90, Cs-137, I-131, Ra-226 |
| 9 | **Emerging** | Pharmaceuticals, microplastics, EDCs, nanomaterials |

**Key Explanation (1 mark each for 5 types):**
- **BOD/COD:** Deplete DO; cause fish kills.
- **Heavy metals:** Bioaccumulate; cause cancer, organ damage.
- **Pathogens:** Cause waterborne diseases (typhoid, cholera).
- **Nutrients:** Trigger eutrophication; algal blooms.
- **Pesticides:** Persistent; bioaccumulate; endocrine disruptors.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Harmful Effects + BIS Standards**

**(i) Effects on Human Health (2 marks):**
- **Bacterial diseases:** Cholera (Vibrio cholerae), Typhoid (Salmonella typhi), Dysentery (Shigella).
- **Viral diseases:** Hepatitis A/E, Polio, Rotavirus diarrhea.
- **Chemical toxicity:** Lead → nervous system; Mercury → Minamata disease; Arsenic → cancers.
- **Fluorosis:** From excess fluoride (> 1.5 mg/L).
- **Methemoglobinemia:** "Blue Baby Syndrome" from nitrates.

**(ii) Effects on Aquatic Ecosystem (2.5 marks):**
- **DO depletion** from organic loading → fish suffocation.
- **Eutrophication** from N, P → algal bloom → anoxia → biodiversity loss.
- **Biomagnification** of heavy metals and pesticides in food chain.
- **Thermal pollution** from power plants → reduces DO, alters species composition.
- **Bioaccumulation** in fish and shellfish → human health risk.

**(iii) Effects on Agriculture (1.5 marks):**
- **Salinization** of soil due to saline effluents.
- **Heavy metal uptake** by crops → enters food chain.
- **Pathogen contamination** of vegetables via irrigation with raw sewage.
- Reduced crop yield and soil fertility.

**BIS (IS 10500) Drinking Water Standards — 5 Pollutants (1 mark):**

| Pollutant | Maximum Permissible Limit (mg/L) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Lead (Pb) | 0.05 |
| Arsenic (As) | 0.01 |
| Nitrate (NO₃) | 45 |
| Fluoride (F⁻) | 1.5 |
| Total Chromium (Cr) | 0.05 |
| Cadmium (Cd) | 0.003 |
| Mercury (Hg) | 0.001 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Sewage" with "Sewerage":** Sewage = liquid waste; Sewerage = pipe network. Examiners **deduct ½ mark** for this mistake.
> 2. **Forgetting numerical units:** A BOD value of "250" without "mg/L" is incomplete. Always state units.
> 3. **Missing BIS limits:** When asked for "drinking water standards," quoting WHO without IS 10500 is considered incomplete in Indian context.
> 4. **Mixing up PE formula:** Some students use 0.06 instead of 0.08 kg/capita/day. The KTU standard is **0.08 kg BOD/day per person** (CPHEEO). Memorize this!
> 5. **Omitting diagrams:** In 14-mark questions, a well-labeled diagram (sewer network, pollutant classification tree) is worth **1–2 marks** even if not explicitly asked. Always include one.
> 6. **No mention of Emerging Pollutants:** KTU 2024 syllabus specifically highlights PPCPs, microplastics, and EDCs. Skipping these shows outdated preparation.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Wastewater** = used water from domestic, industrial, agricultural, and storm sources; contains **0.1% impurities**.
- [x] **Sources:** Domestic (toilet, kitchen, bath, laundry), Industrial (varies by sector), Stormwater, Agricultural, I/I.
- [x] **Sullage** = wastewater **excluding** toilet waste (greywater).
- [x] **Sewage** = Sullage + Fecal matter (blackwater).
- [x] **Sewerage** = pipe network carrying sewage.
- [x] **Quantity of sewage** $Q_{sewage} = 0.8 \times q \times P$ (in lpcd × persons).
- [x] **Population Equivalent (PE)** uses **0.08 kg BOD/capita/day** as standard.
- [x] **Storm flow** uses Rational Method: $Q = C \times I \times A$.
- [x] **Wastewater Strength:** Strong > 400; Medium 200–400; Weak < 200 mg/L BOD.
- [x] **Pollutants (8 categories):** Physical, Chemical (organic + inorganic), Biological, Nutrients, Heavy Metals, Pesticides, Radioactive, Emerging.
- [x] **Effects on Health:** Cholera, Typhoid, Fluorosis, Minamata (Hg), Itai-Itai (Cd), Blue Baby (NO₃), Cancer (As, Cr).
- [x] **Effects on Aquatic Life:** DO depletion, Eutrophication, Bioaccumulation, Biomagnification, Thermal pollution.
- [x] **Effects on Agriculture:** Salinization, heavy metal uptake, pathogen contamination.
- [x] **BIS (IS 10500) limits to remember:** Pb 0.05; As 0.01; F⁻ 1.5; NO₃ 45; Hg 0.001; Cd 0.003 mg/L.
- [x] **Municipal sewage BOD₅** = 200–300 mg/L; COD = 400–600 mg/L; TSS = 200–400 mg/L; pH = 7.0–8.0.
- [x] **Color:** Fresh = grey; Septic = black.
- [x] **Odor:** Fresh = soapy; Septic = H₂S (rotten egg).
- [x] **Final answer unit for daily sewage flow** = MLD (Million Litres per Day).
- [x] **Always include a labeled diagram** in 14-mark answers.
- [x] **Mention Emerging Pollutants** (microplastics, PPCPs, EDCs) for 2024 scheme relevance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wastewater Sampling, Preservation and Analysis - Physical and Chemical Characteristics of Wastewater - Determination of Solids and Organic Matter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT404 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wastewater Sampling, Preservation and Analysis - Physical and Chemical Characteristics of Wastewater - Determination of Solids and Organic Matter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wastewater Sampling – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *Wastewater sampling* is the scientifically planned procedure of collecting a representative portion of wastewater from a defined source (sewer, treatment unit, river, industrial effluent) at a specific point and time, for the purpose of evaluating its physical, chemical, and biological characteristics.

A sample is said to be **representative** only when its composition closely mirrors the true average characteristics of the bulk wastewater stream from which it is drawn. If the sample deviates, all subsequent laboratory analyses — no matter how precise — will be **analytically valid but environmentally meaningless**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
Think of wastewater sampling like a doctor drawing blood from a patient. A doctor cannot test the entire body's blood — they take a small sample and infer the health of the whole body. If they draw blood from the wrong vein, at the wrong time (e.g., immediately after the patient has eaten), the test results will be misleading — even though the lab work itself is flawless. Similarly, in environmental engineering, a poorly collected wastewater sample is a "misleading blood test" for a city's sewer system.

### 1.2 Preservation – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Preservation* refers to the physical or chemical treatment applied to a wastewater sample immediately after collection, with the objective of **inhibiting biological, chemical, and physical changes** between the time of sampling and the time of analysis.

The ultimate goal of preservation is to keep the sample as close as possible to its **in-situ (original, in-the-pipe) condition**, because wastewater is a chemically dynamic fluid that begins reacting the instant it leaves its source.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine taking a cup of fresh milk out of the fridge and leaving it on a kitchen counter for 6 hours. Bacteria will multiply, fats will oxidize, and the milk's chemistry will completely change. To stop this, we refrigerate it (physical preservation) or add a chemical preservative. Wastewater behaves identically — only the "spoilage agents" are different (aerobic bacteria, oxidation of sulfides, volatilization of ammonia, etc.).

### 1.3 Physical Characteristics of Wastewater

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Physical characteristics* are the observable, measurable attributes of wastewater that can be detected by human senses or simple instruments, without any chemical reaction.

These include:
- **Color**
- **Odor**
- **Temperature**
- **Turbidity**
- **Solids** (Total, Suspended, Dissolved, Volatile, Fixed, Settleable)
- **Conductivity** (sometimes grouped under physical)

**Conceptual Analogy:** Physical characteristics are like the "vital signs" of wastewater — temperature, appearance, and clarity. Just as a fever tells a doctor about an infection, a foul smell or dark brown color tells an environmental engineer about pollution sources.

### 1.4 Chemical Characteristics of Wastewater

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Chemical characteristics* are the molecular and ionic composition of wastewater, including its organic content, nutrient load, dissolved gases, and inorganic constituents, determined by wet chemistry or instrumental analysis.

These include:
- **Organic Matter:** BOD, COD, TOC, TOD
- **Inorganic Matter:** Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), Sulfur (S), Chlorides (Cl⁻)
- **Dissolved Gases:** Dissolved Oxygen (DO), CO₂, H₂S, CH₄
- **pH and Alkalinity**
- **Heavy Metals and Trace Contaminants**

**Conceptual Analogy:** Chemical characteristics are the "blood test report" of wastewater — they reveal invisible information about health, contamination, and treatability that physical observation alone cannot detect.

### 1.5 Determination of Solids

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The term *solids* in wastewater engineering refers to the **residue remaining after evaporation and drying of a known volume of sample at a defined temperature (103–105 °C)**, encompassing both dissolved and suspended matter.

### 1.6 Determination of Organic Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Organic matter* determination involves quantifying the biodegradable and non-biodegradable carbonaceous content of wastewater, primarily through three interlinked parameters: **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD), Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), and Total Organic Carbon (TOC).**

**Conceptual Analogy:** Organic matter is the "food" that bacteria consume in a treatment plant. Determining organic content is equivalent to measuring how much "lunch" is on the table for the microbes that will clean the water.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Wastewater Samples

The **type of sample** required depends entirely on the **objective of the investigation**. KTU examiners frequently test this classification.

| Sample Type | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Grab Sample** | Single, instantaneous collection at one point and one time | Routine monitoring; parameters that do not vary much (pH, DO, residual Cl₂) |
| **Composite Sample** | A mixture of several grab samples collected at equal time intervals, or proportional to flow | Daily average characteristics; regulatory compliance |
| **Integrated Sample** | A mixture of grab samples collected from different points simultaneously | When stream characteristics vary across cross-section (rivers, large sewers) |

> [!TIP]
> **Composite samples CANNOT be used for:** DO, residual chlorine, temperature, dissolved sulfide, oil & grease — because these change rapidly with time and the act of mixing will chemically alter them.

### 2.2 Wastewater Preservation Techniques — The "Why" Behind Each

Every preservation method targets a **specific degradation mechanism**:

- **Refrigeration at 4 °C** → slows biological activity (most universal method)
- **Acidification (H₂SO₄ to pH < 2)** → inhibits bacterial action, preserves nitrogen species
- **Alkalization (NaOH to pH > 11)** → preserves cyanide, sulfide, and phenolic compounds
- **Chemical addition (HgCl₂)** → strong biological inhibitor; used when refrigeration is insufficient
- **Freezing (-20 °C)** → halts all biological and most chemical reactions

> [!WARNING]
> The choice of preservation technique **must not alter the parameter being measured**. For example, acidifying a sample for COD analysis is forbidden because the H₂SO₄ will interfere with the dichromate reaction.

### 2.3 Physical Characteristics — Detailed Analytical Breakdown

#### 2.3.1 Color
- **True Color:** Caused by dissolved substances; measured after filtration through 0.45 µm filter
- **Apparent Color:** Includes suspended matter; measured on unfiltered sample
- Unit: **Platinum-Cobalt (Pt-Co) units** (also called Hazen units)
- Fresh sewage: light brownish-grey → Aged sewage: black (due to sulfide formation)

#### 2.3.2 Odor
- Indicative of **anaerobic decomposition**
- Caused by gases: H₂S, mercaptans, indole, skatole, amines
- Measured subjectively (qualitative) or by **Threshold Odor Number (TON)**
- TON = (Volume of sample causing detectable odor) / (Volume needed to dilute)

> [!NOTE]
> TON formula appears very frequently in KTU exams. Master it.

#### 2.3.3 Temperature
- Important because it affects:
  - **Biological reaction rates** (doubles for every 10 °C rise, up to ~30 °C)
  - **DO saturation** (warmer water holds less oxygen)
  - **Viscosity and settling**
- Always measured in-situ; not on lab sample.

#### 2.3.4 Turbidity
- Caused by suspended and colloidal matter
- Measured using **Nephelometric Turbidity Unit (NTU)** via a nephelometer
- Optical property: light scattering at 90° to incident beam

### 2.4 Solids Classification — The Cornerstone of KTU Module 1

Solids are classified based on **two questions**: (a) Does the particle settle? (b) Does it volatilize on ignition at 550 °C?

| Solids Type | Symbol | Definition | Operational Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Solids** | TS | Residue after evaporation at 103–105 °C | All matter that is not water |
| **Total Suspended Solids** | TSS | Solids retained on a 1.5 µm glass-fiber filter (after drying at 103–105 °C) | Particulates causing turbidity |
| **Total Dissolved Solids** | TDS | Solids passing through the 1.5 µm filter (after drying) | Salts and small organics |
| **Volatile Suspended Solids** | VSS | Loss of weight on ignition at 550 °C | Organic fraction of TSS |
| **Fixed Suspended Solids** | FSS | Remaining residue after ignition | Inorganic fraction of TSS |
| **Settleable Solids** | SS | Solids that settle in an Imhoff cone in 1 hour | Design parameter for primary clarifiers |

**Key Identity (must memorize):**
$$TS = TSS + TDS$$
$$TSS = VSS + FSS$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TS = TSS + TDS$ | All in mg/L | Solids mass balance |
| 2 | $TSS = VSS + FSS$ | All in mg/L | Solids composition |
| 3 | $VSS = TSS - FSS$ | All in mg/L | Organic fraction of solids |
| 4 | $BOD_5 = \dfrac{DO_1 - DO_2}{P}$ (diluted) | mg/L | Standard 5-day BOD |
| 5 | $BOD_5 = (D_1 - D_2) - \dfrac{(B_1 - B_2)(1 - P)}{P}$ (with seed correction) | mg/L | Seeded BOD |
| 6 | $COD = \dfrac{(A - B) \times N \times 8000}{V_{sample}}$ | mg/L O₂ | Standard dichromate COD |
| 7 | $TON = \dfrac{V_{sample} + V_{diluent}}{V_{sample}}$ | Dimensionless | Threshold Odor Number |
| 8 | $k_1$ (deoxygenation constant) | day⁻¹ | BOD kinetics |
| 9 | $L_t = L_0 (1 - e^{-k_1 t})$ | mg/L | First-order BOD equation |
| 10 | $BOD_u = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5 k_1}}$ | mg/L | Ultimate BOD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants you must remember for KTU 2024 exams:**
> - Drying temperature for TS/TSS/TDS = **103–105 °C**
> - Ignition temperature for VSS/FSS = **550 ± 50 °C**
> - Standard BOD incubation = **20 °C for 5 days**
> - pH of standard BOD bottle water = **~7.2 (phosphate buffer)**
> - Theoretical oxygen demand of glucose C₆H₁₂O₆ = **1.067 mg O₂/mg glucose**

### 2.6 BOD Kinetics — The "Why" Behind Organic Matter Decay

BOD is fundamentally a **first-order biochemical reaction** (monomolecular decay):

$$\dfrac{dL_t}{dt} = -k_1 L_t$$

Where:
- $L_t$ = BOD remaining to be exerted at time $t$ (mg/L)
- $L_0$ = Ultimate (total) BOD at $t=0$ (mg/L)
- $k_1$ = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹), typically **0.1 to 0.5 day⁻¹ at 20 °C for domestic sewage**

**The BOD Exerted at time t** is:
$$y_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0(1 - e^{-k_1 t})$$

This is the **workhorse equation** for all KTU BOD problems. Note that $\lim_{t \to \infty} y_t = L_0$, confirming the name "ultimate BOD."

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solids measurement** determines **sludge production rates** in clarifiers → directly impacts digester design and disposal cost.
- **BOD** dictates **aeration tank volume** in activated sludge plants.
- **COD** provides a **rapid (<3 hour) estimate of organic load** when BOD results take 5 days.
- **TOC** is the **most accurate measure of total organics** but is rarely used in routine Indian municipal monitoring due to instrument cost.
- The typical ratio for domestic sewage: **BOD : COD : TOC ≈ 1 : 2 : 0.7** (commonly tested in KTU numericals).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Solids Relationships

**Starting Point — Definitions by Laboratory Operation:**

Let:
- $m_e$ = mass of empty evaporating dish (mg)
- $m_{ts}$ = mass of dish + residue after evaporation at 103–105 °C (for TS) (mg)
- $m_{ds}$ = mass of dish + dried residue retained on filter (for TSS) (mg)
- $m_d$ = mass of empty filter (mg)
- $V$ = sample volume (mL)

**Step 1 — Total Solids (TS) Derivation:**
$$TS = \dfrac{m_{ts} - m_e}{V} \times 1000 \quad \text{(mg/L)}$$

The factor 1000 converts mL to L, assuming mass is in mg.

**Step 2 — Total Suspended Solids (TSS) Derivation:**
$$TSS = \dfrac{m_{ds} - m_d}{V} \times 1000 \quad \text{(mg/L)}$$

**Step 3 — Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) Derivation:**
A separate filtrate is evaporated. If $m_{f}$ is mass of dish + filtrate residue:
$$TDS = \dfrac{m_{f} - m_e}{V} \times 1000 \quad \text{(mg/L)}$$

**Step 4 — Mass Balance Proof (TS = TSS + TDS):**
By conservation of mass, the total residue equals the sum of the two filter-defined fractions:
$$TS = TSS + TDS$$

**Step 5 — VSS and FSS Derivation:**

After TSS is determined, the filter + residue is ignited at 550 °C. The organic matter burns off:
- $m_{vs}$ = mass of filter + residue after ignition (mg)

$$VSS = \dfrac{m_{ds} - m_{vs}}{V} \times 1000 \quad \text{(mg/L)}$$
$$FSS = \dfrac{m_{vs} - m_d}{V} \times 1000 \quad \text{(mg/L)}$$

Therefore:
$$TSS = VSS + FSS \quad \square$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of BOD Kinetics

**Step 1 — First-Order Rate Law:**
The rate at which organic matter decays is proportional to the remaining unoxidized organic matter:
$$\dfrac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 L$$

**Step 2 — Separation of Variables:**
$$\dfrac{dL}{L} = -k_1 \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integration from $t=0$ to $t=t$:**
$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \dfrac{dL}{L} = -k_1 \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln(L_t) - \ln(L_0) = -k_1 t$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiation:**
$$\ln\left(\dfrac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k_1 t$$

$$\dfrac{L_t}{L_0} = e^{-k_1 t}$$

$$L_t = L_0 \, e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Step 5 — BOD Exerted at time t:**
The oxygen consumed up to time $t$ equals the organic matter oxidized:
$$y_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 - L_0 e^{-k_1 t} = L_0(1 - e^{-k_1 t}) \quad \square$$

**Step 6 — Solving for Ultimate BOD from 5-Day BOD:**
At $t = 5$ days:
$$BOD_5 = L_0(1 - e^{-5 k_1})$$

Rearranging:
$$L_0 = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5 k_1}} \quad \square$$

This is the **most-tested BOD formula in KTU exams**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Seeded BOD Equation

When the dilution water already contains a small population of seed microorganisms (BOD > 0.2 mg/L in dilution water), correction is needed:

**Step 1 — Define Variables:**
- $D_1$ = DO of diluted sample on Day 0 (mg/L)
- $D_2$ = DO of diluted sample on Day 5 (mg/L)
- $B_1$ = DO of seed control on Day 0 (mg/L)
- $B_2$ = DO of seed control on Day 5 (mg/L)
- $P$ = decimal fraction of sample in dilution (e.g., 0.02 for 2% dilution)

**Step 2 — Net Oxygen Demand of Sample:**
$$BOD_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - P)}{P} \quad \square$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- $(D_1 - D_2)$ = total oxygen depletion in the bottle (sample + seed)
- $(B_1 - B_2)$ = oxygen depletion due to seed alone
- Multiplying by $(1 - P)$ accounts for the fact that the seed occupies $(1-P)$ fraction of the bottle
- Dividing by $P$ scales the demand back to the original (undiluted) sample.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Solids Calculation (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A 50 mL wastewater sample is filtered through a pre-weighed glass-fiber filter (mass = 1.0648 g). After drying at 105 °C, the filter + residue weighs 1.0682 g. After ignition at 550 °C, the mass is 1.0659 g. Calculate TS, TSS, VSS, and FSS if a parallel evaporation for TS gave a dish+residue mass of 56.7243 g and empty dish mass of 56.7021 g.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — TSS:**
$$TSS = \dfrac{1068.2 - 1064.8}{50} \times 1000 = \dfrac{3.4}{50} \times 1000 = 68 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2 — VSS (loss on ignition):**
$$VSS = \dfrac{1068.2 - 1065.9}{50} \times 1000 = \dfrac{2.3}{50} \times 1000 = 46 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — FSS (residue after ignition):**
$$FSS = \dfrac{1065.9 - 1064.8}{50} \times 1000 = \dfrac{1.1}{50} \times 1000 = 22 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Verification:** $VSS + FSS = 46 + 22 = 68 = TSS$ ✓

**Step 4 — TS (separate evaporation):**
$$TS = \dfrac{56724.3 - 56702.1}{50} \times 1000 = \dfrac{22.2}{50} \times 1000 = 444 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 5 — TDS (from identity):**
$$TDS = TS - TSS = 444 - 68 = 376 \text{ mg/L}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — BOD Kinetics (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A wastewater sample has a 5-day BOD of 200 mg/L at 20 °C. If the deoxygenation constant is $k_1 = 0.23 \text{ day}^{-1}$, calculate: (a) the ultimate BOD $L_0$, (b) the BOD exerted in 7 days, (c) the BOD remaining after 7 days.

**Solution:**

**(a) Ultimate BOD:**
$$L_0 = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5 k_1}}$$

Compute the exponent:
$$5 k_1 = 5 \times 0.23 = 1.15$$

$$e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$

$$1 - e^{-1.15} = 1 - 0.3166 = 0.6834$$

$$L_0 = \dfrac{200}{0.6834} = 292.6 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(b) BOD exerted in 7 days:**
$$y_7 = L_0 (1 - e^{-7 k_1}) = 292.6 (1 - e^{-1.61})$$

$$e^{-1.61} = 0.1999$$

$$y_7 = 292.6 \times (1 - 0.1999) = 292.6 \times 0.8001 = 234.1 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(c) BOD remaining after 7 days:**
$$L_7 = L_0 - y_7 = 292.6 - 234.1 = 58.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

Or directly:
$$L_7 = L_0 \, e^{-7 k_1} = 292.6 \times 0.1999 = 58.5 \text{ mg/L} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — BOD vs COD vs TOC

| Parameter | BOD | COD | TOC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What it measures** | Biodegradable organics | Total (bio + non-bio) organics | All carbon-bound organics |
| **Oxidant** | Microorganisms | Dichromate (H₂Cr₂O₇) | High-temp combustion or UV-persulfate |
| **Time required** | 5 days (standard) | 2–3 hours | 5–10 minutes |
| **Result unit** | mg/L O₂ | mg/L O₂ | mg/L C |
| **Includes non-biodegradable?** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Includes inorganics that consume O₂?** | No | Yes (e.g., Cl⁻, Fe²⁺) | No |
| **Typical ratio for domestic sewage** | 1 | ~2 | ~0.7 |
| **KTU exam relevance** | Highest | Highest | Moderate |

> [!TIP]
> The relationship $BOD/COD$ ratio is the **biodegradability index**. A ratio > 0.5 means easily treatable; < 0.3 means difficult (typical of industrial effluents). This is a guaranteed KTU question.
