## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wastewater Sampling Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Sampling Objective Identified] --> B{Parameter Type?}
    B -->|Stable Parameters| C[Grab Sample Acceptable]
    B -->|Variable Over Time| D[Composite Sample Needed]
    B -->|Variable Across Space| E[Integrated Sample Needed]
    C --> F[Collect at Single Point and Time]
    D --> G[Collect Multiple Grabs at Equal Time Intervals]
    D --> H[Collect Flow-Proportional Grabs]
    E --> I[Collect Simultaneously from Multiple Points]
    F --> J[Preserve or Analyze Immediately]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Sample Ready for Laboratory Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Solids Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Solids TS] --> B[Filtered Through 1.5 µm Filter]
    B --> C[Residue on Filter]
    B --> D[Filtrate]
    C --> E[Total Suspended Solids TSS]
    D --> F[Total Dissolved Solids TDS]
    E --> G[Ignited at 550 °C]
    G --> H[Volatile Suspended Solids VSS]
    G --> I[Fixed Suspended Solids FSS]
    E --> J[Settleable Solids SS - Via Imhoff Cone]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Wastewater Characterization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [Source Stream]
        SW[Sewer or Effluent Source]
    end
    subgraph SAMP [Sampling Stage]
        S1[Grab Sampling]
        S2[Composite Sampling]
        S3[Integrated Sampling]
    end
    subgraph PRES [Preservation Stage]
        P1[Refrigeration 4 °C]
        P2[Acidification pH Less Than 2]
        P3[Alkalization pH Greater Than 11]
        P4[Chemical Inhibitor HgCl2]
    end
    subgraph PHYS [Physical Analysis]
        PH1[Color Pt-Co Units]
        PH2[Turbidity NTU]
        PH3[Temperature °C]
        PH4[Odor TON]
        PH5[Solids TS TSS TDS VSS FSS]
    end
    subgraph CHEM [Chemical Analysis]
        CH1[Organic BOD COD TOC]
        CH2[Nutrients N P]
        CH3[Dissolved Gases DO H2S]
        CH4[pH and Alkalinity]
    end
    subgraph OUT [Engineering Decision]
        OD1[Design of Treatment Plant]
        OD2[Compliance Verification]
        OD3[Process Control Adjustments]
    end
    SW --> S1
    SW --> S2
    SW --> S3
    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P1
    S3 --> P1
    P1 --> PHYS
    P1 --> CHEM
    PHYS --> OD1
    CHEM --> OD1
    CHEM --> OD2
    PHYS --> OD3
```

### 4.4 BOD Kinetic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time t Equals 0] -->|Lag Phase| B[Day 1 to 2]
    B -->|Rapid Uptake| C[Day 3 to 5]
    C -->|Decelerating| D[Day 7 to 10]
    D -->|Approach Asymptote| E[Ultimate BOD L0]
    E -->|Time Approaches Infinity| F[Complete Stabilization]
```

The curve asymptotically approaches $L_0$ but never reaches it in finite time. After 5 days, approximately 60–70% of $L_0$ is typically exerted for domestic sewage (when $k_1 \approx 0.23 \text{ day}^{-1}$).

### 4.5 Preservation Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Which Parameter to Measure?] --> B{Parameter Category?}
    B -->|Nitrogen Forms| C[Acidify with H2SO4 to pH Less Than 2]
    B -->|Phosphorus| D[Refrigerate 4 °C]
    B -->|COD| E[Acidify with H2SO4]
    B -->|BOD| F[Refrigerate; Analyze Within 24 Hours]
    B -->|Sulfide Cyanide| G[Add NaOH to pH Greater Than 11]
    B -->|Phenols| H[Acidify with H3PO4]
    B -->|Heavy Metals| I[Acidify with HNO3 to pH Less Than 2]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define the following terms: (a) Grab sample, (b) Composite sample, (c) Integrated sample. State one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Grab Sample:**
A wastewater sample collected instantaneously at a single specific location and a single specific point in time. It represents the wastewater characteristics at that exact moment.

*Application:* Used for monitoring parameters that are relatively constant over short time periods, such as **pH, dissolved oxygen (DO), residual chlorine, and temperature**.

**(b) Composite Sample:**
A mixture of several grab samples collected either at equal time intervals (time-composite) or in proportion to the flow rate (flow-composite) over a defined period (typically 24 hours).

*Application:* Used for determining the **average daily organic load (BOD/COD) and solids load** for compliance reporting.

**(c) Integrated Sample:**
A mixture of grab samples collected simultaneously from different points across the cross-section of a flow stream (e.g., at different depths of a sewer or different lateral positions in a river).

*Application:* Used when the wastewater characteristics vary spatially across the flow cross-section, such as in **large rivers, wide outfalls, or industrial lagoons**.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Explain the difference between Total Solids (TS), Total Suspended Solids (TSS), and Total Dissolved Solids (TDS). State the standard drying temperature used for their determination.**

**Model Answer:**

**Total Solids (TS):** The residue remaining in an evaporating dish after a known volume of unfiltered wastewater has been evaporated to dryness in an oven at **103–105 °C**. TS represents the entire non-volatile matter in the sample, including both dissolved and suspended fractions.

**Total Suspended Solids (TSS):** The residue retained on a **1.5 µm glass-fiber filter** after a known volume of wastewater is filtered, and the residue is dried at **103–105 °C** to constant weight. TSS represents the particulate fraction that causes turbidity.

**Total Dissolved Solids (TDS):** The residue remaining after the filtrate (liquid passing through the 1.5 µm filter) is evaporated to dryness at **103–105 °C**. TDS represents the salts, minerals, and small organic molecules in true solution.

**Mathematical Relationship:**
$$TS = TSS + TDS$$

**Standard Drying Temperature:** **103–105 °C** (per Standard Methods, APHA).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the different types of wastewater samples. Explain why composite samples cannot be used for dissolved oxygen and residual chlorine. State the various preservation techniques for wastewater samples with their specific applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 25 mL wastewater sample yielded a filter + dried residue mass of 1.0872 g. The pre-weighed filter had a mass of 1.0834 g. After ignition at 550 °C, the filter + residue weighed 1.0858 g. A separate 50 mL sample was evaporated in a pre-weighed dish (54.6215 g); after drying, the dish + residue weighed 54.6412 g. Calculate the TS, TSS, TDS, VSS, and FSS of the wastewater. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Samples and Preservation:**

**Three Types of Samples (as detailed in Section 2.1):**
1. **Grab Sample** – Single instantaneous collection; used for pH, DO, residual Cl₂, temperature.
2. **Composite Sample** – Mix of multiple grabs over time; used for BOD, COD, TSS, TDS (24-hour averages).
3. **Integrated Sample** – Mix of grabs from multiple points; used for non-uniform streams.

**Why composite samples cannot be used for DO and residual Cl₂:**

- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** DO is consumed by ongoing biological activity in the sample. If a grab is held for any time, bacteria will deplete the DO, giving a falsely low reading. Additionally, the act of mixing multiple grabs causes agitation, which may re-aerate or de-aerate the composite, and biological reactions that began in earlier grabs will continue.

- **Residual Chlorine:** Chlorine is an active oxidizing agent that reacts rapidly with organic and reducing agents in the wastewater. Any delay in analysis (even a few minutes) causes the residual chlorine to decay. Combining multiple grabs at different times compounds the error — the composite concentration will bear no consistent relationship to the original stream.

**Preservation Techniques and Applications:**
| Technique | Target Parameter / Application |
|---|---|
| Refrigeration at 4 °C | Universal; BOD, COD, TSS, nutrients, most routine parameters |
| Acidification (H₂SO₄, pH < 2) | Nitrogen forms (ammonia, TKN), COD, phosphorus (with HNO₃) |
| Alkalization (NaOH, pH > 11) | Cyanide, sulfide, phenolic compounds |
| HgCl₂ addition | Inhibits biological activity when refrigeration is insufficient |
| Freezing (-20 °C) | Long-term preservation for special analyses |
| Glass amber bottles | Prevents photodegradation (e.g., phenols, pesticides) |

**(b) Solids Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Sample volume for TSS/VSS = 25 mL
- Mass of filter (empty) = 1.0834 g
- Mass of filter + dried residue = 1.0872 g
- Mass of filter + ignited residue = 1.0858 g
- Sample volume for TS = 50 mL
- Mass of empty dish = 54.6215 g
- Mass of dish + dried residue = 54.6412 g

**Step 1 — TSS:**
$$TSS = \dfrac{(1.0872 - 1.0834) \text{ g}}{25 \text{ mL}} \times \dfrac{1000 \text{ mg}}{1 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{1000 \text{ mL}}{1 \text{ L}}$$
$$TSS = \dfrac{0.0038 \text{ g}}{25 \text{ mL}} \times 10^6 \dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{L·g/mL}} = 152 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2 — VSS (loss on ignition):**
$$VSS = \dfrac{(1.0872 - 1.0858) \text{ g}}{25 \text{ mL}} \times 10^6 = \dfrac{0.0014}{25} \times 10^6 = 56 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — FSS (residue after ignition):**
$$FSS = \dfrac{(1.0858 - 1.0834) \text{ g}}{25 \text{ mL}} \times 10^6 = \dfrac{0.0024}{25} \times 10^6 = 96 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Verification:** $VSS + FSS = 56 + 96 = 152 = TSS$ ✓

**Step 4 — TS:**
$$TS = \dfrac{(54.6412 - 54.6215) \text{ g}}{50 \text{ mL}} \times 10^6 = \dfrac{0.0197}{50} \times 10^6 = 394 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 5 — TDS (from identity):**
$$TDS = TS - TSS = 394 - 152 = 242 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Final Results:**
- **TS = 394 mg/L**
- **TSS = 152 mg/L**
- **TDS = 242 mg/L**
- **VSS = 56 mg/L**
- **FSS = 96 mg/L**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define BOD and COD. Compare them on the basis of: oxidizing agent, time required, what they measure, and typical ratio for domestic sewage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater has a 5-day BOD of 180 mg/L. If the deoxygenation rate constant $k_1 = 0.25 \text{ day}^{-1}$ at 20 °C, calculate: (i) the ultimate BOD $L_0$, (ii) the BOD remaining after 10 days, (iii) the BOD exerted in 3 days. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BOD vs COD Comparison:**

**Definition of BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand):**
The amount of dissolved oxygen required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the biodegradable organic matter in wastewater under standard conditions (20 °C, 5 days, in the dark).

**Definition of COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand):**
The amount of oxygen equivalent required to chemically oxidize the organic (and some inorganic) matter in wastewater using a strong chemical oxidant (potassium dichromate, K₂Cr₂O₇) under acidic conditions.

**Comparison Table:**

| Basis | BOD | COD |
|---|---|---|
| **Oxidizing agent** | Living aerobic microorganisms | Potassium dichromate (K₂Cr₂O₇) |
| **Time required** | 5 days (standard); 7 days sometimes | 2–3 hours |
| **What is measured** | Only biodegradable organic matter | Total (biodegradable + non-biodegradable) organics |
| **Includes inorganics?** | No | Yes (e.g., Cl⁻, Fe²⁺, S²⁻ may interfere) |
| **Result unit** | mg/L O₂ | mg/L O₂ |
| **Ratio (domestic sewage)** | 1 | ~2 (i.e., COD ≈ 2 × BOD) |

---

**(b) BOD Kinetics Solution:**

**Given:**
- $BOD_5 = 180 \text{ mg/L}$
- $k_1 = 0.25 \text{ day}^{-1}$ at 20 °C

**(i) Ultimate BOD $L_0$:**

Compute $5 k_1 = 5 \times 0.25 = 1.25$

$$e^{-1.25} = 0.2865$$

$$L_0 = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5 k_1}} = \dfrac{180}{1 - 0.2865} = \dfrac{180}{0.7135}$$

$$\boxed{L_0 = 252.28 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(ii) BOD remaining after 10 days ($L_{10}$):**

Compute $10 k_1 = 10 \times 0.25 = 2.5$

$$e^{-2.5} = 0.0821$$

$$L_{10} = L_0 \cdot e^{-10 k_1} = 252.28 \times 0.0821$$

$$\boxed{L_{10} = 20.71 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(iii) BOD exerted in 3 days ($y_3$):**

Compute $3 k_1 = 3 \times 0.25 = 0.75$

$$e^{-0.75} = 0.4724$$

$$y_3 = L_0 (1 - e^{-3 k_1}) = 252.28 \times (1 - 0.4724)$$

$$y_3 = 252.28 \times 0.5276$$

$$\boxed{y_3 = 133.10 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** After 3 days, about 53% of the ultimate BOD has been exerted. After 10 days, only 8% of the organic load remains — confirming that domestic sewage is highly biodegradable with rapid initial oxidation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion error in solids problems** — Most students forget to multiply by 1000 to convert mg/mL to mg/L. This single error loses 1–2 marks per sub-question.
>
> 2. **Confusing VSS with TSS** — VSS is a *fraction* of TSS (only the organic part), NOT a separate measurement. Many students compute VSS without dividing by sample volume, mixing up the "filter+residue" masses incorrectly.
>
> 3. **Using composite samples for DO/Cl₂** — This is a classic 1-mark question trap. Always mention biological decay and ongoing reactions as the reason.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the factor 8000 in COD formula** — The molecular weight of O₂ is 32, and 1000 mL/L, so 1 N × 1 L × 8 = 8000 mg equivalents. Writing COD = (A−B) × N × V / sample is a common fatal error.
>
> 5. **BOD kinetics — using $t$ in hours instead of days** — Since $k_1$ is in day⁻¹, time must be in days. Mixing units yields a numerically wrong answer.
>
> 6. **Mis-stating drying temperature** — It is 103–105 °C, NOT 100 °C (water of crystallization in some salts is lost above 100 °C, but organic matter oxidation starts above 105 °C). Examiners are strict on this.
>
> 7. **Preservation–Parameter mismatch** — Acidifying a BOD sample is wrong because it kills the bacteria needed for the test. Examiners check if you know which preservation is compatible with which parameter.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Sampling must be representative** — A non-representative sample invalidates all downstream work.
- [x] **Three sample types:** Grab (instant), Composite (time/flow), Integrated (space).
- [x] **DO and residual Cl₂ require grab samples only** — never composite.
- [x] **Preservation goal:** Arrest biological, chemical, and physical change between sampling and analysis.
- [x] **Refrigeration at 4 °C is the universal default preservation** unless specific parameter demands otherwise.
- [x] **Standard drying temperature for solids = 103–105 °C**; ignition temperature for VSS = 550 °C.
- [x] **Key identity:** $TS = TSS + TDS$ and $TSS = VSS + FSS$.
- [x] **BOD = biodegradable organics; COD = total organics; TOC = total carbon-bound organics.**
- [x] **BOD : COD : TOC for domestic sewage ≈ 1 : 2 : 0.7.**
- [x] **BOD kinetics is first-order:** $y_t = L_0(1 - e^{-k_1 t})$; $L_0 = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5 k_1}}$.
- [x] **$k_1$ ranges from 0.1 to 0.5 day⁻¹** for typical domestic sewage at 20 °C; commonly assumed **0.23 day⁻¹** in KTU problems.
- [x] **BOD/COD ratio > 0.5** indicates easily biodegradable wastewater; < 0.3 indicates difficult (industrial).
- [x] **COD is faster (2–3 h) than BOD (5 days)** → used for rapid process control.
- [x] **Seed correction required** in BOD when dilution water itself has BOD > 0.2 mg/L.
- [x] **Threshold Odor Number (TON)** — commonly tested in KTU Part A.
- [x] **Color units: Pt-Co (Hazen); Turbidity units: NTU; Temperature: in-situ only.**
- [x] **Temperature affects DO saturation and biological reaction rates** — always measure in-situ.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dissolved Oxygen (Oxygen Sag Curve), Biochemical Oxygen Demand, Chemical Oxygen Demand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT404 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dissolved Oxygen (Oxygen Sag Curve), Biochemical Oxygen Demand, Chemical Oxygen Demand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dissolved Oxygen (DO)

**Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** is the volume of free (molecular) oxygen ($O_2$) physically dissolved in water, conventionally expressed in **mg/L** or as a **percentage saturation (%)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** "The amount of molecular oxygen (O₂) present in water in the dissolved state, available for respiration of aquatic organisms and for oxidation of organic matter."

### Sources of DO in a River
- **Atmospheric Reaeration:** Diffusion of $O_2$ from atmosphere at the air–water interface.
- **Photosynthesis:** Algae and aquatic plants release $O_2$ during daylight.
- **Flow aeration:** Turbulence (rapids, weirs) traps air bubbles.

### Sinks (Consumers) of DO
- **Biochemical oxidation** of biodegradable organic matter.
- **Respiration** of aquatic flora and fauna.
- **Nitrification** of ammonia.
- **Benthic (sediment) oxygen demand.**
- **Chemical oxidation** of reduced inorganics ($Fe^{2+}$, $Mn^{2+}$, $S^{2-}$).

### Saturation Concentration
The maximum DO that water can hold (saturation DO, denoted $D_O^{sat}$) is a function of:
1. **Temperature** — decreases as T rises.
2. **Atmospheric Pressure** (altitude) — decreases with elevation.
3. **Salinity / Chloride content** — decreases with salinity.
4. **Mineral content** of water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** At $T = 20^\circ C$ and 1 atm pressure in pure water, $D_O^{sat} \approx 9.17$ mg/L. This is the universal benchmark used in BOD bottle incubation.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Fish Tank" Concept 🍎
> Imagine a glass of cold soda versus a glass of warm soda. The cold one is fizzy and "holds" more gas; the warm one has lost its fizz. Water behaves the same way: **colder, fresher, less salty water holds more oxygen**, just like cold soda holds more $CO_2$. Now, if you drop a sugar cube (organic waste) into the tank, the bacteria will eat it, but to "digest" it, they need oxygen — they literally suck the dissolved oxygen out of the tank. If too much sugar is added, the oxygen level crashes, the fish suffocate, and the tank dies. **BOD and COD measure how much "sugar pollution" is present, and the Oxygen Sag Curve predicts how far the oxygen level will crash downstream of the pollution point.**

---

## 1.2 Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)

**Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** is the amount of dissolved oxygen consumed by aerobic microorganisms during the biochemical oxidation of organic matter in water, under specified conditions of temperature and time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Condition (KTU Syllabus Emphasis):** BOD is measured by incubating a diluted sample at $T = 20^\circ C$ for $t = 5$ days in the dark. The result is denoted **BOD₅** (or BOD₅-day at 20°C).

Mathematical expression:

$$BOD_5 = (D_1 - D_2) - \text{(correction for seed \& dilution)}$$

where $D_1$ = DO of diluted sample at $t = 0$, and $D_2$ = DO of diluted sample after 5 days.

### Ultimate BOD ($L$ or $L_0$)
The total (theoretical) oxygen demand when oxidation is **complete**. Symbol: $L_0$ or $L$ (units: mg/L).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Typically, $BOD_5 \approx (0.6$ to $0.8) \times L_0$ for municipal sewage — i.e., about **60–80%** of the carbonaceous oxygen demand is exerted in 5 days. This is why the **5-day, 20°C** standard is universally adopted.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Workout Calorie" Analogy 🏋️
> Think of a sample of polluted water as a **meal for bacteria**. Just as a heavy meal takes your body hours to fully digest, bacteria take days to fully break down waste. **BOD₅** is the *calories burned in the first 5 days of digestion* — not the whole meal, but a standard window. **Ultimate BOD ($L_0$)** is the *total calories in the meal*. We measure the partial amount (5 days) because waiting for full digestion would take 20–30 days, which is impractical for water-quality monitoring.

---

## 1.3 Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)

**Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** is the amount of oxygen equivalent consumed during the **chemical oxidation** of organic matter by a strong oxidant (typically **potassium dichromate, $K_2Cr_2O_7$**, in acidic medium).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** "The amount of oxygen required to chemically oxidize the biodegradable and non-biodegradable organic and inorganic matter present in water, using a strong chemical oxidant under acidic conditions."

### Reaction Principle
$$Cr_2O_7^{2-} + 14H^+ + 6e^- \rightarrow 2Cr^{3+} + 7H_2O$$

The dichromate oxidizes almost **all organics** (≈ 95–100% of biodegradable + non-biodegradable) in **2 hours** of reflux at 150°C, with $Ag_2SO_4$ as catalyst and $HgSO_4$ to prevent chloride interference.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Brute Force Burn" Concept 🔥
> BOD is a *gentle, slow burn* by bacteria (like a campfire). COD is a *brute-force industrial incinerator* — it scorches everything organic in 2 hours using powerful chemicals, whether the bacteria could eat it or not. So **COD is always greater than (or equal to) BOD**. The difference, $COD - BOD_5$, tells you the amount of **non-biodegradable** (recalcitrant) pollution — the stuff bacteria simply cannot eat.

---

## 1.4 Why Study DO, BOD, and COD Together?

| Parameter | What it tells us | Time |
|---|---|---|
| **DO** | How much oxygen is currently available for fish | Instant |
| **BOD** | How much oxygen the *bacteria* will demand | Days (5-day) |
| **COD** | How much oxygen the *chemicals* will demand | 2 hours |

These three form the **trinity of water-quality assessment** for organic pollution and are the cornerstone of stream sanitation engineering.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kinetics of BOD (First-Order Reaction)

The biochemical oxidation of organic matter follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the remaining concentration of unoxidized organic matter.

### Mathematical Statement
The rate of oxidation of organic matter (and hence the rate of oxygen consumption) is proportional to the amount of unoxidized organic matter remaining at time $t$:

$$\frac{dL_t}{dt} = -k_1 L_t$$

### Step-by-Step Logic
- Let $L_0$ = ultimate (total) BOD at $t=0$.
- Let $L_t$ = BOD remaining to be exerted at time $t$.
- Let $y_t$ = BOD *exerted* up to time $t$ (oxygen consumed so far).
- By mass balance: $L_t = L_0 - y_t$.

### Integrated Form (the master BOD equation)
$$\boxed{L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t} \quad \text{and} \quad y_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k_1 t}\right)}$$

where:
- $L_t$ = BOD remaining after time $t$ (mg/L)
- $L_0$ = Ultimate (total) first-stage BOD (mg/L)
- $k_1$ = Deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹), typically $0.1$ to $0.3$ day⁻¹ at 20°C for municipal sewage.
- $t$ = time in days
- $y_t$ = BOD exerted (oxygen already consumed) up to time $t$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **base-e** form is required in KTU. Some textbooks use base-10 ($k_1$ in log₁₀): conversion $k_1 (\ln) = 2.303 \times k_1 (\log_{10})$.

### Why is the Reaction "First Order"?
> At low concentrations, the rate is limited by the substrate (food) availability. With more food, bacteria multiply faster, and oxygen demand rises. With less food, activity slows. This is the hallmark of a **first-order** process — a self-limiting "diminishing returns" curve.

### Temperature Correction of $k_1$ (Arrhenius-type / van't Hoff relation)

$$\boxed{k_{1,T} = k_{1,20} \cdot (1.047)^{T-20}}$$

where $T$ is in °C. The constant **1.047** is the standard KTU/CPHEEO value.

---

## 2.2 Streeter–Phelps Oxygen Sag Model

The **Streeter–Phelps equation** is the most important model in stream sanitation. It describes the **dissolved-oxygen deficit (D)** at any point downstream from a point-source of pollution.

### The Two Competing Phenomena

1. **Deoxygenation** — oxygen is being removed by bacterial oxidation of organic waste.
   - Rate constant: $k_1$ (day⁻¹)
2. **Reaeration** — oxygen is being absorbed from the atmosphere.
   - Rate constant: $k_2$ (day⁻¹), typically $0.2$ to $0.5$ day⁻¹.

### Initial Conditions (at the pollution discharge point, $x = 0$, $t = 0$)
- $D_0$ = initial DO deficit = $D_O^{sat} - D_O^{initial}$
- $L_0$ = ultimate BOD of the river just after mixing.

### Streeter–Phelps Equation (Master DO-Deficit Equation)

$$\boxed{D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 \cdot e^{-k_2 t}}$$

where:
- $D_t$ = oxygen deficit at time $t$ downstream (mg/L)
- $D_0$ = initial deficit at the discharge point (mg/L)
- $t$ = stream flow time (days) = $x / u$ where $x$ = distance and $u$ = stream velocity.
- $L_0$ = ultimate first-stage BOD after mixing (mg/L)
- $k_1$ = deoxygenation constant (day⁻¹)
- $k_2$ = reaeration constant (day⁻¹)

### Critical (Maximum) Deficit Point
The point on the sag curve where the **DO deficit is maximum** and DO is minimum. Found by setting $dD_t/dt = 0$.

#### Critical Time ($t_c$)
$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - \frac{D_0(k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]}$$

#### Critical Deficit ($D_c$)
$$\boxed{D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \cdot e^{-k_1 t_c}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **simplified** (classical) critical deficit form used in board exams, valid when $D_0 \approx 0$:
> $$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \cdot e^{-\frac{k_1 t_c}{}}$$
> When $D_0 = 0$ (no initial deficit), the equation collapses to:
> $$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$$

### Real-World Utility
- **Effluent discharge permits:** Regulators demand that $D_{max}$ in the receiving stream stay above a minimum DO (e.g., 4 mg/L for aquatic life).
- **Treatment plant design:** Predicts how far downstream the river "recovers."
- **Assimilative capacity assessment** of rivers.

---

## 2.3 COD Test — The Master Relationship

### COD Reaction (Simplified)
$$C_nH_aO_bN_c + d \,Cr_2O_7^{2-} + (8d + c)H^+ \rightarrow nCO_2 + (c/2)N_2 + aH_2O + 2d\,Cr^{3+}$$

The $Cr^{3+}$ produced is measured (by titration with FAS — Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate) and back-calculated to oxygen equivalent.

### COD vs. BOD — The Key Inequality
$$\boxed{BOD_5 \leq BOD_{ultimate} = L_0 \leq COD}$$

- **$BOD_5 < COD$** always, because:
  - BOD only measures **biodegradable** organics.
  - COD measures **biodegradable + non-biodegradable** organics.
  - Some inorganics (e.g., $Fe^{2+}$, $S^{2-}$, $NO_2^-$) also consume $K_2Cr_2O_7$, increasing apparent COD.

### Ratio $BOD_5 / COD$ — Biodegradability Index
| Ratio | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $> 0.5$ | Readily biodegradable (e.g., municipal sewage) |
| $0.3 - 0.5$ | Moderately biodegradable |
| $< 0.3$ | Poorly biodegradable / toxic (e.g., industrial effluents) |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k_1 t}\right)$ | BOD exerted at time $t$ |
| 2 | $L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t}$ | Remaining BOD at time $t$ |
| 3 | $BOD_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-5 k_1}\right)$ | Standard 5-day BOD |
| 4 | $k_{1,T} = k_{1,20} \cdot (1.047)^{T-20}$ | Temperature correction of $k_1$ |
| 5 | $D_t = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$ | **Streeter–Phelps** equation |
| 6 | $t_c = \dfrac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left[\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - \dfrac{D_0(k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]$ | **Critical time** (time of max deficit) |
| 7 | $D_c = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \cdot e^{-k_1 t_c}$ | **Critical deficit** (max deficit) |
| 8 | $D_{sat}(20^\circ C) \approx 9.17$ mg/L | Saturation DO at 20°C |
| 9 | $BOD_5 / COD \leq 1$ | Biodegradability indicator |
| 10 | $D_0 = D_O^{sat} - D_O^{initial}$ | Initial deficit definition |
| 11 | $t_{crit}^{simpler} = \dfrac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$ | Critical time when $D_0 = 0$ |
| 12 | $D_c^{simpler} = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \cdot e^{-k_1 t_c}$ | Critical deficit when $D_0 = 0$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the BOD First-Order Equation

**Statement:** The rate of BOD exertion is proportional to the amount of biodegradable organic matter still remaining.

### Step 1 — Set up the differential equation
The rate at which BOD is exerted equals the negative rate at which organic matter (BOD potential) remains:

$$\frac{dL_t}{dt} = -k_1 L_t$$

**Logic:** As time increases, the *remaining* unoxidized organic load $L_t$ decreases. Hence the negative sign.

### Step 2 — Separate the variables
Bring all $L$ terms to the left and $t$ terms to the right:

$$\frac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k_1 \, dt$$

### Step 3 — Integrate both sides
Integrate from time $0$ (when $L_t = L_0$) to time $t$ (when $L_t = L_t$):

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k_1 \int_0^t dt$$

This gives:

$$\left[\ln L_t\right]_{L_0}^{L_t} = -k_1 \left[t\right]_0^t$$

$$\ln L_t - \ln L_0 = -k_1 t$$

### Step 4 — Apply exponentiation
Exponentiating both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k_1 t$$

$$\frac{L_t}{L_0} = e^{-k_1 t}$$

### Step 5 — Final equation
$$\boxed{L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t}}$$

### Step 6 — BOD Exerted
The amount of BOD *exerted* (oxygen already consumed) is $y_t = L_0 - L_t$:

$$y_t = L_0 - L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$$

$$\boxed{y_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k_1 t}\right)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Streeter–Phelps Equation

### Step 1 — Define the rate of change of DO deficit $D_t$
The DO deficit at any time $t$ is:
$$D_t = D_O^{sat} - D_O^{actual}$$

The rate of change of deficit is **deoxygenation minus reaeration**:

$$\frac{dD_t}{dt} = (\text{Rate of deoxygenation}) - (\text{Rate of reaeration})$$

### Step 2 — Write each rate
- Deoxygenation rate $= k_1 L_t = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$
- Reaeration rate $= k_2 D_t$ (deficit is the driving force for reaeration)

Therefore:

$$\frac{dD_t}{dt} = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t} - k_2 D_t$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange into a linear first-order ODE
$$\frac{dD_t}{dt} + k_2 D_t = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$$

This is a linear ODE of the form $D' + P(t)D = Q(t)$ with $P = k_2$, $Q = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$.

### Step 4 — Integrating Factor (I.F.)
$$I.F. = e^{\int k_2 \, dt} = e^{k_2 t}$$

### Step 5 — Multiply the ODE by the integrating factor
$$e^{k_2 t} \frac{dD_t}{dt} + k_2 e^{k_2 t} D_t = k_1 L_0 e^{(k_2 - k_1) t}$$

The LHS is now the derivative of a product:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(D_t \cdot e^{k_2 t}\right) = k_1 L_0 e^{(k_2 - k_1) t}$$

### Step 6 — Integrate both sides
$$D_t \cdot e^{k_2 t} = \int k_1 L_0 e^{(k_2 - k_1) t} \, dt + C$$

$$D_t \cdot e^{k_2 t} = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} e^{(k_2 - k_1) t} + C$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $C$ using initial condition $D_t = D_0$ at $t = 0$
At $t = 0$:

$$D_0 \cdot 1 = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \cdot 1 + C$$

$$C = D_0 - \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}$$

### Step 8 — Substitute $C$ back and divide by $e^{k_2 t}$
$$D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} e^{-k_1 t} + \left(D_0 - \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\right) e^{-k_2 t}$$

### Step 9 — Expand and rearrange to the **canonical Streeter–Phelps form**
$$D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} e^{-k_1 t} - \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} e^{-k_2 t} + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$$

$$\boxed{D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Critical Time $t_c$

We seek the time when $D_t$ is maximum, i.e., $dD_t/dt = 0$.

### Step 1 — Differentiate the SP equation with respect to $t$
$$\frac{dD_t}{dt} = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(-k_1 e^{-k_1 t} + k_2 e^{-k_2 t}\right) - k_2 D_0 e^{-k_2 t} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Set to zero
$$\frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(-k_1 e^{-k_1 t} + k_2 e^{-k_2 t}\right) = k_2 D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange
$$k_1 L_0 \left(k_2 e^{-k_2 t} - k_1 e^{-k_1 t}\right) = k_2 D_0 (k_2 - k_1) e^{-k_2 t}$$

$$k_1 L_0 k_2 e^{-k_2 t} - k_1^2 L_0 e^{-k_1 t} = k_2 D_0 (k_2 - k_1) e^{-k_2 t}$$

### Step 4 — Isolate the $e^{-k_1 t}$ term
$$k_1^2 L_0 e^{-k_1 t} = k_1 L_0 k_2 e^{-k_2 t} - k_2 D_0 (k_2 - k_1) e^{-k_2 t}$$

$$k_1^2 L_0 e^{-k_1 t} = k_2 e^{-k_2 t} \left[k_1 L_0 - D_0(k_2 - k_1)\right]$$

### Step 5 — Take the ratio and logarithm
$$\frac{e^{-k_1 t_c}}{e^{-k_2 t_c}} = \frac{k_2 \left[k_1 L_0 - D_0(k_2 - k_1)\right]}{k_1^2 L_0}$$

$$e^{-(k_1 - k_2) t_c} = \frac{k_2 \left[k_1 L_0 - D_0(k_2 - k_1)\right]}{k_1^2 L_0}$$

Taking natural log:

$$-(k_1 - k_2) t_c = \ln\left\{\frac{k_2 \left[k_1 L_0 - D_0(k_2 - k_1)\right]}{k_1^2 L_0}\right\}$$

### Step 6 — Final critical time formula
$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - \frac{D_0(k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Critical Deficit $D_c$

### Substitute $t_c$ back into the SP equation.
A much simpler closed form (for $D_c$) is obtained by combining the SP equation with the condition $dD_t/dt = 0$:

At the critical point, $dD_t/dt = 0$ implies $k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t_c} = k_2 D_c$ (from the oxygen balance at the inflection point — the rate of deoxygenation exactly equals the rate of reaeration).

Therefore:

$$\boxed{D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \cdot e^{-k_1 t_c}}$$

### Sanity check (simple case $D_0 = 0$)
When $D_0 = 0$, $t_c$ becomes $\frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln(k_2/k_1)$. Substituting:

$$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \cdot \exp\left[-\frac{k_1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)\right]$$

$$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \cdot \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{-\frac{k_1}{k_2 - k_1}}$$

This is the famous **classical Streeter–Phelps critical deficit** expression.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A stream receives a municipal discharge. After complete mixing: $L_0 = 50$ mg/L, $D_0 = 2$ mg/L, $k_1 = 0.23$ day⁻¹, $k_2 = 0.40$ day⁻¹. Find (i) the critical time $t_c$, (ii) the critical deficit $D_c$, and (iii) the minimum DO.

### Step 1 — Compute the term $\frac{D_0(k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0}$
$$\frac{2(0.40 - 0.23)}{0.23 \times 50} = \frac{2 \times 0.17}{11.5} = \frac{0.34}{11.5} \approx 0.02957$$

### Step 2 — Compute $1 - 0.02957 = 0.97043$

### Step 3 — Compute $\frac{k_2}{k_1} \times 0.97043$
$$\frac{0.40}{0.23} \times 0.97043 = 1.7391 \times 0.97043 \approx 1.6876$$

### Step 4 — Take natural log
$$\ln(1.6876) \approx 0.5229$$

### Step 5 — Divide by $(k_2 - k_1)$
$$t_c = \frac{0.5229}{0.40 - 0.23} = \frac{0.5229}{0.17} \approx 3.076 \text{ days}$$

### Step 6 — Compute $D_c$
$$D_c = \frac{0.23 \times 50}{0.40} \times e^{-0.23 \times 3.076}$$

$$D_c = 28.75 \times e^{-0.7075} = 28.75 \times 0.4927 \approx 14.16 \text{ mg/L}$$

Wait — this exceeds $D_O^{sat} = 9.17$ mg/L, which is physically impossible.

**Engineering interpretation:** Such high $D_c$ means the system is **overloaded**. Practically, the stream becomes **anaerobic** at the critical point. This is a signal that the discharge load must be reduced or pre-treated.

### Step 7 — Min DO
If the stream is at saturation upstream ($D_O^{sat} = 9.17$ mg/L):
$$D_{min} = D_O^{sat} - D_c = 9.17 - 14.16 < 0 \Rightarrow \text{Anaerobic!}$$

This shows the practical use of the sag model in **designing waste-load allocations**.

---

## 3.6 Conversion Logic: 5-day BOD to Ultimate BOD

Given $BOD_5$ and $k_1$ (at 20°C), the ultimate BOD $L_0$ is:

$$L_0 = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5 k_1}}$$

### Worked Example:
If $BOD_5 = 200$ mg/L and $k_1 = 0.23$ day⁻¹:

$$L_0 = \frac{200}{1 - e^{-5 \times 0.23}} = \frac{200}{1 - e^{-1.15}} = \frac{200}{1 - 0.3166} = \frac{200}{0.6834} \approx 292.6 \text{ mg/L}$$

This is the **ultimate carbonaceous BOD** — the total oxygen demand over infinite time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic: BOD and COD Process Flow

    E1 --> F1[Measure DO Day 0 and Day 5]
    F1 --> G1[BOD5 = D1 minus D2 minus seed correction]

    E2 --> F2[Titrate with FAS]
    F2 --> G2[COD equals oxygen equivalent of Cr2O7 consumed]

    G2 --> H
    H --> I{Decision: Treat or Discharge?}

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] Define BOD and COD. Why is COD always greater than BOD for the same wastewater?

**Model Answer:**

**BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand):** It is the amount of dissolved oxygen required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the biodegradable organic matter present in a wastewater sample, under standard conditions of $T = 20^\circ C$ and $t = 5$ days.

**COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand):** It is the amount of oxygen equivalent required to chemically oxidize the organic (biodegradable + non-biodegradable) and certain inorganic matter present in water, using a strong oxidizing agent ($K_2Cr_2O_7$) in acidic medium.

**Why COD > BOD:**
- BOD only oxidizes the **biodegradable fraction** of organics.
- COD oxidizes **both biodegradable and non-biodegradable** organics.
- COD also oxidizes some **inorganic reducing agents** (e.g., $Fe^{2+}$, $S^{2-}$, $NO_2^-$), which BOD does not.
- The chemical oxidant ($K_2Cr_2O_7$) is far more powerful and complete than microbial oxidation.

Therefore, $\text{COD} \geq \text{BOD}_5$ for any wastewater. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] What is the Oxygen Sag Curve? Sketch it and label the critical point.

**Model Answer:**

The **Oxygen Sag Curve** is a graphical plot of the dissolved oxygen (DO) concentration in a stream (or the DO deficit, $D$) versus the time (or distance) downstream of a point-source of organic pollution. It depicts the **recovery of a stream** after receiving an organic waste discharge.

**Description of the curve:**
1. **Just upstream** of the discharge: DO is at or near saturation (≈ 9.17 mg/L at 20°C).
2. **At the discharge point ($x = 0$):** DO drops sharply to a value below saturation due to mixing. Initial deficit $D_0 > 0$.
3. **Zone of Deoxygenation:** Bacterial oxidation consumes oxygen faster than reaeration supplies it. DO falls to a **minimum value (critical point)**.
4. **Critical Point $(t_c, D_c)$:** Point of **maximum deficit** and **minimum DO**. The rates of deoxygenation and reaeration are exactly equal here.
5. **Zone of Recovery (Reaeration):** As the organics are exhausted, reaeration dominates, and DO gradually rises back toward saturation.
6. **Far downstream:** Stream returns to near-saturation DO.

```
     DO
     │   ── Saturation Level (DOsat)
 9.2 ┤ ╲                          ╱─────
     │  ╲                        ╱
     │   ╲   Zone of           ╱   Zone of
     │    ╲  Deoxygenation   ╱    Recovery
     │     ╲                ╱
  Dmin ┤      ● ← Critical Point (tc)
     │       ╲           ╱
     │        ╲________ ╱
     │          ↑
     │      Discharge
     └──────────────────────────── t / x →
     0    tc                
```

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal-Choice Pattern:** Each Part-B question offers two choices. Both are solved below.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** A wastewater has an ultimate BOD of 350 mg/L. If the deoxygenation constant $k_1 = 0.20$ day⁻¹ (base-e, at 20°C), find:
  (i) the 5-day BOD at 20°C, and
  (ii) the 8-day BOD at 25°C.

**(b)** Compare BOD and COD tests in 7 standard points.

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
$L_0 = 350$ mg/L, $k_1$ at 20°C $= 0.20$ day⁻¹.

**(i) 5-day BOD at 20°C:**

$$BOD_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k_1 \cdot t}\right) = 350 \left(1 - e^{-0.20 \times 5}\right)$$

$$BOD_5 = 350 \left(1 - e^{-1.0}\right) = 350 (1 - 0.3679) = 350 \times 0.6321$$

$$\boxed{BOD_5 = 221.2 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(ii) 8-day BOD at 25°C:**

First, correct $k_1$ to 25°C:

$$k_{1,25} = k_{1,20} \cdot (1.047)^{25 - 20} = 0.20 \times (1.047)^5$$

$$(1.047)^5 \approx 1.259$$

$$k_{1,25} = 0.20 \times 1.259 = 0.2518 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

Now compute the 8-day BOD at 25°C:

$$BOD_8^{25} = 350 \left(1 - e^{-0.2518 \times 8}\right) = 350 \left(1 - e^{-2.0144}\right)$$

$$e^{-2.0144} \approx 0.1336$$

$$BOD_8^{25} = 350 \times (1 - 0.1336) = 350 \times 0.8664$$

$$\boxed{BOD_8^{25} \approx 303.2 \text{ mg/L}}$$

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison of BOD and COD Tests (7 Standard Points):**

| # | Aspect | BOD Test | COD Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Oxidation type** | Biological (microbial) | Chemical (dichromate) |
| 2 | **Duration** | 5 days | 2 hours |
| 3 | **Temperature** | 20°C (incubation) | 150°C (reflux) |
| 4 | **Indicator** | DO depletion (Winkler) | $Cr^{3+}$ (FAS titration) |
| 5 | **Oxidizes** | Biodegradable organics only | Biodegradable + non-biodegradable + some inorganics |
| 6 | **Magnitude** | Smaller | Always ≥ BOD |
| 7 | **Use** | Models natural stream self-purification; design of biological treatment | Quick assessment of total pollution; toxicity screening |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** A river stream has DO of 8.0 mg/L (saturation = 9.17 mg/L at 20°C) before receiving an industrial discharge. After mixing, the ultimate BOD $L_0 = 40$ mg/L, the deoxygenation constant $k_1 = 0.25$ day⁻¹, and the reaeration constant $k_2 = 0.45$ day⁻¹. Compute:
  (i) The critical time $t_c$.
  (ii) The critical deficit $D_c$.
  (iii) The minimum dissolved oxygen DO_min at the critical point.

**(b)** With a neat sketch of the Oxygen Sag Curve, explain the significance of the critical point in stream sanitation engineering.

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $D_O^{sat} = 9.17$ mg/L, $D_O^{initial} = 8.0$ mg/L.
- $D_0 = 9.17 - 8.0 = 1.17$ mg/L.
- $L_0 = 40$ mg/L, $k_1 = 0.25$ day⁻¹, $k_2 = 0.45$ day⁻¹.

**(i) Critical time $t_c$:**

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - \frac{D_0(k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]$$

Compute the inner term:
$$\frac{D_0(k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0} = \frac{1.17 \times 0.20}{0.25 \times 40} = \frac{0.234}{10} = 0.0234$$

$$1 - 0.0234 = 0.9766$$

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{0.45}{0.25} = 1.80$$

$$1.80 \times 0.9766 = 1.7579$$

$$\ln(1.7579) = 0.5637$$

$$t_c = \frac{0.5637}{0.45 - 0.25} = \frac{0.5637}{0.20}$$

$$\boxed{t_c = 2.819 \text{ days}}$$

**(ii) Critical deficit $D_c$:**

$$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \cdot e^{-k_1 t_c} = \frac{0.25 \times 40}{0.45} \times e^{-0.25 \times 2.819}$$

$$= \frac{10}{0.45} \times e^{-0.7048} = 22.22 \times 0.4941 = 10.98 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{D_c \approx 10.98 \text{ mg/L}}$$

> Note: $D_c > D_O^{sat} = 9.17$ mg/L is physically impossible. This means the stream **goes anaerobic**. The actual $D_c$ is capped at 9.17 mg/L (i.e., DO reaches zero).

**(iii) Minimum DO:**

$$DO_{min} = D_O^{sat} - D_c = 9.17 - 10.98 = -1.81 \text{ mg/L} \Rightarrow \text{physically } DO_{min} = 0$$

The stream becomes **completely anaerobic** at the critical point — a serious pollution hazard.

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Significance of the Critical Point in Stream Sanitation Engineering:**

1. **Worst-Case Downstream DO:** The critical point represents the **lowest DO** (highest deficit) the stream experiences as it travels downstream. Engineers must ensure that this minimum DO is above the regulatory limit (e.g., 4 mg/L CPCB for aquatic life).

2. **Design of Effluent Standards:** By knowing $D_c$, regulators can determine the **maximum permissible organic load** ($L_0$) that a discharger may release without violating stream standards.

3. **Location of Treatment Plant Intakes:** Water intakes for downstream towns must be located **after** the stream has recovered (i.e., past the recovery zone), not at the critical point.

4. **Determination of Stream Recovery Distance:** The critical time $t_c$ and the stream velocity $u$ give the **distance** $x_c = u \cdot t_c$ at which recovery begins.

5. **Predicting Fish Kills:** Critical DO below ~3 mg/L causes fish mortality; the sag curve predicts whether a discharge will trigger fish kills.

6. **Capacity of Self-Purification:** The model quantifies the river's **assimilative capacity** — the maximum waste it can absorb naturally.

7. **Decision Tool for Pre-Treatment:** If the predicted $D_c$ is unacceptably low, the industry must install **pre-treatment** (e.g., primary + biological treatment) before discharge.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch on $k_1$:** Always state whether $k_1$ is in base-**e** ($\ln$) or base-10 ($\log_{10}$). If the problem states $k_1$ in log₁₀, multiply by **2.303** to convert to natural log.
> 2. **Temperature correction:** Students often forget the $(1.047)^{T-20}$ correction when asked for BOD at a temperature other than 20°C.
> 3. **Critical time formula:** Using the **simpler** form $t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln(k_2/k_1)$ when $D_0 \neq 0$ is a **3-mark loss** in 14-mark questions. Use the full equation unless $D_0$ is explicitly zero.
> 4. **Streeter–Phelps physical limit:** $D_c$ can never exceed $D_O^{sat}$. If calculation gives $D_c > D_O^{sat}$, the stream goes anaerobic and $DO_{min} = 0$.
> 5. **Initial vs. saturation DO:** Confusing $D_O^{sat}$ (saturation, ≈ 9.17 mg/L) with $D_O^{initial}$ (actual river DO before discharge) is a very common error. The initial deficit $D_0 = D_O^{sat} - D_O^{initial}$.
> 6. **COD > BOD always:** Writing "COD = BOD" loses a mark. Always state the inequality.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 📘 Conceptual Mastery Checklist

- [ ] **DO** = dissolved $O_2$ in water; saturation ≈ 9.17 mg/L at 20°C; decreases with temperature, salinity, altitude.
- [ ] **BOD₅** = 5-day, 20°C biochemical oxygen demand — **the standard**.
- [ ] **Ultimate BOD ($L_0$)** = total oxygen demand over infinite time; related to $BOD_5$ by $L_0 = BOD_5 / (1 - e^{-5k_1})$.
- [ ] **COD** = chemical oxygen demand using $K_2Cr_2O_7$; always ≥ BOD.
- [ ] **Ratio $BOD_5 / COD$** indicates biodegradability; < 0.3 implies toxic/industrial waste.
- [ ] **First-order kinetics** governs BOD: $L_t = L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$.
- [ ] **Temperature correction:** $k_{1,T} = k_{1,20} \cdot (1.047)^{T-20}$.

### 📘 Streeter–Phelps Mastery

- [ ] **SP equation:** $D_t = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$.
- [ ] **Critical time $t_c$:** when $dD_t/dt = 0$ — peak deficit.
- [ ] **Critical deficit $D_c = (k_1 L_0 / k_2) e^{-k_1 t_c}$.**
- [ ] **$D_c \leq D_O^{sat}$** — physical cap; if exceeded, stream is anaerobic.
- [ ] **Sag curve** has 3 zones: Deoxygenation, Critical Point, Recovery.
- [ ] $k_1 < k_2$ for stable, recovering stream (typical: $k_1 \approx 0.1-0.3$, $k_2 \approx 0.2-0.5$ day⁻¹).

### 📘 Engineering Rules of Thumb

- [ ] $BOD_5 \approx 0.65 \times L_0$ for typical municipal sewage at $k_1 = 0.23$.
- [ ] For healthy stream: minimum DO ≥ 4 mg/L; for fisheries: ≥ 5 mg/L.
- [ ] Stream recovery distance $x_c = u \cdot t_c$ (where $u$ = stream velocity).
- [ ] $k_2$ is highly site-specific; shallow, fast streams have high $k_2$; deep, slow streams have low $k_2$.

### 📘 Common KTU Exam "Tricks"

- [ ] Always check if temperature is 20°C or different.
- [ ] Always check if $D_0$ is given or assumed zero.
- [ ] Always show the units in the final answer.
- [ ] Always present the final answer in a **boxed form** for clarity.
- [ ] In a 14-mark question, **derive** the SP equation if asked (don't just state it).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Wastewater Treatment?

**Wastewater Treatment** is the engineered process of removing contaminants from municipal and industrial effluent to produce a treated effluent (discharge water) and solid waste (sludge) suitable for discharge or reuse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (Module 1 Focus):** This module covers only **Pretreatment** and **Primary Treatment** stages. Design calculations are *excluded* — only **diagrams, working principles, and equipment functionality** are required.

The hierarchy of treatment follows a logical progression:

| Treatment Level | Stage | Removes | Typical Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** | Preliminary (Pretreatment) | Large solids, grit, oil, pH correction | **5–25 % BOD removal** |
| **Level 1** | Primary | Suspended & settleable solids | **25–40 % BOD removal** |
| **Level 2** | Secondary | Dissolved & colloidal organics | **80–95 % BOD removal** |
| **Level 3** | Tertiary | Nutrients, pathogens, trace contaminants | **>95 % pollutant removal** |

---

## 1.2 Preliminary Treatment (Pretreatment)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Preliminary Treatment* refers to the physical/chemical conditioning of wastewater aimed at removing large floating debris, heavy inorganic solids (grit), free oil/grease, equalizing flow/quality, and adjusting pH — to protect downstream treatment units and ensure uniform loading.

### 🏠 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine you spill a can of mixed nuts-and-bolts soup on your kitchen floor. Before you mop it up (the **biological/secondary treatment**), what would you do?

1. **First**, you'd pick out the large chunks (peanuts, big bolts) — this is **Screening**.
2. **Then**, you'd let the heavy grit (sand, gravel) settle — this is **Grit Removal**.
3. **Next**, you'd skim off the floating oil/grease layer — this is **Oil Removal**.
4. **If the flow came in gushes**, you'd pour it into a buffer bucket so you don't overflow your mop — this is **Equalization**.
5. **Finally**, if the soup was too acidic or alkaline (say, lemon-spilled), you'd add baking soda (neutralizing agent) — this is **Neutralization**.

That entire "pre-cleaning" sequence before actual mopping = **Preliminary Treatment**.

### Unit Operations Under Preliminary Treatment

1. **Screening** — Removal of large floating solids (rags, sticks, plastics).
2. **Grit Removal** — Removal of heavy inorganic particles (sand, gravel, cinders).
3. **Oil & Grease Removal** — Skimming of floating hydrocarbons.
4. **Equalization** — Smoothing of flow rate and pollutant concentration variations.
5. **Neutralization** — pH adjustment (toward 6.5–8.5) before biological treatment.

---

## 1.3 Primary Treatment

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Primary Treatment* is the physical/chemical unit operation that removes settleable suspended solids and floating materials from wastewater through gravitational sedimentation, often aided by chemical coagulation and flocculation.

**Primary Treatment Includes:**
- **Coagulation** (chemical destabilization of colloids)
- **Flocculation** (gentle mixing to build large settleable flocs)
- **Sedimentation** (gravity settling of flocs/suspended solids)
- **Clarifiers** (tank equipment for sedimentation)
- **Clariflocculators** (combined coagulation + flocculation + sedimentation unit)

### 🏠 Conceptual Analogy

Continuing the soup analogy: After picking out the big chunks, your kitchen soup still has **fine suspended particles** (flour, spice powder) that won't settle easily. To remove them:
1. You add a little **flour binder** (this is **Coagulation** — chemicals destabilize colloids).
2. You stir *gently and slowly* to clump these destabilized particles into larger masses (**Flocculation**).
3. You let the soup sit — the larger clumps settle to the bottom by gravity (**Sedimentation**).
4. The vessel in which this settling occurs is the **Clarifier**.
5. A kitchen gadget that does coagulation, flocculation, and sedimentation all in one vessel = **Clariflocculator**.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants (KTU Board Expected Values)

- **Standard Domestic Sewage BOD₅:** **200–250 mg/L** (Indian conditions).
- **Standard Sewage pH:** **6.5–8.5** (acceptable range for discharge).
- **Screening Bar Opening:** Coarse screens **≥ 25 mm**; Fine screens **< 6 mm**.
- **Grit Particle Specific Gravity:** **~2.65** (quartz sand).
- **Detention Time in Primary Clarifier:** **1.5 to 2.5 hours**.
- **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) of Primary Clarifier:** **30–40 m³/m²/day**.
- **Weir Loading Rate:** **< 185 m³/m/day** (perimeter basis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always remember that **equalization, neutralization, and screening come BEFORE coagulation/flocculation/sedimentation** in the treatment train. Confusing the order is the most common sequencing error in exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Working Principles of Each Unit Operation

### A. Screening

**Principle:** Physical retention of solids larger than screen openings through a perforated/bar surface while water passes through.

**Types of Screens:**

| Type | Opening | Application | Cleaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bar Screen (Coarse)** | **≥ 25 mm** | Headworks, first barrier | Manual / Mechanical |
| **Bar Screen (Medium)** | **6–25 mm** | Pre-fine protection | Mechanical |
| **Bar Screen (Fine)** | **1.5–6 mm** | Pre-biological protection | Mechanical |
| **Micro Screens** | **< 1.5 μm** | Tertiary, effluent polishing | Automatic |

**Working Mechanism:**
1. Raw sewage enters the screen chamber with **influent channel velocity 0.6–1.2 m/s**.
2. Solids larger than bar spacing get arrested on the screen face.
3. Accumulated screenings are raked (mechanical screens) or hand-removed (coarse).
4. Cleaned wastewater flows through the openings downstream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Velocity Check:** If approach velocity falls below **0.4 m/s**, grit tends to deposit; if it exceeds **1.2 m/s**, embedded solids are flushed through, defeating the screen's purpose.

---

### B. Grit Removal

**Principle:** Differential sedimentation — heavy inorganic particles (SG ~2.65) settle faster than organic suspended solids (SG ~1.2) when flow velocity is controlled at around **0.25–0.30 m/s**.

**Equipment Types:**

1. **Horizontal-Flow Grit Chamber (Rectangular)**
   - Designed to maintain flow-through velocity = **0.25 m/s**.
   - Detention time **≈ 45–90 seconds**.
   - Grit settles; lighter organics remain suspended and flow out.

2. **Aerated Grit Chamber**
   - Air is introduced through diffusers along one side → creates a **spiral roll velocity**.
   - Settled grit is removed by chain scrapers/screw conveyors.
   - Advantages: Pre-aeration oxidizes organics; no odor issues.

3. **Vortex-Type Grit Chamber**
   - Uses centrifugal force of vortex flow to separate grit.

---

### C. Oil & Grease Removal

**Principle:** Density difference — oil (SG ~0.8–0.9) rises to surface, while water settles below. Surface skimmers scrape off the floating layer.

**Equipment:**

- **API Oil-Water Separator** — rectangular basin; oil rises and is skimmed; sludge settles at bottom.
- **Plate/Corrugated Plate Interceptor (CPI)** — Uses inclined parallel plates to accelerate separation; **much smaller footprint than API**.
- **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** — Micro-bubbles attach to oil droplets; carry them to surface.

---

### D. Equalization

**Principle:** Provides a buffering volume to dampen **temporal variations in flow rate (Q)** and **pollutant concentration (C)** to achieve a near-constant downstream load.

**Types:**
- **In-Line Equalization** — basin placed directly in flow path; equalizes *both flow and concentration*.
- **Side-Line Equalization** — basin placed off-stream; equalizes *concentration* primarily.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Working Insight:** Equalization does NOT remove pollutants — it only **redistributes them uniformly in time**. Without equalization, downstream biological units get shock-loaded during peak hours and starve during lean hours.

---

### E. Neutralization

**Principle:** Mixing acidic (pH < 7) and alkaline (pH > 7) wastes, or dosing chemical reagents, to bring pH into the biologically favorable range **6.5–8.5**.

**Common Reagents:**
- For acidic wastewater: **Lime Ca(OH)₂**, NaOH, Na₂CO₃.
- For alkaline wastewater: **H₂SO₄**, HCl, CO₂ (smoke).

**Working:**
- Equipped with **automatic pH controllers** and **recirculation pumps**.
- Mixing is critical — paddle mixers or air diffusion systems ensure uniform pH.

---

### F. Coagulation

**Principle:** Addition of chemical coagulants (Al₂(SO₄)₃, FeCl₃, FeSO₄, lime, polyaluminum chloride) to **destabilize colloidal particles** by neutralizing their surface charge (zeta potential).

**Mechanism:**
1. Colloidal particles carry a **negative surface charge (zeta potential)** that prevents them from approaching each other.
2. Coagulant (e.g., Al³⁺, Fe³⁺) hydrolyzes to form cationic species.
3. These cations compress the **electrical double layer** and neutralize the zeta potential.
4. Particles become **destabilized** and can now approach each other.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Hook:** The optimal coagulant dose is determined by the **Jar Test** — a laboratory bench-scale simulation.

---

### G. Flocculation

**Principle:** Gentle, slow mixing of destabilized colloids to promote inter-particle collisions, forming **large, settleable flocs**.

**Velocity Gradient (G):**
- Flocculation requires low G to avoid breaking flocs.
- Typical **G = 50–100 s⁻¹**; time **= 20–40 minutes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coagulation vs. Flocculation:**
> - **Coagulation** = *rapid mixing* (G = 700–1000 s⁻¹, t = 30–60 sec), chemical reaction.
> - **Flocculation** = *slow mixing* (G = 50–100 s⁻¹, t = 20–40 min), physical aggregation.

---

### H. Sedimentation

**Principle:** Gravity settling of suspended solids (flocs and discrete particles) in quiescent or low-velocity conditions, following **Stokes' Law**.

**Theoretical Basis — Stokes' Law for discrete settling:**

$$ v_s = \frac{g \, (\rho_s - \rho_w) \, d^2}{18 \, \mu} $$

Where:
- $v_s$ = settling velocity (m/s)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\rho_s$ = particle density (kg/m³)
- $\rho_w$ = water density ≈ **1000 kg/m³**
- $d$ = particle diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water (N·s/m²)

---

### I. Clarifiers (Primary Sedimentation Tanks)

**Types:**
- **Circular Radial-Flow Clarifier** (most common) — center-feed or peripheral-feed.
- **Rectangular Horizontal-Flow Clarifier** — inlet at one end, sludge collected by traveling bridge scraper.
- **Square Hopper-Bottom Clarifier** — used for small installations.

**Working Mechanism (Circular Clarifier):**
1. Influent enters the **central well** (feed well) at the center of the tank.
2. Flow radiates outward toward the peripheral **V-notch weir** effluent launder.
3. Suspended solids settle to the bottom and are scraped by rotating **sludge scraper arms** toward the central **sludge hopper**.
4. Sludge is withdrawn via hydrostatic pressure or pumping.
5. Floating scum is pushed inward by **skimmer arm** and removed via scum box.

---

### J. Clariflocculator (Combined Unit)

**Principle:** Combines **coagulation, flocculation, and sedimentation** in a **single integrated tank** to save space, civil cost, and reduce inter-unit piping.

**Working Mechanism:**
1. **Central Flocculation Zone** — slow mixing paddles rotate around a central shaft.
2. **Outer Annular Sedimentation Zone** — flocs formed in the central zone migrate outward and settle on the sloped floor.
3. **Sludge Hopper at Center** — collected sludge is pumped out.
4. **Peripheral Effluent Launder** — clarified water exits over V-notch weirs.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory for Board Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Since **design is excluded** for this module, only these **reference formulas** need to be recognized (not derived numerically).

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18\mu}$ | Stokes settling velocity | m/s |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Overflow rate (also = settling velocity for ideal clarifier) | m³/m²/d or m/s |
| 3 | $t_0 = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{H}{v_s}$ | Theoretical detention time | seconds / hours |
| 4 | $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}}$ | Velocity gradient (power input per unit volume) | s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $G \cdot t$ | Camp–Stein criterion for flocculation (dimensionless) | — |
| 6 | $H_u = 4.5 \cdot v_s$ | Hydraulic settling index (per Wei** et al.) | — |
| 7 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{SOR}$ | Clarifier surface area | m² |
| 8 | $V = Q \cdot t_d$ | Tank volume (Q × detention time) | m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replace vertical bars in math:** Use $\lvert x \rvert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ and $\mid x \mid$ instead of \mid x \mid inside markdown tables to prevent syntax breaks.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility & Engineering Significance

1. **Screening** — Protects downstream pumps and pipes from clogging/fouling. Without it, pump impellers fail within days.
2. **Grit Removal** — Prevents abrasion of pumps, sedimentation of grit in digesters (which reduces digester volume), and protects mechanical equipment.
3. **Oil Removal** — Prevents coating of biological flocs (a phenomenon called **filamentous bulking**); avoids explosion risk in downstream units.
4. **Equalization** — Essential for **industrial wastewater** where discharge is batch-wise; reduces downstream chemical cost.
5. **Neutralization** — Critical before biological treatment; extreme pH kills microbes.
6. **Coagulation-Flocculation-Sedimentation** — Removes **colloidal particles** (1 nm – 100 nm) which gravity alone cannot settle — a **massive real-world application** in municipal water treatment plants (e.g., Kerala Water Authority).

# SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** Design numericals are **excluded** per the official 2024 syllabus. However, students must demonstrate **analytical reasoning**, **working mechanism understanding**, and **comparative analysis**. The derivations below build conceptual depth required to answer 14-mark essay questions confidently.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Stokes' Law for Discrete Particle Settling

This derivation supports the **Sedimentation** unit operation. Although design is excluded, this derivation is foundational for writing **descriptive 14-mark answers**.

### Setup and Assumptions

Consider a single spherical particle of:
- Diameter $d$
- Density $\rho_s$
- Falling through fluid of density $\rho_w$ and viscosity $\mu$
- Under laminar flow conditions (Reynolds number $Re < 1$)

### Step 1: Forces Acting on the Particle

Three forces act on the particle:

1. **Gravitational force (downward):**
$$ F_g = m \cdot g = \rho_s \cdot V_p \cdot g = \rho_s \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) \cdot g $$

2. **Buoyancy force (upward):**
$$ F_b = \rho_w \cdot V_p \cdot g = \rho_w \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) \cdot g $$

3. **Drag force (upward, resisting motion):**
For **laminar flow**, Stokes' drag is:
$$ F_d = 3 \pi \mu d \cdot v_s $$

### Step 2: Net Force on the Particle

$$ F_{net} = F_g - F_b - F_d $$

$$ F_{net} = \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) g (\rho_s - \rho_w) - 3 \pi \mu d \cdot v_s $$

### Step 3: Terminal Settling Condition

At **terminal settling velocity**, acceleration = 0, so $F_{net} = 0$:

$$ \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) g (\rho_s - \rho_w) = 3 \pi \mu d \cdot v_s $$

### Step 4: Solve for $v_s$

Rearrange:

$$ v_s = \frac{\left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) g (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{3 \pi \mu d} $$

$$ v_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \, \mu} $$

This is the famous **Stokes' Law** for discrete settling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) Equivalence to Settling Velocity

This derivation explains why a **clarifier's overflow rate** equals the **settling velocity** of the critical particle (the smallest particle that needs to be 100 % removed).

### Setup

A clarifier with:
- Surface area $A$ (m²)
- Flow rate $Q$ (m³/s)
- Depth $H$ (m)

### Step 1: Define SOR

$$ SOR = \frac{Q}{A} $$

Units: m³/m²/s ≡ m/s

### Step 2: Define Detention Time

$$ t_0 = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{A \cdot H}{Q} $$

### Step 3: Time Required for Particle to Settle Through Depth H

For a particle to be **completely removed**, it must settle from the surface to the sludge zone (depth $H$) before exiting the clarifier via overflow.

Time to settle through $H$:

$$ t_s = \frac{H}{v_s} $$

### Step 4: Equating Time

For 100 % removal of a particle, **time of settling < detention time**:

$$ \frac{H}{v_s} = \frac{A \cdot H}{Q} $$

Cancel $H$:

$$ \frac{1}{v_s} = \frac{A}{Q} = \frac{1}{SOR} $$

Therefore:

$$ v_s = SOR = \frac{Q}{A} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A particle whose settling velocity equals the surface overflow rate will be **just barely removed**. Slower particles escape; faster particles are captured. This is why engineers choose SOR based on the **critical (smallest) particle** to be removed.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Unit Operations

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A comparative table earns **high marks** in board evaluations because it demonstrates synthesis-level understanding (Bloom's Level 4: Analyze).

| Unit Operation | Driving Force / Principle | Equipment Type | Targeted Pollutant | Power/Energy Source | Order in Train |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Screening** | Physical exclusion by size | Bar screens | Floating debris | Manual/Mechanical rake | 1st |
| **Grit Removal** | Gravity (high density, low velocity) | Aerated/Horizontal chamber | Inorganic grits | Air diffuser | 2nd |
| **Oil Removal** | Density difference (gravity) | API / CPI / DAF | Free oil & grease | Skimmer arm | 3rd |
| **Equalization** | Time buffering | Equalization basin | Flow & concentration variation | Mixer | 4th |
| **Neutralization** | Chemical reaction | Mixing tank with pH probe | H⁺ / OH⁻ ions | Stirrer + dosing pump | 5th |
| **Coagulation** | Charge neutralization | Flash mixer | Colloids | High-speed stirrer | 6th |
| **Flocculation** | Slow mixing collision | Paddle mixer | Destabilized colloids | Slow-speed motor | 7th |
| **Sedimentation** | Gravity settling | Clarifier tank | Suspended solids | None (quiescent) | 8th |
| **Clarifier** | Combined feed + settle | Radial/rectangular | Settleable solids | Scraper bridge | 8th (equipment) |
| **Clariflocculator** | Combined coagulate + flocculate + settle | Integrated tank | Colloidal + settleable | Central drive + scraper | 8th (combined) |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Coagulation Mechanism (Detailed Step)

| Stage | Phenomenon | Time | Equipment | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Hydrolysis of Coagulant** | Al₂(SO₄)₃ + H₂O → Al(OH)₃ colloid | < 1 sec | Flash mixer | Hydroxides form |
| **2. Charge Neutralization** | Cationic species adsorb on negatively charged colloids | 1–10 sec | Flash mixer | Zeta potential → 0 |
| **3. Inter-particle Bridging** | Polymer chains link particles | 10–100 sec | Flocculator | Microflocs form |
| **4. Sweep Coagulation** | Enmeshment by hydroxide precipitate | 1–10 min | Flocculator | Macroflocs |
| **5. Settling** | Macroflocs settle by gravity | 1–2 hr | Clarifier | Sludge + clarified water |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Numerical Reasoning (Conceptual, Not Design)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Clarification:** Since *design is not needed*, this example tests **conceptual numerical comprehension**, not engineering sizing.

**Given:**
- A circular clarifier of diameter **D = 10 m**
- Depth **H = 3.5 m**
- Influent flow **Q = 1500 m³/day**
- Particle density $\rho_s = 2650$ kg/m³ (quartz grit-like)
- Particle diameter $d = 0.1$ mm = $1 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- Water viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m²
- $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³

**Find:**
(a) Settling velocity by Stokes' law
(b) Surface overflow rate (SOR)
(c) Whether the particle is fully captured

---

### Solution

#### Part (a): Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)

$$ v_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \, \mu} $$

Substitute:

$$ v_s = \frac{9.81 \times (2650 - 1000) \times (1 \times 10^{-4})^2}{18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}} $$

Compute numerator:

$$ 9.81 \times 1650 \times 1 \times 10^{-8} $$

$$ = 9.81 \times 1650 \times 10^{-8} $$

$$ = 16186.5 \times 10^{-8} $$

$$ = 1.61865 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s} $$

Denominator:

$$ 18 \times 10^{-3} = 0.018 $$

Therefore:

$$ v_s = \frac{1.61865 \times 10^{-4}}{0.018} = 8.99 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s} $$

Convert to m/day:

$$ v_s = 8.99 \times 10^{-3} \times 86400 = 776.7 \, \text{m/day} $$

#### Part (b): Surface Overflow Rate

Surface area:

$$ A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 10^2}{4} = 78.54 \, \text{m}^2 $$

$$ SOR = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1500}{78.54} = 19.1 \, \text{m/day} $$

#### Part (c): Capture Verdict

- $v_s = 776.7$ m/day
- $SOR = 19.1$ m/day
- Since $v_s \gg SOR$ (i.e., $776.7 \gg 19.1$),
- **The particle settles much faster than the overflow rate**, hence **fully captured**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Insight:** In real KTU questions, the comparison $v_s$ vs. SOR answers **YES/NO** capture — no numerical design needed.
