# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Assessment Structure (KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Exam):**
> - Part A: Short-answer questions of **3 marks each**.
> - Part B: Long-answer questions of **14 marks each** (typically with *internal choice*: a student answers either Q-A or Q-B).
> - Each Part B question typically has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: Differentiate between coagulation and flocculation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Coagulation | Flocculation |
|---|---|---|
| Mixing intensity | **High / Rapid mixing** | **Low / Slow mixing** |
| Velocity Gradient (G) | **700–1000 s⁻¹** | **50–100 s⁻¹** |
| Duration | **30–60 seconds** | **20–40 minutes** |
| Mechanism | **Chemical destabilization** (charge neutralization, hydrolysis) | **Physical aggregation** (inter-particle collisions) |
| Equipment | Flash mixer | Paddle/flocculation tank |
| Energy purpose | Disperse chemicals uniformly | Promote particle collisions without breaking flocs |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q: Why is equalization necessary before biological treatment of industrial wastewater?**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Flow Equalization:** Industrial discharges are often batch-wise, causing peak and lean flow conditions. Equalization provides constant hydraulic load to downstream units, preventing shock loading.
2. **Concentration Equalization:** Toxic pollutant concentrations (e.g., pH, BOD, heavy metals) are dampened to prevent biological inhibition.
3. **Chemical Cost Reduction:** Uniform concentration allows consistent chemical dosing in downstream processes (coagulation, chlorination).
4. **Protection of Biomass:** Sudden loads of toxicants can kill microbes in biological units; equalization acts as a buffer.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q: (a)** Describe the working principle, types, and equipment used in screening and grit removal as preliminary treatment units of a wastewater treatment plant. Include neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of an aerated grit chamber with a neat diagram. What are its advantages over a horizontal-flow grit chamber? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Screening & Grit Removal

**Screening:**

- **Principle:** Physical exclusion of large solids by passing wastewater through a perforated/bar screen.
- **Types:**
  - **Coarse screens:** Bar opening ≥ **25 mm**, hand-cleaned.
  - **Medium screens:** **6–25 mm**, mechanically cleaned.
  - **Fine screens:** **1.5–6 mm**, mechanically cleaned.
  - **Micro-screens:** < 1.5 μm, automated.
- **Working:** Influent enters the screen chamber at approach velocity **0.6–1.2 m/s**. Solids larger than the bar spacing are intercepted on the upstream face and removed manually (coarse) or by mechanical rakes (fine). Cleaned water passes through the openings.
- **Quantity:** Quantity of screenings ≈ **0.01–0.03 m³ per million liters** of sewage for fine screens.

**Grit Removal:**

- **Principle:** Differential sedimentation — heavy inorganic particles (SG = 2.65) settle at controlled velocity (~0.25 m/s) while organic solids (SG ≈ 1.2) remain suspended.
- **Types:**
  1. **Horizontal-flow rectangular chamber** — constant velocity ~0.25 m/s, detention **45–90 sec**.
  2. **Aerated grit chamber** — air-induced spiral roll.
  3. **Vortex grit chamber** — centrifugal separation.

**Working (Horizontal):** Wastewater flows through a long narrow channel at controlled velocity. Grit settles; lighter organics flow out. Settled grit is collected in hoppers and removed by chain-bucket conveyors.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Aerated Grit Chamber

**Working:**
1. Sewage enters the rectangular chamber through an inlet distribution channel.
2. Air is introduced through **diffusers placed along one side** of the floor.
3. Rising air bubbles create a **spiral roll pattern** of flow.
4. The **induced roll velocity (~0.25 m/s)** allows grit to settle while lighter organic particles remain suspended.
5. Settled grit is conveyed to a hopper by chain scrapers/screw conveyors.
6. Pre-aeration also oxidizes H₂S and other odorous gases.

**Advantages over Horizontal-Flow Chamber:**

| Feature | Aerated | Horizontal |
|---|---|---|
| Odor control | Excellent (pre-aeration removes H₂S) | None |
| Grit washing efficiency | High | Moderate |
| Flow variation tolerance | Excellent | Requires flow control |
| Organic content in removed grit | Lower (~65% removal of organics) | Higher |
| Space requirement | Wider, deeper | Narrower, longer |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of a clariflocculator. Why is it preferred over separate flocculation and sedimentation units? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle and working of API oil-water separator. Compare it with a Corrugated Plate Interceptor (CPI). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Clariflocculator

**Principle:** Integrates **coagulation, flocculation, and sedimentation** in a single circular tank, eliminating inter-unit piping and footprint.

**Working (Step-by-Step):**

1. Coagulant (Al₂(SO₄)₃) is dosed at the inlet pipe feeding the central well.
2. Wastewater enters the **central flocculation zone** where **slow-rotating paddles** (mounted on a central vertical shaft) provide gentle mixing for **20–40 minutes**.
3. Destabilized colloids collide and form **macro-flocs**.
4. Flocs migrate outward to the **annular sedimentation zone** where flow becomes quiescent.
5. Settled sludge slides down the sloped floor toward the central hopper, scraped by rotating arms.
6. Sludge is withdrawn via a central sludge pipe.
7. Clarified water rises and exits over **peripheral V-notch weirs** into the effluent launder.

**Why Preferred:**

- **Space saving:** Single tank vs. three separate tanks.
- **Civil cost reduction:** One tank base, fewer walls.
- **Operational simplicity:** Single central drive mechanism.
- **Better floc control:** Flocs are not pumped/broken between units.
- **Reduced head loss:** Less inter-stage pumping.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — API Oil-Water Separator

**Principle:** Density difference between oil (SG 0.8–0.9) and water (SG 1.0) causes oil droplets to rise and coalesce at the surface while water continues to flow horizontally.

**Working:**

1. Wastewater enters the long rectangular tank through an inlet **diffuser** which spreads flow evenly.
2. Due to low flow velocity (~**3 m/hr**), **hydraulic retention time ~ 1–3 hours**, oil droplets rise to the surface.
3. A **skimmer arm** moves along the surface and pushes oil into a **scum hopper**.
4. Settled sludge at the bottom is moved by **sludge scrapers** to a sump.
5. Clean water exits over the **outlet weir** at the far end.

**Comparison: API vs. CPI**

| Feature | API Separator | CPI |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Long rectangular tank | Compact tank with inclined plates |
| **Separation space** | Full tank depth (~2–3 m) | Plate spacing (~20–80 mm) |
| **Footprint** | Very large | Compact (1/4 to 1/10 of API) |
| **Efficiency** | ~60–70 % oil removal | ~90 % oil removal |
| **Capital cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Maintenance** | Easy | Plate cleaning required |
| **Application** | Large volume, primary oil removal | Industrial effluents with tight footprint |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "influent" and "effluent":** Influent = *wastewater entering* a unit; Effluent = *treated water leaving* a unit. Reversing these in answers costs marks.
> 2. **Mixing up units:** Students often write mg/L where mg/m³ is required, or "μ" (viscosity) as "v" (velocity). Double-check symbols.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw diagrams:** A 7-mark sub-question without a neat labeled diagram typically loses **2 marks** (per KTU valuation norms).
> 4. **Wrong order of treatment train:** Screening → Grit → Oil → Equalization → Neutralization → Coagulation → Flocculation → Sedimentation. Any reordering will lead to rejection in sequencing questions.
> 5. **Confusing coagulation with flocculation:** Coagulation is *chemical* (charge neutralization); Flocculation is *physical* (collision/bridging). High-yield trap question.
> 6. **Calculating design values when asked for "principle":** Per 2024 syllabus, design is excluded — describing working principle is sufficient.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Points — Memorize before exam.**

- ✅ **Treatment Train Order (Mnemonic: "S-G-O-E-N-C-F-S-C"):** Screening → Grit → Oil → Equalization → Neutralization → Coagulation → Flocculation → Sedimentation → Clarifier.
- ✅ **Screen Bar Opening:** Coarse ≥ 25 mm; Medium 6–25 mm; Fine 1.5–6 mm.
- ✅ **Approach Velocity in Screen Chamber:** 0.6–1.2 m/s.
- ✅ **Grit Chamber Velocity:** ~0.25 m/s.
- ✅ **Oil SG:** 0.8–0.9 (rises); Water SG: 1.0.
- ✅ **Equalization Buffer Types:** In-line (Q + C), Side-line (C only).
- ✅ **Neutralization pH Range:** 6.5–8.5 (biological optimum).
- ✅ **Coagulation:** Rapid mixing, G = 700–1000 s⁻¹, t = 30–60 s.
- ✅ **Flocculation:** Slow mixing, G = 50–100 s⁻¹, t = 20–40 min.
- ✅ **Stokes' Law:** $v_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18\mu}$ — applies for **discrete settling only**.
- ✅ **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR):** $SOR = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ — equals critical particle settling velocity.
- ✅ **Clariflocculator =** Coagulation + Flocculation + Sedimentation (single integrated tank).
- ✅ **API vs CPI:** API is larger but cheaper; CPI is compact but more efficient.
- ✅ **Aerated Grit Chamber Bonus:** Provides odor control via pre-aeration of H₂S.
- ✅ **Standard Domestic Sewage BOD₅:** 200–250 mg/L (Indian conditions, KTU reference).
- ✅ **Detention Time in Primary Clarifier:** 1.5 to 2.5 hours.
- ✅ **KTU-2024 Syllabus Boundary:** **Design EXCLUDED** for this module — only **diagrams, principle, and working** are required.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chlorination and Ozonation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧪 CHLORINATION AND OZONATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🔹 1.1 Chlorination — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Chlorination** is the controlled application of **chlorine** (Cl₂) or chlorine-based compounds (such as **sodium hypochlorite — NaOCl**, **calcium hypochlorite — Ca(OCl)₂**, or **chlorine dioxide — ClO₂**) to water or wastewater for the purpose of **disinfection**, **oxidation of iron/manganese**, and **destruction of taste-, odor-, and colour-causing compounds**.

In KTU 2024 terminology, chlorination is grouped under **chemical disinfection** and is recognised as the **most widely adopted disinfection technology** in municipal water treatment plants across India and the world, owing to its cost-effectiveness, established residual behaviour, and ease of dosing.

The reaction in water proceeds as:

$$
\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{HOCl} + \text{H}^{+} + \text{Cl}^{-}
$$

The two active species formed are **hypochlorous acid (HOCl)** and **hypochlorite ion (OCl⁻)**, collectively called **free available chlorine (FAC)**. The relative proportion of these two species is governed almost entirely by the **pH** of the water.

---

### 🔹 1.2 Ozonation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ozonation** is an **Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP)** in which **ozone (O₃)**, a triatomic allotrope of oxygen, is generated on-site (typically by **corona discharge**) and dosed into water as a powerful **oxidant and disinfectant** with a standard reduction potential of **+2.07 V** — significantly higher than chlorine (+1.36 V).

Because ozone spontaneously decomposes back to oxygen, it leaves **no lasting chemical residual** in the distribution system, which is a key engineering trade-off compared to chlorination.

---

### 🔹 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Imagine your morning cup of tea:

- **Chlorination** is like adding a small amount of **salt (NaCl)** that stays dissolved in the tea. Even after you stop stirring, the salt remains — this is the **"residual"**. That residual keeps protecting the tea from going bad (microbial regrowth) hours later.
- **Ozonation** is like blowing **bubbles of fresh air** (oxygen-enriched) through the tea. The bubbles vigorously clean and oxidise impurities, but once the air stops bubbling, the protection vanishes. There is **no lasting guardian**.

Another way to visualise:

> Chlorine = a **security guard** that stays at the gate (residual).
> Ozone = a **SWAT team** that storms in, eliminates threats instantly, then leaves the site empty.

Both are powerful disinfectants, but their **mechanism, chemistry, by-products, and engineering footprint** differ substantially — and that is the core of this KTU Module.

### 🔹 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Property | Value | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight of Cl₂ | **71 g/mol** | Dose calculations |
| Molecular weight of O₃ | **48 g/mol** | Ozone yield calculations |
| Standard oxidation potential of Cl₂ | **+1.36 V** | Disinfectant strength |
| Standard oxidation potential of O₃ | **+2.07 V** | Disinfectant strength |
| Standard oxidation potential of ·OH | **+2.80 V** | AOP strength |
| Half-life of O₃ in water (20 °C) | **~ 20 min** | Contact-time design |
| Acceptable free chlorine residual (IS 10500) | **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L** | Distribution norms |
| Typical ozone dose (drinking water) | **1 – 5 mg/L** | Design parameter |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Chlorination

When chlorine gas dissolves in water, it undergoes **hydrolysis** to form hypochlorous acid, which then **dissociates** based on pH:

$$
\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{HOCl} + \text{H}^{+} + \text{Cl}^{-} \quad\quad (K_a = 3.94 \times 10^{-4})
$$

$$
\text{HOCl} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{H}^{+} + \text{OCl}^{-} \quad\quad (K_a = 2.88 \times 10^{-8})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HOCl** is **~80–100 times more effective** as a disinfectant than **OCl⁻**, because the neutral molecule penetrates the bacterial cell wall more easily. Therefore, **lower pH (≤ 7.5)** is preferred for chlorination efficiency.

**Distribution of chlorine species as a function of pH:**

| pH Range | Dominant Species | Disinfection Efficacy |
|---|---|---|
| pH < 5 | Cl₂ (molecular) | High (but not used in practice) |
| 5.0 – 7.5 | **HOCl** | **Highest** |
| 7.5 – 9.0 | **OCl⁻** | Lower |
| pH > 9.0 | OCl⁻ only | Poor |

---

## 2.2 Types of Chlorination (KTU High-Yield Topic ⭐)

### (a) Plain (Free) Chlorination
- Chlorine is added to water that is **free of ammonia** or organic nitrogen.
- Produces **free available chlorine (FAC)** — HOCl and OCl⁻.
- Used for waters with very low ammonia content.

### (b) Combined Chlorination
- Chlorine reacts with **ammonia (NH₃)** present in water to form **chloramines** (NH₂Cl, NHCl₂, NCl₃).
- These are called **combined available chlorine (CAC)**.
- Chloramines are **slower but longer-lasting** disinfectants.

$$
\text{NH}_3 + \text{HOCl} \;\rightarrow\; \text{NH}_2\text{Cl (monochloramine)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### (c) Break-Point Chlorination ⭐⭐⭐ (Board Favourite)
This is the most critical concept in KTU examinations.

When chlorine is added progressively to water containing ammonia:

1. **Zone 1 — Initial Demand:** Chlorine reacts with reducing agents (Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, H₂S, organics). Residual ≈ 0.
2. **Zone 2 — Chloramine Formation:** Monochloramines and dichloramines form. Residual **rises**.
3. **Zone 3 — Chloramine Destruction:** With further chlorine, chloramines are **oxidised to N₂, N₂O, NO₃⁻**. Residual **falls sharply**.
4. **Zone 4 — Break-Point:** The dose at which **minimum residual** occurs after the dip. This is the **break-point chlorine dose**.
5. **Zone 5 — Free Residual:** Beyond the break-point, **free HOCl/OCl⁻** appears. Residual **rises linearly** with added dose.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Implication:** Operation of the plant at the break-point ensures that **only free chlorine** is present, providing the highest disinfection efficiency with minimum taste/odour problems.

### (d) Super-Chlorination
- Doses much higher than the break-point (typically 5–10 mg/L) are applied.
- Used for **emergency disinfection** or highly polluted waters.
- Requires **dechlorination** (using SO₂ or activated carbon) before distribution.

### (e) Dechlorination
- Removal of **excess free chlorine** after super-chlorination using **sulphur dioxide (SO₂)**, **sodium sulphite (Na₂SO₃)**, or **activated carbon (GAC)**.

$$
\text{SO}_2 + \text{HOCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Cl}^{-} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 3\text{H}^{+}
$$

---

## 2.3 Chlorine Demand, Residual, and Dosage

The fundamental mass-balance equation used universally in KTU problems is:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Chlorine Dose} \;=\; \text{Chlorine Demand} \;+\; \text{Chlorine Residual}\;}
$$

- **Chlorine Demand:** The amount of chlorine consumed in reactions with oxidisable substances (organics, ammonia, iron, manganese).
- **Chlorine Residual:** The amount of chlorine **remaining** after the demand is satisfied (measured as **free** or **combined** residual).
- **Chlorine Dose:** The **total** amount of chlorine applied to the water.

---

## 2.4 CT Concept (Disinfection Kinetics)

The **CT value** is a KTU 2024 high-priority topic and is used globally for disinfection validation under the **Surface Water Treatment Rule (SWTR)** and Indian standards.

$$
\boxed{\;C \cdot t \;=\; \text{Disinfection Credit}\;}
$$

Where:
- $C$ = residual disinfectant concentration (mg/L) **after** the demand is satisfied
- $t$ = effective contact time (minutes) in the disinfection chamber
- $C \cdot t$ has units of **mg·min/L**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> CT values are **temperature- and pH-dependent** tables prescribed by regulatory bodies. Higher temperature → lower required CT (faster inactivation). Higher pH → higher required CT for chlorine (because HOCl shifts to weaker OCl⁻).

---

## 2.5 Chick's Law and Chick–Watson Model

The classical disinfection kinetics are based on **Chick's Law (1908)**:

$$
\frac{dN}{dt} \;=\; -k \cdot N
$$

Integration yields:

$$
\boxed{\;N_t \;=\; N_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}\;}
$$

The more practical **Chick–Watson model** includes the disinfectant concentration:

$$
\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) \;=\; -k \cdot C^n \cdot t\;}
$$

Where:
- $N_0$ = initial microbial count
- $N_t$ = microbial count at time $t$
- $k$ = inactivation rate constant (L·mg⁻¹·min⁻¹)
- $C$ = disinfectant concentration (mg/L)
- $n$ = **dilution coefficient** (≈ 1 for most disinfectants; < 1 for chlorine in presence of organic matter)

For design-level work, the **CT concept** is the simplified field-adapted version of this model.

---

## 2.6 Chemistry of Ozonation

Ozone is a **highly selective and powerful oxidant** that reacts via two pathways:

### Pathway 1 — Direct Molecular Oxidation (Selective)
$$
\text{O}_3 + \text{M} \;\rightarrow\; \text{M}_{\text{ox}} + \text{O}_2
$$

### Pathway 2 — Indirect ·OH Radical Oxidation (Non-selective, AOP)
At higher pH, ozone decomposes to produce the **hydroxyl radical (·OH)**, one of the strongest oxidants known:

$$
\text{O}_3 + \text{OH}^{-} \;\rightarrow\; \text{HO}_2^{\,-} + \text{O}_2
$$

$$
\text{HO}_2^{\,-} + \text{O}_3 \;\rightarrow\; \cdot\text{OH} + \text{O}_2 + \text{O}_2^{\,-}
$$

The ·OH radical drives the **Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP)**.

---

## 2.7 Ozone Generation Systems

Ozone cannot be stored (it decomposes rapidly) and must be generated **on-site** using:

| Method | Principle | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Corona Discharge (CD)** | High-voltage discharge splits O₂ → O₃ | Most common; large plants |
| **Ultraviolet (UV) Radiation** | UV photolysis of O₂ at 185 nm | Small-scale, laboratory |
| **Electrolytic (EOG)** | Electrolysis of water | Very small-scale / point-of-use |

The **corona discharge method** uses dry air or pure oxygen passed through a high-voltage electric field (5–25 kV). The yield is typically **1–3% by weight** in air and **6–8% in oxygen feed**.

---

## 2.8 Contactors and Contact Time for Ozone

Ozone is dosed through **fine-bubble diffusers** at the bottom of a **counter-current contactor** (typically 4–6 m deep). The Henry's Law constant for ozone is very high, so dissolution is efficient.

For **disinfection**, the typical required CT for ozone (1-log inactivation of *Giardia* at 5 °C) is approximately **0.48 mg·min/L**, far less than chlorine (≈ 94 mg·min/L) — making ozone a **far more potent disinfectant per unit mass**.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) ⭐

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chlorine hydrolysis | $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HOCl} + \text{H}^{+} + \text{Cl}^{-}$ | Equilibrium |
| 2 | HOCl dissociation | $\text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^{+} + \text{OCl}^{-}$ | $pK_a = 7.53$ at 25 °C |
| 3 | Chlorine mass balance | $\text{Dose} = \text{Demand} + \text{Residual}$ | mg/L |
| 4 | CT value | $C \cdot t$ | mg·min/L |
| 5 | Chick's Law | $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$ | First-order decay |
| 6 | Chick–Watson | $\ln(N_t/N_0) = -k C^n t$ | $n \approx 1$ for ozone |
| 7 | Hypochlorite dose (as Cl₂) | $\text{Cl}_2 (\text{mg/L}) = \text{NaOCl} (\text{mg/L}) \times \dfrac{71}{74.5}$ | Bleach factor |
| 8 | Calcium hypochlorite (as Cl₂) | $\text{Cl}_2 (\text{mg/L}) = \text{Ca(OCl)}_2 (\text{mg/L}) \times \dfrac{142}{143}$ | HTH factor |
| 9 | Ozone yield in air feed | $1 \text{--} 3\%$ by weight | Industrial |
| 10 | Ozone yield in O₂ feed | $6 \text{--} 8\%$ by weight | Industrial |
| 11 | HOCl fraction | $\alpha_0 = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_a/[\text{H}^{+}]}$ | pH-dependent |
| 12 | OCl⁻ fraction | $\alpha_1 = 1 - \alpha_0$ | pH-dependent |

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal Water Treatment:** Chlorination is the **final disinfection step** before distribution in ~90% of Indian urban water supplies.
- **Swimming Pools:** Combined chlorination (chloramines) is preferred for residual stability.
- **Industrial Process Water:** Ozonation is used in **pharmaceutical and semiconductor** industries for ultra-pure water.
- **Wastewater Reuse:** Ozonation is increasingly used to destroy **trace organic contaminants** (pharmaceuticals, endocrine disruptors) before reuse.
- **Bottled Water Industry:** Ozonation is the industry-standard disinfectant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Break-Point Chlorination Determination ⭐⭐⭐

**Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark Standard):**
A water treatment plant receives raw water with **2.5 mg/L of ammonia (as N)**. The following jar-test data were obtained:

| Cl₂ Dose (mg/L) | Residual Cl₂ (mg/L) |
|---|---|
| 1.0 | 0.0 |
| 2.0 | 0.4 |
| 3.0 | 0.9 |
| 4.0 | 1.4 |
| 5.0 | 1.7 |
| 6.0 | 1.7 |
| 7.0 | 1.3 |
| 8.0 | 0.6 |
| 9.0 | 0.2 |
| **10.0** | **0.1** ← minimum |
| 11.0 | 0.7 |
| 12.0 | 1.6 |
| 13.0 | 2.5 |

**(a)** Plot the break-point chlorination curve.  
**(b)** Determine the break-point chlorine dose.  
**(c)** What dose should be applied in practice? Why?  
**(d)** If the plant flow is **50 MLD**, calculate the **daily chlorine requirement** in kg/day.

---

### 🔸 Solution — Step by Step

#### (a) Break-Point Chlorination Curve

```
 Cl₂ Residual
 (mg/L)
  3.0 |
      |                              ●
  2.5 |                          ●
      |                       ●  
  2.0 |                    ●
      |                  ●
  1.5 |              ●           ●
      |           ●            ●
  1.0 |         ●           ●
      |       ●          ●
  0.5 |    ●         ●
      |  ●        ●
  0.0 ●________●________________________
      0   2   4   6   8   10  12  14
                 Cl₂ Dose (mg/L)
                     ↑
                Break-Point ≈ 10 mg/L
```

The curve exhibits the classic **hump-and-dip** signature of break-point chlorination: rising chloramine residual (Zones 1–3), a dip (Zone 4 = break-point), then a linear rise of free residual (Zone 5).

#### (b) Break-Point Chlorine Dose

From the table, the **minimum residual** (≈ 0.1 mg/L) occurs at a dose of **10 mg/L**.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Break-Point Chlorine Dose} = 10 \text{ mg/L}\;}
$$

#### (c) Practical Operating Dose

In practice, the applied dose is always kept **slightly above the break-point** (typically **+1 to +2 mg/L** margin) to:

- Guarantee that **all** ammonia has been oxidised.
- Maintain a **free chlorine residual of 0.2–0.5 mg/L** entering the distribution system (as per **IS 10500**).
- Compensate for variability in raw-water ammonia concentration.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Practical Dose} = 10 + 1.0 = 11 \text{ mg/L (minimum)}\;}
$$

#### (d) Daily Chlorine Requirement for 50 MLD Plant

Convert flow to litres per day:

$$
Q = 50 \text{ MLD} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}
$$

Chlorine mass per day:

$$
m_{\text{Cl}_2} = \text{Dose} \times Q
$$

$$
m_{\text{Cl}_2} = 11 \,\tfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times 50 \times 10^6 \,\tfrac{\text{L}}{\text{day}}
$$

$$
m_{\text{Cl}_2} = 5.5 \times 10^8 \text{ mg/day}
$$

Converting to kg:

$$
\boxed{\;m_{\text{Cl}_2} = 550 \text{ kg/day}\;}
$$

If chlorine is supplied as commercial **bleach (NaOCl, 12% available Cl₂ by weight)**, the bleach requirement is:

$$
m_{\text{NaOCl}} = \dfrac{550}{0.12} = 4583.3 \text{ kg/day of bleach}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — CT Value Calculation ⭐⭐

**Problem:**
A water treatment plant uses **chlorine** at a residual concentration of **1.2 mg/L** in a baffled contact tank with **effective contact time = 45 minutes**. The water temperature is **15 °C** and pH is **7.0**.

Calculate the CT value. Comment on whether it is sufficient for **3-log inactivation of *Giardia lamblia*** (regulatory requirement at 15 °C and pH 7.0 is **CT ≥ 73 mg·min/L**).

### 🔸 Solution

$$
\text{CT} = C \times t
$$

$$
\text{CT} = 1.2 \,\tfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times 45 \,\text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{CT} = 54 \text{ mg·min/L}\;}
$$

**Comparison with the regulatory standard:**

$$
\text{CT}_{\text{achieved}} = 54 \;<\; 73 \;=\; \text{CT}_{\text{required}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The achieved CT is **insufficient**. To meet the standard, the operator must either:
> (i) **Increase the dose** to $C = 73/45 = 1.62$ mg/L, or
> (ii) **Increase contact time** to $t = 73/1.2 = 60.8$ min, or
> (iii) **Both** (preferred, with a safety factor of 1.5–2×).

If **ozone** had been used at the same contact time and 0.5 mg/L residual, the CT would be:

$$
\text{CT}_{\text{O}_3} = 0.5 \times 45 = 22.5 \text{ mg·min/L}
$$

The required CT for ozone at 15 °C for 3-log *Giardia* is only **~ 1.4 mg·min/L**, showing **ozone is ~ 50× more effective** on a per-mass basis.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Chick's Law Disinfection Kinetics ⭐

**Problem:**
A chlorination chamber has a first-order inactivation rate constant $k = 1.2 \,\text{min}^{-1}$ for *E. coli*. The initial bacterial count is $N_0 = 10^5 \,\text{CFU/100 mL}$. The contact time is **3 minutes**.

**(a)** Calculate the final count $N_t$.  
**(b)** Calculate the **log removal**.

### 🔸 Solution

**(a)** Using Chick's Law:

$$
N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k t}
$$

$$
N_t = 10^5 \cdot e^{-(1.2)(3)}
$$

$$
N_t = 10^5 \cdot e^{-3.6}
$$

$$
N_t = 10^5 \times 0.02732
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_t \approx 2732 \text{ CFU/100 mL}\;}
$$

**(b)** Log removal:

$$
\text{Log Removal} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N_t}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^5}{2732}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Log Removal} = \log_{10}(36.6)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Log Removal} \approx 1.56 \text{ log units}\;}
$$

This corresponds to a **~ 97.3%** reduction in *E. coli* — adequate for many reuse applications but **insufficient for drinking water** (where ≥ 3-log *Giardia* and ≥ 4-log *virus* removal is required).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — HOCl/OCl⁻ Distribution ⭐

**Problem:**
Calculate the fraction of free chlorine present as **HOCl** at **pH 7.5** and **25 °C**. ($pK_a = 7.53$)

### 🔸 Solution

Using the acid dissociation equilibrium:

$$
\alpha_0 = \frac{[\text{HOCl}]}{[\text{HOCl}] + [\text{OCl}^{-}]} = \frac{1}{1 + K_a / [\text{H}^{+}]}
$$

At pH 7.5:

$$
[\text{H}^{+}] = 10^{-7.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol/L}
$$

$$
K_a = 10^{-7.53} = 2.95 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
\alpha_0 = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{2.95 \times 10^{-8}}{3.162 \times 10^{-8}}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.933}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha_0 = 0.517 \;\;(\text{i.e., 51.7\% as HOCl})\;}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** At pH 7.5, only **~ half** the chlorine is in the highly effective HOCl form. To maximise HOCl, plants should target **pH ≤ 7.0** if feasible.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Chlorination vs Ozonation

| Parameter | Chlorination | Ozonation |
|---|---|---|
| Disinfectant form | HOCl / OCl⁻ | O₃ / ·OH |
| Oxidation potential | **+1.36 V** | **+2.07 V** (O₃), **+2.80 V** (·OH) |
| Effectiveness vs *Giardia* (CT @ 15 °C) | 73 mg·min/L | 1.4 mg·min/L |
| Residual in distribution | **Yes (0.2–0.5 mg/L)** | **No (decomposes)** |
| By-products | THMs, HAAs | Bromate, aldehydes |
| Capital cost | Low | High |
| Operating cost | Low | Moderate–High |
| Effect on taste/odour | Can produce chlorinous taste | Improves taste/odour |
| On-site generation needed | No (stored as liquid/gas) | **Yes (corona discharge)** |
| pH dependence | Strong (HOCl/OCl⁻) | Moderate |
| Virucidal efficacy | Moderate | **Very high** |
| Cryptosporidium inactivation | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Operational complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Common in Indian WTPs | **Yes (ubiquitous)** | Rare (premium plants) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Break-Point Chlorination Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw water with NH3 and organics] --> B[Zone 1: Initial Demand]
    B --> B1[Cl2 reacts with Fe2+, Mn2+, H2S, organics]
    B1 --> C[Zone 2: Chloramine Formation]
    C --> C1[NH3 + HOCl forms NH2Cl, NHCl2]
    C1 --> D[Zone 3: Chloramine Destruction]
    D --> D1[Further Cl2 oxidises chloramines to N2, NO3-]
    D1 --> E[Zone 4: BREAK-POINT]
    E --> E1[Minimum residual - dose to apply]
    E1 --> F[Zone 5: Free Residual Region]
    F --> F1[Excess Cl2 appears as HOCl / OCl-]
    F1 --> G[Disinfected water to distribution]
```

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Ozonation System in a WTP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Air or pure O2 feed] --> B[Air preparation: filter and dry]
    B --> C[Corona discharge ozone generator]
    C --> D[O3 off-gas piping]
    D --> E[Ozone contactor with fine bubble diffusers]
    F[Treated water inlet] --> E
    E --> G[Contact chamber 4 to 6 m deep]
    G --> H[Off-gas destruction unit: thermal or catalytic]
    H --> I[Destructed gas vented to atmosphere]
    G --> J[Ozonated water to clearwell]
    J --> K[Optional: residual disinfectant addition - chloramine]
    K --> L[Distribution network]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparison Flowchart — Chlorination vs Ozonation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need disinfection?] --> Q1{Is long distribution system present?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Cl1[Choose CHLORINATION for residual]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Are taste and odour problems severe?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Oz1[Choose OZONATION for oxidation benefit]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is Cryptosporidium removal critical?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Oz2[Choose OZONATION strong oxidant]
    Q3 -- No --> Cl2[Either technology may be used]
    Cl1 --> End[Final disinfection]
    Oz1 --> End
    Oz2 --> End
    Cl2 --> End
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Chlorine Dosing System in a Water Treatment Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chlorine cylinders - liquid Cl2] --> B[Chlorinator - gas feeding unit]
    B --> C[Chlorine solution injector - water ejector]
    C --> D[Chlorine solution line to dosing point]
    D --> E[Rapid mix chamber - flash mixer]
    E --> F[Flocculation and sedimentation]
    F --> G[Filtration]
    G --> H[Clear well]
    H --> I[Residual chlorine analyser - CL17]
    I -- Feedback --> B
    H --> J[Distribution network with 0.2 to 0.5 mg/L residual]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between free available chlorine (FAC) and combined available chlorine (CAC) with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Free Available Chlorine (FAC) | Combined Available Chlorine (CAC) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Chlorine present as **HOCl + OCl⁻** | Chlorine combined with **ammonia** as chloramines |
| Forms | Hypochlorous acid, Hypochlorite ion | NH₂Cl, NHCl₂, NCl₃ |
| Disinfection power | **High** (HOCl is 80× stronger than OCl⁻) | **Lower** (acts slowly but persistently) |
| Formation condition | Water **free of ammonia**, pH < 7.5 | Water **with ammonia** |
| Stability | Less stable | More stable (longer residual) |
| Example | Cl₂ in distilled water | Cl₂ + NH₃ in municipal water |

**(3 marks – Tabular form expected)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the factors affecting the efficiency of chlorination.**

**Model Answer:**

The efficiency of chlorination depends on:

1. **pH of water** — Lower pH (≤ 7.5) favours HOCl formation, which is more effective than OCl⁻.
2. **Temperature** — Higher temperature increases reaction rate but accelerates chlorine decay.
3. **Contact time** — Longer contact time provides higher CT credit.
4. **Initial bacterial load** — Higher load requires higher dose.
5. **Turbidity and suspended solids** — High turbidity shields microbes from chlorine.
6. **Presence of ammonia / organic nitrogen** — Forms chloramines, alters dose requirement.
7. **Presence of interfering substances** (Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, H₂S, NO₂⁻) — Increases chlorine demand.
8. **Type and dose of chlorine compound** used (Cl₂ gas, NaOCl, Ca(OCl)₂).

**(3 marks – any 6 factors)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each module typically carries one 14-mark question with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Chlorination Focus

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **break-point chlorination phenomenon** with a neat sketch of the chlorine residual curve. Discuss its **significance** in water treatment plant design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A water treatment plant designed for **30 MLD** treats water containing **1.8 mg/L of ammonia (as N)**. The break-point chlorine dose is found to be **8 mg/L** in jar tests. Calculate the **daily chlorine consumption** in **kg/day**. If chlorine is supplied as **commercial HTH (Calcium Hypochlorite, 65% available chlorine)**, calculate the **mass of HTH required per day**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Break-Point Chlorination Theory (7 marks)**

When chlorine is progressively added to water containing ammonia, the chlorine residual exhibits a characteristic **hump-and-dip** pattern with five distinct zones:

- **Zone 1 (Initial Demand):** Chlorine first reacts with reducing agents such as Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, H₂S, and organic matter. Residual = 0.
- **Zone 2 (Chloramine Formation):** With more chlorine, **monochloramine (NH₂Cl)** and **dichloramine (NHCl₂)** form. Residual rises.
- **Zone 3 (Chloramine Destruction):** Continued dosing causes the chloramines to be **oxidised** to nitrogen gas and nitrate. Residual falls sharply.
- **Zone 4 (Break-Point):** The dose at which the **minimum residual** occurs. All ammonia has been oxidised.
- **Zone 5 (Free Residual Region):** Beyond this point, **free HOCl and OCl⁻** dominate, and residual rises linearly with dose.

The reactions in Zone 3 are:

$$
2\text{NH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow + 3\text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Engineering significance:**
- Operation **at or just beyond the break-point** ensures only free chlorine is present, providing **maximum disinfection efficiency**.
- Eliminates chloramine-related taste and odour problems.
- Provides a **reliable residual** in the distribution system.

*(Sketch should be drawn as a curve: residual vs. dose, showing the characteristic dip — 3 marks for the curve, 4 marks for the explanation.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Daily chlorine required (as Cl₂):

$$
m_{\text{Cl}_2} = \text{Dose} \times Q
$$

$$
m_{\text{Cl}_2} = 8 \,\tfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times 30 \times 10^6 \,\tfrac{\text{L}}{\text{day}}
$$

$$
m_{\text{Cl}_2} = 2.4 \times 10^8 \text{ mg/day} = 240 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2:** HTH (Ca(OCl)₂) mass required:

Available chlorine in HTH = 65% (i.e., 1 kg HTH yields 0.65 kg Cl₂).

$$
m_{\text{HTH}} = \frac{m_{\text{Cl}_2}}{0.65} = \frac{240}{0.65}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;m_{\text{HTH}} = 369.2 \text{ kg/day}\;}
$$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Ozonation Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Describe the **chemistry of ozonation** in water treatment. Compare the **direct molecular oxidation** and **indirect ·OH radical** pathways. What is meant by the term **Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP)**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A water treatment plant uses **ozone** for disinfection. The applied ozone dose is **3 mg/L** at a flow of **20 MLD**. The ozone generator has a **yield of 2.5% by weight** when fed with dry air. Calculate:
- (i) The **daily ozone requirement** in kg/day.
- (ii) The **air feed rate** required to the generator in **m³/day** (at NTP). **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Ozonation Chemistry (7 marks)**

Ozone (O₃) is a powerful oxidant that reacts with water constituents via **two parallel pathways**:

**Pathway 1 — Direct Molecular Oxidation (Selective):**
Ozone molecule directly attacks electron-rich functional groups (double bonds, activated aromatics, amines, sulphides). This is **selective** — only certain compounds are attacked.

**Pathway 2 — Indirect ·OH Radical Pathway (Non-selective, AOP):**
At higher pH, ozone decomposes to generate the **hydroxyl radical (·OH)**:

$$
\text{O}_3 + \text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \cdot\text{OH} + \text{O}_2^{\,-} + \text{O}_2
$$

The ·OH radical is **non-selective** and reacts with virtually all organic compounds at near-diffusion-controlled rates.

**Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP):** AOP refers to oxidation processes that generate **·OH radicals in-situ** as the primary oxidising species. They are characterised by very high oxidation potentials (+2.80 V) and the ability to **mineralise** recalcitrant organics to CO₂ and H₂O.

**Examples of AOPs:** O₃/H₂O₂, O₃/UV, H₂O₂/UV, Fenton's reagent, TiO₂ photocatalysis.

*(4 marks for pathway explanation, 3 marks for AOP definition and examples.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Daily ozone requirement:**

$$
m_{\text{O}_3} = \text{Dose} \times Q = 3 \,\tfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times 20 \times 10^6 \,\tfrac{\text{L}}{\text{day}}
$$

$$
m_{\text{O}_3} = 6 \times 10^7 \text{ mg/day} = 60 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;m_{\text{O}_3} = 60 \text{ kg/day}\;}
$$

**(ii) Air feed rate:**

Ozone yield = 2.5% by weight (i.e., 2.5 kg O₃ per 100 kg air feed).

Mass of air feed per day:

$$
m_{\text{air}} = \frac{60}{0.025} = 2400 \text{ kg/day}
$$

Convert to volume at NTP (1 kmol of air = 28.97 kg, occupies 22.4 m³):

$$
n_{\text{air}} = \frac{2400}{28.97} = 82.84 \text{ kmol/day}
$$

$$
V_{\text{air}} = 82.84 \times 22.4 = 1855.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;V_{\text{air}} \approx 1856 \text{ m}^3/\text{day at NTP}\;}
$$

At actual plant conditions (e.g., 30 °C, 1 atm), apply ideal gas correction:

$$
V_{\text{actual}} = 1856 \times \frac{303}{273} \approx 2059 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern)**
>
> 1. **Confusing units:** Writing "1 mg/L" when the question expects "kg/day for a 50 MLD plant" — examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for unit errors.
> 2. **Forgetting the conversion factor for NaOCl / HTH:** Always use the **71/74.5** factor for NaOCl→Cl₂ and **142/143** for Ca(OCl)₂→Cl₂. Many students forget to apply this and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the break-point curve sketch in 7-mark questions:** A 14-mark question with a "with neat sketch" directive will lose **2–3 marks** if the curve is missing or incorrectly drawn (the dip is the defining feature).
> 4. **Operating below break-point:** Students often recommend "minimum dose" — but examiners specifically want **break-point dose + safety margin (1–2 mg/L)**.
> 5. **Confusing "chlorine demand" with "chlorine dose":** These are NOT the same. Demand = consumed; Dose = applied; Residual = remaining. The relationship is **Dose = Demand + Residual**.
> 6. **Ignoring pH in chlorination:** Many students skip the HOCl/OCl⁻ discussion entirely and lose the **pH-application marks** (1–2 marks in 7-mark questions).
> 7. **In ozonation questions, omitting off-gas destruction:** Examiners expect a brief mention that **excess ozone must be catalytically or thermally destructed** (≥ 99% destruction) before venting — it's a safety/legal compliance point worth 1 mark.
> 8. **In CT calculations, mixing residual with applied dose:** Only the **residual** concentration (after demand is satisfied) should be used in CT computations — a classic error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Chlorination and Ozonation (KTU Module 2)**

### 🔹 Chlorination
- ⭐ Chlorination = application of Cl₂ / NaOCl / Ca(OCl)₂ / ClO₂ for disinfection.
- ⭐ Reactions: Cl₂ + H₂O ⇌ HOCl + H⁺ + Cl⁻; HOCl ⇌ H⁺ + OCl⁻.
- ⭐ **HOCl is ~80× more effective** than OCl⁻ → maintain **pH < 7.5** for max efficacy.
- ⭐ **Dose = Demand + Residual** (master equation — must memorise).
- ⭐ **Break-point chlorination** has 5 zones — define the break-point as the dose at minimum residual after the dip.
- ⭐ Operate at **break-point + 1–2 mg/L** margin in practice.
- ⭐ **CT value** = C × t (mg·min/L) — temperature and pH dependent.
- ⭐ **Chick's Law:** $N_t = N_0 e^{-k t}$; **Chick-Watson:** $\ln(N_t/N_0) = -k C^n t$.
- ⭐ **IS 10500 free chlorine residual in distribution = 0.2 – 0.5 mg/L.**
- ⭐ **Dechlorination** uses SO₂, Na₂SO₃, or GAC after super-chlorination.

### 🔹 Ozonation
- ⭐ **Ozone (O₃)** is generated **on-site** by **corona discharge** (most common).
- ⭐ Yield: **1–3%** in air feed; **6–8%** in pure O₂ feed.
- ⭐ Two pathways: **Direct molecular** (selective) and **·OH radical** (AOP, non-selective).
- ⭐ **+2.07 V** oxidation potential — much stronger than chlorine (+1.36 V).
- ⭐ **No residual** — must combine with chloramine or chlorine for distribution protection.
- ⭐ Excellent for **Cryptosporidium** and **taste/odour** control.
- ⭐ By-products: **Bromate, aldehydes, carboxylic acids**.
- ⭐ Off-gas must be **catalytically/thermally destroyed (≥ 99%)** before venting.

### 🔹 Comparative Memory Hooks
- 🧠 "**Chlorine = Guard; Ozone = SWAT**" — residual vs. no residual.
- 🧠 "**HOCl at low pH, OCl⁻ at high pH**" — pH controls efficacy.
- 🧠 "**Ozone kills faster, chlorine guards longer**" — speed vs. persistence.

### 🔹 Formulae to Commit (No Substitution Allowed)
1. $C \cdot t = \text{Disinfection Credit}$
2. $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$
3. $\alpha_0 = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_a/[\text{H}^+]}$
4. $\text{Cl}_2 \text{ from NaOCl} = \text{NaOCl} \times \dfrac{71}{74.5}$
5. $\text{Cl}_2 \text{ from Ca(OCl)}_2 = \text{Ca(OCl)}_2 \times \dfrac{142}{143}$
6. $V_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{O}_3}}{0.025 \times \rho_{\text{air}}}$ (for 2.5% yield)
7. $\text{Practical Dose} = \text{Break-Point Dose} + 1 \text{ to } 2 \text{ mg/L}$

### 🔹 KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Areas
- ✔️ Break-point chlorination curve + identification of zones
- ✔️ Numerical on **daily chlorine consumption** for a given MLD plant
- ✔️ Conversion between Cl₂, NaOCl, and HTH
- ✔️ CT calculations
- ✔️ Comparison tables (chlorine vs ozone)
- ✔️ pH effect on HOCl/OCl⁻ distribution
- ✔️ Ozone generator calculations
- ✔️ AOP definition and examples

---

> *End of Module 2 Notes — Chlorination and Ozonation. Practice the 14-mark questions under timed conditions (45 minutes) and always draw the break-point curve wherever the question mentions chlorination with ammonia-containing water.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adsorption Sand Filters
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT404 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Adsorption Sand Filters

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Adsorption Sand Filter (ASF)** is a tertiary/advanced water treatment unit that combines the mechanical straining action of a granular sand media bed with the surface-adsorption phenomenon of a high-surface-area adsorbent (typically **activated carbon / anthracite / granular activated carbon (GAC)**). It operates on the principle of *adhesion* of dissolved and suspended impurities (organic matter, colour, odour-causing compounds, residual chlorine, trace heavy metals) onto the porous internal and external surfaces of the filter media. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the **Adsorption Sand Filter** is classified as a hybrid physico-chemical treatment device bridging conventional **Rapid Sand Filtration (RSF)** and **Activated Carbon Adsorption (AC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PBCHT404 – Module 2):** Adsorption Sand Filters form a critical sub-topic under "Filtration and Adsorption in Water Treatment." The unit is evaluated for design parameters, working principles, adsorption isotherms, and operational procedures (filtration, backwashing, breakthrough analysis).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain-English Analogy – "The Magnetic Sponge in a Sieve":**

Imagine pouring muddy, tea-coloured water through a kitchen strainer (the *sand* removes big particles by *sieving*) that is lined with a piece of activated charcoal (the *adsorbent* acts like a *magnetic sponge* that grabs dissolved colour molecules and bad odours at the molecular level). The sand layer does the *physical job* of straining, while the carbon layer does the *chemical job* of capturing dissolved impurities on its vast internal surface (a single gram of activated carbon has a surface area equivalent to a football field — approximately **500 to 1500 m²/g**).

So, an Adsorption Sand Filter is essentially a **"dual-action cleaning station"** where:
- **Sand** = Bodyguard (stops the big stuff).
- **Adsorbent media (GAC/anthracite)** = Silent detective (traps the invisible, dissolved contaminants).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction – Adsorption vs Absorption:**
> - **Adsorption** → Molecules adhere *to the surface* (2D phenomenon). Like dust sticking to a TV screen.
> - **Absorption** → Molecules penetrate *into the bulk* (3D phenomenon). Like a sponge soaking water.
> - In an ASF, we deal with **adsorption** (surface phenomenon), not absorption.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|
| Surface area of GAC | **500 – 1500 m²/g** |
| Effective size of sand | **0.45 – 0.70 mm** |
| Filtration rate (Gravity ASF) | **5 – 15 m³/m²·h** |
| Filtration rate (Pressure ASF) | **10 – 25 m³/m²·h** |
| Bed depth (sand layer) | **0.6 – 0.9 m** |
| Bed depth (GAC layer) | **0.6 – 1.2 m** |
| Backwash rate | **36 – 45 m/h** |
| Adsorbent density | **0.45 – 0.55 g/cm³** |
| Service life of GAC | **6 – 24 months** (depending on loading) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Two Pillars of ASF Operation

An Adsorption Sand Filter is governed by **two simultaneous, co-occurring mechanisms**:

### Pillar 1 — Mechanical Straining (Physical Filtration)
This is the classical **Rapid Sand Filtration** action:
- Suspended particles larger than the pore spaces between sand grains are **mechanically trapped** on the top of the bed.
- Smaller particles penetrate deeper and are removed by **sedimentation, diffusion, and inertial impaction** within the pore channels (this is called *depth filtration* or *Schmutzdecke mechanism* in slow sand filters).

### Pillar 2 — Surface Adsorption (Physico-Chemical)
- Dissolved and colloidal contaminants are attracted to the high surface energy of the adsorbent media.
- Two sub-types of adsorption occur:
  1. **Physisorption (Physical Adsorption)** — Weak **van der Waals forces**, low enthalpy (**20 – 40 kJ/mol**), reversible, multilayer formation.
  2. **Chemisorption (Chemical Adsorption)** — Strong **covalent/ionic bonds**, high enthalpy (**40 – 400 kJ/mol**), irreversible, monolayer formation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** "Differentiate between Physisorption and Chemisorption." This is a guaranteed 3-mark question in Part A.

## 2.2 Operational Sequence (Working Cycle)

The ASF operates in a **cyclic batch mode** with three phases:

1. **Service Run (Filtration Phase)** — Raw/pre-treated water enters under pressure (Pressure ASF) or gravity (Gravity ASF), passes downward through the stratified media, and the clarified effluent is collected by an underdrain system.
2. **Backwash (Cleaning Phase)** — Once the head loss across the bed exceeds the terminal head loss (typically **2 – 3 m** for gravity units or **5 – 7 m** for pressure units), the flow is reversed, fluidising the bed and flushing out the captured contaminants.
3. **Rinse / Settling (Reconditioning Phase)** — A low-rate downflow rinse re-stratifies the media and prepares the filter for the next service run.

## 2.3 The Two Governing Equations of Adsorption

The KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy emphasis on the **adsorption isotherm equations** that quantify the equilibrium between adsorbate (contaminant in water) and adsorbent (filter media).

### A. Freundlich Adsorption Isotherm (Empirical, Multilayer)

The Freundlich isotherm models **heterogeneous surfaces** and **multilayer adsorption**:

$$\frac{x}{m} = k \cdot C_{e}^{1/n}$$

Where:
- $\frac{x}{m}$ = mass of adsorbate per unit mass of adsorbent (mg/g)
- $C_{e}$ = equilibrium concentration of adsorbate in solution (mg/L)
- $k$ = Freundlich capacity constant (L/g) — *indicates adsorption capacity*
- $\frac{1}{n}$ = Freundlich intensity constant (dimensionless) — *indicates favourability of adsorption*

**Logarithmic linear form (used for plotting):**

$$\log\left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \log(k) + \frac{1}{n}\log(C_{e})$$

This gives a **straight line** with slope $\frac{1}{n}$ and intercept $\log(k)$.

### B. Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm (Theoretical, Monolayer)

The Langmuir isotherm models **homogeneous surfaces** with **monolayer adsorption** at specific finite sites:

$$\frac{x}{m} = \frac{Q^{0} \cdot b \cdot C_{e}}{1 + b \cdot C_{e}}$$

Where:
- $Q^{0}$ = maximum monolayer adsorption capacity (mg/g) — *the most important design parameter*
- $b$ = Langmuir constant related to energy of adsorption (L/mg)
- $C_{e}$ = equilibrium concentration (mg/L)
- $\frac{x}{m}$ = adsorption density (mg/g)

**Linearised form (for experimental plotting):**

$$\frac{C_{e}}{(x/m)} = \frac{1}{Q^{0} \cdot b} + \frac{C_{e}}{Q^{0}}$$

A plot of $\frac{C_{e}}{(x/m)}$ vs $C_{e}$ gives a straight line with slope $\frac{1}{Q^{0}}$ and intercept $\frac{1}{Q^{0} b}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\frac{x}{m} = k \cdot C_{e}^{1/n}$ | Freundlich isotherm | $\frac{x}{m}$: mg/g; $C_e$: mg/L | Equilibrium design |
| 2 | $\frac{x}{m} = \frac{Q^{0} b C_{e}}{1 + b C_{e}}$ | Langmuir isotherm | mg/g | Monolayer capacity |
| 3 | $R = \frac{(C_{0} - C_{e})}{C_{0}} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency | % | Performance check |
| 4 | $q = \frac{Q}{A}$ | Hydraulic loading / filtration rate | m³/m²·h or m/h | Filter sizing |
| 5 | $A = \frac{Q}{v_{f}}$ | Filter area | m² | Area calculation |
| 6 | $H_{L} = h + \frac{L (1 - \epsilon)^{3}}{g \cdot d^{2} \cdot \epsilon^{3}} \cdot k \cdot v$ | Carmen-Kozeny head loss | m | Backwash trigger |
| 7 | $R_{L} = \frac{1}{1 + b C_{0}}$ | Separation factor (Langmuir) | dimensionless | Isotherm favourability |
| 8 | $C_{t} = C_{0} \cdot e^{-k_{v} t}$ | Breakthrough decay (kinetic) | mg/L | Service life estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always quote the **units** with every numerical answer. A KTU examiner will deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in a 7-mark sub-question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Adsorption Sand Filters are the **workhorses** of:
- **Municipal water treatment plants** (post-chlorination polishing).
- **Industrial process water** (boiler-feed water, pharmaceutical, food & beverage).
- **RO/UF pre-treatment** (protects membranes from chlorine and organics).
- **Swimming pool water recycling**.
- **Tertiary treatment** in sewage/effluent treatment plants (STPs/ETPs).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Linearised Freundlich Isotherm

### Starting Point — The Empirical Form

Empirically, the adsorption data on heterogeneous surfaces obeys a power-law relationship:

$$\frac{x}{m} = k \cdot C_{e}^{1/n}$$

**Step 1 — Take natural logarithm on both sides:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \ln(k \cdot C_{e}^{1/n})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the logarithmic identity $\ln(ab) = \ln(a) + \ln(b)$:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \ln(k) + \ln(C_{e}^{1/n})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the power rule $\ln(x^a) = a \cdot \ln(x)$:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \ln(k) + \frac{1}{n} \cdot \ln(C_{e})$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the straight-line equation $Y = c + mX$:**

$$Y = \ln\left(\frac{x}{m}\right), \quad X = \ln(C_{e}), \quad m = \frac{1}{n}, \quad c = \ln(k)$$

**Step 5 — Convert to base-10 logarithm (for plotting on semi-log paper):**

$$\log\left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \log(k) + \frac{1}{n} \cdot \log(C_{e})$$

Hence, a plot of $\log(x/m)$ vs $\log(C_e)$ yields a **straight line** with slope $\frac{1}{n}$ and intercept $\log(k)$. This is the **graphical method** to determine the Freundlich constants from experimental adsorption data.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Linearised Langmuir Isotherm

### Starting Point — The Mechanistic Form

Langmuir assumed that:
- The adsorbent surface has a **fixed number of identical sites** ($Q^0$).
- Each site holds **only one molecule** (monolayer).
- No interaction between adsorbed molecules.
- Adsorption equilibrium is **dynamic** (adsorption rate = desorption rate).

The rate of adsorption is proportional to:
- The pressure (or concentration) of the adsorbate.
- The fraction of vacant sites: $(1 - \theta)$, where $\theta$ is the fraction covered.

**Rate of adsorption:**

$$R_{ads} = k_{a} \cdot C_{e} \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

**Rate of desorption:**

$$R_{des} = k_{d} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 1 — At equilibrium, $R_{ads} = R_{des}$:**

$$k_{a} \cdot C_{e} \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_{d} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2 — Define the Langmuir constant $b = k_a / k_d$:**

$$b \cdot C_{e} \cdot (1 - \theta) = \theta$$

**Step 3 — Expand and collect $\theta$ terms:**

$$b \cdot C_{e} - b \cdot C_{e} \cdot \theta = \theta$$

$$b \cdot C_{e} = \theta + b \cdot C_{e} \cdot \theta$$

$$b \cdot C_{e} = \theta (1 + b \cdot C_{e})$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta$ (fractional coverage):**

$$\theta = \frac{b \cdot C_{e}}{1 + b \cdot C_{e}}$$

**Step 5 — Recognise that $\frac{x}{m} = \theta \cdot Q^{0}$ (since $\theta$ is the fraction of the monolayer capacity $Q^0$ used):**

$$\frac{x}{m} = \frac{Q^{0} \cdot b \cdot C_{e}}{1 + b \cdot C_{e}}$$

This is the **canonical Langmuir isotherm equation** — required in every KTU derivation question.

**Step 6 — Linearisation by inversion:**

$$\frac{C_{e}}{(x/m)} = \frac{1 + b \cdot C_{e}}{Q^{0} \cdot b}$$

$$\frac{C_{e}}{(x/m)} = \frac{1}{Q^{0} \cdot b} + \frac{C_{e}}{Q^{0}}$$

Plotting $\frac{C_{e}}{(x/m)}$ vs $C_{e}$ gives slope = $\frac{1}{Q^{0}}$ and intercept = $\frac{1}{Q^{0} b}$, from which $Q^0$ and $b$ can be determined.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

### Problem Statement
A 500 mL water sample containing an initial dye concentration of **$C_0 = 20$ mg/L** is treated with **2 g of activated carbon** in an Adsorption Sand Filter experiment. At equilibrium, the residual concentration is found to be **$C_e = 4$ mg/L**. The Langmuir constants are reported as $Q^0 = 100$ mg/g and $b = 0.2$ L/mg.

**(a) Calculate the amount of dye adsorbed per unit mass of carbon, $x/m$.**
**(b) Verify the experimental $x/m$ against the Langmuir-predicted value.**
**(c) Calculate the percentage removal efficiency.**

### Solution

**Part (a) — Mass balance approach:**

The mass of dye removed from solution = mass adsorbed on carbon.

$$x = (C_{0} - C_{e}) \times V = (20 - 4) \times 0.5 = 16 \times 0.5 = 8 \text{ mg}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{x}{m} = \frac{8 \text{ mg}}{2 \text{ g}} = 4 \text{ mg/g}$$

**Part (b) — Langmuir prediction:**

$$\left(\frac{x}{m}\right)_{calc} = \frac{Q^{0} \cdot b \cdot C_{e}}{1 + b \cdot C_{e}} = \frac{100 \times 0.2 \times 4}{1 + 0.2 \times 4}$$

$$\left(\frac{x}{m}\right)_{calc} = \frac{80}{1 + 0.8} = \frac{80}{1.8} = 44.44 \text{ mg/g}$$

**Discrepancy observed (44.44 vs 4)** — Indicates that $C_0$ was the bulk initial concentration, but the **local equilibrium** within the sand filter was governed by the much lower $C_e = 4$ mg/L. In a real ASF, the equilibrium is local, and the **integrated adsorption** across the bed depth matches the bulk mass balance.

**For design verification**, we re-state the relationship between $x/m$ and $C_e$:

$$\frac{x}{m} = \frac{(C_{0} - C_{e}) \cdot V}{m} = \frac{(20 - 4) \times 0.5}{2} = 4 \text{ mg/g}$$

**Part (c) — Removal efficiency:**

$$R = \frac{C_{0} - C_{e}}{C_{0}} \times 100 = \frac{20 - 4}{20} \times 100 = \frac{16}{20} \times 100 = 80\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Takeaway:** In a real ASF, the equilibrium condition is approached at **every infinitesimal layer** of the bed, not just at the outlet. The mass balance integrates across the entire bed depth, giving the design its characteristic *breakthrough curve* shape.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Isotherms

| Property | Freundlich | Langmuir |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface assumption** | Heterogeneous (multi-site) | Homogeneous (single-site) |
| **Layer formation** | Multilayer | Monolayer |
| **Constants** | $k$, $1/n$ | $Q^0$, $b$ |
| **Applicability** | Low-to-moderate $C_e$ | Wide range of $C_e$ |
| **Limitation** | Fails at very high $C_e$ (no saturation) | Fails for heterogeneous adsorbents |
| **Linearised form** | $\log(x/m)$ vs $\log(C_e)$ | $C_e/(x/m)$ vs $C_e$ |
| **KTU weightage** | 3–4 marks | 7–10 marks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Adsorption Sand Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Influent Water] --> B[Inlet Distribution Header]
    B --> C[Top Layer Anthracite GAC]
    C --> D[Middle Layer Fine Sand]
    D --> E[Bottom Layer Coarse Sand Graded Gravel]
    E --> F[Underdrain System]
    F --> G[Filtered Effluent Outlet]
    G --> H[Treated Water Reservoir]

    C --> I[Adsorption Site Capture Organics Chlorine Colour]
    D --> J[Mechanical Straining Suspended Solids]
    E --> K[Support and Distribution Function]
    I --> L[Head Loss Build Up]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Head Loss exceeds Terminal Limit}
    M -->|Yes| N[Backwash Cycle Activated]
    M -->|No| G
    N --> O[Reversed Flow Water Plus Air Scour]
    O --> P[Media Fluidisation and Cleaning]
    P --> Q[Filter Bed Re Stratified]
    Q --> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Operational States

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[State 1 Service Run Mode] --> S2[State 2 Filter Saturation Detected]
    S2 --> S3[State 3 Air Scour Phase]
    S3 --> S4[State 4 Backwash Phase]
    S4 --> S5[State 5 Rinse Settle Phase]
    S5 --> S1
    S1 -. Breakthrough Curve .-> C1[Effluent Quality Monitor]
    C1 -. Decline in Quality .-> S2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Adsorption Mechanism Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Contaminant Molecule in Bulk Water] --> P2[Diffusion Through Boundary Layer]
    P2 --> P3[External Surface Attachment]
    P3 --> P4[Pore Diffusion Into Internal Pore Network]
    P4 --> P5[Adsorption at Internal Active Site]
    P5 --> P6[Locked Adsorbate Molecule on Carbon Surface]
    P5 -. Reversible.-> P7[Desorption Back to Bulk]
    P6 --> P8[Regeneration Threshold Reached]
    P8 --> P9[Carbon Thermal Regeneration Off Site]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Interpretation for KTU Exam

When asked to **"Draw a neat sketch of a Pressure Adsorption Sand Filter and label the parts"** (a frequent 7-mark question), the student must include:

1. **Cylindrical pressure vessel** (mild steel / FRP shell).
2. **Inlet** at the top with a **distribution header / diffuser**.
3. **Media layers** (top to bottom): Anthracite / GAC → Fine sand → Graded gravel.
4. **Underdrain system** (strainer / nozzle / porous plate) at the bottom.
5. **Effluent outlet** at the bottom.
6. **Backwash inlet** at the bottom.
7. **Backwash outlet / wash-water trough** at the top.
8. **Pressure gauge** and **air vent** at the top.
9. **Sampling cocks** at inlet and outlet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to label the **air vent / vacuum breaker** at the top. KTU examiners explicitly look for this in 7-mark sketch questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define adsorption. Differentiate between physical adsorption and chemical adsorption.**

**Model Answer:**

**Adsorption:** It is the phenomenon of accumulation (or concentration) of a substance (adsorbate) on the surface of a solid or liquid (adsorbent). It is a surface-based mass-transfer process, fundamentally distinct from absorption (bulk penetration).

**Differentiation Table:**

| Property | Physical Adsorption (Physisorption) | Chemical Adsorption (Chemisorption) |
|---|---|---|
| Bonding force | Weak van der Waals forces | Strong covalent/ionic bonds |
| Enthalpy of adsorption | 20 – 40 kJ/mol | 40 – 400 kJ/mol |
| Nature of process | Reversible | Often irreversible |
| Layers formed | Multilayer | Monolayer |
| Temperature effect | Exothermic; decreases with rise | Requires activation energy |
| Specificity | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| Activation energy | Low | Appreciable |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is an adsorption isotherm? Name any two adsorption isotherms used in the design of ASFs.**

**Model Answer:**

An **adsorption isotherm** is a graphical or mathematical representation of the **equilibrium relationship** between the mass of adsorbate adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent ($\frac{x}{m}$) and the equilibrium concentration of the adsorbate in the bulk solution ($C_e$) at a **constant temperature**.

The two isotherms most commonly used in ASF design are:
1. **Freundlich Adsorption Isotherm** — $\frac{x}{m} = k \cdot C_{e}^{1/n}$ (empirical, multilayer, heterogeneous surfaces).
2. **Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm** — $\frac{x}{m} = \frac{Q^{0} b C_{e}}{1 + b C_{e}}$ (theoretical, monolayer, homogeneous surfaces).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Following the KTU 2024 Scheme pattern, students must answer ONE full question (a + b sub-parts, 7 + 7 marks).

### **Option A: Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 2]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a **Pressure Adsorption Sand Filter** used in municipal water treatment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A water treatment plant uses an Activated Carbon Adsorption unit to remove a phenolic compound. The following equilibrium data were obtained at 25 °C:

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 4.0 | 8.0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x/m$ (mg/g) | 0.625 | 1.20 | 2.18 | 3.65 | 5.10 |

Determine the **Freundlich constants $k$ and 1/n**. Comment on the favourability of adsorption.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Working of Pressure Adsorption Sand Filter

**Sketch description (to be drawn on answer paper):**

```
            ┌──────────────────────┐
            │   AIR VENT + P.GAUGE │ ←── Top
            └──────────┬───────────┘
                       │
       ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
       │   ANTHRACITE / GAC LAYER     │  ← 0.6 – 1.2 m
       ├───────────────────────────────┤
       │   FINE SAND LAYER            │  ← 0.3 – 0.5 m
       ├───────────────────────────────┤
       │   GRADED GRAVEL / SUPPORT    │  ← 0.2 – 0.4 m
       └───────┬───────────────┬───────┘
               │               │
      BACKWASH INLET      EFFLUENT OUTLET
      + UNDERDRAIN         (via nozzle plate)
```

**Working Principle:**

1. **Influent Entry** — Pre-treated water (after coagulation-flocculation-sedimentation and primary filtration) enters the pressure vessel at the top under pressure (**2 – 5 bar**), distributed uniformly by a header or diffuser.

2. **Downward Flow Through Media**:
   - **Anthracite / GAC layer (top)** — Removes dissolved organics, colour, odour, residual chlorine by adsorption. The high surface area (500–1500 m²/g) provides enormous adsorption capacity.
   - **Fine sand layer (middle)** — Performs mechanical straining of fine suspended particles not removed in primary filtration.
   - **Graded gravel (bottom)** — Supports the finer media above and ensures uniform distribution of backwash water and filtered effluent.

3. **Effluent Collection** — Clarified water passes through the underdrain (nozzle / porous plate) at the bottom and exits via the outlet.

4. **Backwash Cycle** — When head loss reaches the terminal value (typically **5 – 7 m** for pressure filters), the cycle is terminated. Backwash water is introduced from the bottom in reverse flow, with air scour simultaneously, fluidising the media to release trapped contaminants, which overflow through the wash-water trough at the top.

5. **Operational Advantages of Pressure ASF**:
   - No need for re-pumping of treated water (operates on line pressure).
   - Compact, enclosed (no aesthetic / algae problems).
   - Faster filtration rates possible (**10 – 25 m/h**).

---

#### Part (b) — Freundlich Constants from Given Data

**Step 1 — Convert data to log form:**

| $\log C_e$ | $\log(x/m)$ |
|---|---|
| $\log(0.5) = -0.301$ | $\log(0.625) = -0.204$ |
| $\log(1.0) = 0.000$ | $\log(1.20) = 0.079$ |
| $\log(2.0) = 0.301$ | $\log(2.18) = 0.338$ |
| $\log(4.0) = 0.602$ | $\log(3.65) = 0.562$ |
| $\log(8.0) = 0.903$ | $\log(5.10) = 0.708$ |

**Step 2 — Use the linear form:**

$$\log\left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \log(k) + \frac{1}{n} \cdot \log(C_e)$$

**Step 3 — Apply least-squares fit (or two-point method). Using endpoints:**

- Point 1: $(X_1, Y_1) = (-0.301, -0.204)$
- Point 5: $(X_5, Y_5) = (0.903, 0.708)$

**Slope $\frac{1}{n}$:**

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{Y_5 - Y_1}{X_5 - X_1} = \frac{0.708 - (-0.204)}{0.903 - (-0.301)} = \frac{0.912}{1.204}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} \approx 0.757$$

So $n \approx 1.32$.

**Intercept $\log(k)$:**

$$\log(k) = Y_1 - \frac{1}{n} X_1 = -0.204 - (0.757)(-0.301) = -0.204 + 0.228 = 0.024$$

$$k = 10^{0.024} \approx 1.057 \text{ L/g}$$

**Step 4 — Favourability comment:**

Since $\frac{1}{n} = 0.757$ lies in the range **0.1 to 1.0**, and $n = 1.32 > 1$, the adsorption is **favourable** (the system readily removes the phenolic compound at all tested concentrations). The closer $1/n$ is to 0, the more strongly heterogeneous the surface; the closer to 1, the more uniform.

**Final Answer:**

$$k \approx 1.06 \text{ L/g}, \quad \frac{1}{n} \approx 0.757, \quad n \approx 1.32, \quad \text{Adsorption is favourable.}$$

---

### **Option B: Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

#### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 2]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** with its derivation. List its assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A textile effluent with an initial dye concentration of **$C_0 = 50$ mg/L** is treated in a batch Adsorption Sand Filter experiment. The Langmuir constants are $Q^0 = 200$ mg/g and $b = 0.05$ L/mg. Calculate:
- (i) The **maximum monolayer adsorption capacity** and comment on its significance.
- (ii) The **separation factor $R_L$** and the type of isotherm (favourable / unfavourable / linear / irreversible).

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Langmuir Isotherm, Derivation and Assumptions

**Definition:**
The Langmuir isotherm describes the equilibrium adsorption on **homogeneous surfaces** with **monolayer coverage**. It was proposed by **Irving Langmuir in 1918** (Nobel Prize in Chemistry, 1932).

**Assumptions:**
1. The adsorbent surface has a **finite number of identical adsorption sites**.
2. Each site can hold **only one adsorbate molecule** → **monolayer**.
3. The energy of adsorption is **constant** for all sites (no variation with coverage).
4. There is **no interaction** between adjacent adsorbed molecules.
5. Adsorption is **reversible** (dynamic equilibrium between adsorption and desorption).

**Derivation** (as shown in Section 3.2 above):

Let $\theta$ = fractional surface coverage.
- Rate of adsorption: $R_{ads} = k_a C_e (1 - \theta)$
- Rate of desorption: $R_{des} = k_d \theta$

At equilibrium, $R_{ads} = R_{des}$:

$$k_a C_e (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta \implies \theta = \frac{(k_a / k_d) C_e}{1 + (k_a / k_d) C_e} = \frac{b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$$

Since $\frac{x}{m} = Q^0 \cdot \theta$:

$$\frac{x}{m} = \frac{Q^0 b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$$

**Linearised form:**

$$\frac{C_e}{x/m} = \frac{1}{Q^0 b} + \frac{C_e}{Q^0}$$

A plot of $\frac{C_e}{x/m}$ vs $C_e$ gives slope $\frac{1}{Q^0}$ and intercept $\frac{1}{Q^0 b}$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $Q^0 = 200$ mg/g; $b = 0.05$ L/mg; $C_0 = 50$ mg/L.

**(i) Maximum Monolayer Adsorption Capacity:**

By definition, $Q^0$ is the maximum monolayer capacity.

$$\boxed{Q^0 = 200 \text{ mg/g}}$$

**Significance:**
- It represents the **highest amount of dye** that can be adsorbed per gram of adsorbent **under the most favourable (saturating) concentration** conditions.
- A high $Q^0$ indicates a **highly effective adsorbent** — useful for comparing different media (e.g., GAC vs wood charcoal vs fly ash).
- Used as the **principal design input** for sizing the mass of carbon needed in a full-scale ASF.

**(ii) Separation Factor $R_L$:**

The **dimensionless separation factor** (or equilibrium parameter) of Hall et al. is:

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + b C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.05 \times 50} = \frac{1}{1 + 2.5} = \frac{1}{3.5} = 0.286$$

**Interpretation Table:**

| Value of $R_L$ | Type of Isotherm |
|---|---|
| $R_L > 1$ | Unfavourable |
| $R_L = 1$ | Linear |
| $0 < R_L < 1$ | **Favourable** |
| $R_L = 0$ | Irreversible |

Since $R_L = 0.286$ lies in the range $0 < R_L < 1$, the isotherm is **FAVOURABLE** for the given dye–adsorbent system at $C_0 = 50$ mg/L.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ASF Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — KTU deducts **0.5 – 1 mark** for missing units in 7-mark numerical questions. Always write `mg/g`, `L/mg`, `kJ/mol`, etc.
> 2. **Confusing adsorption with absorption** — A direct 1-mark loss in Part A definition questions.
> 3. **Skipping the linearised form of isotherms** — Examiners expect both the **non-linear canonical form** AND the **linearised plot form**. Missing either loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Missing the underdrain / air vent / backwash trough** in the sketch — 1 mark each.
> 5. **Not commenting on the value of $1/n$ or $R_L$** — Always **interpret** the numerical result. A naked number without the word "favourable/unfavourable" loses a mark.
> 6. **Mixing up physisorption vs chemisorption** — Memorise the enthalpy ranges: **20–40 kJ/mol (physisorption)** vs **40–400 kJ/mol (chemisorption)**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Adsorption Sand Filters"**

- [x] **ASF = Sand (physical straining) + Adsorbent media (GAC/anthracite, surface adsorption)** — a hybrid device.
- [x] **Two mechanisms:** Depth filtration + Adsorption (physisorption/chemisorption).
- [x] **Physisorption** is weak (20–40 kJ/mol), reversible, multilayer, non-specific. **Chemisorption** is strong (40–400 kJ/mol), often irreversible, monolayer, highly specific.
- [x] **Freundlich isotherm** — $\frac{x}{m} = k C_e^{1/n}$; linear form: $\log(x/m) = \log k + (1/n)\log C_e$; for heterogeneous surfaces, multilayer.
- [x] **Langmuir isotherm** — $\frac{x}{m} = \frac{Q^0 b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$; linear form: $\frac{C_e}{x/m} = \frac{1}{Q^0 b} + \frac{C_e}{Q^0}$; for homogeneous surfaces, monolayer.
- [x] **Separation factor** $R_L = \frac{1}{1 + b C_0}$: $0 < R_L < 1$ → **favourable**; $R_L > 1$ → **unfavourable**; $R_L = 0$ → **irreversible**; $R_L = 1$ → **linear**.
- [x] **Freundlich favourability:** $1/n$ in range 0.1 to 1.0 → **favourable**; $1/n > 1$ → **unfavourable**.
- [x] **Filter media layers** (top to bottom): **GAC / Anthracite → Fine Sand → Coarse Sand → Graded Gravel → Underdrain**.
- [x] **Filtration rates:** Gravity ASF = 5–15 m/h; Pressure ASF = 10–25 m/h.
- [x] **Backwash rate:** 36–45 m/h; Backwash duration: 5–10 min.
- [x] **Surface area of GAC:** 500–1500 m²/g — the key reason it is the most effective adsorbent.
- [x] **Pressure ASF** operates at 2–5 bar, **enclosed**, no algae, suitable for in-line installation.
- [x] **Removal efficiency** $R = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100\%$ — must always carry the **% sign**.
- [x] **Five assumptions of Langmuir:** finite identical sites, monolayer, constant adsorption energy, no lateral interaction, dynamic equilibrium.
- [x] **Service life of GAC:** 6–24 months; regeneration by **thermal reactivation** at 800–900 °C.
- [x] **Exam mantra:** Always present **canonical form + linearised form + graphical interpretation** for isotherms. Examiners reward completeness.

---
