# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hi-Vol Particulate Concentration

**Given:** Initial filter weight $W_{i} = 4.6231$ g; Final filter weight $W_{f} = 4.6842$ g. Total air volume sampled $V = 1440$ m³ (24 hr × 1 m³/min). Temperature $T_{a} = 308$ K, Pressure $P_{a} = 745$ mmHg, Water vapour pressure $P_{w} = 28$ mmHg. Standard: $T_{std} = 298$ K, $P_{std} = 760$ mmHg.

**Step 1 — Mass of particulates collected:**
$$\Delta W = W_{f} - W_{i} = 4.6842 - 4.6231 = 0.0611 \text{ g} = 61{,}100 \text{ μg}$$

**Step 2 — Correct volume to STP:**
$$V_{STP} = V \times \dfrac{(P_{a} - P_{w})}{P_{std}} \times \dfrac{T_{std}}{T_{a}}$$

$$V_{STP} = 1440 \times \dfrac{(745 - 28)}{760} \times \dfrac{298}{308}$$

$$V_{STP} = 1440 \times \dfrac{717}{760} \times \dfrac{298}{308}$$

$$V_{STP} = 1440 \times 0.9434 \times 0.9675$$

$$V_{STP} = 1314.6 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Concentration:**
$$C = \dfrac{\Delta W}{V_{STP}} = \dfrac{61{,}100 \text{ μg}}{1314.6 \text{ m}^{3}}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 46.48 \text{ μg/m}^{3}}$$

> **Result interpretation:** This is *well below* the **CPCB 24-hr NAAQS for PM₁₀ of 100 μg/m³**, so the air is within limits *for PM₁₀*, but a true PM₁₀ test would still require cyclone pre-separation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Isokinetic Nozzle Selection

**Given:** Stack diameter $D_{s} = 1.0$ m. Stack gas velocity $V_{s} = 12$ m/s. Stack temperature $T_{s} = 423$ K; Pressure $P_{s} = 101.3$ kPa. Available nozzle diameters: 4, 6, 8, 10 mm.

**Step 1 — Apply isokinetic condition:**
$$V_{n} = V_{s} \times \left(\dfrac{D_{s}}{D_{n}}\right)^{2}$$

The required $V_{n}$ for each nozzle:
- For $D_{n} = 4$ mm: $V_{n} = 12 \times (1.0/0.004)^{2}$ → *impractically high (> 750 km/s)*
- For $D_{n} = 6$ mm: similar impracticality
- For $D_{n} = 8$ mm: $V_{n} = 12 \times (1.0/0.008)^{2} = 12 \times 15{,}625 = 187{,}500$ m/s → impractical
- For $D_{n} = 10$ mm: $V_{n} = 12 \times (100)^{2} = 120{,}000$ m/s → impractical

> **Conclusion:** With $V_{s} = 12$ m/s, the gas will flow *into* the nozzle without any pump at $V_n = V_s$ (since stack flow is forced by the induced draft fan). The pump need only overcome the resistance; $V_n$ is *self-regulating* to the isokinetic value at the duct-static pressure.

**Step 2 — Required sampling flow rate at the nozzle:**
$$Q_{n} = V_{n} \times A_{n} = V_{s} \times A_{s} = 12 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 1.0^{2}}{4} = 9.425 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Select a practical sampling pump & nozzle:** In real sampling, a much smaller subsample is taken (e.g., $Q_n \approx 0.001$ to 0.02 m³/min) using a smaller sub-nozzle. The isokinetic condition still applies:
$$V_{n}^{\text{sub}} = V_{s} \times (D_{s}/D_{n}^{\text{sub}})^{2}$$

For example, with a sub-nozzle $D_n^{sub} = 6$ mm:
$$V_{n}^{sub} = 12 \times (1000/6)^{2} = 12 \times 27{,}777.8 = 333{,}333 \text{ m/s} \text{ (still theoretical)}$$

**Realistic engineering approach:** Use the *condition* $V_n = V_s$ to size the **sampling pump flow rate** to match the *fractional area* sampled. With $D_n = 6$ mm:
$$Q_{n} = V_{s} \times A_{s} \times (D_{n}/D_{s})^{2} = 12 \times 0.785 \times (0.006/1.0)^{2}$$
$$Q_{n} = 12 \times 0.785 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-5} = 3.39 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$
$$Q_{n} \approx 20.35 \text{ L/min}$$

> **This is the working isokinetic sampling rate.** A 6 mm nozzle at 20.35 L/min matches the 12 m/s duct velocity, preserving isokinetic flow.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Concentration from Spectrophotometric Reading (West–Gaeke for SO₂)

**Given:** Calibration slope from standards: m = 0.0324 absorbance units per μg SO₂ (1 cm cell). Blank absorbance = 0.005. Sample absorbance = 0.245. Sampling rate = 1.0 L/min for 60 min. Temperature = 303 K, Pressure = 750 mmHg.

**Step 1 — Volume of air sampled (corrected to STP):**
$$V_{STP} = Q \times t \times \dfrac{T_{std}}{T_{a}} \times \dfrac{P_{a}}{P_{std}}$$
$$V_{STP} = 1.0 \times 60 \times \dfrac{298}{303} \times \dfrac{750}{760}$$
$$V_{STP} = 60 \times 0.9835 \times 0.9868 = 58.22 \text{ L} = 0.05822 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Net absorbance:**
$$A_{net} = A_{sample} - A_{blank} = 0.245 - 0.005 = 0.240$$

**Step 3 — Mass of SO₂ in absorbing solution (using Beer's law):**
$$m_{SO_2} = \dfrac{A_{net}}{\text{slope}} = \dfrac{0.240}{0.0324} = 7.407 \text{ μg}$$

**Step 4 — Concentration in ambient air:**
$$C_{SO_2} = \dfrac{m_{SO_2}}{V_{STP}} = \dfrac{7.407 \text{ μg}}{0.05822 \text{ m}^{3}}$$

$$\boxed{C_{SO_2} \approx 127.2 \text{ μg/m}^{3}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The NAAQS 24-hr limit is **80 μg/m³**, so this is a **violation** — a regulatory action trigger.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

> [!NOTE]
> **The following decision matrix is highly exam-relevant.** KTU questions often ask: *"Which method is used for X, and why?"*

| Pollutant | CPCB Reference Method | Principle | Working Range | Detection Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SPM / TSP** | Gravimetric (Hi-Vol) | Filtration + weighing | 10 – 1000 μg/m³ | 10 μg/m³ |
| **PM₁₀** | Gravimetric (Hi-Vol + cyclone) | Inertial separation + weighing | 10 – 500 μg/m³ | 10 μg/m³ |
| **PM₂.₅** | Gravimetric (WINS impactor) | Impaction + weighing | 5 – 500 μg/m³ | 5 μg/m³ |
| **SO₂** | West–Gaeke (TCM) | Colorimetric (pararosaniline) | 25 – 1000 μg/m³ | 6 μg/m³ |
| **NO₂** | Jacob–Hochheiser (modified) | Griess–Saltzman colorimetry | 5 – 1000 μg/m³ | 4 μg/m³ |
| **NOx** | Chemiluminescence | NO + O₃ reaction → hν | 0.5 – 5000 ppb | 0.5 ppb |
| **CO** | NDIR | IR absorption at 4.67 μm | 0.5 – 100 ppm | 0.5 ppm |
| **O₃** | UV Photometric | UV absorption at 254 nm | 1 – 500 ppb | 1 ppb |
| **Pb** | AAS / ICP-MS | Filter digestion → analysis | 0.01 – 50 μg/m³ | 0.01 μg/m³ |
| **BaP** | HPLC-FL | Solvent extraction + fluorescence | 0.05 – 50 ng/m³ | 0.05 ng/m³ |
| **NH₃** | Indophenol blue | Colorimetric | 2 – 200 μg/m³ | 2 μg/m³ |
| **Stack PM** | EPA Method 5 / 17 | Isokinetic filtration + weighing | Variable | 1 mg/m³ |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Practice Set (Self-Worked Examples)

### Example A — Hi-Vol Concentration
A Hi-Vol runs for 24 hours at 1.1 m³/min. Filter gains 0.0820 g. Find concentration at STP.

$$V = 1.1 \times 60 \times 24 = 1584 \text{ m}^{3}$$

Assume STP correction factor ≈ 0.95:
$$V_{STP} = 1584 \times 0.95 = 1504.8 \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$\Delta W = 0.0820 \text{ g} = 82{,}000 \text{ μg}$$

$$C = 82000 / 1504.8 = 54.49 \text{ μg/m}^{3}$$

### Example B — Isokinetic Ratio Check
$D_{s} = 0.6$ m, $D_{n} = 0.006$ m, $V_{s} = 10$ m/s, measured $V_n = 8$ m/s. Compute I.

$$I = \dfrac{V_{n}}{V_{s}} = 8 / 10 = 0.8$$

> **Result:** I = 0.8 → *out of the 0.95–1.15 EPA window* → **isokinetic violation → sample is invalid**.

### Example C — Stack Emission Rate
PM concentration in stack = 50 mg/m³, stack flow $Q_s = 25$ m³/s. Find emission rate.

$$E = C \times Q_s = 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}^{3} \times 25 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} = 1.25 \text{ kg/s}$$

> Convert: $1.25 \times 3600 = 4500$ kg/h emission of PM from that stack.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Quality Monitoring Objective] --> B{Source Identified?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Select Monitoring Site per CPCB NAMP]
    B -- No --> D[Apply Gaussian Plume Modeling for Site Selection]
    C --> E[Choose Sampling Method]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Particulate or Gaseous Pollutant?}
    F -- Particulate --> G[High Volume Air Sampler with Size-Selective Inlet]
    F -- Gaseous --> H[Impinger Train with Absorbing Reagent]
    G --> G1[Gravimetric Analysis on Filter]
    H --> H1[Colorimetric / Titrimetric Analysis on Solution]
    G1 --> I[Compute Concentration in microgram per cubic meter]
    H1 --> I
    I --> J[Apply CPCB NAAQS Comparison]
    J --> K{Above NAAQS Limit?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Trigger Corrective Action and Public Advisory]
    K -- No --> M[Continue Routine Monitoring Cycle]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — High Volume Air Sampler Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ambient Air Inlet with Shelter] --> B[Glass Fibre Filter Holder 8x10 inch]
    B --> C[Brush Type Blower Motor 1 to 1.7 cubic meter per minute]
    C --> D[Rotameter with Critical Orifice for Flow Control]
    D --> E[Digital Timer and Volumetric Gas Meter]
    E --> F[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    B -.-> G[Pre weighed Filter Wi grams]
    B -.-> H[Post Sampling Filter Wf grams]
    H --> I[Analytical Balance 0.1 mg sensitivity]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Mass Difference = Wf minus Wi]
    J --> K[STP Corrected Volume Calculation]
    K --> L[Final SPM Concentration in microgram per cubic meter]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Stack Sampling Train (EPA Method 5)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stack Gas with Particulates] --> B[Nozzle with Isokinetic Tip Diameter Dn]
    B --> C[Heated Probe to Prevent Condensation]
    C --> D[Primary Filter Holder with Glass Fibre Filter]
    D --> E[Impinger Set for Moisture Collection]
    E --> F[Impinger with Silica Gel for Final Drying]
    F --> G[Dry Gas Meter for Volume Measurement]
    G --> H[Vacuum Pump for Sample Pull]
    H --> I[Exhaust]
    B -.-> J[Velocity Probe S-type Pitot]
    J -.-> K[Stack Velocity Vs in meter per second]
    K -.-> L[Isokinetic Check: Vn equals Vs times Ds over Dn squared]
    D --> M[Filter Drying and Weighing in Lab]
    E --> N[Moisture Mass Determination]
    M --> O[PM Mass Emission Rate Calculation]
    N --> O
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is isokinetic sampling? Why is it important in stack monitoring?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Isokinetic sampling is the technique of drawing a representative gas sample from a duct/stack such that the **velocity at the inlet of the sampling nozzle equals the velocity of the undisturbed gas stream** in the duct (Reference: IS 11255 / EPA Method 5).
>
> **Importance:** Particulate sampling is highly sensitive to the relative motion between the gas stream and the sampler. If the nozzle velocity deviates from the stream velocity, larger particles get **deflected** by inertia and are either over-sampled or under-sampled. Isokinetic sampling ensures **streamlines enter the nozzle undistorted**, yielding a truly representative sample. The acceptable isokinetic ratio window is **0.95 to 1.15**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the components of a Hi-Volume Air Sampler and state the typical flow rate.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Components of HVAS:
> 1. **Inlet shelter with size-selective cap** – to exclude large debris/insects.
> 2. **Glass-fibre filter holder** – 8″ × 10″ filter.
> 3. **Brush-type blower motor** – high-flow suction.
> 4. **Rotameter with critical orifice** – flow regulation.
> 5. **Gas meter / Volume counter** – cumulative volume.
> 6. **Digital timer** – 24-hr cycle.
>
> **Typical flow rate:** **1.0 to 1.7 m³/min** (standard CPCB rate: 1.13 m³/min).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **omit units** in final answers (e.g., writing concentration without μg/m³). **Always carry units** through every step.
> - In isokinetic questions, students write the formula but **fail to justify** the velocity ratio range.
> - In West–Gaeke / Jacob–Hochheiser questions, students forget to mention **wavelength** of measurement, which costs 1 mark.
> - STP correction is **NOT** applied in many numerical solutions — this is a **direct loss of 1–2 marks**.
> - For Part B, **neat diagrams** are mandatory. Sketches without labels receive partial credit only.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a)** Explain the principle, construction, and operation of a **High Volume Air Sampler (Hi-Vol)** for measurement of Total Suspended Particulates (TSP) with a neat labelled diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **West–Gaeke (TCM) method** for the determination of SO₂ in ambient air. State the chemistry, reagent, wavelength, and the formula for concentration. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Hi-Vol Sampler (7 marks)

**Principle (1 mark):**
Ambient air is drawn at a known, controlled high flow rate through a pre-weighed glass-fibre filter. Particulates are trapped on the filter; the mass gained by the filter (after conditioning) divided by the STP-corrected volume of air sampled gives the TSP/SPM concentration.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Aluminium or MS shelter with **omnidirectional inlet** (with rain guard).
- Filter holder accommodating an **8″ × 10″ glass-fibre filter** (Type A/E, Gelman).
- **Blower motor** with carbon brushes; flow capacity 1.0–1.7 m³/min.
- **Rotameter + critical orifice** (typically 2-hole orifice) for flow control.
- **Volumetric gas meter** (wet- or dry-type).
- **Elapsed time indicator / digital timer**.

**Operation (2 marks):**
1. Condition the filter at 20–25 °C, 40–50 % RH for 24 hr, weigh to constant mass $W_{i}$ (to 0.1 mg).
2. Mount the filter; run the sampler for **24 hours** continuously.
3. Record the **elapsed time**, **average flow rate**, and **gas meter reading**.
4. Condition the loaded filter similarly for 24 hr and weigh to get $W_{f}$.
5. Compute TSP via:
$$C_{TSP} = \dfrac{(W_{f} - W_{i}) \times 10^{6}}{V_{STP}} \text{ in μg/m}^{3}$$

**Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.2 (HVAS block diagram)** of these notes for the labelled schematic. (In the exam, draw a labelled cross-section: shelter → filter → blower → rotameter → gas meter → exhaust, with arrows indicating flow direction.)

---

#### Model Solution (b) — West–Gaeke Method (7 marks)

**Principle (1 mark):**
SO₂ from ambient air is absorbed in a dilute solution of **sodium tetrachloromercurate (TCM)** to form a stable dichlorosulfitomercurate complex. This is reacted with **p-rosaniline** and **formaldehyde** to develop a **red-violet dye**, whose absorbance is measured spectrophotometrically.

**Reagents (1.5 marks):**
- **Absorbing reagent:** 0.04 M TCM, with 0.06 % EDTA and 0.002 % DTPA (prevents oxidation and metal interference).
- **Formaldehyde** (0.2 % aqueous).
- **p-Rosaniline** solution (0.04 % in HCl).
- **Standard SO₂ solution**: sodium metabisulfite (Na₂S₂O₅).

**Chemistry (1.5 marks):**
1. **Absorption:**
$$\text{SO}_2 + [\text{HgCl}_4]^{2-} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow [\text{HgCl}_2(\text{SO}_3)]^{2-} + 2\text{HCl}$$
2. **Colour development (Bleached p-Rosaniline method):**
$$[\text{HgCl}_2(\text{SO}_3)]^{2-} + \text{HCHO} + \text{Pararosaniline} \rightarrow \text{Red-Violet Dye}$$

**Measurement (1.5 marks):**
- Spectrophotometer at **λ = 560 nm** (some texts: 548 nm) with 10 mm path length.
- Read absorbance; subtract blank.
- Plot calibration curve (Absorbance vs. μg SO₂).

**Concentration formula (1.5 marks):**
$$C_{SO_2} = \dfrac{(A - A_{0}) \times V_{f}}{V_{s} \times V_{STP} \times m}$$

where:
- $A$ = sample absorbance, $A_{0}$ = blank absorbance
- $V_{f}$ = final volume of absorbing solution (mL)
- $V_{s}$ = aliquot taken for colour development (mL)
- $V_{STP}$ = STP-corrected volume of air sampled (m³)
- $m$ = slope of calibration curve (absorbance per μg SO₂)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Define **isokinetic sampling**. Derive the **isokinetic condition** and explain what happens to sample representativeness when the isokinetic ratio is **less than 0.95** or **greater than 1.15**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **Modified Jacob–Hochheiser method** for the estimation of **NO₂** in ambient air. Give the relevant reactions and wavelength of measurement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Isokinetic Sampling (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Isokinetic sampling is the process of drawing a gas sample from a flowing stream such that the **velocity of the gas entering the sampling nozzle is exactly equal to the velocity of the undisturbed gas stream** in the duct.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
By continuity of mass flow:
$$V_{n} A_{n} = V_{s} A_{s}$$

For circular cross-sections, $A = \pi D^{2}/4$:
$$V_{n} \dfrac{\pi D_{n}^{2}}{4} = V_{s} \dfrac{\pi D_{s}^{2}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{V_{n} = V_{s} \times \left(\dfrac{D_{s}}{D_{n}}\right)^{2}}$$

Isokinetic ratio:
$$I = \dfrac{V_{n}}{V_{s}} = \left(\dfrac{D_{s}}{D_{n}}\right)^{2}$$

**Effect of Deviation (3 marks):**

| Condition | $V_n$ vs $V_s$ | Effect on Sampling |
|---|---|---|
| $I < 0.95$ | $V_n < V_s$ | Sample streamlines diverge; **large particles (inertia)** fail to enter the nozzle and continue downstream. Result: **under-estimation of coarse PM**. |
| $I > 1.15$ | $V_n > V_s$ | Sample streamlines converge; large particles are **sucked in disproportionately** from the surrounding flow. Result: **over-estimation of coarse PM**. |
| $0.95 \le I \le 1.15$ | Within tolerance | Sample is **representative** and acceptable (US EPA Method 5). |
| **Particles < 3 μm** | Stokes number very small | **Insensitive to isokinetic error** — follow streamlines faithfully. |

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Modified Jacob–Hochheiser Method for NO₂ (7 marks)

**Principle (1 mark):**
NO₂ from ambient air is absorbed in a solution of **sodium hydroxide + sodium arsenite**. The resulting nitrite ion is reacted with **sulfanilic acid** and **N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine (NEDA)** to form a **pink azo dye**, measured spectrophotometrically. (Sodium arsenite suppresses oxidation of nitrite to nitrate.)

**Apparatus (1 mark):**
Refer to the impinger train description in **Section 2.3** of these notes. Two impingers in series, each with 25 mL of absorbing reagent.

**Chemistry (2 marks):**
1. **Absorption:**
$$2 \text{NO}_2 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaNO}_2 + \text{NaNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
2. **Diazotization:**
$$\text{NaNO}_2 + \text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{NH}_2)(\text{SO}_3\text{H}) \rightarrow \text{Diazonium Salt}$$
3. **Coupling:**
$$\text{Diazonium Salt} + \text{NEDA} \rightarrow \text{Pink Azo Dye}$$

**Measurement (1.5 marks):**
- Wait **10–15 minutes** for full colour development.
- Measure absorbance at **λ = 540 nm** (some texts: 550 nm) in 10 mm cell.
- Subtract blank absorbance.
- Read concentration from calibration graph (Absorbance vs. μg NO₂).

**Distinguishing NO from NO₂ (1.5 marks):**
- The NaOH-arsenite reagent does **NOT** absorb NO.
- To estimate **total NOx**: oxidize NO to NO₂ using a **CrO₃ oxidation tube** (at room temperature), then absorb as NO₂.
- **NO₂ alone** = Absorbance from impinger without CrO₃ tube.
- **NOx** = Absorbance from impinger with CrO₃ tube.
- **NO** = NOx − NO₂.

**Flow diagram (refer to Section 2.3):**
Ambient air → Probe filter (particulates) → Impinger 1 (NaOH + NaAsO₂) → Impinger 2 (same, backup) → Drying tube (silica gel) → Gas meter → Pump → Exhaust.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Sampling and Analysis of Air Pollutants**

### **1. Sampling Fundamentals**
- Sampling is governed by **mass continuity** (for PM) and **chemical reactivity** (for gases).
- Three governing principles: **Isokinetic** (velocity match), **Isoaxial** (alignment with flow), **Isothermal** (no temperature change).

### **2. Particulate Sampling**
- **Hi-Vol Sampler (HVAS):** 1.0–1.7 m³/min, 24 hr, glass-fibre filter, gravimetric.
- **PM₁₀:** Cyclone inlet attached to Hi-Vol (50 % cut at 10 μm).
- **PM₂.₅:** WINS impactor or Sharp-cut cyclone.
- Formula: $C = \Delta W / V_{STP}$ (units: **μg/m³**).

### **3. Stack Sampling (EPA Method 5 / 17)**
- **Isokinetic condition:** $V_n = V_s \times (D_s / D_n)^2$.
- **Acceptable range:** $0.95 \le I \le 1.15$.
- Use **S-type pitot tube** for velocity measurement.
- Pitot formula: $V_s = K_p \times \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_g}$.

### **4. Gaseous Sampling — Wet Chemistry**
- **SO₂ — West–Gaeke (TCM):** Absorbent = sodium tetrachloromercurate; dye = p-rosaniline + HCHO; λ = **560 nm**; LOD ~ 6 μg/m³.
- **NO₂ — Modified Jacob–Hochheiser:** Absorbent = NaOH + NaAsO₂; dye = Sulfanilic acid + NEDA (Griess); λ = **540 nm**; LOD ~ 4 μg/m³.
- **NOx — Chemiluminescence:** NO + O₃ → NO₂* + hν (600–875 nm); LOD ~ 0.5 ppb.

### **5. Continuous Analyzers**
- **CO — NDIR:** IR absorption at **4.67 μm**, Beer–Lambert law.
- **O₃ — UV Photometric:** Absorption at **254 nm**.
- **Hydrocarbons — FID:** Flame ionization detection (CₙHₘ + O₂ → ions → current).
- **SO₂/NO₂ (online) — UV Fluorescence / Chemiluminescence.**

### **6. STP Correction — *Always Apply in Numerical Solutions***
$$V_{STP} = V \times \dfrac{(P_{a} - P_{w})}{P_{std}} \times \dfrac{T_{std}}{T_{a}}$$

### **7. Key Indian Standards (Must Quote in Answers)**
- **IS 5182 (Part II):** SO₂ (West–Gaeke).
- **IS 5182 (Part VI):** NOx (Griess–Saltzman).
- **IS 5182 (Part X):** CO (NDIR).
- **IS 5182 (Part XIV):** Hi-Vol for SPM.
- **IS 5182 (Part XXIII):** PM₂.₅.
- **IS 11255:** Stack emissions (Part 1–22).

### **8. CPCB NAAQS — 24-hr Averages (Memorize)**
- **PM₁₀:** 100 μg/m³ ; **PM₂.₅:** 60 μg/m³
- **SO₂:** 80 μg/m³ ; **NO₂:** 80 μg/m³
- **O₃:** 100 μg/m³ (8-hr) ; **CO:** 4 mg/m³ (8-hr)
- **Pb:** 1.0 μg/m³ ; **NH₃:** 400 μg/m³ ; **BaP:** 1 ng/m³

### **9. Common KTU Question Triggers**
- *"Why is the filter conditioned for 24 hours before weighing?"* → to bring to **equilibrium RH** and avoid weight fluctuations.
- *"Why is the filter conditioned in a desiccator?"* → to prevent moisture absorption affecting gravimetric accuracy.
- *"Why is the isokinetic ratio not exactly 1.0 in practice?"* → practical pump limitations; acceptable range is 0.95–1.15.
- *"What is the role of EDTA/DTPA in TCM?"* → **masks interfering metal ions** (e.g., Fe, Mn) that could oxidize sulfite.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always pair every method with: **(1) principle, (2) reagent, (3) wavelength, (4) detection limit, (5) governing IS standard.** This 5-point structure is what KTU examiners reward.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Air Pollution - Global Warming and Ozone Depletion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT404 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effects of Air Pollution - Global Warming and Ozone Depletion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Global Warming — Formal Academic Definition

**Global Warming** is the long-term, sustained increase in the Earth's average surface temperature caused primarily by the accumulation of anthropogenic **greenhouse gases (GHGs)** in the atmosphere. These gases absorb and re-emit infrared (long-wave) terrestrial radiation, thereby enhancing the natural greenhouse effect and disrupting the planet's radiative energy balance.

Mathematically, the concept is governed by the **Radiative Forcing Equation**, which the IPCC formally defines as:

$$\Delta F = \frac{\Delta C \cdot \alpha}{\left[1 + \left(\frac{C - C_0}{C_{1/2}}\right)\right]}$$

where:
- $\Delta F$ = Change in radiative forcing (W/m²)
- $\Delta C$ = Change in GHG concentration from pre-industrial era
- $\alpha$ = Radiative efficiency constant (W/m²/ppb)
- $C$ = Current concentration
- $C_0$ = Pre-industrial baseline concentration
- $C_{1/2}$ = Half-saturation concentration

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly differentiate between the *natural greenhouse effect* (life-sustaining) and the *enhanced greenhouse effect* (anthropogenic, harmful). Board questions frequently test this conceptual distinction.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Blanket Around the Earth"

Imagine the Earth as a person sleeping under a thin cotton blanket on a winter night. The blanket traps body heat and keeps the person warm — this is the **natural greenhouse effect**, which makes Earth habitable at **15 °C average temperature** instead of a frigid **-18 °C**.

Now imagine layering **five extra woollen blankets** over the cotton one. The person begins to overheat, sweat, and become uncomfortable. This is precisely what **Global Warming** represents — an unnatural, thick atmospheric "blanket" of GHGs trapping far more heat than the planet's climate system can dissipate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The problem is NOT the blanket itself (greenhouse effect is natural and necessary), but the **excess thickness** added by human emissions. This is the single most important conceptual distinction for KTU board exams.

## 1.3 Ozone Layer Depletion — Formal Definition

**Ozone Depletion** refers to the thinning of the stratospheric ozone layer (located 15–35 km above Earth's surface), primarily caused by the catalytic destruction of ozone ($O_3$) molecules by **reactive halogen radicals** such as chlorine ($Cl^\bullet$) and bromine ($Br^\bullet$), released from anthropogenic **Ozone-Depleting Substances (ODS)** like chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), halons, and carbon tetrachloride.

The phenomenon is most pronounced over **Antarctica**, where the formation of the *Antarctic Ozone Hole* during the Southern Hemisphere spring (September–October) is a globally recognized environmental crisis.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sun-Screen Wearing Off"

Think of the ozone layer as a **natural SPF-30 sunscreen** protecting every living organism on Earth from harmful **Ultraviolet-B (UV-B, 280–315 nm)** radiation. Under normal conditions, this sunscreen is replenished naturally through the Chapman Cycle.

Now, imagine spraying a strong industrial solvent on that sunscreen every day for decades. The protective film becomes patchy, develops holes, and eventually offers almost no protection. This is exactly what CFCs have done to the stratospheric ozone layer — a phenomenon the 1987 **Montreal Protocol** was designed to combat.

## 1.5 Critical Standard Metrics (Bolded for Exam Recall)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory High-Yield Values for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Pre-industrial CO₂ concentration:** 280 ppm
> - **Current CO₂ concentration (2024):** ~422 ppm
> - **Global Warming Potential (GWP) of CO₂:** 1 (reference gas)
> - **GWP of CH₄:** 28–36 (over 100 years)
> - **GWP of N₂O:** 265–298
> - **GWP of CFC-12:** 10,900
> - **GWP of SF₆:** 23,500
> - **Stratospheric ozone concentration:** ~300 Dobson Units (DU)
> - **Antarctic Ozone Hole minimum:** < 100 DU
> - **Mean Earth surface temperature increase since 1880:** ~1.1 °C

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Major Greenhouse Gases (GHGs)

The KTU syllabus mandates a detailed understanding of each major GHG. The table below provides the engineering-grade analysis:

| Gas | Pre-industrial Level | Current Level | Lifetime (Years) | GWP (100-yr) | Major Anthropogenic Sources |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Dioxide ($CO_2$) | 280 ppm | 422 ppm | Variable (50–200) | **1** | Fossil fuel combustion, cement, deforestation |
| Methane ($CH_4$) | 700 ppb | 1900 ppb | **12** | **28–36** | Livestock, rice paddies, landfills, natural gas leaks |
| Nitrous Oxide ($N_2O$) | 270 ppb | 333 ppb | **114** | **265–298** | Fertilizers, industrial processes, biomass burning |
| Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) | 0 | Rising | 1–270 | 12 – 14,800 | Refrigerants, air conditioners (CFC replacements) |
| Perfluorocarbons (PFCs) | 0 | Rising | 2,600 – 50,000 | 7,390 – 12,200 | Aluminum production, electronics manufacturing |
| Sulphur Hexafluoride ($SF_6$) | 0 | Rising | **3,200** | **23,500** | Electrical insulation, magnesium smelting |

## 2.2 The Six-Step Logical Mechanism of Global Warming

The KTU board frequently asks for the "process" behind global warming. The step-by-step mechanism is:

1. **Solar Shortwave Incoming Radiation:** The Sun emits shortwave radiation (wavelength 0.2 – 4 µm), primarily in the visible and UV spectrum. Approximately 30% is reflected back by clouds, aerosols, and the Earth's surface (albedo effect).

2. **Terrestrial Surface Absorption:** Of the incoming 70%, Earth's surface (land and oceans) absorbs the majority, warming up and re-emitting energy as **longwave infrared radiation** (wavelength 4 – 100 µm).

3. **Atmospheric Transmission:** Longwave IR radiation rises through the atmosphere. Some passes through unimpeded, but GHGs possess vibrational modes that absorb specific IR wavelengths.

4. **Selective IR Absorption by GHGs:** Molecules with **3 or more atoms** (e.g., $CO_2$, $CH_4$, $H_2O$, $N_2O$) absorb IR photons and enter excited vibrational states. Diatomic gases ($N_2$, $O_2$) cannot absorb IR and are transparent to it.

5. **Isotropic Re-emission:** Excited GHG molecules re-emit IR radiation in **all directions** (4π steradians). A portion returns to the Earth's surface as **counter-radiation**, providing the warming effect.

6. **Radiative Equilibrium Disruption:** When anthropogenic emissions exceed the natural sink capacity (oceans, forests, soil), the net absorbed energy increases, causing the planet to warm until a new equilibrium is established.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The global climate system follows a **negative feedback** mechanism (Stefan-Boltzmann law: $F = \sigma T^4$). For every 1 °C temperature rise, outgoing longwave radiation increases by ~3.3 W/m² (climate sensitivity parameter). However, positive feedbacks (water vapor, ice-albedo) amplify the warming.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation Number | Formula | Description | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2.1** | $F = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (outgoing IR flux) | Calculating Earth's radiative equilibrium |
| **2.2** | $\lambda = \dfrac{\Delta T_s}{\Delta F}$ | Climate Sensitivity Parameter (K/(W/m²)) | Predicting temperature change from forcing |
| **2.3** | $E = h \cdot c / \lambda$ | Photon Energy (Planck-Einstein) | UV radiation damage to biomolecules |
| **2.4** | $GWP_x = \dfrac{\int_0^{TH} a_x \cdot [x(t)] dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot [CO_2(t)] dt}$ | Global Warming Potential (IPCC definition) | Comparing GHG impacts for policy |
| **2.5** | $O_3 + Cl^\bullet \rightarrow ClO^\bullet + O_2$ | Molina CFC Destruction Step 1 | Stratospheric ozone chemistry |
| **2.6** | $ClO^\bullet + O \rightarrow Cl^\bullet + O_2$ | Molina CFC Destruction Step 2 (Net zero Cl loss) | Catalytic chain propagation |
| **2.7** | $D = \dfrac{\int_{z_1}^{z_2} n_{O_3}(z) dz}{2.69 \times 10^{16}}$ | Dobson Unit Definition (column ozone) | Measuring stratospheric ozone thickness |
| **2.8** | $\Delta T = k \cdot \Delta[CO_2]$ | Empirical Climate Sensitivity (k ≈ 0.0085 °C/ppm) | First-order temperature projections |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Formula 2.4:** The absolute value brackets $\vert x \vert$ have been deliberately avoided inside the table. Use $\mid x \mid$ notation in handwritten answer sheets to prevent markdown parsing errors. The integration horizon $TH$ is typically 20, 100, or 500 years depending on the assessment scope.

## 2.4 The Three-Step Chapman Cycle of Natural Ozone Formation

To fully grasp ozone **depletion**, students must first understand ozone **formation**. The Chapman Cycle (1930) describes the steady-state natural production of stratospheric ozone:

**Step 1 — Photodissociation of Molecular Oxygen:**
$$O_2 + h\nu \quad (\lambda < 242 \text{ nm}) \rightarrow 2O^\bullet$$

**Step 2 — Ozone Formation (Three-Body Collision):**
$$O^\bullet + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$
where M is a third-body collision partner (typically $N_2$ or $O_2$) that absorbs excess vibrational energy.

**Step 3 — Ozone Photolysis (UV Shielding):**
$$O_3 + h\nu \quad (240 < \lambda < 320 \text{ nm}) \rightarrow O_2 + O^\bullet$$

**Net Steady-State Cycle:** The Chapman Cycle produces a natural ozone layer with a peak concentration of ~8 ppm at ~25 km altitude. This is the natural equilibrium that CFCs disrupt.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The phenomena covered in this topic have profound applications in:

- **Carbon Trading Markets:** Article 6 of the Paris Agreement uses GWP-weighted $CO_2$-equivalents ($CO_2e$) for emissions accounting.
- **Refrigerant Design:** Engineers use GWP and Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) to select refrigerants (e.g., transition from R-12 to R-134a to HFOs like R-1234yf).
- **Building Energy Codes:** Radiative forcing principles inform green building standards (IGBC, LEED, GRIHA).
- **Satellite Remote Sensing:** TOMS, OMI, and Sentinel-5P instruments use backscatter UV spectroscopy to measure column ozone in Dobson Units.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Temperature of the Earth with Greenhouse Effect

The KTU 2024 scheme frequently requires deriving the effective surface temperature with and without GHGs. We perform the complete derivation here.

### Step 1 — Energy Balance Without Atmosphere (Bare Rock Model)

The Earth intercepts solar flux over its circular cross-sectional area ($\pi R_E^2$) but radiates from its entire spherical surface ($4\pi R_E^2$). Setting **incoming = outgoing**:

$$\text{Solar Absorbed} = \text{Thermal Emitted}$$

$$(1 - \alpha) \cdot S_0 \cdot \pi R_E^2 = \sigma T_e^4 \cdot 4\pi R_E^2$$

**Textual Logic:** The fraction $(1 - \alpha)$ accounts for planetary albedo (Earth's albedo $\alpha \approx 0.30$). The factor $\pi R_E^2$ vs $4\pi R_E^2$ converts intercepted flux to emitted flux over the entire sphere.

**Algebraic Simplification:** Cancel $\pi R_E^2$ from both sides:

$$(1 - \alpha) \cdot S_0 = 4 \sigma T_e^4$$

Solving for equilibrium temperature $T_e$:

$$T_e = \left[ \dfrac{(1 - \alpha) \cdot S_0}{4\sigma} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Evaluation**

Substitute the standard values:
- $S_0 = 1361$ W/m² (Solar Constant at top of atmosphere)
- $\alpha = 0.30$ (Earth's Bond albedo)
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) (Stefan-Boltzmann constant)

$$T_e = \left[ \dfrac{(1 - 0.30) \times 1361}{4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \right]^{1/4}$$

$$T_e = \left[ \dfrac{0.70 \times 1361}{2.268 \times 10^{-7}} \right]^{1/4}$$

$$T_e = \left[ \dfrac{952.7}{2.268 \times 10^{-7}} \right]^{1/4}$$

$$T_e = \left[ 4.201 \times 10^{9} \right]^{1/4}$$

$$T_e \approx 254.6 \text{ K} \quad (\approx -18.5 \text{ °C})$$

**Step 3 — Introducing the Greenhouse Effect (Single-Layer Atmosphere Model)**

In this simplified model, the atmosphere is treated as a single blackbody layer that is **fully transparent to shortwave** but **fully opaque to longwave** radiation. The atmosphere absorbs all IR emitted by the surface and re-emits IR both upward (to space) and downward (to surface, as counter-radiation).

**Energy Balance at the Top of Atmosphere (TOA):**

$$\text{Outgoing IR} = \text{Incoming Absorbed Solar}$$

$$\sigma T_a^4 = \frac{(1 - \alpha) S_0}{4}$$

This gives atmospheric temperature $T_a = T_e = 254.6$ K (same as bare rock).

**Energy Balance at the Earth's Surface:**

The surface receives:
1. Absorbed solar: $\frac{(1-\alpha)S_0}{4}$
2. Atmospheric counter-radiation: $\sigma T_a^4$

The surface emits IR: $\sigma T_s^4$

Setting surface energy balance:

$$\sigma T_s^4 = \frac{(1-\alpha)S_0}{4} + \sigma T_a^4$$

Substituting $\sigma T_a^4 = \frac{(1-\alpha)S_0}{4}$:

$$\sigma T_s^4 = \frac{(1-\alpha)S_0}{4} + \frac{(1-\alpha)S_0}{4}$$

$$\sigma T_s^4 = 2 \times \frac{(1-\alpha)S_0}{4}$$

$$T_s = 2^{1/4} \cdot T_e$$

**Step 4 — Final Numerical Result**

$$T_s = 2^{0.25} \times 254.6 \text{ K} = 1.189 \times 254.6$$

$$T_s \approx 302.7 \text{ K} \quad (\approx 29.5 \text{ °C})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** The single-layer model overestimates the actual surface temperature (~288 K or 15 °C). The discrepancy arises because real atmosphere is NOT a perfect blackbody. Real GHG emissivity $\epsilon \approx 0.78$, giving $T_s = \left(\frac{2}{2-\epsilon}\right)^{1/4} T_e = (1.55)^{1/4} \times 254.6 \approx 288.5$ K. **For KTU board exams, the simplified $2^{1/4}$ derivation is acceptable and expected.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Net Global Warming Potential (GWP) of a Gas Mixture

**Problem Statement:** A city emits annually:
- 1,000 tonnes of $CH_4$ (GWP = 28)
- 50 tonnes of $N_2O$ (GWP = 298)
- 500 tonnes of $HFC$-134a (GWP = 1,430)

Calculate the total **$CO_2$-equivalent** emissions and the percentage contribution of each gas.

**Step 1 — Convert Each Gas to $CO_2$-equivalents**

$$E_{CO_2e} = \sum_i m_i \times GWP_i$$

$$E_{CH_4} = 1{,}000 \times 28 = 28{,}000 \text{ tonnes } CO_2e$$

$$E_{N_2O} = 50 \times 298 = 14{,}900 \text{ tonnes } CO_2e$$

$$E_{HFC} = 500 \times 1{,}430 = 715{,}000 \text{ tonnes } CO_2e$$

**Step 2 — Total Equivalent Emissions**

$$E_{total} = 28{,}000 + 14{,}900 + 715{,}000 = 757{,}900 \text{ tonnes } CO_2e$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Contributions**

$$\%CH_4 = \frac{28{,}000}{757{,}900} \times 100 = 3.69\%$$

$$\%N_2O = \frac{14{,}900}{757{,}900} \times 100 = 1.97\%$$

$$\%HFC = \frac{715{,}000}{757{,}900} \times 100 = 94.34\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Despite the lowest mass emission, HFC-134a contributes **94% of the warming impact** due to its high GWP. This is why the **Kigali Amendment (2016)** to the Montreal Protocol mandates HFC phase-down, not just CFC phase-out.

## 3.3 Derivation: Ozone Column Reduction by Chlorine Catalysis

**The Molina Mechanism (1974 — Nobel Prize 1995):**

Each chlorine atom catalytically destroys up to **100,000 ozone molecules** before being deactivated. The full chain is:

**Initiation (UV Photolysis of CFC-12 in stratosphere):**
$$CF_2Cl_2 + h\nu \rightarrow CF_2Cl^\bullet + Cl^\bullet$$

**Propagation Cycle (Net Reaction: 2 O₃ → 3 O₂):**

Cycle Iteration 1:
$$Cl^\bullet + O_3 \rightarrow ClO^\bullet + O_2$$
$$ClO^\bullet + O \rightarrow Cl^\bullet + O_2$$
$$\text{Net: } O_3 + O \rightarrow 2O_2$$

Cycle Iteration 2 (with the regenerated $Cl^\bullet$):
$$Cl^\bullet + O_3 \rightarrow ClO^\bullet + O_2$$
$$ClO^\bullet + O \rightarrow Cl^\bullet + O_2$$
$$\text{Net: } O_3 + O \rightarrow 2O_2$$

**Chain Termination:** Reactions with $CH_4$, $NO_2$, or formation of $HCl$ / $ClONO_2$ reservoirs.

**Quantitative Result per Chlorine Atom:**

If a single $Cl^\bullet$ participates in **N = 100,000** catalytic cycles before termination:

$$\text{Total Ozone Destroyed} = 2N = 200{,}000 \text{ molecules of } O_3$$

For 1 mole of CFC-12 releasing 2 moles of $Cl^\bullet$:

$$\text{Ozone destroyed} = 2 \times 2 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 10^5 = 2.41 \times 10^{30} \text{ molecules per mole of CFC}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Board Error:** Students often forget the factor of **2** in the net reaction. The catalytic cycle destroys **2 ozone molecules per cycle**, not 1. Always show the net equation clearly in answer sheets.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Global Warming vs Ozone Depletion

| Parameter | Global Warming | Ozone Depletion |
|---|---|---|
| **Affected Atmospheric Layer** | Troposphere (0–12 km) | Stratosphere (15–35 km) |
| **Primary Pollutants** | $CO_2$, $CH_4$, $N_2O$, CFCs, HFCs | CFCs, Halons, $CCl_4$, HCFCs, $NO_x$ |
| **Key Metric** | GWP (Global Warming Potential) | ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential) |
| **Reference Gas** | $CO_2$ (GWP = 1) | CFC-11 (ODP = 1) |
| **Dominant Effect** | Climate change, sea-level rise | Increased UV-B at surface |
| **Spatial Pattern** | Global, but polar-amplified | Strongly polar, especially Antarctic |
| **Temporal Pattern** | Decadal to centennial | Seasonal (Spring) in polar regions |
| **Governing Treaty** | UNFCCC, Kyoto Protocol, Paris Agreement | Vienna Convention, Montreal Protocol (1987) |
| **Recovery Time** | Decades to centuries | ~50–70 years (projected) |
| **Photolytic Wavelength** | Infrared (re-emission) | UV-C (190–220 nm) |
| **Chemical Mechanism** | Vibrational IR absorption | Catalytic radical chain |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Global Warming Potential (GWP) and explain its significance in climate policy. *(Module 4 — KTU Pattern)*

**Model Answer:**

Global Warming Potential (GWP) is a dimensionless metric that compares the radiative forcing effect of a given mass of a greenhouse gas to that of an equivalent mass of $CO_2$ over a specified time horizon (typically 100 years). Mathematically:

$$GWP_x = \frac{\int_0^{TH} a_x \cdot C_x(t) \, dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot C_{CO_2}(t) \, dt}$$

where $a$ is the radiative efficiency, $C(t)$ is the time-decaying concentration, and $TH$ is the time horizon.

**Significance in Policy:** GWP enables policymakers to express emissions of all GHGs (methane, nitrous oxide, CFCs, etc.) as **$CO_2$-equivalents ($CO_2e$)**, allowing unified carbon accounting under the **UNFCCC, Kyoto Protocol, and Paris Agreement**. A gas with GWP = 28 (methane) means 1 tonne of $CH_4$ traps 28 times more heat than 1 tonne of $CO_2$ over 100 years.

---

### Question 2: What is the Dobson Unit (DU) and how is the Antarctic Ozone Hole defined? *(Module 4 — KTU Pattern)*

**Model Answer:**

The **Dobson Unit (DU)** is the standard measurement unit for total column ozone, defined as the thickness (in micrometers) of a pure ozone layer at standard temperature and pressure (STP) that would contain the same number of molecules as the actual stratospheric column. Mathematically:

$$1 \text{ DU} = 2.69 \times 10^{16} \text{ molecules of } O_3 \text{ per cm}^2$$

A typical mid-latitude ozone column is approximately **300 DU** (3 mm at STP).

The **Antarctic Ozone Hole** is defined as the region over Antarctica where total column ozone drops **below 220 DU**, as established by the Antarctic Treaty and used by NASA/NOAA for monitoring purposes. The lowest recorded value was **73 DU** in October 1999.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Discuss in detail the causes, mechanisms, effects, and control strategies of Global Warming. *(Module 4 — 14 Marks)*

**Question A Model Solution:**

**(a) Causes of Global Warming — 7 Marks**

The primary causes of global warming can be categorized as follows:

1. **Anthropogenic CO₂ Emissions (~73% of total GHG emissions):**
   - Fossil fuel combustion in power plants, vehicles, and industries
   - Cement production (calcination of $CaCO_3$)
   - Deforestation reducing carbon sink capacity

2. **Methane ($CH_4$) Emissions:**
   - Enteric fermentation in ruminants (cattle, sheep)
   - Rice paddy cultivation under flooded conditions
   - Landfills and municipal solid waste
   - Leakage from natural gas pipelines

3. **Nitrous Oxide ($N_2O$) Emissions:**
   - Synthetic nitrogen fertilizer overuse
   - Industrial chemical processes (nitric acid, adipic acid production)
   - Biomass burning

4. **Fluorinated Gases (HFCs, PFCs, $SF_6$):**
   - Refrigerant leakage
   - Semiconductor manufacturing
   - Electrical switchgear insulation

**(b) Mechanism, Effects, and Control Strategies — 7 Marks**

**Mechanism:** Solar shortwave radiation penetrates the atmosphere and is absorbed by the Earth's surface. The warmed surface re-emits longwave infrared radiation, which is selectively absorbed by triatomic and polyatomic GHG molecules. These excited molecules re-emit IR isotropically, with a significant portion returning to the surface as counter-radiation, causing net warming.

**Effects of Global Warming:**

| Sector | Specific Impact |
|---|---|
| Cryosphere | Glacier retreat, Arctic sea-ice decline (~13% per decade) |
| Hydrosphere | Sea-level rise (~3.4 mm/year), ocean acidification (pH drop of 0.1) |
| Atmosphere | Increased frequency of heatwaves, hurricanes, and extreme precipitation |
| Biosphere | Coral bleaching, species migration, biodiversity loss |
| Human Systems | Food security threats, climate refugee crises, economic damage |

**Control Strategies:**
- Transition to renewable energy (solar, wind, hydro, nuclear)
- Energy efficiency and electrification of transport (EVs)
- Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) technologies
- Afforestation and reforestation (carbon sinks)
- International cooperation under **Paris Agreement (2015)** — limit warming to 1.5 °C
- Carbon pricing, emissions trading systems (ETS)
- Lifestyle changes: plant-rich diets, reduced air travel

---

### Question B: Explain the mechanism of stratospheric ozone depletion by CFCs with chemical equations. Discuss the Montreal Protocol and its impact. *(Module 4 — 14 Marks)*

**Question B Model Solution:**

**(a) Stratospheric Ozone Depletion Mechanism — 7 Marks**

Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) are synthetic halogenated hydrocarbons (e.g., CFC-12: $CF_2Cl_2$, CFC-11: $CCl_3F$) that were widely used as refrigerants, propellants, and foam-blowing agents until the 1980s. Their ozone-depleting mechanism proceeds in distinct stages:

**Stage 1 — Tropospheric Inertness:** CFCs are chemically inert in the troposphere due to their strong C–F and C–Cl bonds. They have atmospheric lifetimes of 50–100 years and gradually diffuse into the stratosphere.

**Stage 2 — UV-C Photolysis in Stratosphere:** At altitudes of 20–40 km, high-energy UV-C radiation ($\lambda < 220$ nm) cleaves the C–Cl bond:

$$CF_2Cl_2 + h\nu \rightarrow CF_2Cl^\bullet + Cl^\bullet$$

**Stage 3 — Catalytic Destruction of Ozone (Molina Cycle):**

$$Cl^\bullet + O_3 \rightarrow ClO^\bullet + O_2$$

$$ClO^\bullet + O \rightarrow Cl^\bullet + O_2$$

**Net Reaction:** $2O_3 \rightarrow 3O_2$

The chlorine radical is regenerated, allowing a single $Cl^\bullet$ atom to destroy up to **100,000 ozone molecules** before being sequestered in reservoir species like $HCl$ or $ClONO_2$.

**Stage 4 — Polar Amplification:** In polar winter, the absence of sunlight forms **Polar Stratospheric Clouds (PSCs)**. Heterogeneous reactions on PSC surfaces convert stable chlorine reservoirs into photolytically active forms:

$$HCl + ClONO_2 \xrightarrow{PSC} Cl_2 + HNO_3$$

When sunlight returns in spring, $Cl_2$ photolyzes to release massive $Cl^\bullet$ concentrations, causing the **Antarctic Ozone Hole**.

**(b) Montreal Protocol and Global Impact — 7 Marks**

**Background:** The 1985 **Vienna Convention** for the Protection of the Ozone Layer established a framework for international cooperation. Following the discovery of the Antarctic Ozone Hole in 1985 by Farman, Gardiner, and Shanklin, the **Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer (signed 16 September 1987, entered into force 1 January 1989)** was adopted.

**Key Provisions:**
- Phase-out schedules for 96 chemicals (CFCs, halons, $CCl_4$, HCFCs, methyl bromide)
- Differentiated responsibilities for developed and developing nations
- **Multilateral Fund** providing financial assistance to developing countries
- Trade restrictions on ODS with non-parties

**Subsequent Amendments:**
- **London (1990), Copenhagen (1992), Montreal (1997), Beijing (1999), Kigali (2016)**
- Kigali Amendment specifically targets HFC phase-down (climate co-benefit)

**Impact Assessment:**
- **Universal ratification:** 198 countries (only UN treaty ratified by all members)
- **Ozone layer recovery:** Projected to return to 1980 levels by **2066 over Antarctica, 2045 over Northern Hemisphere**
- **Climate co-benefit:** Avoided ~0.5 °C of additional warming by 2100
- Recognized as the most successful international environmental agreement in history
- Paul Crutzen, Mario Molina, and F. Sherwood Rowland awarded the **1995 Nobel Prize in Chemistry** for their work on ozone depletion

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Natural and Enhanced Greenhouse Effect** — Always clarify which one your answer addresses. The natural effect keeps Earth habitable; the *enhanced* (anthropogenic) effect causes warming.
> 2. **GWP vs ODP Mix-up** — These are two completely different metrics. GWP applies to climate change; ODP applies to ozone depletion. CFCs have BOTH high GWP and high ODP.
> 3. **Forgetting the Factor of 2 in Chapman/Molina Cycles** — Every catalytic cycle destroys TWO $O_3$ molecules, not one. Examiners specifically check for the net reaction.
> 4. **Unit Errors in Dobson Units** — Always express ozone column in DU with a clear definition. Don't confuse Dobson Units with ppm mixing ratios.
> 5. **Treating Ozone Hole as a Hole in the Traditional Sense** — The "hole" is actually a region of severe thinning (not a complete absence) of the ozone layer. The total column falls below 220 DU.
> 6. **Citing Wrong Year for Montreal Protocol** — Signed **1987**, entered into force **1989**, Kigali Amendment **2016**. Mixing up these dates is a frequent 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Notes for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Greenhouse Effect:** Natural phenomenon; necessary for life. Enhanced greenhouse effect = global warming.
- **Key GHGs (in decreasing order of abundance):** $H_2O > CO_2 > CH_4 > N_2O > O_3 > CFCs$
- **CO₂ Reference:** GWP of $CO_2$ is defined as 1. All other GWP values are multiples of this.
- **GWPs to Memorize:** $CH_4$: 28; $N_2O$: 298; CFC-12: 10,900; $SF_6$: 23,500
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $F = \sigma T^4$. Earth equilibrium without atmosphere = 254.6 K (-18.5 °C). With natural greenhouse effect = 288 K (15 °C).
- **Effective Temperature Formula (Single-Layer Model):** $T_s = 2^{1/4} \cdot T_e \approx 303$ K
- **Climate Sensitivity:** ~0.8 °C per W/m² forcing (doubling $CO_2$ ≈ 3 °C warming)
- **Chapman Cycle:** $O_2 + UV \rightarrow 2O \rightarrow +O_2 \rightarrow O_3 \rightarrow +UV \rightarrow O_2 + O$ (steady-state natural balance)
- **Molina Mechanism:** CFCs → UV photolysis → $Cl^\bullet$ radical → catalytic $O_3$ destruction. Net: $2O_3 \rightarrow 3O_2$. One $Cl$ destroys up to 100,000 $O_3$ molecules.
- **Antarctic Ozone Hole:** Defined as < 220 DU; forms during Southern Hemisphere Spring (Sept–Oct); minimum ~73 DU (1999).
- **Dobson Unit (DU):** 1 DU = $2.69 \times 10^{16}$ molecules $O_3$ per cm². Normal column = ~300 DU.
- **PSCs (Polar Stratospheric Clouds):** Heterogeneous chemistry activation sites for chlorine in polar winter.
- **Montreal Protocol (1987):** Most successful international environmental treaty. Universally ratified (198 countries). Expected full ozone recovery by ~2066 (Antarctic), ~2045 (NH).
- **Kigali Amendment (2016):** HFC phase-down to avoid ~0.5 °C additional warming.
- **Distinguish:** Global Warming = tropospheric issue (GHGs); Ozone Depletion = stratospheric issue (ODS). Both involve CFCs but are different phenomena.
- **Climate Tipping Points:** Arctic sea-ice loss, Amazon dieback, permafrost thaw, Atlantic circulation slowdown, ice sheet collapse.
- **Paris Agreement Target:** Limit global warming to **1.5 °C** above pre-industrial levels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Meteorological Phenomena on Air Quality - Lapse Rates, Pressure, Wind, Moisture and Dispersion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT404 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Meteorological Phenomena on Air Quality - Lapse Rates, Pressure, Wind, Moisture and Dispersion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Meteorological Phenomena — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Meteorological Phenomena** governing air quality refer to the state and motion of the lower atmosphere (the *boundary layer*) that physically transport, dilute, entrain, or trap airborne pollutants emitted from industrial stacks, vehicular sources, and area sources. The five governing variables are: **Lapse Rate**, **Atmospheric Pressure**, **Wind**, **Moisture (Humidity)**, and **Dispersion**.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, these variables are the *driving meteorological inputs* to the Gaussian Plume Equation, the Industrial Source Complex (ISC) models, and the AERMOD framework used for predicting ground-level concentrations (GLCs) of criteria pollutants (PM, SO₂, NOₓ, CO).

## 1.2 The Intuitive Picture — A Simple Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Boiling Pot and the Lid"**
> Imagine a kitchen *kadhai* (pot) of water on a stove with a loose transparent lid. The stove (Sun) heats the bottom (ground). Steam (pollutants) rises. If the lid is loose (unstable atmosphere), steam escapes quickly and dissipates — this is good dispersion. If you press the lid down hard (temperature inversion), the steam gets trapped and spreads horizontally — this is poor dispersion and high ground-level pollution. The "lid" is the **temperature inversion layer**, the "stove heat" is the **lapse rate**, the "kitchen draft" is the **wind**, and "humidity in the air" is the **moisture content** of the atmosphere.

## 1.3 The Five Governing Variables — A Snapshot

| S.No. | Phenomenon | Primary Quantity | SI Unit | Pollutant Impact |
|-------|------------|------------------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Lapse Rate | $\Gamma = -dT/dz$ | °C/km or K/m | Governs vertical mixing (stability) |
| 2 | Pressure | $P$ | Pa (N/m²) | Drives wind, defines synoptic systems |
| 3 | Wind | $\vec{V} = (u, v, w)$ | m/s | Advects pollutants downwind |
| 4 | Moisture | RH, Mixing Ratio ($w$) | % , g/kg | Affects wet deposition, plume rise |
| 5 | Dispersion | $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ | m | Quantifies spread of plume |

## 1.4 Syllabus-Relevant Standard Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR):** $\Gamma_d = \mathbf{9.8 \ K/km}$ (often taken as **6.5 °C/km** for environmental engineering in moist air approximations)
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure at Sea Level:** $P_0 = \mathbf{101.325 \ kPa}$
> - **Standard Temperature at Sea Level:** $T_0 = \mathbf{288.15 \ K}$ (15 °C)
> - **Gas Constant for Dry Air:** $R_d = \mathbf{287.04 \ J/(kg \cdot K)}$
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.80665 \ m/s^2}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lapse Rates — The Vertical Temperature Profile

The **lapse rate** is the rate at which the ambient air temperature decreases with increasing altitude. It is the single most important parameter determining atmospheric stability.

### 2.1.1 Three Fundamental Lapse Rates

**(a) Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR) — $\Gamma_e$**
- The *actual* observed vertical temperature profile of the atmosphere at a given place and time.
- Varies with location, season, time of day, and weather conditions.
- Mathematically: $\Gamma_e = -\dfrac{dT}{dz}$

**(b) Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR) — $\Gamma_d$**

For a parcel of dry (or unsaturated) air rising adiabatically:

$$\Gamma_d = \frac{g}{c_{p,d}}$$

where $c_{p,d} = 1004 \ \text{J/(kg·K)}$ is the specific heat of dry air at constant pressure.

$$\Gamma_d = \frac{9.80665}{1004} \approx 0.00976 \ \text{K/m} = 9.76 \ \text{K/km}$$

**(c) Saturated (Moist) Adiabatic Lapse Rate (SALR) — $\Gamma_s$**
- Smaller than $\Gamma_d$ because latent heat is released during condensation.
- Varies with temperature and pressure, but typically $\Gamma_s \approx \mathbf{4}$ to **6 K/km**.

### 2.1.2 Atmospheric Stability — The Comparison Rule

| Condition | ELR vs. Lapse Rate | Stability | Plume Behavior |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------|----------------|
| $\Gamma_e > \Gamma_d$ | Steep, super-adiabatic | **Absolutely Unstable** | Looping, vigorous vertical mixing |
| $\Gamma_e = \Gamma_d$ | Adiabatic | **Neutral** | Coning, steady diffusion |
| $\Gamma_s < \Gamma_e < \Gamma_d$ | Sub-adiabatic | **Conditionally Stable** | Fanning under inversion aloft |
| $\Gamma_e < \Gamma_s$ | Inversion | **Absolutely Stable** | Trapping, fanning, fumigation |

### 2.1.3 Temperature Inversions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **temperature inversion** is the condition where temperature *increases* with altitude (i.e., $\Gamma_e < 0$). Inversions act as a "lid" that suppresses vertical dispersion, leading to severe ground-level pollution episodes (e.g., London smog of 1952, Delhi winter smog).

**Four types of inversions relevant to KTU:**

1. **Radiation Inversion** — Forms on clear, calm nights due to radiative cooling of the ground. Disappears after sunrise.
2. **Advection Inversion** — Warm air moves over a cold surface (e.g., marine layer over cool coastal land).
3. **Subsidence Inversion** — Large-scale sinking of air in a high-pressure system, with compression warming aloft.
4. **Frontal Inversion** — Warm air mass riding over a cold air mass at a weather front.

## 2.2 Atmospheric Pressure

### 2.2.1 Hydrostatic Equation

The pressure at any height $z$ in a static atmosphere is given by:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

Combined with the ideal gas law $P = \rho R_d T$, this yields the **hypsometric equation**:

$$P(z) = P_0 \left( \frac{T_0 - \Gamma_e z}{T_0} \right)^{g/(\Gamma_e R_d)}$$

### 2.2.2 Pressure Gradient Force (PGF)

The horizontal pressure gradient force per unit mass is:

$$\vec{F}_{PGF} = -\frac{1}{\rho} \nabla P$$

This is the *fundamental driver* of wind. Air flows from high pressure (anticyclone) to low pressure (cyclone).

### 2.2.3 Buys-Ballot's Law

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Northern Hemisphere**, if you stand with your back to the wind, the **low pressure is on your left** and the high pressure is on your right. This is the basis for reading synoptic weather maps and routing pollutant plumes.

## 2.3 Wind

### 2.3.1 Wind Definition

Wind is air in motion relative to the Earth's surface, driven by the pressure gradient force and modified by:
1. **Coriolis Force** (apparent, due to Earth's rotation)
2. **Frictional Force** (in the boundary layer)
3. **Centripetal/Centrifugal Force** (around curved isobars)

### 2.3.2 Geostrophic Wind

For straight, parallel isobars with no friction, the pressure gradient force balances the Coriolis force:

$$V_g = \frac{1}{\rho f} \frac{\partial P}{\partial n}$$

where $f = 2 \Omega \sin \phi$ is the Coriolis parameter, $\Omega = 7.292 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s is Earth's angular velocity, and $\phi$ is latitude.

### 2.3.3 Gradient Wind

For curved isobars (around a cyclone or anticyclone), the balance is between PGF, Coriolis, and centripetal force, giving the gradient wind equation.

### 2.3.4 Wind Rose & Pollution Rose

A **wind rose** shows the frequency of wind direction; a **pollution rose** weights directions by pollutant concentration. They are essential for siting industries and for KTU design problems.

## 2.4 Atmospheric Moisture

### 2.4.1 Key Moisture Parameters

- **Specific Humidity ($q$):** Mass of water vapor per unit mass of moist air (g/kg).
- **Mixing Ratio ($w$):** Mass of water vapor per unit mass of *dry* air.
- **Relative Humidity (RH):** $\text{RH} = \dfrac{e}{e_s(T)} \times 100\%$
- **Dew Point ($T_d$):** Temperature at which saturation occurs for a given $e$.

### 2.4.2 Clausius-Clapeyron Equation

The saturation vapor pressure as a function of temperature:

$$e_s(T) = e_{s0} \exp\left( \frac{L}{R_v} \left( \frac{1}{T_0} - \frac{1}{T} \right) \right)$$

where $L = 2.5 \times 10^6$ J/kg is the latent heat of vaporization, $R_v = 461.5$ J/(kg·K) is the gas constant for water vapor, and $e_{s0} = 6.11$ hPa at $T_0 = 273.15$ K.

## 2.5 Atmospheric Dispersion

### 2.5.1 The Gaussian Plume Model (Steady-State)

The fundamental equation taught in KTU for dispersion from a continuous elevated point source:

$$\chi(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{2 \pi u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \exp\left( -\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} \right) \left[ \exp\left( -\frac{(z - H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right) + \exp\left( -\frac{(z + H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right) \right]$$

**Key Variables:**

- $\chi$ = ground-level (or 3-D) pollutant concentration (µg/m³)
- $Q$ = emission rate (µg/s)
- $u$ = mean wind speed at stack height (m/s)
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients (functions of downwind distance $x$ and stability class)
- $H$ = effective stack height = physical height + plume rise $\Delta H$

### 2.5.2 Pasquill-Gifford Stability Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six stability classes (A–F)** are defined by surface wind speed, incoming solar radiation (day), and cloud cover (night). These classes determine $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ from standard lookup curves/equations.

| Class | Stability | Description | $\sigma_z$ Behavior |
|-------|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| A | Very Unstable | Strong insolution, low wind | Rapid growth |
| B | Unstable | Moderate insolution | Fast growth |
| C | Slightly Unstable | Weak insolution | Moderate growth |
| D | Neutral | Overcast, high wind | Linear-ish growth |
| E | Slightly Stable | Slight inversion | Slow growth |
| F | Stable | Strong inversion, low wind | Very slow, then levels off |

### 2.5.3 Effective Stack Height (Briggs Equation — most asked in KTU)

$$H = h_s + \Delta H$$

For buoyant plumes with $u < 1$ m/s (calm conditions):
$$\Delta H = 1.6 F^{1/3} x^{2/3} / u$$
For $u \geq 1$ m/s:
$$\Delta H = 1.6 F^{1/3} (3.5 x_d)^{2/3} / u$$
where buoyancy flux $F = g V_s d_s^2 (T_s - T_a) / (4 T_s)$ and $x_d$ is the downwind distance to final rise.

### 2.5.4 Plume Behavior Patterns (KTU High-Yield)

| Pattern | Condition | Picture |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Looping** | Unstable (A, B), low wind | Plume loops wildly |
| **Coning** | Neutral (D), moderate wind | Cone-shaped spread |
| **Fanning** | Stable (E, F) with aloft emission | Horizontal ribbon, no ground impact |
| **Fumigation** | Unstable surface + inversion aloft | Plume brought to ground, very high GLC |
| **Trapping** | Strong inversion aloft, low emission | Plume trapped below inversion |
| **Lofting** | Inversion below, unstable above | Plume rises, minimal ground impact |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the following table for KTU board exams. All units must accompany numerical answers.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Gamma_d = g/c_{p,d} = 9.76 \times 10^{-3}$ | Dry adiabatic lapse rate | K/m |
| 2 | $P(z) = P_0 (1 - \Gamma_e z / T_0)^{g/(\Gamma_e R_d)}$ | Hypsometric equation | Pa |
| 3 | $V_g = \dfrac{1}{\rho f} \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial n}$ | Geostrophic wind | m/s |
| 4 | $f = 2 \Omega \sin \phi$ | Coriolis parameter | rad/s |
| 5 | $\text{RH} = e / e_s \times 100\%$ | Relative humidity | % |
| 6 | $e_s(T) = 6.11 \exp\left( \dfrac{L}{R_v} \left( \dfrac{1}{273.15} - \dfrac{1}{T} \right) \right)$ | Saturation vapor pressure | hPa |
| 7 | $\chi = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp(-y^2 / 2\sigma_y^2) \left[ \exp(-(z-H)^2/2\sigma_z^2) + \exp(-(z+H)^2/2\sigma_z^2) \right]$ | Gaussian plume (point source, elevated) | µg/m³ |
| 8 | $H = h_s + \Delta H$ | Effective stack height | m |
| 9 | $\Delta H = 1.6 F^{1/3} x^{2/3} / u$ | Plume rise (Briggs) | m |
| 10 | $F = g V_s d_s^2 (T_s - T_a) / (4 T_s)$ | Buoyancy flux | m⁴/s³ |
| 11 | $\sigma_y, \sigma_z = a x^b$ (Pasquill-Gifford) | Dispersion coefficients | m |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Power plant stack design** uses $H$ to ensure $\chi_{max} < NAAQS$ at the fence line.
- **Industrial siting** uses wind roses to place sources upwind of sensitive receptors.
- **Urban air-shed management** uses stability classes to issue smog advisories (e.g., CPCB, India).
- **Emergency response** (chemical spills, Bhopal-type events) uses $u$ and stability to predict plume trajectory in real time.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)

### Statement to Prove
A parcel of dry air rising adiabatically in the atmosphere cools at the rate:
$$\Gamma_d = \frac{g}{c_p} = 9.76 \ \text{K/km}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: First Law of Thermodynamics for an adiabatic process**

For a reversible adiabatic process with no heat exchange ($dQ = 0$):
$$dQ = c_p \, dT - \frac{dP}{\rho} = 0$$

**Text logic:** The energy balance says any work done by the parcel (expansion) must come from internal energy (temperature drop).

**Step 2: Solve for dT**

$$c_p \, dT = \frac{dP}{\rho}$$

$$dT = \frac{dP}{\rho \, c_p}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Hydrostatic Equation**

From the static atmosphere: $dP = -\rho g \, dz$

**Text logic:** As the parcel rises, ambient pressure decreases with height due to the weight of overlying air.

**Step 4: Substitute into the dT expression**

$$dT = \frac{-\rho g \, dz}{\rho \, c_p} = -\frac{g}{c_p} \, dz$$

**Step 5: Define the Lapse Rate**

By definition, the lapse rate is $\Gamma = -\dfrac{dT}{dz}$:

$$\Gamma_d = -\frac{dT}{dz} = \frac{g}{c_p}$$

**Step 6: Insert Numerical Values**

$$\Gamma_d = \frac{9.80665 \ \text{m/s}^2}{1004 \ \text{J/(kg·K)}}$$

$$\Gamma_d = 9.7677 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{K/m}$$

**Step 7: Convert to K/km**

$$\Gamma_d = 9.77 \ \text{K/km} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hypsometric (Pressure-Height) Equation

### Statement
Find the pressure $P(z)$ at altitude $z$ given surface pressure $P_0$ and a constant environmental lapse rate $\Gamma_e$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Start with hydrostatic and ideal gas equations**

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g \qquad \text{and} \qquad P = \rho R_d T$$

**Step 2: Eliminate $\rho$**

From $P = \rho R_d T$, we get $\rho = P / (R_d T)$.

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\frac{P g}{R_d T}$$

**Step 4: Express Temperature as a Function of Height**

Assume $\Gamma_e$ is constant. Then:
$$T(z) = T_0 - \Gamma_e z$$

**Step 5: Separate Variables and Integrate**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R_d} \frac{dz}{T_0 - \Gamma_e z}$$

Integrating from $z=0$ (where $P=P_0$) to $z=z$:

$$\int_{P_0}^{P(z)} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R_d} \int_0^z \frac{dz}{T_0 - \Gamma_e z}$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the integrals**

$$\ln\left( \frac{P(z)}{P_0} \right) = -\frac{g}{R_d} \left[ -\frac{1}{\Gamma_e} \ln(T_0 - \Gamma_e z) + \frac{1}{\Gamma_e} \ln T_0 \right]$$

$$\ln\left( \frac{P(z)}{P_0} \right) = \frac{g}{R_d \Gamma_e} \ln\left( \frac{T_0 - \Gamma_e z}{T_0} \right)$$

**Step 7: Exponentiate to isolate $P(z)$**

$$P(z) = P_0 \left( \frac{T_0 - \Gamma_e z}{T_0} \right)^{g/(R_d \Gamma_e)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gaussian Plume Concentration (Point Source, Elevated, Reflection at Ground)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Start with the steady-state 3-D advection-diffusion equation**

$$u \frac{\partial \chi}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial z^2}$$

where $K_y, K_z$ are eddy diffusivities. Assume $u$ is constant and $K_y, K_z$ are also constant in $x$ (an approximation).

**Step 2: Apply a coordinate transformation along the mean wind**

$$X = x, \quad Y = y, \quad Z = z$$

**Step 3: Solve the diffusion equation by separation of variables or Green's function method**

The fundamental solution (point source at origin) is:
$$\chi = \frac{Q}{(2\pi)^{3/2} \sigma_x \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left( -\frac{X^2}{2\sigma_x^2} - \frac{Y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{Z^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right)$$

**Step 4: Apply the advection approximation — far field, $x \gg 0$**

For $x \gg 0$, $\sigma_x \ll x$, and the term $\exp(-X^2/2\sigma_x^2)$ collapses to a delta function in $X$. This yields the standard 2-D form:

$$\chi = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left( -\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} \right) \exp\left( -\frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right)$$

**Step 5: Add the image source to enforce ground reflection**

The ground is a perfect reflector (no deposition assumed). An image source of equal strength at $z = -H$ must be added. So $z \to (z - H)$ for the real source and $z \to (z + H)$ for the image.

**Step 6: Final Form**

$$\chi(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left( -\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} \right) \left[ \exp\left( -\frac{(z - H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right) + \exp\left( -\frac{(z + H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right) \right]$$

**Step 7: Ground-level concentration ($z = 0$, centerline $y = 0$)**

Setting $z = 0$ and $y = 0$:

$$\chi(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left( -\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Ground-Level Concentration

### Statement
Find the downwind distance $x_{max}$ at which ground-level centerline concentration is maximum.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the centerline ground-level concentration**

$$\chi_{cl}(x) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left( -\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right)$$

**Step 2: Assume Briggs-type power-law forms**

For Pasquill-Gifford stability:
$$\sigma_y = a \, x^{0.894}, \quad \sigma_z = b \, x^{0.894} \quad \text{(or more precisely, } \sigma_z = c x^d \text{)}$$

**Step 3: Take the derivative $d\chi_{cl}/dx = 0$ and solve**

The condition for the maximum gives:
$$\sigma_z(x_{max}) = \frac{H}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 H$$

**Step 4: Compute maximum concentration**

$$\chi_{max} = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y(x_{max}) \sigma_z(x_{max})} e^{-1/2} = \frac{0.117 Q}{u \sigma_y(x_{max}) H} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Gaussian Plume GLC Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A power plant emits SO₂ at $Q = 800$ g/s. Stack height $h_s = 100$ m, stack diameter $d_s = 3$ m, exit velocity $V_s = 15$ m/s, exit temperature $T_s = 420$ K. Ambient $T_a = 293$ K, wind speed at stack height $u = 5$ m/s, stability class D. Compute GLC at $x = 1$ km downwind on the centerline.

**Step 1: Compute buoyancy flux**

$$F = \frac{g V_s d_s^2 (T_s - T_a)}{4 T_s} = \frac{9.81 \times 15 \times 3^2 \times (420 - 293)}{4 \times 420}$$

$$F = \frac{9.81 \times 15 \times 9 \times 127}{1680} = \frac{168{,}164.85}{1680} \approx 100.1 \ \text{m}^4/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 2: Plume rise (Briggs, for unstable/neutral)**

$$\Delta H = \frac{1.6 F^{1/3} x_d^{2/3}}{u}$$

For neutral (D), $x_d = 3.5 \times 100 = 350$ m (typical default for $h_s$ in meters), or use a more direct formulation:
$$\Delta H \approx \frac{1.6 \times (100.1)^{1/3} \times 119^{2/3}}{5}$$

$$\Delta H \approx \frac{1.6 \times 4.64 \times 24.0}{5} = \frac{178.2}{5} \approx 35.6 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Effective stack height**

$$H = h_s + \Delta H = 100 + 35.6 = 135.6 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Dispersion coefficients (Stability D, $x = 1000$ m)**

Standard Pasquill-Gifford values (from ISC tables):
$$\sigma_y(1000) \approx 35 \ \text{m}, \quad \sigma_z(1000) \approx 21 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 5: Ground-level centerline concentration**

$$\chi = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left( -\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right)$$

$$\chi = \frac{800 \times 10^6 \ \mu\text{g/s}}{\pi \times 5 \times 35 \times 21} \exp\left( -\frac{135.6^2}{2 \times 21^2} \right)$$

The denominator: $\pi \times 5 \times 35 \times 21 \approx 11{,}545$

$$\chi_0 = \frac{800 \times 10^6}{11{,}545} \approx 69{,}268 \ \mu\text{g/m}^3$$

The exponential: $\dfrac{135.6^2}{2 \times 441} = \dfrac{18{,}387}{882} \approx 20.85$

$$\exp(-20.85) \approx 8.85 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\chi \approx 69{,}268 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-10} \approx 6.13 \times 10^{-5} \ \mu\text{g/m}^3$$

**This is unrealistically low** because the stack is very tall — illustrates that $H \gg \sigma_z$ gives strong suppression of GLC. (At closer distances, GLC rises; at the $x_{max}$ point, GLC peaks.)

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix in KTU viva or for selecting conditions in design problems.**

| Parameter | Unstable (A) | Neutral (D) | Stable (F) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|------------|
| Lapse Rate | $\Gamma_e > \Gamma_d$ | $\Gamma_e \approx \Gamma_d$ | $\Gamma_e < \Gamma_s$ (or negative) |
| Vertical Mixing | Strong | Moderate | Very weak |
| $\sigma_z$ growth | Fastest | Moderate | Slowest (asymptotic) |
| Plume Type | Looping | Coning | Fanning |
| Plume Rise | Enhanced | Standard | Reduced |
| Pollution Potential | Low (disperses up) | Moderate | High (trapped) |
| Typical Time | Sunny afternoon | Overcast / high wind | Clear night, calm wind |
