# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define *atmospheric stability*. With a neat sketch, explain the three cases of stability in terms of ELR, DALR, and SALR.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Atmospheric stability is the ability of the atmosphere to resist or enhance vertical motion of air parcels.

**Case 1 — Absolutely Unstable (1 mark):**
$\Gamma_e > \Gamma_d$ — a rising parcel always remains warmer (less dense) than its surroundings, so it accelerates upward spontaneously. Plume behavior: looping, vigorous vertical mixing.

**Case 2 — Neutral (0.5 mark):**
$\Gamma_e = \Gamma_d$ — the parcel temperature matches the environment; no restoring or driving force. Plume behavior: coning.

**Case 3 — Absolutely Stable / Inversion (1 mark):**
$\Gamma_e < \Gamma_s < \Gamma_d$ — a rising parcel becomes cooler (denser) and sinks back; vertical motion is suppressed. Plume behavior: fanning, fumigation, trapping.

**Sketch (0.5 mark):** Three straight lines for ELR with slopes steeper than, equal to, and less than $\Gamma_d$, plotted against height.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Distinguish between **wind rose** and **pollution rose**. Why is a pollution rose more useful in environmental engineering?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Wind Rose (1.5 marks):** A polar diagram showing the **frequency of wind direction** (and often speed classes) at a location over a period (e.g., annual). Each "petal" length represents the percentage of time the wind blew from that direction.

**Pollution Rose (1.5 marks):** A polar diagram showing **pollutant concentration** as a function of wind direction. The length of each petal represents the *average concentration* of a pollutant (e.g., PM₁₀, SO₂) when the wind blew from that direction.

**Why pollution rose is more useful:**
It directly identifies the *sources* of pollution upwind of the monitoring station, integrating both meteorology and source strength. It reveals which direction the dominant source lies, helping in regulatory action, source apportionment, and industrial siting decisions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B has an **internal choice**. Below are two alternatives — **Question A** and **Question B** — covering the same topic from different angles. Each has 7-mark sub-parts with complete model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Lapse Rates, Inversions & Plume Behavior

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / 2024 Model Paper]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the four types of temperature inversions with suitable diagrams. Discuss their role in air pollution episodes.**

**Model Answer:**

A **temperature inversion** is a layer in which temperature *increases* with altitude, i.e., $\Gamma_e < 0$ K/m, acting as a stable "lid" that suppresses vertical mixing.

**1. Radiation (Nocturnal) Inversion (2 marks):**
On clear, calm nights, the ground radiates long-wave energy to space and cools rapidly. The air in contact with the ground is cooled by conduction, while the air aloft remains warm. A strong inversion (often 5–10 °C per 100 m) builds up, typically 100–400 m thick. Disappears after sunrise when solar heating erodes it from below.

**2. Advection Inversion (1.5 marks):**
Formed when warm air moves horizontally over a colder surface (e.g., warm maritime air over a cold land surface in winter; or warm air over a snow-covered region). The lower layers of the warm air are cooled from below, creating an inversion. Common in coastal industrial areas.

**3. Subsidence Inversion (2 marks):**
Found in large, persistent high-pressure systems (anticyclones). As air sinks, it is compressed and warmed adiabatically. The sinking motion creates a temperature inversion several hundred meters above ground. Common in subtropical deserts and during winter in northern India (Delhi). This is a major contributor to the Delhi smog phenomenon.

**4. Frontal Inversion (1.5 marks):**
At a weather front, a warm air mass overrides a colder, denser air mass. The boundary between the two air masses forms an inversion. Lasts from hours to a day, depending on frontal speed.

**Role in Air Pollution (1 mark):**
Inversions act as a lid preventing the vertical dispersion of pollutants. Combined with low wind speeds, they lead to the buildup of high GLCs of PM, SO₂, NOₓ, and VOCs. Famous episodes: London 1952 (4,000 deaths), Delhi winter smog, Los Angeles photochemical smog events.

**Diagrams (each inversion should have a temperature-vs-altitude sketch showing the "crossover"):**
Radiation: ground-level cold, warm aloft. Subsidence: warm air aloft compressed, cool below. Frontal: warm air mass over cold air mass.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A coal-fired power plant emits SO₂ at 600 g/s through a stack of height 100 m and diameter 4 m. The exit gas velocity is 18 m/s and temperature 415 K. Ambient air temperature is 298 K, wind speed at stack height is 4 m/s, and stability class is D. Calculate the effective stack height using Briggs' equation and estimate the ground-level centerline concentration at 2 km downwind. Use Pasquill-Gifford $\sigma_y = 68$ m, $\sigma_z = 35$ m at $x = 2000$ m for class D.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Buoyancy Flux (1.5 marks)**

$$F = \frac{g V_s d_s^2 (T_s - T_a)}{4 T_s}$$

$$F = \frac{9.81 \times 18 \times 4^2 \times (415 - 298)}{4 \times 415} = \frac{9.81 \times 18 \times 16 \times 117}{1660}$$

$$F = \frac{330{,}519.36}{1660} \approx 199.1 \ \text{m}^4/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 2: Plume Rise — Briggs formula for neutral stability (2 marks)**

For $u \geq 1$ m/s and $F < 55$, use the standard Briggs equation. The distance to final rise $x_f \approx 3.5 h_s = 3.5 \times 100 = 350$ m:

$$\Delta H = \frac{1.6 \, F^{1/3} \, x_f^{2/3}}{u}$$

$$\Delta H = \frac{1.6 \times (199.1)^{1/3} \times (350)^{2/3}}{4}$$

Compute cube root: $(199.1)^{1/3} \approx 5.85$
Compute power: $(350)^{2/3} = (350)^{0.667} \approx 49.65$

$$\Delta H = \frac{1.6 \times 5.85 \times 49.65}{4} = \frac{464.7}{4} \approx 116.2 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Effective Stack Height (0.5 mark)**

$$H = h_s + \Delta H = 100 + 116.2 = 216.2 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Ground-Level Centerline Concentration (3 marks)**

$$\chi(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left( -\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right)$$

Substitute values (with $Q = 600 \times 10^6$ µg/s):

$$\chi = \frac{600 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 4 \times 68 \times 35} \exp\left( -\frac{216.2^2}{2 \times 35^2} \right)$$

Denominator: $\pi \times 4 \times 68 \times 35 \approx 29{,}907$

$$\chi_0 = \frac{6 \times 10^8}{29{,}907} \approx 20{,}062 \ \mu\text{g/m}^3$$

Exponent: $\dfrac{216.2^2}{2 \times 35^2} = \dfrac{46{,}742.4}{2450} \approx 19.08$

$$\exp(-19.08) \approx 5.04 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\chi \approx 20{,}062 \times 5.04 \times 10^{-9} \approx 1.01 \times 10^{-4} \ \mu\text{g/m}^3$$

**Interpretation:** GLC is extremely low because the effective stack height (216 m) far exceeds the vertical dispersion coefficient $\sigma_z$ (35 m) at 2 km. The plume has not yet reached the ground — the maximum GLC will occur further downwind at $x_{max}$ where $\sigma_z \approx H/\sqrt{2} \approx 153$ m.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Atmospheric Pressure, Wind & Humidity

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / 2024 Model Paper]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the geostrophic wind equation. State the assumptions made. How is it modified in the atmospheric boundary layer due to friction?**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady-state flow (no acceleration, $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$).
2. Straight, parallel isobars (no curvature).
3. No friction (free atmosphere, above the boundary layer).
4. Constant density (incompressible approximation) or hydrostatic atmosphere.
5. Coriolis parameter $f$ is constant (uniform latitude).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a horizontal air parcel in the free atmosphere. Two forces act on it:
1. **Pressure Gradient Force (PGF):** $\vec{F}_{PGF} = -\dfrac{1}{\rho} \nabla P$ (directed from high to low pressure)
2. **Coriolis Force:** $\vec{F}_{Cor} = -f \hat{k} \times \vec{V}$ (deflects to the right in the Northern Hemisphere)

In steady state, these balance:
$$-\frac{1}{\rho} \nabla P - f \hat{k} \times \vec{V} = 0$$

Let isobars be parallel to the y-axis, so $\partial P/\partial y = 0$ and $\partial P/\partial x \neq 0$. Let $u, v$ be the x and y components of wind:

**x-component:** $-\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + f v = 0$

**y-component:** $-f u = 0 \implies u = 0$ — no flow in the x-direction (across isobars)

From the x-equation:
$$v = \frac{1}{\rho f} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

Define the pressure gradient normal to the isobars: $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial n} = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x}$ (PGF points toward low pressure, i.e., toward negative x).

$$V_g = \frac{1}{\rho f} \frac{\partial P}{\partial n}$$

This is the **geostrophic wind equation**. Wind flows *parallel* to isobars, with low pressure on the left (Northern Hemisphere).

**Modification due to friction in the boundary layer (1 mark):**
Friction acts opposite to the wind direction, reducing the Coriolis force. The balance becomes a 3-way PGF–Coriolis–Friction equilibrium. The wind then *crosses the isobars at an angle* (typically 10–30° over land, 10–20° over sea) from high to low pressure, with a reduced speed. This is why pollutants from a high-pressure ridge spiral *outward* (anticyclonic) and pollutants from a low spiral *inward* (cyclonic).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The relative humidity at 30 °C is 65%. Calculate (i) the saturation vapor pressure, (ii) the actual vapor pressure, (iii) the dew point temperature, and (iv) the specific humidity. Use the Magnus formula: $e_s = 6.11 \times 10^{(7.5T)/(T + 237.3)}$ with $T$ in °C and $e_s$ in hPa.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Saturation vapor pressure (1.5 marks)**

$$e_s(30) = 6.11 \times 10^{(7.5 \times 30)/(30 + 237.3)} = 6.11 \times 10^{225/267.3}$$

$$\frac{225}{267.3} = 0.8418$$

$$e_s(30) = 6.11 \times 10^{0.8418} = 6.11 \times 6.946 = 42.44 \ \text{hPa}$$

**Step 2: Actual vapor pressure (1.5 marks)**

$$e = \text{RH} \times e_s = 0.65 \times 42.44 = 27.59 \ \text{hPa}$$

**Step 3: Dew point temperature (2 marks)**

Set $e_s(T_d) = 27.59$ hPa. Solve the Magnus equation for $T_d$:

$$27.59 = 6.11 \times 10^{7.5 T_d / (T_d + 237.3)}$$

$$10^{7.5 T_d / (T_d + 237.3)} = 27.59 / 6.11 = 4.515$$

$$\frac{7.5 T_d}{T_d + 237.3} = \log_{10}(4.515) = 0.6547$$

$$7.5 T_d = 0.6547 T_d + 155.42$$

$$T_d (7.5 - 0.6547) = 155.42$$

$$T_d = \frac{155.42}{6.8453} = 22.71 \ ^\circ \text{C}$$

**Step 4: Specific humidity (2 marks)**

Specific humidity $q$ in g/kg is given by:
$$q = \frac{0.622 \, e}{P - 0.378 \, e}$$

Assuming $P = 1013.25$ hPa (standard):

$$q = \frac{0.622 \times 27.59}{1013.25 - 0.378 \times 27.59} = \frac{17.16}{1013.25 - 10.43} = \frac{17.16}{1002.82}$$

$$q = 0.01711 \ \text{kg/kg} = 17.11 \ \text{g/kg}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Units in Briggs plume rise:** Forgetting to convert $h_s$ to meters or $F$ to consistent SI units. Marks lost: 1–2 per question.
> 2. **Mixing up $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$** in the Gaussian equation — $\sigma_y$ is *horizontal* (crosswind), $\sigma_z$ is *vertical*. Writing them swapped costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the image source** — the ground-level formula has $\exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2)$ (single exponential, no factor of 2), but the full 3-D form has two exponentials (one real, one image). Marks lost: 1.
> 4. **Stability class identification** — confusing night and day classes. Day: A (strong sun) → D (overcast). Night: E (thin/moderate cloud) → F (clear/light wind). Marks lost: 1.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** in derivation questions. Geostrophic wind: at least 4–5 assumptions (steady, straight isobars, no friction, etc.). Marks lost: 1.
> 6. **Dimensional inconsistency** — wind speed in km/h instead of m/s; $Q$ in g/s instead of µg/s in the Gaussian equation. Always convert to SI.
> 7. **Dew point calculation** — using the wrong inverse of Magnus. Many students forget to take $\log_{10}$ correctly, leading to errors in $T_d$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must know before KTU exam):**
> - [ ] Define lapse rate, ELR, DALR ($\approx 9.76$ K/km), and SALR ($\approx 4$–6 K/km).
> - [ ] State the **3 stability cases** with inequalities relating $\Gamma_e$, $\Gamma_d$, $\Gamma_s$.
> - [ ] Name and explain **4 types of inversions**: Radiation, Advection, Subsidence, Frontal.
> - [ ] Write the **hydrostatic equation** $dP/dz = -\rho g$ and derive the **hypsometric equation**.
> - [ ] Derive and state the **geostrophic wind equation** $V_g = (1/\rho f)(\partial P/\partial n)$.
> - [ ] State **Buys-Ballot's law** (NH: back to wind, low on left).
> - [ ] Define **specific humidity, mixing ratio, RH, dew point** — know their formulas.
> - [ ] Write the **Clausius-Clapeyron** or **Magnus equation** for $e_s(T)$.
> - [ ] Write the **full Gaussian Plume Equation** with the image source.
> - [ ] Identify the **6 Pasquill-Gifford stability classes** A–F.
> - [ ] State the **6 plume behavior patterns**: Looping, Coning, Fanning, Fumigation, Trapping, Lofting.
> - [ ] Apply the **Briggs plume rise** equation and compute **effective stack height**.
> - [ ] Compute **ground-level centerline concentration** at any $x$ and identify the condition for $\chi_{max}$.
> - [ ] Distinguish between **wind rose and pollution rose**.
> - [ ] Recognize that **subsidence inversions** + **low wind + emissions** = severe pollution episodes (e.g., Delhi winter).
> - [ ] Always include **units** in numerical answers — common KTU deduction if missing.
> - [ ] For **GLC problems**, the maximum occurs at $\sigma_z(x_{max}) = H/\sqrt{2}$ — keep this as a ready formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT404 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Pollution Control Methods and Equipment (Brief Overview - Diagram, Principle and Working of Equipment Only, Design Not Needed) - Control Devices for Particulate Contaminants: Gravitational Settling, Centrifugal Collectors, Wet Collectors, Fabric Filters and Electrostatic Precipitators (ESP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Air Pollution Control — The Big Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Air Pollution Control (APC)** refers to the systematic application of physical, chemical, and engineering principles to **remove, reduce, or neutralize** contaminants released into the atmosphere from industrial, mobile, and area sources, ensuring that ambient air quality conforms to prescribed National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS).

Particulate matter (PM) is one of the two major categories of air pollutants (the other being gases/vapours). Particulate contaminants vary widely in size, density, and shape — from visible soot (>100 µm) to invisible respirable PM₂.₅ (<2.5 µm). **No single control device can economically handle the entire size spectrum**, so engineers classify devices by the particle size range they best target.

### 1.2 Categorization of Particulate Control Devices (PCDs)

Based on the **physical principle** used to separate the particle from the carrier gas stream:

| Category | Governing Physical Force | Typical Effective Size Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gravitational Settling Chambers** | Gravity (g) | > 50 µm (large, heavy particles) |
| **Centrifugal Collectors (Cyclones)** | Centrifugal Inertia | 5 – 50 µm |
| **Wet Collectors (Scrubbers)** | Inertial impaction on liquid droplets | 1 – 10 µm |
| **Fabric Filters (Baghouses)** | Sieving, impaction, diffusion, electrostatic | 0.1 – 10 µm |
| **Electrostatic Precipitators (ESP)** | Electrostatic attraction of charged particles | 0.01 – 10 µm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The order above is also the order of **decreasing size of particles** they target. As particle size shrinks, the required separation force grows exponentially, hence the technology shifts from simple gravity to sophisticated electrostatic fields.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Saree Sieve"

Imagine you are at home and want to separate the **coarse flour** from a fine powder. You would:

- Let the **heaviest grains fall** to the table by simply tapping the bowl — *that is **Gravitational Settling***.
- Spin the bowl rapidly so heavier grains are thrown to the outside — *that is a **Cyclone***.
- Blow the dust out of the air and wash it with a water mist — *that is a **Wet Scrubber***.
- Hold a cloth over the mouth of the bowl so only air passes — *that is a **Fabric Filter***.
- Rub the bowl with a charged balloon first so the dust is "stuck" to the bowl walls — *that is an **Electrostatic Precipitator***.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition (KTU Board Standard):** The **Overall Collection Efficiency (η)** of any control device is defined as:
> $$\eta = \frac{Mass\ of\ particulate\ collected\ in\ the\ device}{Mass\ of\ particulate\ entering\ the\ device} = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}}$$
> where $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are the **inlet** and **outlet** mass concentrations (mg/m³) of particulates, measured under **standard conditions of 25 °C and 1 atm (1.013 × 10⁵ Pa)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gravitational Settling Chambers

### Operating Principle
The dirty gas stream enters a large, low-velocity chamber. Because the horizontal gas velocity ($v_g$) is reduced sharply, the suspended particles, under the influence of **gravity (g = 9.81 m/s²)**, settle onto the collection hoppers at the bottom. A baffle plate at the inlet ensures uniform flow distribution.

- **Key Assumption:** Particles fall at their **terminal settling velocity ($v_s$)**, governed by Stokes' Drag Force balance.
- **Critical Design Parameter:** The **horizontal gas velocity** must be kept low enough to allow particles to fall to the floor *before* the gas exits the chamber.
- **Stokes' Regime Limit:** This laminar flow analysis is valid only when the particle **Reynolds number, $Re_p = (d_p \cdot v_s \cdot \rho) / \mu$**, is less than **0.1 (laminar regime)**, where $\rho$ = gas density, $\mu$ = gas dynamic viscosity.

### Industrial Applications
Used as **pre-cleaners** upstream of more efficient devices (cyclones, ESPs) to remove large, abrasive particles and reduce dust load. Common in cement kilns, mineral processing, and steel plants.

---

## 2.2 Centrifugal Collectors (Cyclones)

### Operating Principle
Dirty gas enters a **tangential inlet** at high velocity, creating a swirling vortex inside a cylindrical-conical body. The **centrifugal force ($F_c = m \cdot v_t^2 / r$)** throws the particles radially outward against the wall, where they lose momentum, slide down the conical wall, and fall into the dust hopper. The cleaned gas exits through the **vortex finder** at the top as a smaller, inner vortex.

- **Key Insight:** Cyclone efficiency depends strongly on the **inlet velocity** (typically 15–30 m/s) and the **ratio of cyclone body diameter to outlet diameter**.
- **Efficiency Limitation:** Very fine particles (<5 µm) experience negligible centrifugal force and escape with the vortex finder.
- **High-Efficiency Designs:** Stairmand, Lapple, Swift designs offer 90–99 % efficiency for particles > 5 µm.

### Industrial Applications
- Pre-cleaning for high-efficiency devices (back-end of cement kilns, boiler plants).
- Standalone collectors for **woodworking, metal grinding, and mineral processing** industries.
- Recovery of valuable product dust (sugar, milk powder, catalyst fines).

---

## 2.3 Wet Collectors (Scrubbers)

### Operating Principle
A **scrubber** uses a liquid (usually water) sprayed as fine droplets into the gas stream. Particulates are collected by **inertial impaction, interception, and diffusion** onto the droplet surface. The contaminated droplets are then separated from the gas in a mist eliminator (demister pad) and collected as slurry. 

**Common Types:**
- **Spray Tower** — simple, low-pressure drop; moderate efficiency (>10 µm).
- **Venturi Scrubber** — high velocity at the throat (60–180 m/s); very high efficiency for sub-micron particles; used for hazardous fumes and metallurgical operations.
- **Packed Bed Scrubber** — gas bubbles through liquid film on packing; used when simultaneous **gas absorption (SO₂, HCl, NH₃)** is required.

### Industrial Applications
- **Acid mists** (H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄) in fertilizer and chemical plants.
- **Foundry fumes** (silica, metal oxides).
- **Incinerators and metallurgical furnaces** — combined SO₂ + PM control.
- **Pharmaceutical and fertilizer industries** — handles hygroscopic, sticky, or combustible dust.

---

## 2.4 Fabric Filters (Baghouses)

### Operating Principle
A **baghouse** consists of an array of **vertical tubular fabric bags** (woven or felted; natural or synthetic fibres) suspended from a tube sheet. Dirty gas passes either **from inside-to-out** (most common, pulse-jet type) or **outside-to-in** (reverse-air type). Particles are captured by four mechanisms:

1. **Sieving** — particles larger than fabric pores are physically blocked.
2. **Inertial Impaction** — gas streamlines bend around fibres; particles > 1 µm continue and strike the fibre.
3. **Interception** — particles follow streamlines but touch the fibre as they pass.
4. **Diffusion (Brownian)** — submicron particles < 0.5 µm wander into fibres by random motion.

A **dust cake (filter cake)** builds up on the fabric — paradoxically, this is the *actual* filter medium that gives baghouses their extremely high efficiency (>99.9 %). The cake is periodically dislodged by **mechanical shaking, reverse air, or pulse-jet cleaning**.

### Industrial Applications
- **Cement, lime, and gypsum** plants.
- **Asphalt and mineral** processing.
- **Carbon black, pigments, and pharmaceutical powders**.
- **Coal-fired boilers** (with high-temperature fabrics like Nomex or P-84).

---

## 2.5 Electrostatic Precipitators (ESP)

### Operating Principle
A **high-voltage DC field (typically 30,000 – 100,000 V)** is established between:
- **Discharge electrodes** (thin wires) — generate a **corona discharge** that ionises gas molecules.
- **Collecting electrodes** (large plates or pipes) — attract the now-charged (typically negative) particles.

Negatively charged particles migrate across the gas stream toward the positive collection plates (**drift velocity, $w$**), where they are neutralized and collected. The accumulated dust layer is dislodged by **periodic rapping** and falls into hoppers.

**Key Features:**
- **Low pressure drop** (< 250 Pa) → low operating cost.
- **Very high efficiency** (> 99.5 %) achievable for fine particles.
- Can handle **very large gas volumes** (used in coal-fired power stations of 1000+ MW capacity).
- Performance sensitive to **dust resistivity** (10⁴ – 10¹⁰ Ω·cm is ideal); too low or too high causes problems.

### Industrial Applications
- **Thermal power plants** (largest single application globally).
- **Cement, iron & steel, pulp & paper industries**.
- **Sulphuric acid mist** collection.
- **Smelters and foundries**.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects **all board-exam-relevant formulas** for this module. Pay special attention to units, the **Stokes regime validity**, and the **Deutsch-Anderson equation** (a guaranteed 14-mark question favourite).

| # | Device / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Validity / Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Collection Efficiency (General)** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ | $C$ = concentration (mg/m³) | $0 \le \eta \le 1$; penetration $Pt = 1 - \eta$ |
| 2 | **Penetration (ktu Board Term)** | $Pt = 1 - \eta$ | Dimensionless | Used in series: $Pt_{total} = Pt_1 \times Pt_2 \times \dots$ |
| 3 | **Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes)** | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot d_p^2 \cdot (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \mu}$ | $g$ = 9.81 m/s²; $d_p$ = particle diameter (m); $\rho_p$ = particle density (kg/m³); $\rho$ = gas density (kg/m³); $\mu$ = gas dynamic viscosity (kg/(m·s)) | **Laminar regime** $Re_p < 0.1$; typical for $d_p < 50\ \mu m$ |
| 4 | **Stokes Number ($Stk$)** | $Stk = \dfrac{\rho_p \cdot d_p^2 \cdot v}{18 \mu \cdot D_c}$ | $v$ = relative velocity; $D_c$ = characteristic length | Dimensionless; $Stk \gg 1$ ⇒ good impaction |
| 5 | **Cut Diameter (Cyclone, $d_{50}$)** | $d_{50} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)}}$ | $B$ = inlet width; $N_e$ = effective number of turns; $v_i$ = inlet velocity | Diameter collected with 50 % efficiency |
| 6 | **Cyclone Pressure Drop** | $\Delta P = \xi \cdot \dfrac{\rho v_i^2}{2}$ | $\xi$ = friction factor (8–18); $v_i$ = inlet velocity | Higher $\xi$ ⇒ higher efficiency, more fan power |
| 7 | **Scrubber Collection Efficiency (Lapple)** | $\eta = 1 - \exp(-k \cdot Stk)$ | $k$ = empirical constant; $Stk$ = Stokes number | High-pressure Venturi ⇒ $Stk \gg 1 \Rightarrow \eta \to 1$ |
| 8 | **ESP Migration Velocity ($w$)** | $w = \dfrac{q \cdot E_p}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$ | $q$ = particle charge (C); $E_p$ = collecting field (V/m) | "Drift velocity" toward collecting electrode |
| 9 | **Deutsch-Anderson Equation (ESP)** | $\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}\right)$ | $A$ = collection area (m²); $Q$ = gas flow rate (m³/s) | **The** governing ESP efficiency equation; specific collection area $= A/Q$ in s/m |
| 10 | **Specific Collection Area (SCA)** | $SCA = \dfrac{A}{Q}$ | s/m | KTU boards: $SCA = 30\text{–}60$ s/m for typical ESPs |
| 11 | **Particulate Emission (mass flow)** | $\dot{m} = C \cdot Q$ | $C$ = concentration; $Q$ = volumetric flow | Used in emission compliance calculations |
| 12 | **Hopper Volume (cyclone/ESP)** | $V_h = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{collected}}{\rho_{bulk}}$ | $\dot{m}$ = collected mass rate; $\rho_{bulk}$ = bulk density | Design parameter — **not required for this topic** |
| 13 | **Fabric Filter Drag (Geldart)** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{K_c \cdot \mu \cdot v}{g_c} + \dfrac{K_d \cdot \rho_{cake} \cdot v^2}{g_c}$ | $K_c$ = viscous resistance; $K_d$ = cake resistance | Indicates pressure drop rises with cake build-up |
| 14 | **Respirable Particulate (PM₂.₅)** | $d_p \le 2.5\ \mu m$ | NAAQS standard: 60 µg/m³ (24-hr) | Alveolar-penetrating fraction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions for KTU 2024 Numericals:** Temperature $T = 25\ ^\circ C$ (298 K); Pressure $P = 1\ atm$ ($1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa); Air viscosity $\mu_{air} = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\ Pa \cdot s$; Air density $\rho_{air} = 1.184\ kg/m^3$ at these conditions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, derivations will fetch **5–7 marks** in 14-mark questions. Master the **Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes)** and **Deutsch-Anderson** derivations — these are the two that examiners set year after year.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Terminal Settling Velocity ($v_s$) for a Spherical Particle in the Stokes Regime

### Step 1: Free-Body Diagram of a Single Particle in Still Air

A particle falling under gravity experiences **three forces**:

1. **Weight (downward):** $\ F_g = m_p \cdot g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot \rho_p \cdot g$
2. **Buoyancy (upward):** $\ F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot \rho \cdot g$
3. **Drag Force (opposes motion, hence upward during fall):** $\ F_d$

**Net downward force** when the particle has reached *terminal* velocity (acceleration = 0):

$$
F_g - F_b - F_d = 0
$$

### Step 2: Express the Drag Force in the Stokes (Laminar) Regime

For $Re_p < 0.1$, Stokes' law applies:

$$
F_d = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_s
$$

where $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity** of the gas and $v_s$ is the instantaneous settling velocity.

### Step 3: Substitute into the Equilibrium Equation

$$
\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p g - \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho g - 3 \pi \mu d_p v_s = 0
$$

### Step 4: Simplify and Group the Density Terms

Factor out the common terms $\pi d_p$:

$$
\frac{\pi d_p^3 g}{6} (\rho_p - \rho) = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_s
$$

### Step 5: Isolate $v_s$

Divide both sides by $3 \pi \mu d_p$:

$$
v_s = \frac{d_p^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \mu}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - $v_s \propto d_p^2$ — halving the particle diameter reduces its fall speed by **4 times**. This is why fine PM₂.₅ is virtually impossible to remove by gravity.
> - $v_s \propto (\rho_p - \rho)$ — denser particles (e.g., metal oxides, $\rho_p \approx 5000\ kg/m^3$) settle far faster than light fly-ash particles.
> - $v_s \propto 1/\mu$ — heating the gas (which reduces $\mu$) actually speeds up settling (small effect).

### Step 6: Validity Check — When Does the Stokes Regime Apply?

The **particle Reynolds number** is:

$$
Re_p = \frac{d_p \cdot v_s \cdot \rho}{\mu} = \frac{d_p^3 \cdot g \cdot \rho \cdot (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \mu^2}
$$

The Stokes formula is **valid only when $Re_p < 0.1$**. For larger $Re_p$, the drag law transitions to the **Newton regime**, and a different terminal velocity expression applies.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Deutsch-Anderson Equation for ESP Collection Efficiency

### Step 1: Geometric and Physical Setup

Consider a small gas element of cross-section $dA$ moving through an ESP at velocity $u$ (gas velocity, m/s). The gas carries a uniform concentration of charged particulates $C$ (kg/m³). The particles have a migration velocity $w$ (m/s) perpendicular to the gas flow, toward the collection plate.

### Step 2: Rate of Particle Removal across an Elemental Area $dA$

The **mass flow of particulates** passing through the element $dA$ is:

$$
d\dot{m} = C \cdot u \cdot dA
$$

Of these, the **fraction removed** per unit time is governed by the ratio of the migration velocity $w$ to the gas velocity $u$:

$$
\text{Fractional removal rate} = \frac{w}{u}
$$

Therefore, the **rate of particle removal** at this element is:

$$
d(\text{removed}) = C \cdot u \cdot dA \cdot \frac{w}{u} = C \cdot w \cdot dA
$$

### Step 3: Mass Balance on the Control Element

The rate at which particulates are removed from the gas stream **equals the rate at which the gas concentration drops** as it traverses $dA$:

$$
- Q \cdot dC = C \cdot w \cdot dA
$$

(The negative sign indicates concentration *decreases* along the flow path.)

### Step 4: Convert to a Differential Equation

Since $Q$ is constant (steady-state, incompressible gas flow approximation), we can write:

$$
- Q \cdot dC = C \cdot w \cdot dA
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{dC}{C} = -\frac{w}{Q} dA
$$

### Step 5: Integrate over the Full Collection Area

Integrate from the inlet ($C_{in}$, at $A = 0$) to the outlet ($C_{out}$, at $A = A$):

$$
\int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC}{C} = -\frac{w}{Q} \int_0^A dA
$$

### Step 6: Evaluate the Integrals

The left side yields $\ln(C_{out}) - \ln(C_{in}) = \ln(C_{out}/C_{in})$, and the right side yields $-wA/Q$:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) = -\frac{wA}{Q}
$$

### Step 7: Exponentiate Both Sides

$$
\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{wA}{Q}\right)
$$

### Step 8: Substitute the Definition of Collection Efficiency

Since $\eta = 1 - (C_{out}/C_{in})$:

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{wA}{Q}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - $\eta \to 1$ as the **Specific Collection Area** $A/Q$ grows large.
> - $\eta \to 0$ if either the migration velocity $w$ is very small (low resistivity, weak field) or the gas flow rate $Q$ is very high (residence time too short).
> - To increase $\eta$ from 90 % to 99 %, the exponent $(wA/Q)$ must increase from 2.30 to 4.61 — **doubling the specific collection area or doubling $w$** (e.g., by increasing voltage).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Five Devices (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **frequently asked as a 7-mark sub-question**. Memorize it for both the theory exam and viva.

| Feature | Settling Chamber | Cyclone | Wet Scrubber | Fabric Filter | ESP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Particle Size Range** | > 50 µm | 5 – 50 µm | 1 – 10 µm | 0.1 – 10 µm | 0.01 – 10 µm |
| **Typical Efficiency** | < 50 % | 70 – 95 % | 80 – 95 % | > 99.9 % | > 99.5 % |
| **Pressure Drop (Pa)** | 50 – 150 | 500 – 1500 | 500 – 4000 | 1000 – 2500 | 100 – 250 |
| **Operating Cost** | Very low | Low | High (water + sludge) | Moderate (bag replacement) | Moderate (electricity) |
| **Capital Cost** | Very low | Low | Moderate | Moderate – High | Very High |
| **Water Use** | None | None | High | None | None (for PM only) |
| **Handles Sticky/Hygroscopic Dust?** | Yes | Poorly | Yes | Poorly (clogs) | Yes |
| **Handles Combustible Dust?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Risk of fire | Risk of explosion |
| **Handles Corrosive Gases?** | Yes | Yes | Yes (absorbs them) | Limited by fabric | Limited by electrodes |
| **Sub-micron Efficiency** | Negligible | Poor | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| **Space Requirement** | Very large | Compact | Moderate | Moderate – Large | Very large |
| **Examples in Industry** | Cement, minerals | Sawmills, biomass | Foundries, fertilizer | Cement, asphalt | Thermal power plants |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Deutsch-Anderson Application (Typical 7-Mark KTU Numerical)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 type):** A coal-fired boiler has an ESP handling a flue gas flow of $Q = 100\ m^3/s$. The measured migration velocity of the fly-ash particles is $w = 0.12\ m/s$. The specific collection area installed is $SCA = 40\ s/m$. Calculate the **overall collection efficiency** and the **outlet dust concentration** if the inlet concentration is $C_{in} = 15\ g/m^3$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the available data.
$Q = 100\ m^3/s$, $w = 0.12\ m/s$, $SCA = A/Q = 40\ s/m$, $C_{in} = 15\ g/m^3$.

**Step 2:** Apply the Deutsch-Anderson equation.

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{wA}{Q}\right) = 1 - \exp(-w \cdot SCA)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp[-(0.12)(40)] = 1 - \exp(-4.8)
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the exponential.

$$
\exp(-4.8) = 0.00823
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the efficiency.

$$
\eta = 1 - 0.00823 = 0.9918 \approx 99.18\ \%
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the outlet concentration.

$$
C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot (1 - \eta) = 15 \times 0.00823 = 0.1235\ g/m^3 = 123.5\ mg/m^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A 1 % drop in efficiency means the outlet dust nearly **doubles**. Hence, ESPs are designed with substantial safety margins in $A/Q$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly asks for the **Diagram, Principle and Working of Equipment**. The following Mermaid block diagrams map the **internal functional architecture** of each device and their **process flow**. They are designed to be board-exam friendly — students are expected to redraw simplified versions during the exam.

## 4.1 Master Functional Flow — Particulate Control Device Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Polluted Gas Inlet<br/>PM laden dirty stream"] --> B["Pre Separator Stage<br/>Gravitational Settling Chamber"]
    B --> C["Primary Collector<br/>Centrifugal Cyclone"]
    C --> D["Secondary Collector<br/>Choice of Wet Scrubber<br/>Fabric Filter or ESP"]
    D --> E["Cleaned Gas Outlet<br/>to Stack via Induced Draft Fan"]
    B --> F["Coarse Dust Hoppers<br/>periodic emptying"]
    C --> F
    D --> G["Fine Dust Collection Hoppers"]
    D --> H["Slurry / Waste Liquids<br/>scrubber water"]
    F --> I["Material Recovery<br/>or Disposal"]
    G --> I
    H --> J["Effluent Treatment Plant"]
```

## 4.2 Gravitational Settling Chamber — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Inlet Duct<br/>dirty flue gas"] --> B["Inlet Baffle Plate<br/>spreads flow evenly"]
    B --> C["Settling Zone<br/>low velocity chamber<br/>length L, height H"]
    C --> D["Particle Settles<br/>under gravity g"]
    D --> E["Dust Hoppers<br/>at chamber bottom"]
    C --> F["Outlet Duct<br/>cleaned gas to next stage"]
    E --> G["Manual or<br/>Screw Conveyor<br/>dust discharge"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Logic:** Because the chamber cross-section is large, gas velocity drops from ~10 m/s at the inlet to ~0.5 m/s inside. The particle's horizontal transit time must exceed its **fall time** from the top to the bottom of the chamber.

## 4.3 Cyclone Separator — Tangential Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Tangential Inlet<br/>dirty gas at 15 to 25 m/s"] --> B["Cylindrical Body<br/>outer vortex forms"]
    B --> C["Conical Section<br/>outer vortex descends"]
    C --> D["Apex of Cone<br/>solids exit point"]
    D --> E["Dust Hopper<br/>collected particulates"]
    B --> F["Vortex Finder<br/>inner upward vortex"]
    F --> G["Cleaned Gas Outlet<br/>exits at top"]
    C --> H["Secondary<br/>Return Flow<br/>particles in inner<br/>vortex re-enter outer vortex"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Logic:** The dirty gas spirals **downward** along the wall (outer vortex), reverses direction at the conical apex, and spirals **upward** through the vortex finder. Particles flung to the wall lose momentum and slide down. The return-flow path ensures *rebounding* particles are re-captured, boosting efficiency.

## 4.4 Wet Scrubber (Venturi Type) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Dirty Gas Inlet<br/>at 10 to 20 m/s"] --> B["Converging Section<br/>of Venturi"]
    C["Liquid Spray Nozzle<br/>fine water droplets"] --> B
    B --> D["Throat<br/>high velocity 60 to 180 m/s<br/>atomization and impact"]
    D --> E["Diverging Section<br/>pressure recovery"]
    D --> F["Particle Droplet<br/>Inertial Impaction<br/>PM captured on liquid"]
    E --> G["Cyclonic Separator<br/>Mist Eliminator"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Slurry Outlet<br/>to ETP"]
    G --> I["Cleaned Gas Outlet<br/>to stack"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Logic:** In the **throat**, gas velocity is so high that injected water is **atomized into tiny droplets** (~50–500 µm). The relative velocity between dust and droplets is so large that even submicron particles cannot follow the streamlines and **impact** the droplets. The laden droplets are then removed in the downstream **cyclonic mist eliminator**.

## 4.5 Fabric Filter (Pulse-Jet Baghouse) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Dirty Gas Inlet<br/>plenum below bags"] --> B["Tube Sheet<br/>gas distribution plate"]
    B --> C["Fabric Filter Bags<br/>woven or felted<br/>outward filtration"]
    C --> D["Cages<br/>internal wire support<br/>keeps bag open"]
    C --> E["Cleaned Gas Plenum<br/>above bags"]
    E --> F["Outlet Duct<br/>to stack"]
    C --> G["Dust Cake Buildup<br/>on inner bag surface"]
    G --> H["Pulse Jet Valve<br/>short blast of compressed air"]
    H --> I["Cake Dislodges<br/>falls to hopper"]
    I --> J["Hopper with Screw<br/>Conveyor Discharge"]
    H --> K["Compressed Air<br/>Tank Reservoir"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Logic:** Filtration occurs **from inside to outside**. The pulse-jet sends a brief shock of compressed air (4–6 bar) down the centre of each bag, **inflating** it instantly and shaking off the dust cake. The cycle repeats every few minutes per row of bags (off-line cleaning).

## 4.6 Electrostatic Precipitator (Plate-Wire, Single-Stage) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Dirty Gas Inlet<br/>distribution screens<br/>ensure uniform flow"] --> B["High Voltage Field Zone<br/>discharge wires between plates"]
    C["H T Rectifier Set<br/>20 to 100 kV DC"] --> B
    B --> D["Corona Discharge<br/>gas ionised<br/>particles charged negative"]
    D --> E["Migration Zone<br/>particles drift to plates<br/>at velocity w"]
    E --> F["Collection Plates<br/>vertical steel plates<br/>dust accumulates"]
    F --> G["Rapping Mechanism<br/>periodic hammer strike"]
    G --> H["Dust falls to Hoppers"]
    F --> I["Clean Gas Outlet<br/>to ID fan and stack"]
    H --> J["Hopper Discharge<br/>screw or chain conveyor"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Logic:** Particles acquire a **negative charge** in the high-intensity corona region near the discharge wires. They then migrate across the gas stream — under the electrostatic attraction of the **positive collection plates** — at a characteristic **migration velocity $w$**. The Deutsch-Anderson equation (Section 3.2) governs overall collection efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A demands **precise, two-to-three-sentence answers** worth 3 marks each. Avoid rambling; use the bold terms in the model answers — they are what examiners look for.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "collection efficiency" and "penetration" of a particulate control device. How are they related to each other?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Collection efficiency ($\eta$)** is the *fraction* of inlet particulates removed by the control device: $\eta = (C_{in} - C_{out})/C_{in}$.
- **Penetration ($Pt$)** is the *fraction* of inlet particulates that escape untreated: $Pt = 1 - \eta$.
- They sum to unity. For devices connected **in series**, the overall penetration is the **product** of individual penetrations: $Pt_{total} = Pt_1 \times Pt_2 \times \dots$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why are Electrostatic Precipitators (ESPs) preferred over Fabric Filters for large thermal power plants?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Very high collection efficiency (> 99.5 %)** even for sub-micron fly-ash particles.
- **Very low pressure drop** (100 – 250 Pa) — drastically lower than fabric filters (1000 – 2500 Pa) — leading to **lower fan power consumption** and lower operating cost.
- **Handles huge gas volumes** (millions of m³/hr) typical of 1000 MW+ thermal plants.
- However, ESP efficiency is highly sensitive to **dust resistivity**, and the **capital cost** is high.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B follows the **ESE (End Semester Exam) internal choice** pattern — students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. Both questions below are 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Model solutions are exhaustive — use them as full write-up templates.**

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **working principle and components of a Cyclone Separator**. Why is it preferred as a pre-cleaner upstream of high-efficiency devices? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A thermal power plant uses a **fabric filter (baghouse)** to control fly-ash emissions. The inlet dust concentration is $12\ g/m^3$ at a gas flow of $50\ m^3/s$. The overall collection efficiency is 99.9 %. Calculate: **(i)** the outlet dust concentration, **(ii)** the mass of fly-ash collected per day, and **(iii)** the **penetration** and comment on whether NAAQS (PM₁₀ limit = 100 µg/m³ for 24 hours) is met. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Definition:** A cyclone is a **centrifugal collector** that uses **inertial separation** to remove particulates from a gas stream using the vortex motion imparted by a tangential inlet.

**Key Components (with brief description — for diagram, refer to Section 4.3):**

1. **Tangential Inlet** — Introduces dirty gas at high velocity (15 – 25 m/s), creating a swirling motion.
2. **Cylindrical Body** — Provides the chamber where the **outer vortex** descends along the walls.
3. **Conical Section** — Accelerates the outer vortex as the cross-section narrows, increasing centrifugal force.
4. **Vortex Finder** — A central pipe projecting into the cylinder; it draws the **inner vortex** of cleaned gas upward to the outlet.
5. **Dust Hopper** — Collects particles that have lost momentum and slide down the cone walls.
6. **Outlet (Gas Exit)** — Releases cleaned gas to the next stage or stack.

**Working Principle:**
- The tangential inlet creates a **double vortex**: an outer downward vortex and an inner upward vortex.
- Particles in the rotating gas experience a **centrifugal force $F_c = m v^2 / r$**, which is many times the force of gravity — this throws them toward the wall.
- The heavier particles lose kinetic energy, leave the gas stream, slide down the walls, and are collected in the hopper.
- The cleaned gas reverses direction at the apex and exits through the vortex finder.

**Why Cyclones are Preferred as Pre-Cleaners:**

- **Inexpensive and simple** to construct and operate.
- **No moving parts**, no water, no electricity — extremely low O&M cost.
- Effectively removes the **high-mass, coarse fraction** (> 10 µm) which would otherwise overload the downstream high-efficiency device.
- Reduces the **thermal load and dust loading** on the downstream ESP/fabric filter, thereby **extending the life of bags/electrodes** and reducing maintenance frequency.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given Data:**
$C_{in} = 12\ g/m^3 = 12{,}000\ mg/m^3$;
$Q = 50\ m^3/s$;
$\eta = 99.9\ \% = 0.999$;
$N = 1\ day = 86{,}400\ s$.

**(i) Outlet Dust Concentration:**

$$
C_{out} = C_{in} \times (1 - \eta) = 12{,}000 \times (1 - 0.999) = 12{,}000 \times 0.001 = 12\ mg/m^3
$$

> **Result:** $C_{out} = 12\ mg/m^3 = 12{,}000\ \mu g/m^3$.

**(ii) Mass of Fly-Ash Collected per Day:**

Mass flow rate collected:

$$
\dot{m}_{collected} = \eta \cdot C_{in} \cdot Q = 0.999 \times 12{,}000 \times 50 = 599{,}400\ mg/s
$$

Convert to kg/day:

$$
\dot{m}_{collected} = 599{,}400 \times 10^{-6} \times 86{,}400 = 51{,}788\ kg/day \approx 51.8\ tonnes/day
$$

**(iii) Penetration and NAAQS Compliance Check:**

$$
Pt = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.999 = 0.001 = 0.1\ \%
$$

> **NAAQS Check:** $C_{out} = 12{,}000\ \mu g/m^3 \gg 100\ \mu g/m^3$ (NAAQS PM₁₀ limit).

**Conclusion:** **NAAQS is NOT met.** The baghouse, despite 99.9 % efficiency, cannot meet NAAQS because the **inlet loading is too high** or **a more efficient device (or second-stage control) is needed**. *(This is a classic KTU trap: high efficiency ≠ compliance if inlet is dirty!)*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the **construction, working, and application of a Plate-Type Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Deutsch-Anderson equation** for the collection efficiency of an ESP. An ESP handles a flue gas flow of $250\ m^3/s$. The total collection plate area installed is $10{,}000\ m^2$ and the effective migration velocity of the fly-ash is $0.15\ m/s$. Calculate: **(i)** the **overall collection efficiency** and **(ii)** the **outlet dust concentration** if $C_{in} = 20\ g/m^3$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Introduction:**
An **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** is a high-efficiency particulate control device that uses an **electric field** to charge and collect particulates. It can achieve > 99.5 % efficiency for very fine particles at a very low pressure drop.

**Main Components (refer to Section 4.6 for diagram):**

1. **Discharge Electrodes (Wires)** — Thin (2–3 mm) tungsten or stainless steel wires suspended vertically between collection plates; connected to the **negative terminal** of the HT rectifier (20 – 100 kV DC). Generate the **corona discharge**.
2. **Collection Electrodes (Plates)** — Large vertical steel plates connected to the **positive (grounded) terminal**; provide the surface on which charged dust deposits.
3. **HT Rectifier Set** — Converts AC mains to high-voltage DC for the discharge electrodes.
4. **Rapping Mechanism** — Hammers that periodically strike the electrodes to dislodge the dust cake into hoppers.
5. **Hoppers** — Pyramidal collection chambers at the bottom of each ESP field.
6. **Gas Distribution Screens** — Perforated plates at inlet and outlet to ensure uniform flow across the field.

**Working Principle:**
- The high-voltage field ionises the gas around the **discharge wires**, producing a **corona discharge** (a faint bluish glow accompanied by a hissing sound).
- Electrons emitted from the corona attach to dust particles, giving them a **net negative charge**.
- The **negatively charged particles migrate** under the influence of the electric field toward the **positively charged collection plates**, at a velocity called the **drift velocity $w$**.
- Upon contact, the particles lose their charge, adhere to the plate, and form a **dust cake**.
- The cake is periodically dislodged by **rapping** and collected in hoppers.

**Key Advantages:**
- Extremely low **pressure drop** (100 – 250 Pa) → low fan energy.
- Very high **efficiency (> 99.5 %)** for fine PM.
- Handles **very large gas volumes**.
- Can collect **dry dust** — easy material handling.

**Applications:**
- **Thermal power plants** (pulverised coal-fired boilers — by far the largest application).
- **Cement, iron & steel, and pulp & paper** industries.
- **Sulphuric acid plants** (acid mist).
- **Non-ferrous metal smelters**.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Derivation of Deutsch-Anderson Equation:**

*Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the complete 8-step derivation.* Key steps summarized:

1. Elemental area $dA$ removes a fraction $w/u$ of the gas-borne particulates.
2. Mass balance: $-Q \cdot dC = C \cdot w \cdot dA$.
3. Rearrange: $dC/C = -(w/Q) dA$.
4. Integrate from inlet to outlet: $\ln(C_{out}/C_{in}) = -wA/Q$.
5. Exponentiate: $C_{out}/C_{in} = \exp(-wA/Q)$.
6. Therefore: $\boxed{\eta = 1 - \exp(-wA/Q)}$.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $Q = 250\ m^3/s$; $A = 10{,}000\ m^2$; $w = 0.15\ m/s$; $C_{in} = 20\ g/m^3$.

**(i) Overall Collection Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{wA}{Q}\right) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.15 \times 10{,}000}{250}\right)
$$

$$
= 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{1500}{250}\right) = 1 - \exp(-6) = 1 - 0.00248
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta = 0.9975 \approx 99.75\ \%}
$$

**(ii) Outlet Dust Concentration:**

$$
C_{out} = C_{in} \times (1 - \eta) = 20 \times 0.00248 = 0.0496\ g/m^3 = 49.6\ mg/m^3
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{out} \approx 49.6\ mg/m^3}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The ESP achieves very high efficiency because the **specific collection area $SCA = A/Q = 40\ s/m$** is sufficiently large.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in This Module — Read Carefully!**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Penetration with Efficiency:** Many students write $Pt = \eta$ instead of $Pt = 1 - \eta$. Examiner deducts **1 full mark** per occurrence.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Standard Conditions:** Air viscosity $\mu_{air} = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\ Pa\cdot s$ and $\rho_{air} = 1.184\ kg/m^3$ apply **only at 25 °C and 1 atm**. If the problem states different conditions, you **must apply Sutherland's Law** or the ideal gas law. Forgetting this is a 2-mark penalty.
> 
> 3. **Stokes' Regime Validity:** Writing the terminal settling velocity equation without stating $Re_p < 0.1$ as a validity condition costs you a mark. The examiner expects you to **identify the regime** explicitly.
> 
> 4. **Mixing Up $v_i$ and $v$ in Cyclone Equations:** $v_i$ is the **inlet velocity** at the tangential entry, not the gas velocity inside the cyclone. Using the wrong variable is a common error.
> 
> 5. **ESP Equations: Using $(w \cdot A / Q)$ vs $(w \cdot L / D)$:** The Deutsch-Anderson form uses **$A/Q$ (specific collection area)**. Some students erroneously use the ratio of field length to spacing, which is a different parameter — **2-mark penalty**.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting the Factor $e$ in $\exp(-x)$:** When asked to "evaluate" the exponential, students often write a number like "$\exp(-4.8) = 0$" or skip the calculation. **Always compute the actual decimal value** — it is a 1-mark line item.
> 
> 7. **Diagrams without Labels:** The KTU syllabus explicitly demands a "neat sketch". An **unlabelled diagram = 0 marks** for that sub-part. Always label **all major components, gas flow direction, and any process streams** (water spray, dust hopper, rapping, etc.).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-day revision summary — go through this list 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Five major devices** for particulate control: Settling Chamber → Cyclone → Scrubber → Fabric Filter → ESP. Remember them in **decreasing particle size** and **increasing efficiency**.

- ✅ **Operating principle of each:** gravity; centrifugal force; inertial impaction on liquid droplets; filtration + cake; electrostatic attraction of charged particles.

- ✅ **Collection efficiency formula:** $\eta = (C_{in} - C_{out})/C_{in}$ and **Penetration:** $Pt = 1 - \eta$.

- ✅ **Stokes' Terminal Velocity:** $v_s = g d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) / (18\mu)$. Validity: $Re_p < 0.1$.

- ✅ **Cyclone cut diameter ($d_{50}$):** the particle size collected with 50 % efficiency. Smaller $d_{50}$ = better cyclone design.

- ✅ **Fabric filter mechanisms:** Sieving, Impaction, Interception, Diffusion. The **dust cake is the real filter medium** — it is the key to > 99.9 % efficiency.

- ✅ **ESP Governing Equation:** $\eta = 1 - \exp(-wA/Q)$ — the **Deutsch-Anderson equation** is the **single most important formula** in this module. Specific Collection Area $SCA = A/Q$ typically **30 – 60 s/m**.

- ✅ **Pressure drop ranking (low → high):** ESP < Settling Chamber < Cyclone < Fabric Filter < Venturi Scrubber. (Directly proportional to fan power cost.)

- ✅ **NAAQS PM limits (must memorize):** PM₁₀ = 100 µg/m³ (24-hr); PM₂.₅ = 60 µg/m³ (24-hr).

- ✅ **Standard reference conditions:** $T = 25\ ^\circ C$ (298 K); $P = 1\ atm$ ($1.013 \times 10^5\ Pa$); $\mu_{air} = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\ Pa\cdot s$; $\rho_{air} = 1.184\ kg/m^3$.

- ✅ **Industrial Applications:** ESP → thermal power; Cyclone → cement, biomass; Fabric filter → cement, asphalt; Scrubber → acid mists, foundries; Settling chamber → pre-cleaner.

- ✅ **Key limitation of each device:** Settling chamber (large space, coarse only); Cyclone (poor on submicron); Scrubber (water + sludge); Fabric filter (high T, sticky dusts); ESP (sensitive to dust resistivity).

- ✅ **For KTU 2024 numericals:** *always* state the Deutsch-Anderson equation before substituting. Show **all 5–6 calculation steps** explicitly — partial marks are awarded for each step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Devices for Gaseous Contaminants: Adsorption, Absorption, Condensation, Combustion, Automobile Emission Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT404 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control Devices for Gaseous Contaminants: Adsorption, Absorption, Condensation, Combustion, Automobile Emission Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Gaseous Contaminant Control

**Gaseous contaminants** (also called *gaseous air pollutants* or *volatile pollutants*) are airborne substances in gas/vapour phase that degrade air quality, harm human health, damage materials, and contribute to environmental problems like acid rain, smog, and ozone depletion. Common examples include **SO₂, NOₓ, CO, H₂S, VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds), Hg vapour, NH₃, HCl, and hydrocarbons**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (NEP-Aligned):**
> *Air pollution control devices for gaseous contaminants* are engineered systems that physically or chemically remove, transform, or destroy gaseous pollutants from industrial/automobile exhaust streams **before** they are discharged into the atmosphere, in compliance with **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** and the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.

The five principal control technologies mandated in the KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus are:

| # | Technology | Underlying Principle | Typical Target Pollutants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Adsorption** | Surface adhesion onto a solid | VOCs, odours, Hg, HCl |
| 2 | **Absorption** | Dissolution into a liquid (scrubbing) | SO₂, NOₓ, HF, Cl₂ |
| 3 | **Condensation** | Phase change (vapour → liquid) | High-boiling VOCs, Hg vapour |
| 4 | **Combustion** | High-temperature oxidation | CO, HCs, odorous gases |
| 5 | **Automobile Emission Control** | Catalytic + mechanical reduction | CO, HC, NOₓ, PM |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Five Ways to Clean a Stinky Kitchen" Analogy 🍳**
>
> Imagine a smelly kitchen full of cooking vapours. You can clean it five ways:
> 1. **Adsorption** → A **charcoal filter** on the chimney. Smelly molecules *stick* to the charcoal surface. *(solid captures gas)*
> 2. **Absorption** → A **wet cloth** hung across the room absorbs vapours into water. *(liquid dissolves gas)*
> 3. **Condensation** → A **cold steel lid** held over the pan makes vapour *drip* into liquid. *(gas becomes liquid when cooled)*
> 4. **Combustion** → A **gas burner** that burns the vapours harmlessly into CO₂ + H₂O. *(gas is destroyed by fire)*
> 5. **Automobile Emission Control** → The **catalytic converter** in your car's exhaust does the same burning job, but with a platinum catalyst and clever engineering. *(mobile combustion control)*

This analogy makes the physical principle behind each control device instantly memorable for a KTU student attending their first Environmental Engineering class.

---

## 1.3 Selection Logic — Which Device for Which Pollutant?

The choice of control device depends on the pollutant's **concentration, temperature, flow rate, and chemical nature**. The decision matrix used by environmental engineers is summarised below:

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Heuristic (Industry-Standard):**
> - **Low concentration, high volume** (e.g., ventilation air with VOCs at ppm level) → **Adsorption**
> - **Soluble pollutant, water available** (e.g., SO₂ from power plants) → **Absorption**
> - **High concentration, high boiling point** (e.g., Hg, toluene vapours) → **Condensation**
> - **Combustible organics, low concentration** → **Thermal/Catalytic Incineration**
> - **Mobile source (car/bus exhaust)** → **Catalytic converter + EGR + PCV**

Key physical constants used throughout this module (all at **STP = 25 °C, 1 atm** unless stated otherwise):

- Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ = 0.08206 L·atm·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- Molar volume at STP: **Vₘ = 22.414 L·mol⁻¹** (at 0 °C) / **24.465 L·mol⁻¹** (at 25 °C)
- Standard atmospheric pressure: **P_atm = 101.325 kPa = 1 atm**
- Auto-ignition temperature of typical VOC: **≈ 400–500 °C**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ADSORPTION — Operational Theory

**Adsorption** is the adhesion of atoms, ions, or molecules of a gas (the *adsorbate*) onto the surface of a solid material (the *adsorbent*). It is a **surface phenomenon**, distinct from absorption (which is bulk penetration).

**Two types:**
- **Physisorption** (physical adsorption) — weak **van der Waals** forces, low heat (5–40 kJ/mol), reversible, multi-layer.
- **Chemisorption** (chemical adsorption) — strong **covalent/ionic** bonds, high heat (40–400 kJ/mol), often irreversible, mono-layer.

### 2.1.1 Common Industrial Adsorbents

| Adsorbent | Surface Area (m²/g) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Activated Carbon (AC)** | 800 – 1500 | VOCs, odours, Hg |
| **Silica Gel** | 750 – 800 | Drying, hydrocarbon recovery |
| **Activated Alumina** | 200 – 400 | Fluoride, drying |
| **Zeolites (Molecular Sieves)** | 500 – 800 | Selective separation of gases |
| **Resins / Polymers** | 100 – 500 | Specialty VOCs |

### 2.1.2 Adsorption Isotherms — The Equilibrium Relation

An **adsorption isotherm** describes the equilibrium loading **q** (mass adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent) as a function of the residual concentration **C** (or partial pressure **P**) of the adsorbate at constant temperature.

#### (a) Freundlich Isotherm (Empirical — most commonly tested in KTU)

$$q_e = \frac{x}{m} = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$$

where:
- $q_e$ = mass of adsorbate per unit mass of adsorbent (mg/g)
- $C_e$ = equilibrium concentration of adsorbate in solution/gas (mg/L or ppm)
- $K_F$ = Freundlich capacity constant (L/mg or mg/g)
- $1/n$ = adsorption intensity (dimensionless; **0 < 1/n < 1** for favourable adsorption)

#### (b) Langmuir Isotherm (Theoretical — mono-layer coverage assumption)

$$q_e = \frac{q_{\max} \cdot b \cdot C_e}{1 + b \cdot C_e}$$

where:
- $q_{\max}$ = maximum mono-layer capacity (mg/g)
- $b$ = Langmuir constant related to enthalpy of adsorption (L/mg)

#### (c) BET Isotherm (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller — multi-layer)

$$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m c} + \frac{(c-1)}{V_m c} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$$

where $P_0$ = saturation pressure, $V_m$ = mono-layer volume, $c$ = BET constant.

### 2.1.3 Fixed-Bed Adsorber & Breakthrough Curve

A **fixed-bed adsorber** is a vertical column packed with adsorbent. As the polluted gas stream flows down (or up) through the bed:
- The **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** moves through the bed.
- Initially the outlet concentration ≈ 0.
- After some time, the adsorbent becomes saturated at the inlet side and the MTZ advances.
- The outlet concentration suddenly rises — this is the **breakthrough point** (typically defined as $C_{\text{out}} = 0.05 \, C_{\text{in}}$).
- The bed is **exhausted** when $C_{\text{out}} = 0.95 \, C_{\text{in}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **breakthrough curve** plots $C_{\text{out}}/C_{\text{in}}$ versus time. Its **shape** (steepness) indicates bed efficiency — a steep curve is ideal; a long tail indicates poor mass transfer.

### 2.1.4 Regeneration of Spent Adsorbent

Spent carbon is regenerated by:
- **Steam stripping** (for VOCs — recovered as condensate)
- **Thermal reactivation** in a rotary kiln (850 °C, inert atmosphere)
- **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** — for gases like O₂/N₂ separation
- **Temperature Swing Adsorption (TSA)** — common for solvent recovery

---

## 2.2 ABSORPTION — Operational Theory

**Absorption** (also called *scrubbing* or *gas absorption*) is a unit operation in which one or more gaseous components are **dissolved into a liquid solvent**. The gas mixture contacts the liquid in a tower, and the soluble component transfers from gas phase to liquid phase driven by the **concentration gradient** (or partial pressure gradient).

**Two modes:**
- **Physical absorption** — governed by Henry's law (e.g., CO₂ in water)
- **Chemical absorption** — accompanied by reaction (e.g., SO₂ + NaOH → Na₂SO₃ + H₂O)

### 2.2.1 Henry's Law

$$P_A = H \cdot x_A \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad C_A = H' \cdot P_A$$

where:
- $P_A$ = partial pressure of A in gas phase (atm)
- $x_A$ = mole fraction of A in liquid (–)
- $H$ = Henry's constant (atm·mol/mol)
- $C_A$ = aqueous concentration (mol/L)
- $H'$ = Henry's constant in alternate units (mol·L⁻¹·atm⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> A **low H value** ⇒ gas is **highly soluble** ⇒ easy to absorb (e.g., NH₃, HCl). A **high H value** ⇒ gas is sparingly soluble (e.g., O₂, CO, CH₄).

### 2.2.2 Two-Film Theory & Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient

Whitman's **Two-Film Theory** (1923) postulates that resistance to mass transfer lies in two thin stagnant films at the gas–liquid interface. The flux of species A is:

$$N_A = k_G (P_A - P_{A,i}) = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_A)$$

Defining the **overall mass transfer coefficients** based on gas and liquid phases:

$$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{H}{k_L} \quad ; \quad \frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{H \cdot k_G} + \frac{1}{k_L}$$

The overall driving force is:

$$N_A = K_G (P_A - P_A^*) = K_L (C_A^* - C_A)$$

where $P_A^*$ is the gas partial pressure in equilibrium with the bulk liquid concentration $C_A$ (and vice versa for $C_A^*$).

### 2.2.3 Operating Line & Material Balance for Counter-Current Packed Tower

For a counter-current packed absorption tower with gas flow $G$ (mol/s) and liquid flow $L$ (mol/s), the **operating line** is:

$$Y = \frac{L}{G} X + (Y_1 - \frac{L}{G} X_1)$$

where $Y$ and $X$ are mole ratios of solute in gas and liquid respectively.

**Minimum Liquid Rate:**

$$L_{\min} = G \cdot \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2}$$

where $X_1^*$ is the equilibrium value corresponding to $Y_1$.

**Actual Liquid Rate:** $L = 1.2 \text{ to } 2.0 \times L_{\min}$.

### 2.2.4 Height of Packed Tower — HTU–NTU Concept

$$Z = \int_0^Z dZ = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{G \, dY}{K_G a \, (Y - Y^*)}$$

For dilute systems (constant $G$ and $L$):

$$Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG} = H_{OL} \cdot N_{OL}$$

where:
- $H_{OG} = G / (K_G a \cdot (1 - mG/L))$ = Height of a Transfer Unit (m)
- $N_{OG} = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{dY}{Y - Y^*}$ = Number of Transfer Units (dimensionless)
- $m$ = slope of equilibrium line

For straight operating and equilibrium lines:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{\Delta Y_{LM}} \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta Y_{LM} = \frac{(Y_1 - Y_1^*) - (Y_2 - Y_2^*)}{\ln\left[\frac{Y_1 - Y_1^*}{Y_2 - Y_2^*}\right]}$$

### 2.2.5 Common Industrial Absorbers (Scrubbers)

| Type | Pressure Drop | Efficiency | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Packed Tower** | Low–Medium | High | SO₂, HCl, H₂S |
| **Spray Tower** | Very Low | Low–Medium | Particulate + soluble gas |
| **Venturi Scrubber** | **High** (250–750 mm WC) | **Very High** | Fine PM + soluble gas |
| **Plate Tower** | Medium | High | High gas rates |
| **Cyclonic Spray Tower** | Medium | High | PM + gas |

---

## 2.3 CONDENSATION — Operational Theory

**Condensation** is the process of converting a vapour (or vapour component of a gas stream) into a liquid by **reducing its temperature below its dew point** or **increasing its pressure**. It is used when:
1. The pollutant is **valuable and recoverable** (e.g., mercury, high-purity VOCs).
2. The pollutant has a **high boiling point** relative to the carrier gas.
3. The concentration is **high** (otherwise condensation is uneconomical).

**Two types:**
- **Surface Condenser** — vapour condenses on a cool surface; condensate can be collected.
- **Contact (Direct) Condenser** — coolant sprayed directly into vapour stream.

**Dew Point Equation** (for ideal gas behaviour):

$$T_{dew} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{T_b} - \frac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} \ln\left(\frac{P}{P_{atm}}\right)}$$

where $T_b$ = normal boiling point (K), $\Delta H_{vap}$ = enthalpy of vaporisation, $P$ = partial pressure of vapour.

---

## 2.4 COMBUSTION — Operational Theory

**Combustion** (or **incineration** for air pollution control) is the **high-temperature oxidation** of combustible gaseous pollutants to harmless end products: **CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, and N₂**.

The general reaction for a hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$ is:

$$C_x H_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x\, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\, H_2 O + \text{Heat}$$

**Three Temperature Regimes:**

| Device | Temperature | Residence Time | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Incinerator (Afterburner)** | 700 – 1000 °C | 0.5 – 1.0 s | VOCs, HCs, CO |
| **Catalytic Incinerator** | 300 – 500 °C | 0.1 – 0.5 s | Low concentration organics |
| **Flare** | 1000 – 1500 °C | < 0.1 s | Emergency venting, refinery gases |

**Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement:**

$$O_2^{\,stoich} = \frac{100}{21}\left[x + \frac{y}{4} + z - \frac{w}{2}\right] \quad \text{(vol % of air for } C_xH_yO_zN_w\text{)}$$

**Actual Air-Fuel Ratio (using excess air, EA%):**

$$A/F_{actual} = A/F_{stoich} \times \left(1 + \frac{EA}{100}\right)$$

---

## 2.5 AUTOMOBILE EMISSION CONTROL

A modern petrol car exhaust contains three regulated pollutants: **CO, hydrocarbons (HC), and NOₓ**. The three-way **catalytic converter** is the cornerstone device. Auxiliary systems include:

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** | Returns blow-by gases to intake manifold |
| **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** | Recirculates exhaust to lower combustion T → reduces NOₓ |
| **Evaporative Emission Control (Charcoal Canister)** | Traps fuel vapours from tank |
| **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** | Simultaneously oxidises CO + HC and reduces NOₓ |

**Three-Way Catalytic Converter Reactions:**

$$2\, CO + O_2 \longrightarrow 2\, CO_2 \quad \text{(Oxidation)}$$

$$C_x H_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x\, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\, H_2 O \quad \text{(Oxidation)}$$

$$2\, NO_x \longrightarrow x\, O_2 + N_2 \quad \text{(Reduction)}$$

The TWC is most effective at the **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (A/F ≈ 14.7)** within a narrow λ-window of **0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01** (equivalence ratio $\phi$ ≈ 1), which is maintained by the **closed-loop lambda (O₂) sensor** in the exhaust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bhat (Bureau of Indian Standards) and Bharat Stage (BS) Norms:** India currently follows **BS-VI (Euro 6 equivalent)** norms. KTU questions frequently reference the timeline: BS-I (2000) → BS-II (2005) → BS-III (2010) → BS-IV (2017) → BS-VI (2020, leapfrogged from BS-V).

---

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Freundlich | $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ | mg/g, mg/L | Batch adsorption design |
| 2 | Langmuir | $q_e = \dfrac{q_{\max} b \, C_e}{1 + b C_e}$ | mg/g, L/mg | Mono-layer capacity |
| 3 | Henry's Law | $P_A = H \cdot x_A$ | atm, mol/mol | Solubility check |
| 4 | Two-Film Flux | $N_A = K_G (P_A - P_A^*)$ | mol/(m²·s) | Mass-transfer rate |
| 5 | Overall K relation | $1/K_G = 1/k_G + H/k_L$ | s/m | Identify controlling film |
| 6 | Min. Liquid Rate | $L_{\min} = G \, \dfrac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2}$ | mol/s | Packed tower design |
| 7 | Tower Height | $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ | m | Packed tower sizing |
| 8 | NTU (linear) | $N_{OG} = \dfrac{Y_1 - Y_2}{\Delta Y_{LM}}$ | – | Dilute-system design |
| 9 | Dew Point | $T_{dew}^{-1} = T_b^{-1} - \dfrac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} \ln(P/P_{atm})$ | K | Condensation onset |
| 10 | Combustion Stoich. O₂ | $x + y/4 + z - w/2$ | mol O₂/mol fuel | Incinerator design |
| 11 | Excess Air Correction | $A/F = (A/F)_{stoich} (1 + EA/100)$ | – | Real combustion |
| 12 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = (A/F)_{stoich} / (A/F)_{actual}$ | – | TWC window check |
| 13 | Stoich. A/F (gasoline) | ≈ **14.7** | kg air/kg fuel | Petrol engine target |
| 14 | Breakthrough Time | $t_b = \dfrac{\rho_b \cdot q \cdot A \cdot Z}{Q \cdot C_0}$ | s | Adsorber life |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $K_G$ (overall, gas basis) with $k_G$ (individual film). Remember — **$K_G$ is the combined resistance; $k_G$ is one film only.**

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Freundlich Isotherm Linearisation (for KTU Numerical Problems)

The Freundlich equation is non-linear. To determine $K_F$ and $1/n$ from experimental data, take logarithms:

**Starting equation:**

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$$

**Step 1 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln(q_e) = \ln(K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the logarithm product rule** $\ln(ab) = \ln a + \ln b$:

$$\ln(q_e) = \ln(K_F) + \ln(C_e^{1/n})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the power rule** $\ln(a^b) = b \ln a$:

$$\ln(q_e) = \ln(K_F) + \frac{1}{n} \ln(C_e)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the standard $y = mx + c$ form** with $y = \ln(q_e)$, $x = \ln(C_e)$:

$$\ln(q_e) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{n}}_{\text{slope}} \cdot \ln(C_e) + \underbrace{\ln(K_F)}_{\text{intercept}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation for KTU numerical:**

A plot of $\ln(q_e)$ (Y-axis) versus $\ln(C_e)$ (X-axis) gives a **straight line** of slope $= 1/n$ and intercept $= \ln(K_F)$. From any two experimental points, the KTU student can compute:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\ln q_{e,2} - \ln q_{e,1}}{\ln C_{e,2} - \ln C_{e,1}} \quad \text{and} \quad K_F = \exp(\text{intercept})$$

**KTU Numerical Worked Example:**

Adsorption data on activated carbon: at $C_{e,1} = 1$ mg/L, $q_{e,1} = 2.5$ mg/g; at $C_{e,2} = 5$ mg/L, $q_{e,2} = 5.0$ mg/g. Find $K_F$ and $1/n$.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\ln(5.0) - \ln(2.5)}{\ln(5) - \ln(1)} = \frac{\ln 2}{\ln 5} = \frac{0.6931}{1.6094} = 0.4307$$

Therefore $n = 2.322$ (a value of $n$ between 1 and 10 indicates **favourable adsorption**).

$$K_F = \frac{q_{e,1}}{C_{e,1}^{1/n}} = \frac{2.5}{1^{0.4307}} = 2.5 \text{ mg/g} \cdot (\text{L/mg})^{0.4307}$$

The final Freundlich equation:

$$q_e = 2.5 \cdot C_e^{0.431}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two-Film Theory & Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient

The flux across each film in the gas–liquid interface is:

**Gas-film flux:**

$$N_A = k_G (P_A - P_{A,i})$$

**Liquid-film flux:**

$$N_A = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_A)$$

**Step 1 — Henry's law at the interface** (always at equilibrium):

$$P_{A,i} = H \cdot C_{A,i} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad C_{A,i} = \frac{P_{A,i}}{H}$$

**Step 2 — Henry's law for the bulk liquid** (defines $C_A^*$ as the concentration in equilibrium with $P_A$):

$$C_A^* = \frac{P_A}{H} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad P_A = H \cdot C_A^*$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate interfacial terms.** From the gas-film:

$$P_{A,i} = P_A - \frac{N_A}{k_G}$$

From the liquid-film:

$$C_{A,i} = C_A + \frac{N_A}{k_L}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Henry's interface law:**

$$P_{A,i} = H \cdot C_{A,i} = H \cdot \left(C_A + \frac{N_A}{k_L}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for $P_{A,i}$:**

$$P_A - \frac{N_A}{k_G} = H \cdot C_A + \frac{H \cdot N_A}{k_L}$$

**Step 6 — Collect $N_A$ terms on one side and bulk terms on the other:**

$$N_A \left(\frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{H}{k_L}\right) = P_A - H \cdot C_A = (P_A - P_A^*)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $N_A$ and define $K_G$:**

$$N_A = \frac{P_A - P_A^*}{\dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{H}{k_L}} = K_G (P_A - P_A^*)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{H}{k_L}}$$

**Interpretation:** the two resistances $1/k_G$ and $H/k_L$ are **in series**. The **larger** term controls — if gas is very soluble ($H$ is small), $H/k_L \to 0$ and **gas film controls**; if gas is sparingly soluble ($H$ is large), $1/k_G \to 0$ and **liquid film controls**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Liquid Rate for Counter-Current Packed Tower

Consider a packed absorber with:
- Gas rate $G$ (mol/s, solute-free basis), entering at $Y_1$, leaving at $Y_2$.
- Liquid rate $L$ (mol/s, solvent-free basis), entering at $X_2$, leaving at $X_1$.
- Equilibrium relation $Y^* = mX$ (straight line through origin).

**Step 1 — Component material balance on solute:**

$$G(Y_1 - Y_2) = L(X_1 - X_2)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $X_1$ (the rich liquid leaving):**

$$X_1 = X_2 + \frac{G}{L}(Y_1 - Y_2)$$

**Step 3 — For minimum liquid rate, the operating line touches the equilibrium curve at the bottom** of the tower, i.e. $X_1 = X_1^* = Y_1 / m$. So:

$$L_{\min} \cdot \left(\frac{Y_1}{m} - X_2\right) = G(Y_1 - Y_2)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $L_{\min}$:**

$$\boxed{L_{\min} = \frac{G(Y_1 - Y_2)}{\dfrac{Y_1}{m} - X_2} = G \cdot \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2}}$$

**Step 5 — Practical design** (use 1.5× the minimum as a rule of thumb):

$$L_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \cdot L_{\min}$$

**Key Engineering Insight:** the **slope of the operating line** $= L/G$ must always be **greater than the slope of the equilibrium line $m$** for finite packed height. If $L/G \le m$, no finite tower can achieve the desired separation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stoichiometric Combustion of a Gaseous Fuel

**Fuel:** methane (CH₄), the dominant component of natural gas.

**Step 1 — Write the unbalanced combustion equation:**

$$CH_4 + O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Balance C atoms:** 1 C on the left → 1 CO₂ on the right.

**Step 3 — Balance H atoms:** 4 H on the left → 2 H₂O on the right.

**Step 4 — Balance O atoms:** 2 + 1 = 3 O atoms on the right → 1.5 O₂ on the left:

$$CH_4 + 1.5\, O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\, H_2O$$

**Step 5 — Convert to air** (O₂ is 21 % of air, so air = O₂ / 0.21):

$$\text{Stoich. air} = \frac{1.5}{0.21} = 7.143 \text{ mol air/mol CH}_4$$

**Step 6 — Mass basis** (molar masses: CH₄ = 16, air = 28.97):

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{7.143 \times 28.97}{16} = 12.94 \text{ kg air/kg CH}_4$$

**Step 7 — With 20 % excess air:**

$$(A/F)_{actual} = 12.94 \times 1.20 = 15.53 \text{ kg air/kg CH}_4$$

This is the working value used in **thermal incinerator design**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Three-Way Catalytic Converter Mass Balance

Consider a petrol engine exhausting $V$ m³/s of exhaust at standard conditions, with pollutant concentrations:

- CO: $C_{CO}$ (ppm) before the catalyst, $C_{CO}'$ (ppm) after.
- HC: $C_{HC}$ (ppm) before, $C_{HC}'$ (ppm) after.
- NOₓ: $C_{NO_x}$ (ppm) before, $C_{NO_x}'$ (ppm) after.

**Conversion efficiency for each pollutant:**

$$\eta_{CO} = \frac{C_{CO} - C_{CO}'}{C_{CO}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{HC} = \frac{C_{HC} - C_{HC}'}{C_{HC}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{NO_x} = \frac{C_{NO_x} - C_{NO_x}'}{C_{NO_x}} \times 100\%$$

**KTU Sample Calculation:** A car exhausts 0.05 m³/s. CO at the inlet is 4.5 % and the TWC achieves 95 % conversion. Find the mass of CO removed per hour.

**Step 1 — Inlet CO mole fraction** = 4.5 % = 0.045.

**Step 2 — At STP, molar volume = 0.022414 m³/mol:**

$$\dot{n}_{CO,in} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.045}{0.022414} = 0.1004 \text{ mol/s}$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow (M_CO = 28 g/mol):**

$$\dot{m}_{CO,in} = 0.1004 \times 28 = 2.81 \text{ g/s} = 10.12 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — With 95 % conversion:**

$$\dot{m}_{CO,removed} = 10.12 \times 0.95 = 9.61 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Answer:** **≈ 9.6 kg of CO per hour** is converted to CO₂ by the TWC.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Control Device

| Pollutant Property | Best Device | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Low concentration, valuable VOC | Adsorption (AC) | High surface area; recoverable |
| High concentration, soluble | Absorption | High driving force |
| High boiling point, recoverable | Condensation | Easy phase change |
| Combustible, low concentration | Catalytic combustion | Low fuel cost |
| High concentration, combustible | Thermal incineration / flare | Robust, simple |
| Auto exhaust (CO, HC, NOₓ) | TWC + EGR | Three-way simultaneous |
| Odorous gases (H₂S, NH₃) | Biofilter or AC | Biological or physical |
| Acid gases (HCl, SO₂) | Wet scrubber | Water-soluble, reactive |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 4 — "Why" Questions to Expect:**
> 1. Why is **excess air** required in combustion? *— to ensure complete oxidation of CO to CO₂ (avoid toxic CO slip).*
> 2. Why is **λ ≈ 1** critical for the TWC? *— Below 1 (rich) → CO/HC not oxidised; above 1 (lean) → NOₓ not reduced.*
> 3. Why does **steam regeneration** recover VOCs from activated carbon? *— Hot steam desorbs (opposite of exothermic adsorption); the VOC-steam mixture is condensed, separating the VOC.*
