# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Selection of Gaseous Control Device

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Pollutant and Concentration] --> B{Combustible?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Concentration Level?}
    C -- High --> D[Thermal Incinerator or Flare]
    C -- Low --> E[Catalytic Incinerator or TWC]
    B -- No --> F{Water Soluble?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Absorption / Scrubber]
    F -- No --> H{High Boiling Point?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Condensation]
    H -- No --> J[Adsorption on Activated Carbon]
    A --> K{Mobile Source?}
    K -- Yes --> L[TWC plus EGR plus PCV System]
```

## 4.2 Packed-Tower Absorption Column — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GAS1[Polluted Gas In Y1] --> INT1[Gas Inlet Distributor]
    INT1 --> PACK[Packed Bed with Raschig Rings or Pall Rings]
    PACK --> INT2[Demister Pad]
    INT2 --> GAS2[Clean Gas Out Y2]
    LIQ1[Lean Solvent In X2] --> PUMP[Pump]
    PUMP --> SPRAY[Liquid Distributor at Top]
    SPRAY --> PACK
    PACK --> LIQ2[Rich Solvent Out X1]
    PACK -->|Counter-current contact| PACK
```

## 4.3 Fixed-Bed Adsorber with Breakthrough Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dirty Gas In] --> B[Fan or Blower]
    B --> C[Inlet Concentration C0]
    C --> D[Activated Carbon Bed]
    D --> E[Outlet Sampler]
    E --> F[Breakthrough Detector]
    F --> G{Concentration exceeds 0.05 C0?}
    G -- No --> H[Continue Operation]
    G -- Yes --> I[Saturated - Initiate Regeneration Cycle]
    I --> J[Hot Steam or Hot Nitrogen]
    J --> K[Desorbed VOC to Condenser]
    K --> L[Liquid VOC Recovery]
    L --> M[Cooled Adsorbent Ready for Reuse]
```

## 4.4 Three-Way Catalytic Converter — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Cylinders] --> B[Exhaust Manifold]
    B --> C[Lambda Oxygen Sensor Upstream]
    C --> D[ECU Feedback Loop]
    D --> A
    C --> E[Three-Way Catalytic Converter]
    E --> F[Lambda Oxygen Sensor Downstream]
    F --> D
    E --> G[Reduction Catalyst Rhodium]
    E --> H[Oxidation Catalyst Platinum and Palladium]
    G --> I[NOx converted to N2 and O2]
    H --> J[CO and HC converted to CO2 and H2O]
    I --> K[Tailpipe Clean Exhaust]
    J --> K
```

## 4.5 Thermal Incinerator (Afterburner) — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process VOCs in Air Stream] --> B[Preheater using Waste Heat]
    B --> C[Burner with Natural Gas Support]
    C --> D[Combustion Chamber 700 to 1000 C]
    D --> E[Residence Time 0.5 to 1.0 second]
    E --> F[Heat Exchanger Recovers Energy]
    F --> G[Stack to Atmosphere]
    F -->|Heat Recovery Loop| B
    D --> H[CO and HC completely oxidised to CO2 and H2O]
```

## 4.6 Condenser System for VOC Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Process Gas with VOCs] --> B[Cyclone or Pre-filter]
    B --> C[Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger]
    C -->|Cooling Water| CW[CW In]
    CW --> CWO[CW Out]
    C --> D[Condensate Collection Vessel]
    D --> E[Liquid VOC - Recovered Product]
    C --> F[Non-condensable Tail Gas]
    F --> G{Above emission limit?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Send to Adsorber or Incinerator]
    G -- No --> I[Vent to Atmosphere]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Distinguish between **physisorption** and **chemisorption**. Give two examples of industrial adsorbents.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|
| **Bonding** | van der Waals (weak) | Covalent / ionic (strong) |
| **Heat of adsorption** | 5 – 40 kJ/mol | 40 – 400 kJ/mol |
| **Layers** | Multi-layer possible | Strictly mono-layer |
| **Reversibility** | Reversible | Often irreversible |
| **Temperature** | Low (below critical T) | High |
| **Activation energy** | None | Required |
| **Specificity** | Non-specific | Highly specific |

**Examples of industrial adsorbents:** *Activated carbon* (VOCs, mercury), *silica gel* (drying), *zeolites / molecular sieves* (gas separation), *activated alumina* (fluoride removal).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is the role of the **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** system in an automobile? Mention the function of the **lambda (O₂) sensor**.*

**Model Answer:**

**PCV system role:** During the compression stroke of a petrol engine, a small amount of the air–fuel mixture **blows past the piston rings** into the crankcase. If released to the atmosphere, this would emit unburnt hydrocarbons. The PCV valve routes these blow-by gases back to the **intake manifold**, where they are re-burnt in the engine — eliminating HC emissions and protecting the engine oil from contamination.

**Lambda (O₂) sensor function:** The lambda sensor measures the **residual oxygen content** in the exhaust gas and sends a voltage signal (≈ 0.1 V lean ↔ 0.9 V rich) to the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**. The ECU then adjusts the fuel injector pulse width to maintain the **air–fuel ratio at stoichiometry (A/F ≈ 14.7, λ = 1)**, which is essential for the **three-way catalytic converter** to function efficiently (oxidation of CO + HC and reduction of NOₓ simultaneously).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 model paper]**

**(a)** *Derive the **Freundlich adsorption isotherm**. The following data are obtained for the adsorption of a dye on activated carbon:*

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | 1 | 2 | 4 | 8 | 12 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_e$ (mg/g) | 2.5 | 3.6 | 4.8 | 6.4 | 7.5 |

*Determine the constants $K_F$ and $1/n$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **fixed-bed adsorber** and discuss the **breakthrough curve**. Mention how a saturated bed is regenerated. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q-A(a) — Freundlich Isotherm (7 marks)**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} \;\Longrightarrow\; \ln q_e = \ln K_F + \frac{1}{n} \ln C_e$$

This is a straight line in $\ln q_e$ vs. $\ln C_e$ with slope $1/n$ and intercept $\ln K_F$.

**Calculation (4 marks):** Tabulate $\ln C_e$ and $\ln q_e$:

| $\ln C_e$ | 0.000 | 0.693 | 1.386 | 2.079 | 2.485 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\ln q_e$ | 0.916 | 1.281 | 1.569 | 1.856 | 2.015 |

Apply **least squares**:

$$n = 5, \quad \sum x = 6.643, \quad \sum y = 7.637, \quad \sum xy = 11.799, \quad \sum x^2 = 12.799$$

$$\text{slope } (1/n) = \frac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2} = \frac{5(11.799) - (6.643)(7.637)}{5(12.799) - (6.643)^2} = \frac{58.995 - 50.734}{63.995 - 44.130} = \frac{8.261}{19.865} = 0.416$$

$$\text{intercept } (\ln K_F) = \frac{\sum y - \text{slope}\cdot \sum x}{n} = \frac{7.637 - 0.416 \times 6.643}{5} = \frac{7.637 - 2.764}{5} = 0.975$$

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{n} = 0.416, \quad K_F = e^{0.975} = 2.65 \text{ mg/g} \cdot (\text{L/mg})^{0.416}}$$

**Final equation:** $q_e = 2.65 \, C_e^{0.416}$

**Validity check:** $1/n = 0.416$ is between 0 and 1 → **favourable adsorption** ✔.

---

### **Model Solution for Q-A(b) — Fixed-Bed Adsorber (7 marks)**

**Working (3 marks):** A fixed-bed adsorber is a vertical cylindrical column packed with adsorbent (typically activated carbon). The pollutant-laden gas enters from the top (or bottom) and passes through the bed. The most soluble/adsorbable molecules are captured in the **first layers** of the bed. As the bed saturates near the inlet, the **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** of length $Z_{MTZ}$ travels down the bed. Initially, outlet concentration is essentially zero. As the MTZ approaches the exit, the outlet concentration begins to rise.

**Breakthrough curve (2 marks):** A plot of $\dfrac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}}$ versus time is the breakthrough curve:

- **Breakthrough time $t_b$:** when $C_{\text{out}} = 0.05 \, C_{\text{in}}$ (5 % of inlet).
- **Exhaustion time $t_e$:** when $C_{\text{out}} = 0.95 \, C_{\text{in}}$ (95 % of inlet).
- A **steep curve** (small $Z_{MTZ}$) ⇒ high mass-transfer efficiency.
- A **shallow, elongated** curve ⇒ poor design.

**Regeneration (2 marks):** Once $C_{\text{out}}$ approaches the regulatory limit, the bed is **switched to regeneration mode** while a parallel bed takes the load. Common methods:

1. **Steam stripping** — low-pressure steam (100–150 °C) flows through the bed, desorbing the VOCs. The steam + VOC mixture is condensed; VOCs separate as a water-immiscible layer.
2. **Thermal reactivation** in a rotary kiln (800–900 °C, inert atmosphere) for non-volatile adsorbates.
3. **Pressure-Swing Adsorption (PSA)** — rapid pressure cycling; used in air-separation plants.
4. **Temperature-Swing Adsorption (TSA)** — used in solvent recovery and gas drying.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice to Q-A]**

**(a)** *A counter-current packed tower is to be designed to absorb **SO₂ from air** using water. The inlet gas contains 5 mol % SO₂ at a rate of $G = 100$ kmol/h (solute-free), and the exit gas must contain $Y_2 = 0.001$. The entering water is solute-free ($X_2 = 0$). The equilibrium relation is $Y^* = 30X$. Take $L = 1.5 \, L_{\min}$.* **Calculate: (i) $L_{\min}$, (ii) actual $L$, (iii) $X_1$ (mol ratio of SO₂ in exit water), (iv) HTU if $K_G a = 0.0002$ kmol/(m³·s·kPa).** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain with a schematic the working of a **three-way catalytic converter (TWC)** for an automobile. State the role of the **EGR** and **charcoal canister** in reducing vehicular emissions. Why must the air–fuel ratio be maintained at stoichiometry for the TWC to be effective?* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q-B(a) — Absorption Tower Design (7 marks)**

Convert inlet gas composition to mole ratio:

$$Y_1 = \frac{y_1}{1 - y_1} = \frac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.05263 \text{ kmol SO}_2 / \text{kmol air}$$

Given: $Y_2 = 0.001$, $X_2 = 0$, $G = 100$ kmol/h, $m = 30$.

**(i) Minimum liquid rate (2 marks):**

$$L_{\min} = G \cdot \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2} = G \cdot \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{Y_1/m - X_2} = 100 \cdot \frac{0.05263 - 0.001}{0.05263/30 - 0}$$

$$L_{\min} = 100 \cdot \frac{0.05163}{0.001754} = 100 \cdot 29.43 = 2943 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**(ii) Actual liquid rate (1 mark):**

$$L = 1.5 \times L_{\min} = 1.5 \times 2943 = 4415 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**(iii) $X_1$ in exit water (2 marks):** From the operating line $G(Y_1 - Y_2) = L(X_1 - X_2)$:

$$X_1 = \frac{G(Y_1 - Y_2)}{L} = \frac{100 \times (0.05263 - 0.001)}{4415} = \frac{5.163}{4415} = 0.001169 \text{ kmol SO}_2/\text{kmol water}$$

**(iv) HTU calculation (2 marks):**

$$H_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_G a \cdot (1 - mG/L)}$$

First compute $mG/L$:

$$\frac{mG}{L} = \frac{30 \times 100}{4415} = 0.6795$$

$$1 - mG/L = 1 - 0.6795 = 0.3205$$

Now $G = 100$ kmol/h $= 100/3600 = 0.02778$ kmol/s. $K_G a = 0.0002$ kmol/(m³·s·kPa) (units already consistent in y-basis when partial pressure is in kPa).

$$H_{OG} = \frac{0.02778}{0.0002 \times 0.3205} = \frac{0.02778}{6.41 \times 10^{-5}} = 433.4 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{H_{OG} \approx 433 \text{ m}}$$

(Real packed towers operate with $H_{OG} \approx 0.5$ – 1.5 m, so the $K_G a$ value in the question is illustrative; the method is what KTU tests.)

---

### **Model Solution for Q-B(b) — TWC, EGR, Charcoal Canister (7 marks)**

**Three-Way Catalytic Converter (3 marks):** The TWC is a stainless-steel canister in the exhaust line containing a **ceramic honeycomb monolith** (cordierite) wash-coated with **noble metals** — platinum (Pt) and palladium (Pd) for oxidation, and rhodium (Rh) for reduction. As hot exhaust (≈ 400–600 °C) flows through:

$$2\, CO + O_2 \rightarrow 2\, CO_2 \quad \text{(oxidation, Pt/Pd)}$$

$$C_x H_y + (x + y/4) O_2 \rightarrow x\, CO_2 + (y/2) H_2O \quad \text{(oxidation)}$$

$$2\, NO_x \rightarrow x\, O_2 + N_2 \quad \text{(reduction, Rh)}$$

**EGR – Exhaust Gas Recirculation (2 marks):** Routes a portion of the exhaust (5–15 %) back to the intake manifold. The exhaust gas is **inert**, lowering peak combustion temperature (from ≈ 2200 K to ≈ 1800 K). Since NOₓ forms via the Zeldovich mechanism at high T (N₂ + O₂ → NO at T > 1500 K), reducing T sharply decreases NOₓ. Typical NOₓ reduction: **50–70 %**.

**Charcoal canister (evaporative control) (1 mark):** Traps fuel vapours from the tank and carburettor (in older cars) on activated carbon. When the engine runs, fresh air is drawn through the canister, desorbing the trapped hydrocarbons, which are then burnt in the engine. Prevents **HC evaporative emissions** when the car is parked in the sun.

**Why stoichiometry? (1 mark):** At $\lambda = 1$ (A/F ≈ 14.7 for gasoline), the exhaust gas contains trace O₂ and CO simultaneously. The Pt/Rh catalysts operate in a **narrow 1 % λ window** (0.99 – 1.01). Rich mixture (λ < 1): no O₂ → CO and HC slip past un-oxidised. Lean mixture (λ > 1): O₂ present → Rh cannot reduce NOₓ. **Modern cars use two O₂ sensors + ECU to maintain λ ≈ 1.000 ± 0.005.**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 4 Questions:**
> 1. **Freundlich linearisation (3 marks lost/year):** Students forget to take **logarithms** and solve directly — the equation is non-linear. KTU expects the $y = mx + c$ derivation explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $K_G$ and $k_G$:** $K_G$ = overall (combined) coefficient; $k_G$ = gas-film coefficient. Writing $N_A = k_G(P_A - P_A^*)$ is **wrong** — that violates the two-film theory.
> 3. **Forgetting the $L = 1.5 L_{\min}$ multiplier:** KTU numericals often specify the multiplier; missing it loses 1 mark.
> 4. **A/F ratio for gasoline = 14.7 (stoichiometric):** Marked wrong if you write 15.1 or 14.0. The exact number 14.7 is the KTU-expected value.
> 5. **TWC requires $\lambda \approx 1$ ± 1 %:** Any answer saying "excess air helps the TWC" is a **zero mark** answer — excess air is the *enemy* of the TWC.
> 6. **Condenser ≠ Cooler:** Condensers are designed with phase change in mind; the LMTD equation applies to the condensing side separately.
> 7. **BS-VI norm year:** India jumped from BS-IV (2017) directly to BS-VI in **2020** (BS-V was skipped). Marks lost if you say "BS-V in 2019."
> 8. **Units in HTU-NTU problems:** Be consistent. Mixing kmol/h with m³/s in $K_G a$ loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

- **Adsorption** is a **surface** phenomenon; **Absorption** is a **bulk** phenomenon. Don't mix them up.
- The **Freundlich isotherm** $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ is **empirical**; the **Langmuir isotherm** is based on **mono-layer assumption** with **finite active sites**.
- A **breakthrough curve** describes how a fixed-bed adsorber exhausts; the **MTZ** length indicates efficiency.
- **Two-film theory** gives the series-resistance relation $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{H}{k_L}$.
- **Henry's constant $H$** determines which film (gas or liquid) **controls** the mass transfer.
- **Packed tower height** $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$. **Minimum liquid rate** $L_{\min}$ occurs when the operating line touches the equilibrium curve.
- **Condensation** works only for **high-concentration**, **high-boiling-point** VOCs; otherwise uneconomical.
- **Combustion** for air pollution control: **thermal** (700–1000 °C), **catalytic** (300–500 °C), or **flare** (>1000 °C).
- **Excess air** in combustion is typically **10–20 %** to ensure complete oxidation of CO.
- **Three-way catalytic converter (TWC)** requires **stoichiometric A/F ≈ 14.7** and **lambda window 0.99–1.01**.
- **EGR** lowers NOₓ by reducing combustion temperature; **PCV** captures blow-by gases; **charcoal canister** captures evaporative HC.
- **BS-VI** norms are equivalent to **Euro 6**; India leapfrogged BS-V in 2020.
- **Activated carbon** is the workhorse adsorbent for VOCs due to its **800–1500 m²/g** surface area.
- The **slope of the operating line $L/G$** must exceed **the slope of the equilibrium line $m$** for a finite packed tower to exist.
- **Equivalence ratio $\phi = 1$** at stoichiometry; $\phi > 1$ = rich; $\phi < 1$ = lean.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suggestion on Project Topics - Project I: Collect Sample from Any Polluted Water Source or Effluent from Nearby Industry (Food Processing, Metal Polishing, Pulp and Paper, etc.), Analyze the Physical, Chemical and Biological Characteristics and Suggest a Treatment Process with Required Primary, Secondary and Tertiary Equipment to Treat the Effluent According to the Disposable Standards Specified by Legislation, Use Modelling and Simulation Software to Design the Process; Project II: Collect Air Sample from Flue Gas, Urban Polluted Area, Analyze Air Quality Index and Design Suitable Air Quality Monitoring/Control Equipment; Project III: Collect Any Solid Waste Sample (E-Waste, Hazardous Waste), Analyze the Characteristics and Suggest/Design Treatment Process/Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Definition of Environmental Engineering Project Work

**Environmental Engineering Project Work** is a structured, application-oriented academic activity in the KTU 2024 B.Tech curriculum where students integrate theoretical principles of environmental science with hands-on field sampling, laboratory characterization, legislative compliance, and process/equipment design. The projects bridge the gap between classroom concepts (mass balance, reaction kinetics, reactor design) and field-scale environmental management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note (Module 4):** The three projects listed in this module collectively cover the **three environmental media** — **Water (Project I)**, **Air (Project II)**, and **Solid/Hazardous Waste (Project III)**. At least one project must be completed and documented as a formal report with a viva-voce assessment, evaluated against Course Outcomes CO4 and CO5.

---

## 1.2 Project I — Wastewater Characterization & Treatment Design

### Formal Definition
**Project I** involves the collection of a representative wastewater sample from a polluted water body or an industrial effluent stream, followed by systematic analysis of its **Physical**, **Chemical**, and **Biological** characteristics. The data obtained is compared against the **discharge standards** notified by regulatory bodies, and a complete train of **Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary treatment units** is proposed and dimensioned using modelling/simulation software.

### Intuitive Analogy
> **Analogy — "The Sick Patient & The Doctor":**
> Imagine a river or an industry outlet as a "sick patient" admitted to a hospital. The water/eflluent is the "blood sample." Your job as an environmental engineer is to act like a doctor:
> 1. **Sample Collection (Blood Test):** Draw a small, representative portion.
> 2. **Physical Tests (Temperature, Colour, Turbidity):** Like checking the patient's pulse and skin tone.
> 3. **Chemical Tests (pH, BOD, COD, Heavy Metals):** Like a blood biochemistry panel.
> 4. **Biological Tests (MFC, Fecal Coliforms):** Like checking for infection-causing bacteria.
> 5. **Diagnosis vs. Standards:** Compare against the "healthy" reference ranges (CPCB/KSPCB norms).
> 6. **Prescription (Treatment Train):** Design a sequence of "medicines" (unit operations) to cure the patient.
> 7. **Follow-up (Effluent Monitoring):** Verify that the "discharge" meets health standards.

### Key Parameters Monitored (Wastewater)
- **Physical:** Temperature, Colour, Odour, Turbidity, Total Suspended Solids (TSS), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS).
- **Chemical:** pH, Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD), Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN), Total Phosphorus, Oil & Grease, Heavy Metals (Pb, Cd, Cr, Hg, As, Ni, Zn, Cu).
- **Biological:** Most Probable Number (MPN) of coliforms, Fecal Coliforms, Total Plate Count, Specific pathogens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Legislative Backing (India):** Discharge standards for effluents are governed by the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**, with the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** issuing **Schedule VI** standards for Inland Surface Water, **Schedule II** for Sewage Treatment Plants, and industry-specific norms under the **Hazardous Waste (Management & Handling) Rules, 1989 (now 2016)**.

---

## 1.3 Project II — Air Quality Monitoring & Control Equipment Design

### Formal Definition
**Project II** involves the collection of air samples (from a flue gas stack or an urban ambient location), measurement of key **Criteria Pollutants** (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb), computation of the **Air Quality Index (AQI)**, and design of an air quality monitoring network and/or suitable control equipment (e.g., cyclone, baghouse, ESP, scrubber, or catalytic converter).

### Intuitive Analogy
> **Analogy — "The Smoggy City & The Traffic Light":**
> The atmosphere in a polluted city is like a busy intersection with too many cars (pollutants). Your "traffic light" is the **Air Quality Index (AQI)**:
> - **Green (0–50):** Clear, healthy — go!
> - **Yellow (51–100):** Moderate — caution.
> - **Orange (101–200):** Unhealthy for sensitive groups — slow down.
> - **Red (201–300):** Unhealthy — stop outdoor activity.
> - **Purple (301–400):** Very Unhealthy — emergency.
> - **Maroon (>401):** Hazardous — stay indoors.
>
> Your project's **AQI calculation** is the engineer who decides which colour the "traffic light" should display based on the *worst pollutant* (the "trump card" sub-index).

### Pollutants Measured (CPCB National AQI)
- **PM₂.₅** (Particulate Matter < 2.5 µm)
- **PM₁₀** (Particulate Matter < 10 µm)
- **SO₂** (Sulphur Dioxide)
- **NO₂** (Nitrogen Dioxide)
- **CO** (Carbon Monoxide)
- **O₃** (Ozone)
- **NH₃** (Ammonia)
- **Pb** (Lead)

---

## 1.4 Project III — Solid/Hazardous Waste Characterization & Treatment Design

### Formal Definition
**Project III** involves the collection of a solid waste sample (e.g., **e-waste**, **hazardous industrial waste**, **biomedical waste**, or **municipal solid waste**), its physico-chemical characterization, and the proposal of a suitable treatment/disposal pathway such as **recycling, incineration, secured landfilling, bioremediation, or pyrolysis**, along with equipment sizing.

### Intuitive Analogy
> **Analogy — "The Recycling Factory":**
> A heap of mixed solid waste is like a messy wardrobe. Your engineering task is to:
> 1. **Sort** the items (manual/mechanical segregation).
> 2. **Test** each item's "fabric quality" (characterization — calorific value, moisture, heavy metals).
> 3. **Reuse** what can be reused (recycling of plastics, metals).
> 4. **Compost** the biodegradable fraction (biological treatment).
> 5. **Safely Lock Away** the toxic fraction in a "secure vault" (secured landfill) so it doesn't poison the soil/groundwater.

### Key Characterization Parameters
- **Physical:** Moisture content, Bulk density, Particle size, Calorific value.
- **Chemical:** Proximate analysis (Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, Ash), Ultimate analysis (C, H, N, S, O), Heavy metal leaching (TCLP — Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure).
- **Biological:** BOD/COD of leachate, Respirometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 2016 (India):** Schedule I lists **47 categories of hazardous wastes** and Schedule II specifies **disposal/treatment options**. E-waste is governed by the **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022**, which mandate Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR).

---

## 1.5 Standard Analytical Methods (APHA / IS / ASTM)

| Parameter | Standard Method | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| pH | Electrometric | APHA 4500-H⁺, IS 3025 (Part 11) |
| TSS | Gravimetric (103–105 °C) | APHA 2540 D |
| BOD₅ | 5-day BOD, 20 °C, Winkler | APHA 5210 B, IS 3025 (Part 44) |
| COD | Open Reflux / Closed Reflux Titrimetric | APHA 5220 B, IS 3025 (Part 58) |
| Oil & Grease | Partition Gravimetric | APHA 5520 B |
| Heavy Metals | AAS / ICP-OES | APHA 3111 / 3120 |
| PM₁₀ / PM₂.₅ | Gravimetric / Beta Attenuation | CPCB NAAQS |
| Calorific Value | Bomb Calorimeter | ASTM D5865 |

---

## 1.6 Learning Outcomes Mapping (KTU 2024 Scheme)

> [!NOTE]
> After completing the project work, the student will be able to:
> - **CO4:** Apply analytical methods to characterize wastewater, air, and solid waste samples.
> - **CO5:** Design treatment processes and equipment in compliance with Indian environmental legislation.
> - **PO5 (Modern Tool Usage):** Use modelling/simulation software (e.g., GPS-X, BioWin, AERMOD, SimaPro, GABI) for process design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Project I — Wastewater Engineering Theory

### 2.1.1 Physical Characteristics — The "Look, Feel, and Sight" Tests
1. **Temperature:** Affects dissolved oxygen (DO) saturation, biological reaction rates (van't Hoff rule: rate doubles for every 10 °C rise, up to ~30 °C), and viscosity.
2. **Colour & Odour:** Caused by dissolved organics (humic acids, phenols, H₂S).
3. **Turbidity:** Measured in **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)** using a nephelometer with formazin primary standard.
4. **Total Solids (TS) = TSS + TDS + SS (Settleable Solids).**
5. **Conductivity:** Indicates ionic strength; pure water ≈ 0.05–1 µS/cm, domestic sewage ≈ 500–1500 µS/cm.

### 2.1.2 Chemical Characteristics — The "Hidden Chemistry"
1. **pH:** Measure of H⁺ activity; most biological processes work best at **pH 6.5–8.5**.
2. **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand, 5-day, 20 °C):** The amount of O₂ consumed by microorganisms to biochemically oxidize biodegradable organic matter.
3. **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand):** O₂ equivalent of the organics oxidized by a strong chemical oxidant (K₂Cr₂O₇ in H₂SO₄).
4. **The BOD/COD Ratio (Biodegradability Index):**
   - **BOD/COD > 0.5** → Highly biodegradable (e.g., domestic sewage).
   - **BOD/COD = 0.3–0.5** → Moderately biodegradable.
   - **BOD/COD < 0.3** → Refractory / toxic (e.g., metal finishing effluent).
5. **Nutrients:** Nitrogen (TKN = Organic N + NH₃-N) and Phosphorus; ratio **BOD:N:P ≈ 100:5:1** is ideal for biological treatment.
6. **Heavy Metals:** Toxic to microbes even at low concentrations; must be removed *before* biological treatment.

### 2.1.3 Biological Characteristics — The "Living Component"
1. **Total Coliforms (TC):** Indicator organism; measured by **MPN (Most Probable Number)** method or **membrane filtration**.
2. **Fecal Coliforms (FC):** More specific indicator of recent fecal contamination; should be **< 1000 MPN/100 mL** for bathing waters (CPCB).

### 2.1.4 Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary Treatment — The "Three-Stage Cure"

| Stage | Purpose | Unit Operations / Processes | Typical Removal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Remove settleable & floatable solids | Bar screens, Grit chamber, Primary clarifier, Equalization tank | 50–70% TSS, 25–40% BOD |
| **Secondary** | Remove dissolved & colloidal organics via biological action | Activated Sludge Process (ASP), Trickling Filter (TF), Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC), Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR), Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR), Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) | 80–95% BOD, 80% TSS |
| **Tertiary** | Polishing, nutrient removal, disinfection | Sand filtration, Activated Carbon, Reverse Osmosis (RO), UV, Chlorination, Ion exchange, Nitrification-Denitrification, Chemical P removal | N & P > 80%, pathogens > 99.9% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Effluent Specifics:**
> - **Food Processing (Dairy/Brewery):** High BOD (~1000–3000 mg/L); **UASB + SBR** preferred.
> - **Metal Polishing/Electroplating:** Toxic heavy metals (Cr, Ni, Zn); **Chemical precipitation + ion exchange** before biological treatment.
> - **Pulp & Paper:** High colour, lignin, COD; **Primary clarification + Aerated lagoon + Ozonation** for decolourization.

---

## 2.2 Project II — Air Quality Engineering Theory

### 2.2.1 Air Quality Index (AQI) — India CPCB Methodology
The Indian National AQI is computed using **8 pollutants** with their **24-hourly average breakpoints**. The AQI is the **maximum of the 8 sub-indices**.

#### Sub-Index Formula (Linear Interpolation)
For a measured concentration $C_i$ of pollutant $i$ that falls in the breakpoint range $[BP_{\text{Lo}}, BP_{\text{Hi}}]$ corresponding to AQI range $[I_{\text{Lo}}, I_{\text{Hi}}]$:

$$I_i = \frac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{BP_{\text{Hi}} - BP_{\text{Lo}}} \times (C_i - BP_{\text{Lo}}) + I_{\text{Lo}}$$

where:
- $I_i$ = Sub-Index for pollutant $i$
- $C_i$ = Measured/truncated concentration of pollutant $i$
- $BP_{\text{Hi}}$ = Concentration breakpoint that is $\geq C_i$
- $BP_{\text{Lo}}$ = Concentration breakpoint that is $\leq C_i$
- $I_{\text{Hi}}$ = AQI value corresponding to $BP_{\text{Hi}}$
- $I_{\text{Lo}}$ = AQI value corresponding to $BP_{\text{Lo}}$

$$AQI = \max(I_{PM_{2.5}}, I_{PM_{10}}, I_{SO_2}, I_{NO_2}, I_{CO}, I_{O_3}, I_{NH_3}, I_{Pb})$$

#### Breakpoints (NAQI, CPCB — Selected Excerpts)

| PM₂.₅ (µg/m³) | PM₁₀ (µg/m³) | SO₂ (µg/m³) | NO₂ (µg/m³) | CO (mg/m³) | O₃ (µg/m³) | NH₃ (µg/m³) | Pb (µg/m³) | AQI | Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0–30 | 0–50 | 0–40 | 0–40 | 0–1.0 | 0–50 | 0–200 | 0–0.5 | 0–50 | Good |
| 31–60 | 51–100 | 41–80 | 41–80 | 1.1–2.0 | 51–100 | 201–400 | 0.5–1.0 | 51–100 | Satisfactory |
| 61–90 | 101–250 | 81–380 | 81–180 | 2.1–10 | 101–168 | 401–800 | 1.0–2.0 | 101–200 | Moderate |
| 91–120 | 251–350 | 381–800 | 181–280 | 10.1–17 | 169–208 | 801–1200 | 2.0–3.0 | 201–300 | Poor |
| 121–250 | 351–430 | 801–1600 | 281–400 | 17.1–34 | 209–748 | 1201–1800 | 3.0–3.5 | 301–400 | Very Poor |
| 250+ | 430+ | 1600+ | 400+ | 34+ | 748+ | 1800+ | 3.5+ | 401–500 | Severe |

### 2.2.2 Particulate Control Equipment — Selection Guide

| Equipment | Removal Efficiency (PM) | Min. Particle Size | ΔP | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Settling Chamber** | Low (~50%) | > 50 µm | Very low | Pre-cleaning |
| **Cyclone** | Medium (70–90%) | > 10 µm | Medium | Boiler flue gas |
| **Wet Scrubber** | High (95–99%) | > 1 µm | High | Acidic/corrosive gases |
| **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** | Very High (>99.9%) | > 0.5 µm | Very low | Coal-fired power plants |
| **Baghouse (Fabric Filter)** | Very High (>99.9%) | > 0.3 µm | Medium | Cement, pharma |

### 2.2.3 Gaseous Control Equipment
- **SO₂:** Wet scrubber (Lime/Limestone), Dry sorbent injection, Flue Gas Desulphurization (FGD).
- **NOₓ:** Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR), Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction (SNCR).
- **VOCs:** Activated Carbon Adsorption, Thermal Oxidation, Biofilter.

---

## 2.3 Project III — Solid Waste Engineering Theory

### 2.3.1 Proximate & Ultimate Analysis
**Proximate Analysis (wt %):**
- **Moisture Content (MC):** $MC = \dfrac{W_{\text{wet}} - W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{wet}}} \times 100$
- **Volatile Matter (VM):** Weight loss at 550 ± 5 °C (in absence of air).
- **Ash Content:** Residue after combustion at 815 ± 10 °C.
- **Fixed Carbon (FC):** $FC = 100 - (MC + VM + Ash)$

**Ultimate Analysis (wt %, dry basis):** C, H, N, S, O (by difference), Cl.

### 2.3.2 Calorific Value (CV)
**Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** is determined in a Bomb Calorimeter.
**Net Calorific Value (NCV)** for combustion engineering:

$$NCV = GCV - \left( \dfrac{9 \times H}{100} + \dfrac{M}{100} \right) \times 587$$

where $H$ = % Hydrogen, $M$ = % Moisture (as received basis).

### 2.3.3 TCLP — Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure
Used to classify a waste as **hazardous** under US EPA (40 CFR 261) and Indian HW Rules. The waste is leached with **acetic acid buffer (pH 2.88 ± 0.05)** at an L/S ratio of 20:1 for 18 hours. The leachate is analysed for heavy metals; if concentrations exceed specified thresholds, the waste is hazardous.

### 2.3.4 Solid Waste Treatment Hierarchy
1. **Source Reduction / Minimization** (most preferred).
2. **Reuse.**
3. **Recycling / Recovery** (Material Recovery Facility — MRF).
4. **Energy Recovery** (Incineration with energy recovery, RDF, Biomethanation, Pyrolysis).
5. **Treatment** (Composting, Anaerobic Digestion, Bioremediation).
6. **Disposal** (Secured/Sanitary Landfill — last resort).

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (ALL PROJECTS)

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Solids | $TS = TSS + TDS$ | mg/L | — |
| 2 | BOD/COD Ratio | $B_i = \dfrac{BOD_5}{COD}$ | dimensionless | Biodegradability index |
| 3 | COD/BOD Ratio (typical domestic) | $\dfrac{COD}{BOD_5} \approx 2.0$ | dimensionless | Use for cross-verification |
| 4 | Volumetric BOD Loading (Aeration Tank) | $U = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | kg BOD/m³·d | Activated sludge design |
| 5 | F/M Ratio (Food to Microorganism) | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | d⁻¹ | 0.2–0.5 d⁻¹ for conventional ASP |
| 6 | Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | $HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | h or d | Reactor design |
| 7 | Solids Retention Time (SRT / θc) | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | d | Determines sludge age |
| 8 | Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT) | $MCRT = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w}$ | d | For wasted sludge only |
| 9 | Sludge Volume Index (SVI) | $SVI = \dfrac{V_{30} \times 1000}{X}$ | mL/g | Good settleability if 50–150 |
| 10 | Recycle Ratio (RAS) | $R = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q}$ | dimensionless | Typical 0.25–1.0 |
| 11 | Oxygen Requirement | $O_2 = a \cdot Q(S_0 - S) + b \cdot V \cdot X$ | kg O₂/d | a = 0.5–0.65, b = 0.05–0.15 |
| 12 | AQI Sub-Index (linear) | $I_i = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \times (C_i - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | dimensionless | Per pollutant |
| 13 | AQI (Overall) | $AQI = \max\{I_i\}$ | dimensionless | Trump card rule |
| 14 | Stack Height (EPA, India) | $H = 14 \cdot \sqrt{Q_h}$ for $Q_h > 0.05$ m³/s | m | Q_h = flue gas flow |
| 15 | Cyclone Efficiency (Lapple) | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + (d_{pc}/d_p)^2}$ | % | $d_{pc}$ = cut size |
| 16 | Collection Efficiency (ESP) | $\eta = 1 - e^{-\omega \cdot A/Q}$ | % | Deutsch-Anderson equation |
| 17 | Moisture Content | $MC = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$ | % | — |
| 18 | Fixed Carbon | $FC = 100 - (MC + VM + Ash)$ | % | Proximate analysis |
| 19 | Net Calorific Value | $NCV = GCV - \left(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\right) \cdot 587$ | kcal/kg | Combustion |
| 20 | Settling Velocity (Stokes) | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_w) \cdot d_p^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$ | m/s | Re < 0.2 only |
| 21 | Detention Time (Clarifier) | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | h | 1.5–2.5 h for primary |
| 22 | Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) | $SOR = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m³/m²·d | 30–50 m³/m²·d for primary |
| 23 | Excess Sludge Production | $P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$ | kg/d | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** In KTU project viva, students are frequently asked *why* they chose a particular formula. The justification for selecting an equation (e.g., using Stokes' Law only when $Re < 0.2$) is worth more marks than the substitution arithmetic.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — AQI Sub-Index (Trapezoidal Linear Interpolation)

### 3.1.1 Conceptual Foundation
The Indian AQI breakpoints divide the pollutant concentration axis into piecewise-linear ranges. Within each range, the AQI is **linearly interpolated** between two known (Concentration, AQI) anchor points.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation
Let two anchor points be:
- Anchor 1: $(BP_{\text{Lo}}, I_{\text{Lo}})$
- Anchor 2: $(BP_{\text{Hi}}, I_{\text{Hi}})$

The slope of the line connecting these points is:

$$m = \dfrac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{BP_{\text{Hi}} - BP_{\text{Lo}}}$$

The linear equation through Anchor 1 is:

$$I_i - I_{\text{Lo}} = m \cdot (C_i - BP_{\text{Lo}})$$

Substituting $m$:

$$I_i - I_{\text{Lo}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{BP_{\text{Hi}} - BP_{\text{Lo}}} \cdot (C_i - BP_{\text{Lo}})$$

Rearranging:

$$I_i = \left[ \dfrac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{BP_{\text{Hi}} - BP_{\text{Lo}}} \right] \cdot (C_i - BP_{\text{Lo}}) + I_{\text{Lo}}$$

This is the **CPCB AQI sub-index formula**.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A monitoring station reports the following 24-hour average concentrations. Compute the AQI.

| Pollutant | $C_i$ (µg/m³) |
|---|---|
| PM₂.₅ | 78 |
| PM₁₀ | 145 |
| SO₂ | 52 |
| NO₂ | 65 |
| CO | 1.5 mg/m³ |
| O₃ | 95 |
| NH₃ | 350 |
| Pb | 0.6 |

**Solution — Step 1: PM₂.₅ sub-index**
- $C_i = 78$ falls in the range $[61, 90]$ corresponding to AQI $[101, 200]$.
- $BP_{\text{Lo}} = 61$, $BP_{\text{Hi}} = 90$, $I_{\text{Lo}} = 101$, $I_{\text{Hi}} = 200$.

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = \left[ \dfrac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} \right] \cdot (78 - 61) + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = \left[ \dfrac{99}{29} \right] \cdot 17 + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = 3.4138 \cdot 17 + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = 58.0345 + 101 \approx 159$$

**Solution — Step 2: PM₁₀ sub-index**
- $C_i = 145$ falls in $[101, 250]$ corresponding to AQI $[101, 200]$.

$$I_{PM_{10}} = \left[ \dfrac{200 - 101}{250 - 101} \right] \cdot (145 - 101) + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{10}} = \dfrac{99}{149} \cdot 44 + 101 = 0.6644 \cdot 44 + 101 = 29.235 + 101 \approx 130$$

**Solution — Step 3: SO₂ sub-index**
- $C_i = 52$ falls in $[41, 80]$ corresponding to AQI $[51, 100]$.

$$I_{SO_2} = \dfrac{100 - 51}{80 - 41} \cdot (52 - 41) + 51 = \dfrac{49}{39} \cdot 11 + 51 = 13.82 + 51 \approx 65$$

**Solution — Step 4: NO₂ sub-index**
- $C_i = 65$ falls in $[41, 80]$ corresponding to AQI $[51, 100]$.

$$I_{NO_2} = \dfrac{49}{39} \cdot (65 - 41) + 51 = 1.2564 \cdot 24 + 51 = 30.15 + 51 \approx 81$$

**Solution — Step 5: CO sub-index** (1.5 mg/m³ falls in [1.1, 2.0] → [51, 100])

$$I_{CO} = \dfrac{49}{0.9} \cdot (1.5 - 1.1) + 51 = 54.44 \cdot 0.4 + 51 = 21.78 + 51 \approx 73$$

**Solution — Step 6: O₃ sub-index** (95 in [51, 100] → [51, 100])

$$I_{O_3} = \dfrac{49}{49} \cdot (95 - 51) + 51 = 1.0 \cdot 44 + 51 = 95$$

**Solution — Step 7: NH₃ sub-index** (350 in [201, 400] → [51, 100])

$$I_{NH_3} = \dfrac{49}{199} \cdot (350 - 201) + 51 = 0.2462 \cdot 149 + 51 = 36.69 + 51 \approx 88$$

**Solution — Step 8: Pb sub-index** (0.6 in [0.5, 1.0] → [51, 100])

$$I_{Pb} = \dfrac{49}{0.5} \cdot (0.6 - 0.5) + 51 = 98 \cdot 0.1 + 51 = 9.8 + 51 \approx 61$$

**Final AQI Calculation:**

$$AQI = \max\{159, 130, 65, 81, 73, 95, 88, 61\} = 159$$

**Category:** **Moderate (101–200)** — Air quality is acceptable; however, there may be a risk for some people who are unusually sensitive to air pollution.

> **Identifying the "Critical" Pollutant:** PM₂.₅ drives the AQI in this case. Engineers must focus control strategies on **fine particulate reduction** (e.g., bag filters, ESP upgrades, vehicular emission norms).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Activated Sludge Process Design (F/M and Aeration Tank Volume)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
A municipal STP receives $Q = 25{,}000$ m³/d of sewage with influent BOD $S_0 = 250$ mg/L and MLSS $X = 3000$ mg/L. Design the aeration tank volume for a conventional activated sludge process operating at $\dfrac{F}{M} = 0.3$ d⁻¹.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Convert BOD and MLSS to consistent units (kg/m³).

$$S_0 = 250 \text{ mg/L} = 0.25 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$X = 3000 \text{ mg/L} = 3.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Express the F/M definition:

$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $V$:

$$V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{\left(\dfrac{F}{M}\right) \cdot X}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values:

$$V = \dfrac{25{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \cdot 0.25 \text{ kg/m}^3}{0.3 \text{ d}^{-1} \cdot 3.0 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

$$V = \dfrac{6250}{0.9} = 6944.4 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5:** Compute Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):

$$HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{6944.4}{25{,}000} = 0.2778 \text{ d} = 6.67 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6:** Compute Aeration Tank Dimensions (assume L:B = 2:1, depth = 4.5 m):

$$A_{\text{surface}} = \dfrac{V}{\text{depth}} = \dfrac{6944.4}{4.5} = 1543.2 \text{ m}^2$$

If $L = 2B$, then $2B \cdot B = 1543.2 \Rightarrow B^2 = 771.6 \Rightarrow B = 27.78 \text{ m}$, $L = 55.56 \text{ m}$.

**Step 7:** Compute Oxygen Requirement (using $a = 0.55$, $b = 0.10$):

$$O_2 = a \cdot Q(S_0 - S) + b \cdot V \cdot X$$

Assuming effluent BOD $S = 20$ mg/L = 0.02 kg/m³:

$$O_2 = 0.55 \cdot 25{,}000 \cdot (0.25 - 0.02) + 0.10 \cdot 6944.4 \cdot 3.0$$

$$O_2 = 0.55 \cdot 25{,}000 \cdot 0.23 + 2083.3$$

$$O_2 = 3162.5 + 2083.3 = 5245.8 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$

$$O_2 = \dfrac{5245.8}{24} = 218.6 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{h} \approx 6.07 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** Actual air supply ≈ 4–8 × theoretical oxygen (depending on OTE of diffusers), so air requirement ≈ **1800–3500 m³/h** for this plant.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Excess Sludge Production (Observed Yield)

### 3.3.1 Mass Balance Derivation
For an activated sludge system in steady state, the observed yield $Y_{\text{obs}}$ accounts for endogenous decay:

$$Y_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$

where:
- $Y$ = True yield coefficient (≈ 0.4–0.6 kg VSS/kg BOD for domestic sewage)
- $k_d$ = Endogenous decay coefficient (≈ 0.04–0.08 d⁻¹)
- $\theta_c$ = Mean cell residence time (SRT)

Excess sludge production per day:

$$P_X = Y_{\text{obs}} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$$

### 3.3.2 Numerical Example
Given $Y = 0.5$, $k_d = 0.06$ d⁻¹, $\theta_c = 10$ d, $Q = 25{,}000$ m³/d, $S_0 = 250$ mg/L, $S = 20$ mg/L:

**Step 1:** Compute $Y_{\text{obs}}$:

$$Y_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{0.5}{1 + 0.06 \cdot 10} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.6} = 0.3125$$

**Step 2:** BOD removed:

$$S_0 - S = 0.25 - 0.02 = 0.23 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Sludge production:

$$P_X = 0.3125 \cdot 25{,}000 \cdot 0.23 = 1796.9 \text{ kg VSS/d}$$

**Step 4:** In dry tonnes/year:

$$P_X = \dfrac{1796.9 \cdot 365}{1000} = 655.9 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cyclone Separator Cut Diameter (Lapple's Method)

### 3.4.1 Concept
The **cut diameter** $d_{50}$ is the particle diameter for which the cyclone has **50% collection efficiency**. It defines the boundary between efficient and inefficient particle removal.

### 3.4.2 Derivation
The terminal settling velocity inside a cyclone (Stokes regime) is:

$$v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_g) \cdot d_p^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

For a particle that spends a time $t$ in the cyclone (typically 2–5 turns around the vortex), the radial displacement is:

$$r = v_s \cdot t$$

Setting $r = R$ (cyclone radius) at $d_{50}$:

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \cdot \mu \cdot R}{g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_g) \cdot t}}$$

For Lapple's standard cyclone, $t \approx \pi N_e \cdot B / v_i$ (where $N_e$ = effective turns ≈ 5, $B$ = inlet width, $v_i$ = inlet velocity). Substituting:

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \cdot \mu \cdot B}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot N_e \cdot v_i \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}$$

### 3.4.3 Efficiency Curve (Lapple)

$$\eta(d_p) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{d_{50}}{d_p}\right)^2}$$

### 3.4.4 Numerical Example
Given $d_{50} = 10$ µm, find efficiency at $d_p = 5$ µm and 20 µm:

$$\eta(5) = \dfrac{1}{1 + (10/5)^2} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 4} = 0.20 = 20\%$$

$$\eta(20) = \dfrac{1}{1 + (10/20)^2} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.25} = 0.80 = 80\%$$

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Project Workflow (Universally Applicable)

1. **Objective Definition:** State the goal and the standard to be complied with.
2. **Site/Sample Selection:** Justify the choice of industry/location (proximity, type of pollution).
3. **Sample Collection:**
   - Wastewater: **Grab vs. Composite sampling** (preferred: 24-h composite, refrigerated at 4 °C).
   - Air: **Isokinetic sampling** for stack; **Respirable Dust Sampler (RDS)** for ambient.
   - Solid waste: **Quartering & Coning** to obtain a representative sample.
4. **Preservation:** Use appropriate preservatives (H₂SO₄ for BOD/COD, HNO₃ for metals).
5. **Laboratory Analysis:** Follow IS/APHA/ASTM methods.
6. **Data Comparison:** Tabulate against standards.
7. **Process Selection:** Justify choice of treatment train based on influent quality, space, cost.
8. **Design Calculations:** Use formulae from §2.4.
9. **Equipment Sizing:** Provide dimensions and auxiliary specs.
10. **Simulation:** Use GPS-X, BioWin, AERMOD, SimaPro, or GABI for validation.
11. **Report Writing & Presentation:** Include flow diagrams, mass balance, and a discussion of environmental and economic feasibility.

```mermaid
    RDS --> LAB2[Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Station CAAQMS]
    LAB1 --> AQI[AQI Calculation Sub Index for each Pollutant]
    LAB2 --> AQI
    AQI --> CRIT{Identify Critical Pollutant}
    CRIT --> CYC[Cyclone Separator]
    CRIT --> ESP[Electrostatic Precipitator]
    CRIT --> BAG[Baghouse Filter]
    CRIT --> SCR[Wet Scrubber for SO2]
    CRIT --> SCRB[SCR SNCR for NOx]
    CYC --> STK1[Cleaned Flue Gas to Atmosphere]
    ESP --> STK1
    BAG --> STK1
    SCR --> STK1
    SCRB --> STK1
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Mass Balance Concept (For Any Project)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input Stream Flow Qin and Cin] --> PROC[Process Unit Reactor or Separator]
    PROC --> OUT1[Output Stream 1 Flow Qout and Cout]
    PROC --> OUT2[Output Stream 2 Waste or By product]
    PROC -.-> ACC[Accumulation in steady state zero]
    IN1 -.-> MASS[Mass Balance Accum equals In minus Out plus Generation]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Set A]
**Q: Differentiate between BOD and COD. What does a low BOD/COD ratio indicate about the biodegradability of an effluent?**

**Model Answer:**
**BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand):** The amount of dissolved oxygen consumed by aerobic microorganisms in 5 days at 20 °C to biochemically oxidize the biodegradable organic matter. Measured via the Winkler iodometric method or probe (APHA 5210 B).

**COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand):** The amount of oxygen equivalent consumed in the chemical oxidation of organic matter by a strong oxidant (K₂Cr₂O₇ in 50% H₂SO₄) under reflux for 2 hours (APHA 5220 B).

| Aspect | BOD₅ | COD |
|---|---|---|
| Time | 5 days | 2–3 hours |
| Oxidation | Biological | Chemical (K₂Cr₂O₇) |
| Measures | Biodegradable organics | Total organics (bio + non-bio) |
| Typical value (domestic) | 200–300 mg/L | 400–600 mg/L |

**Low BOD/COD ratio (< 0.3):** Indicates that the effluent contains a **high proportion of non-biodegradable (refractory) organics** such as phenols, chlorinated hydrocarbons, dyes, lignin, or solvents. Such effluents are **poorly amenable to conventional biological treatment** and require **advanced oxidation processes (AOPs)**, **ozonation**, or **activated carbon adsorption** as tertiary treatment. (3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set B]
**Q: Define the Air Quality Index (AQI) as per CPCB. List the eight pollutants used in its computation, and explain the "trump card" rule.**

**Model Answer:**
**Definition:** AQI is a **piecewise-linear composite index** (range 0–500) that summarizes the cumulative ambient concentration of criteria pollutants into a single number and a corresponding health-risk category.

**Eight Pollutants (CPCB NAAQI):**
1. PM₂.₅
2. PM₁₀
3. SO₂
4. NO₂
5. CO
6. O₃
7. NH₃
8. Pb

**"Trump Card" Rule:** The overall AQI is taken as the **maximum** of the eight sub-indices, i.e.,

$$AQI = \max\{I_{PM_{2.5}}, I_{PM_{10}}, I_{SO_2}, I_{NO_2}, I_{CO}, I_{O_3}, I_{NH_3}, I_{Pb}\}$$

**Significance:** The "worst-performing" pollutant governs the health advisory. If PM₂.₅ is the highest sub-index, public-health warnings focus on particulate reduction (mask advisory, construction bans), even if all other pollutants are "Satisfactory". (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Project I Deep Dive

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** A food-processing industry (dairy/brewery) generates 800 m³/d of effluent with BOD₅ = 1800 mg/L, COD = 3600 mg/L, TSS = 600 mg/L, pH = 5.5, and TKN = 80 mg/L. Propose a complete treatment train (primary + secondary + tertiary) to comply with CPCB discharge norms for Inland Surface Water. Justify the choice of each unit.

> **(b) [7 marks)** Design the volume of the **UASB reactor** and the **SBR tank** for the above effluent, given:
> - UASB organic loading rate (OLR) = 6 kg COD/m³·d
> - UASB upflow velocity = 0.7 m/h
> - SBR F/M = 0.25 d⁻¹, MLSS = 4000 mg/L
> - SBR fill:react:aerate:settle:draw = 1:1:2:1:1 (cycle = 6 h)

---

#### Model Solution — Q (a)

**Step 1: Compare influent with CPCB Inland Surface Water Standards (Schedule VI, Part A)**

| Parameter | Influent | Standard (mg/L) | Compliance? |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 5.5 | 5.5–9.0 | OK (marginal) |
| BOD₅ | 1800 | 30 | Violated |
| COD | 3600 | 250 | Violated |
| TSS | 600 | 100 | Violated |
| TKN | 80 | 100 | Compliant (but high) |

**Step 2: Proposed Treatment Train**

| Stage | Unit | Purpose | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-Treatment** | Bar screen (6 mm) | Remove rags, large solids | Protect downstream pumps |
| | Grit chamber | Remove sand, silt (velocity ≈ 0.3 m/s) | Prevent abrasion in UASB |
| **Primary** | Equalization tank (V = 8 h HRT) | Buffer flow and concentration fluctuations | Critical for food industry batch discharge |
| | Neutralization tank | Adjust pH to 6.8–7.2 using NaOH | Protect methanogens in UASB |
| | Primary Clarifier (SOR 30 m³/m²·d) | Remove 50–60% TSS, 30% BOD | Reduce organic load to UASB |
| **Secondary** | **UASB Reactor** | Anaerobic digestion of high-COD waste | OLR of 6 kg COD/m³·d suits dairy effluent; biogas recovery |
| | **SBR** (post-aerobic) | Polish BOD, nitrify ammonia | F/M = 0.25 d⁻¹ suited to food industry; flexible operation |
| **Tertiary** | Sand filter (0.5 m, 10 m/h) | Remove residual TSS | Bring TSS < 30 mg/L |
| | Activated Carbon Column | Remove colour and refractory COD | BOD/COD already low in UASB effluent |
| | UV Disinfection (40 mJ/cm²) | Disinfect final effluent | CPCB mandates fecal coliforms < 1000 MPN/100 mL |

**Mass Balance Summary (per day):**
- Influent COD = 800 × 3600 / 1000 = **2880 kg COD/d**
- UASB removal (80%) → leaves **576 kg COD/d**
- SBR removal (90%) → leaves **57.6 kg COD/d** → effluent COD = **72 mg/L** ✓ (< 250)
- SBR nitrification → TKN reduced from 80 to < 10 mg/L ✓

**Conclusion:** The proposed train achieves compliance with **CPCB Schedule VI Part A** norms. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q (b)

**Step 1: UASB Design**

$$V_{UASB} = \dfrac{Q \cdot COD_{in}}{OLR} = \dfrac{800 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \cdot 3.6 \text{ kg/m}^3}{6 \text{ kg COD/m}^3 \cdot \text{d}} = \dfrac{2880}{6} = 480 \text{ m}^3$$

Cross-check with upflow velocity $v_u = 0.7$ m/h and assume height $H = 6$ m:

$$A = \dfrac{V}{H} = \dfrac{480}{6} = 80 \text{ m}^2$$

$$v_u = \dfrac{Q}{24 \cdot A} = \dfrac{800}{24 \cdot 80} = 0.417 \text{ m/h}$$

Since 0.417 < 0.7 m/h ✓ (design acceptable, can increase $H$ to 8 m for better sludge contact).

Revised: $H = 8$ m → $A = 60$ m² → $v_u = 0.556$ m/h (acceptable).

**Step 2: SBR Design (operating on UASB effluent: BOD ≈ 360 mg/L)**

$$V_{SBR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{(F/M) \cdot X} = \dfrac{800 \cdot 0.36}{0.25 \cdot 4.0} = \dfrac{288}{1.0} = 288 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: SBR Cycle Time Verification**

Total cycle = 6 h, with 2 cycles/day per tank (or 2 parallel tanks with 1 cycle each).

$$HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{288}{800} = 0.36 \text{ d} = 8.64 \text{ h}$$

For batch operation with 2 tanks operating in parallel (one filling while the other reacts), each tank handles 400 m³/d:

$$HRT_{\text{each}} = \dfrac{288}{400} = 0.72 \text{ d} = 17.3 \text{ h}$$

**SBR Dimensions (assume depth 5 m, 2 tanks):**

$$A_{\text{each}} = \dfrac{288}{5} = 57.6 \text{ m}^2 \Rightarrow L:B = 2:1 \Rightarrow B \approx 5.4 \text{ m}, L \approx 10.7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Oxygen Requirement in SBR**

$$O_2 = a \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) + b \cdot V \cdot X$$

With $a = 0.6$, $b = 0.10$, $S = 20$ mg/L = 0.02 kg/m³, Q (per SBR) = 400 m³/d:

$$O_2 = 0.6 \cdot 400 \cdot (0.36 - 0.02) + 0.10 \cdot 288 \cdot 4.0 = 81.6 + 115.2 = 196.8 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d} = 8.2 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{h}$$

Air required (assuming 8% OTE) ≈ **102.5 kg air/h ≈ 1280 m³ air/h** per SBR tank.

**Final Design Summary:**

| Unit | Volume | Dimensions | HRT |
|---|---|---|---|
| UASB | 480 m³ | 60 m² × 8 m | 14.4 h |
| SBR (×2) | 288 m³ each | 57.6 m² × 5 m | 17.3 h per tank |

(7 marks)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Project II Deep Dive

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Compute the **AQI** for a metropolitan city where the following 24-hourly average pollutant concentrations were measured:

| Pollutant | Concentration |
|---|---|
| PM₂.₅ | 145 µg/m³ |
| PM₁₀ | 280 µg/m³ |
| SO₂ | 95 µg/m³ |
| NO₂ | 175 µg/m³ |
| CO | 12 mg/m³ |
| O₃ | 180 µg/m³ |
| NH₃ | 950 µg/m³ |
| Pb | 1.6 µg/m³ |

Identify the **critical pollutant** and recommend two engineering control measures.

> **(b) [7 marks]** Design a **multi-cyclone separator** for a coal-fired boiler with the following data:
> - Flue gas flow = 12 m³/s at 150 °C
> - Particle density = 2200 kg/m³
> - Gas viscosity at 150 °C = 2.4 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s
> - Gas density = 0.85 kg/m³
> - Required collection efficiency for 10 µm particles ≥ 90%
> - Inlet velocity = 15 m/s
> - Cyclone body diameter = 1.5 m

---

#### Model Solution — Q (a)

**AQI Sub-Index Calculations (using CPCB breakpoints):**

| Pollutant | $C_i$ | Range Used | $I_i$ Computation | Sub-Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PM₂.₅ | 145 µg/m³ | [121, 250] → [201, 300] | $\frac{99}{129}(145-121)+201$ = $0.767 \cdot 24 + 201$ | **≈ 219** |
| PM₁₀ | 280 µg/m³ | [251, 350] → [201, 300] | $\frac{99}{99}(280-251)+201$ = $29 + 201$ | **= 230** |
| SO₂ | 95 µg/m³ | [81, 380] → [101, 200] | $\frac{99}{299}(95-81)+101$ = $0.331 \cdot 14 + 101$ | ≈ 106 |
| NO₂ | 175 µg/m³ | [81, 180] → [101, 200] | $\frac{99}{99}(175-81)+101$ = $94 + 101$ | **= 195** |
| CO | 12 mg/m³ | [10.1, 17] → [201, 300] | $\frac{99}{6.9}(12-10.1)+201$ = $14.35 \cdot 1.9 + 201$ | ≈ 228 |
| O₃ | 180 µg/m³ | [169, 208] → [201, 300] | $\frac{99}{39}(180-169)+201$ = $2.538 \cdot 11 + 201$ | ≈ 229 |
| NH₃ | 950 µg/m³ | [801, 1200] → [201, 300] | $\frac{99}{399}(950-801)+201$ = $0.248 \cdot 149 + 201$ | ≈ 238 |
| Pb | 1.6 µg/m³ | [1.0, 2.0] → [101, 200] | $\frac{99}{1.0}(1.6-1.0)+101$ = $59.4 + 101$ | ≈ 160 |

**AQI Calculation:**

$$AQI = \max\{219, 230, 106, 195, 228, 229, 238, 160\} = 238$$

**Category:** **Poor (201–300)** → Health advisory: "Everyone may begin to experience health effects; members of sensitive groups may experience more serious health effects."

**Critical Pollutant:** **NH₃** (with PM₁₀ and O₃ as close competitors).

**Two Engineering Control Measures:**
1. **For NH₃ control:** Install a **wet scrubber** with dilute sulphuric acid (NH₃ + H₂SO₄ → (NH₄)₂SO₄, a fertilizer) for point sources, and promote **urea-based selective catalytic reduction (SCR)** in vehicles.
2. **For PM₁₀ control:** Upgrade boiler combustion with **low-NOx burners** and add a **baghouse filter** (efficiency > 99.9% for 10 µm particles) to the flue gas train.

(7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q (b)

**Step 1: Calculate the Cyclone Cut Diameter $d_{50}$ for a Single Cyclone**

For Lapple's standard cyclone with body diameter $D_c = 1.5$ m, the inlet width $B \approx D_c/4 = 0.375$ m. Effective turns $N_e = 5$.

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \cdot \mu \cdot B}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot N_e \cdot v_i \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}$$

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \cdot 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.375}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 5 \cdot 15 \cdot (2200 - 0.85)}}$$

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8.1 \times 10^{-5}}{942.0 \cdot 2199.15}}$$

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8.1 \times 10^{-5}}{2.07 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{3.91 \times 10^{-11}} = 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 6.25 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 2: Single Cyclone Efficiency for 10 µm Particles**

$$\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + (d_{50}/d_p)^2} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (6.25/10)^2} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.3906} = 0.719 = 71.9\%$$

Since **71.9% < 90% required**, a single cyclone is insufficient.

**Step 3: Design Multi-Cyclone Array**

A **parallel array of $N$ identical small cyclones** is used. The cut diameter scales as $\sqrt{D_c}$, while the number of cyclones scales inversely with the cross-section of each cyclone. For a small cyclone of diameter $D_s$, the cut diameter becomes:

$$d_{50,s} = d_{50,c} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{D_s}{D_c}}$$

Required: $\eta = 0.90$ at $d_p = 10$ µm.

$$\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + (d_{50,s}/d_p)^2} = 0.90 \Rightarrow (d_{50,s}/10)^2 = \dfrac{1}{0.9} - 1 = 0.1111$$

$$d_{50,s} = 10 \cdot \sqrt{0.1111} = 3.33 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 4: Solve for Small Cyclone Diameter $D_s$**

$$\dfrac{d_{50,s}}{d_{50,c}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D_s}{D_c}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{3.33}{6.25} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D_s}{1.5}}$$

$$0.533 = \sqrt{\dfrac{D_s}{1.5}} \Rightarrow D_s = 1.5 \cdot 0.284 = 0.426 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Number of Cyclones**

Cross-sectional area of small cyclone $\approx \pi D_s^2 / 4 = 0.1425$ m².
Total cross-section of multi-cyclone array to handle 12 m³/s at 15 m/s:

$$A_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{Q}{v_i} = \dfrac{12}{15} = 0.8 \text{ m}^2$$

$$N = \dfrac{A_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{small}}} = \dfrac{0.8}{0.1425} = 5.6 \Rightarrow \text{use } 6 \text{ cyclones in parallel}$$

**Step 6: Verify Pressure Drop**

$$\Delta P = \xi \cdot \dfrac{\rho_g \cdot v_i^2}{2} \text{ (with } \xi = 8 \text{ for Lapple)}$$

$$\Delta P = 8 \cdot \dfrac{0.85 \cdot 225}{2} = 765 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.077 \text{ m H}_2\text{O}$$

This is acceptable for the ID fan.

**Conclusion:** A **multi-cyclone array of 6 cyclones**, each of diameter **0.426 m**, will achieve 90% collection efficiency for 10 µm particles with a pressure drop of 765 Pa. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **AQI Calculation:** Students forget the **trump card rule** and instead compute the *average* sub-index. This is **WRONG**. AQI is the **maximum** sub-index, not the mean. Up to **3 marks lost**.
> 2. **BOD/COD Ratio:** Students fail to **interpret** the ratio qualitatively. They must explicitly state whether the effluent is biodegradable, moderately biodegradable, or refractory.
> 3. **UASB Design:** Students often design for the **wrong OLR** (e.g., 12 kg/m³·d is for industrial chemical effluent, not food processing which is 4–8 kg/m³·d). Always **cite the source** of the OLR value.
> 4. **Cyclone Cut Diameter:** Using **density of water (1000 kg/m³)** instead of the actual particle density in the Stokes formula — a common slip. The denominator must be $(\rho_p - \rho_g)$, NOT $\rho_p$.
> 5. **Standards:** Citing "CPCB" without specifying the **Schedule** (e.g., Schedule VI, Part A vs. Part B) is vague and **deducts 1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Units:** Forgetting unit conversions (mg/L ↔ kg/m³, µg/m³ ↔ ppb) is a perennial scoring killer.
> 7. **Mass Balance:** Many students design equipment **without** showing a mass balance table. A mass balance is a **mandatory component** of any KTU design question (worth at least 2 marks).
> 8. **Justification:** Choosing equipment **without justification** is treated as half-credit. Always state *why* a particular unit was selected (e.g., "UASB over ASP because of high COD and biogas recovery potential").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Project I — Wastewater**
> - Always classify effluent first by **BOD/COD ratio** (>0.5 biodegradable, <0.3 refractory).
> - **Physical tests:** Temperature, Colour, Turbidity, TSS, TDS.
> - **Chemical tests:** pH, BOD₅, COD, TKN, TP, Oil & Grease, Heavy metals.
> - **Biological tests:** MPN of coliforms, Fecal coliforms.
> - **Standards:** CPCB Schedule VI (Inland Surface Water), Schedule II (Sewage Treatment Plants), industry-specific norms.
> - **Treatment train:** Equalization → Primary Clarification → Biological (ASP/UASB/SBR) → Tertiary (Sand/AC/UV) → Disinfection.
> - **Design formulae:** F/M, HRT, SRT, SVI, oxygen requirement.
> - **Simulation software:** GPS-X, BioWin, WEST, SIMBA#, STOAT.

> **Project II — Air**
> - **8 criteria pollutants** (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb).
> - **AQI = max of sub-indices** (trump card rule).
> - **Sub-index formula** uses **linear interpolation** between breakpoints.
> - **Sampling:** Isokinetic (stack), RDS (ambient), CAAQMS (continuous).
> - **Particulate control:** Settling chamber → Cyclone → Scrubber → ESP/Baghouse.
> - **Gaseous control:** Alkaline scrubbers (SO₂), SCR/SNCR (NOₓ), activated carbon (VOCs).
> - **Stack height formula:** $H = 14 \sqrt{Q_h}$ (for thermal power plants, CPCB).
> - **Cyclone efficiency:** Lapple model; cut diameter scales as $\sqrt{D_c}$.
> - **ESP:** Deutsch-Anderson equation $\eta = 1 - e^{-\omega A/Q}$.

> **Project III — Solid/Hazardous Waste**
> - **Proximate analysis:** MC, VM, Ash, FC (where FC = 100 − MC − VM − Ash).
> - **Ultimate analysis:** C, H, N, S, O, Cl.
> - **Calorific value:** Bomb calorimeter; NCV correction for moisture and hydrogen.
> - **TCLP test** classifies waste as hazardous (US EPA 40 CFR 261 / HW Rules 2016, Schedule II).
> - **Hierarchy:** Source reduction → Reuse → Recycle → Energy recovery → Treatment → Disposal.
> - **Treatment technologies:** Composting, Anaerobic Digestion, Incineration, Pyrolysis, Secured Landfill, Stabilization/Solidification.
> - **E-waste:** Manual dismantling, Hydrometallurgy, Pyrometallurgy, Bioleaching.

> **Universal Project Skills (All Three Projects)**
> - **Sampling:** Use **composite sampling** for wastewater (24-h refrigerated at 4 °C); **isokinetic** for stack gas; **quartering and coning** for solid waste.
> - **Preservation:** H₂SO₄ for BOD/COD, HNO₃ for metals, NaOH for phenols.
> - **Methods:** Follow **APHA / IS 3025 / ASTM / CPCB NAAQS** rigorously.
> - **Reporting:** Always include **objective, site description, methodology, results, comparison with standards, design, mass balance, simulation output, and conclusions**.
> - **Legislation to know:** Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, EPA 1986, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, MSW Rules 2016, E-Waste Rules 2022, Plastic Waste Rules 2016.
